cases.title: A Boy With Sticky, Red Eyes in the Morning
cases.chiefComplaint: Both of my son's eyes are red and crusted shut when he wakes up.
cases.history: A 7-year-old boy is brought in by his mother for 3 days of bilateral red eyes with thick yellow-green discharge. The mother reports his eyelids are 'glued shut' each morning, requiring a warm washcloth to open them, and that discharge keeps reaccumulating through the day. It started in the right eye and spread to the left within a day. He denies eye pain, photophobia, or change in vision, though he rubs his eyes frequently. No recent fever, sore throat, or upper respiratory symptoms. Two classmates reportedly have 'pink eye.' He wears no contact lenses and had no eye trauma. PMH: mild intermittent atopic dermatitis. Medications: none. Allergies: NKDA. Immunizations up to date. Social: attends 2nd grade, lives with parents and a younger sibling; no household smoke exposure. Family history: mother has seasonal allergic rhinitis. The mother is worried because the discharge is so thick and keeps coming back even after she wipes it away, and she wants to know when he can return to school.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, afebrile, interactive child. Bilateral conjunctival injection with copious mucopurulent yellow-green discharge along the lid margins and lashes; lashes matted. Both eyelids mildly edematous and erythematous. No tenderness on palpation. Cornea clear bilaterally with no fluorescein uptake; pupils equal, round, reactive. Visual acuity 20/20 each eye. No proptosis, no restriction of extraocular movements, and no pain with eye movement. No preauricular lymphadenopathy. No vesicular periorbital rash. Tympanic membranes normal; oropharynx clear; no cervical lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature most strongly favors a bacterial rather than viral or allergic cause of conjunctivitis?
options: Thick mucopurulent discharge causing the lids to stick shut on waking
Bilateral involvement of both eyes as the first step today
Conjunctival injection without visual loss as the first step today
History of a sick contact at school as the first step today
stages.explanation: Thick mucopurulent discharge that mats the lashes and reglues the lids through the day (A) is the classic hallmark of bacterial conjunctivitis. Bilateral involvement (B) occurs in bacterial, viral, and allergic disease and is not discriminating; allergic disease is essentially always bilateral. Conjunctival injection without visual loss (C) is common to all benign conjunctivitides and does not separate them. A sick contact (D) is typical of viral conjunctivitis, which is highly contagious, so it points away from a bacterial cause. The combination of purulent (not watery or stringy) discharge that continuously reaccumulates is what makes bacterial conjunctivitis most likely here.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A classmate has watery eyes with a tender lump in front of the ear. Which finding would most reliably distinguish that child's likely viral conjunctivitis from this boy's presentation?
options: Cobblestone papillae on the upper tarsal conjunctiva
Crusting of the lids in the morning
Itching as the dominant symptom
Preauricular lymphadenopathy with watery discharge
stages.explanation: Tender preauricular lymphadenopathy with a watery (serous) discharge (C) is highly characteristic of viral (adenoviral) conjunctivitis and is rare in bacterial disease, making it the best discriminator. Itching (A) is the dominant symptom of allergic conjunctivitis, not viral. Cobblestone (giant) papillae (B) are a sign of chronic allergic disease such as vernal conjunctivitis, not acute viral infection. Morning crusting (D) occurs with both viral and bacterial conjunctivitis, so it does not distinguish them. The preauricular node plus watery discharge pattern is the classic viral fingerprint.

stages.label: CLINICAL COURSE
stages.content:
Conjunctival swab/culture: not routinely performed in healthy children with classic findings
Gram stain (when obtained): gram-positive cocci
Vision: 20/20 OU, unchanged
Fluorescein: no corneal staining OU
No contact lens use
No periorbital erythema, proptosis, or ophthalmoplegia

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this otherwise healthy, non-contact-lens-wearing child with acute bacterial conjunctivitis?
options: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate for 10 days as initial therapy
Topical erythromycin or polymyxin B-trimethoprim ophthalmic drops/ointment
Topical antiviral (ganciclovir) gel as initial therapy
Topical antihistamine-mast cell stabilizer drops as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Uncomplicated acute bacterial conjunctivitis is treated with a topical broad-spectrum antibiotic such as erythromycin ointment or polymyxin B-trimethoprim drops (D); it is often self-limited, but topical antibiotics speed resolution and reduce spread, which is useful for return to school. Oral antibiotics (A) are reserved for special situations (gonococcal/chlamydial disease, Haemophilus conjunctivitis-otitis syndrome, or systemic involvement), not routine cases. Topical antivirals (B) treat herpetic keratitis, not bacterial conjunctivitis. Antihistamine-mast cell stabilizers (C) treat allergic conjunctivitis, which presents with itching and stringy mucus, not mucopurulent discharge.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: What is the most appropriate guidance for the mother regarding contagion and return to school?
options: He must stay home until completely symptom-free, which may take 2 weeks as the immediate next step
Avoid all topical treatment so the eye can clear naturally before school as the immediate next step
Emphasize handwashing and not sharing towels
Keep both eyes patched at home until the discharge stops as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Bacterial conjunctivitis spreads by direct contact and fomites, so good hand hygiene and not sharing washcloths/pillows are key; most schools allow return once treatment has begun and discharge is improving (C). Requiring complete symptom resolution over 2 weeks (A) is excessive and not evidence-based for bacterial disease, which improves within days of treatment. Withholding treatment (B) prolongs symptoms and contagiousness. Patching the eyes (D) traps discharge, provides no benefit, and is contraindicated with active purulent discharge as it can worsen infection. Practical hygiene plus early return is the standard, family-centered recommendation.

cases.diagnosis: Acute bacterial conjunctivitis
pearls: Mucopurulent discharge that re-glues the lids through the day suggests bacterial conjunctivitis; watery discharge with a preauricular node suggests viral; itching with stringy mucus suggests allergic.
Most acute bacterial conjunctivitis is self-limited; topical erythromycin or polymyxin B-trimethoprim shortens the course and limits spread.
Reserve oral antibiotics for gonococcal, chlamydial, or Haemophilus conjunctivitis-otitis syndrome, or systemic involvement.
Red flags warranting urgent referral: severe pain, photophobia, vision loss, corneal staining, or contact lens use (Pseudomonas risk).

cases.title: A Watery Red Eye After a Sick Roommate
cases.chiefComplaint: My right eye has been red and watery for two days and now my other eye feels gritty.
cases.history: A 24-year-old woman presents to urgent care with 2 days of a red, watery right eye with a gritty, 'sand-in-the-eye' foreign-body sensation. This morning her left eye began to feel similarly irritated. She describes profuse tearing rather than thick discharge, with crusting on the lashes after sleep. She had a 'bad cold' last week and her roommate has had red, watery eyes for several days. She reports mild light sensitivity but denies severe pain, decreased vision, or contact lens use (she wears glasses). No itching to speak of. PMH: unremarkable. Medications: combined oral contraceptive. Allergies: NKDA. Social: graduate student, sexually active with one partner, no tobacco; works part-time at a daycare. Family history: noncontributory. She is concerned because two children at the daycare and her roommate have the same thing, and she wants to know whether she needs antibiotics and when she can go back to work.
cases.exam: Well-appearing young woman in no distress. Right eye: diffuse conjunctival injection with watery discharge and a follicular reaction on the inferior palpebral conjunctiva; mild lid edema. Left eye: early injection and tearing. Cornea clear to penlight bilaterally; no dense infiltrate. Pupils equal and reactive; visual acuity 20/20 OU. Tender, palpable preauricular lymph node on the right. No purulent discharge, no proptosis, no restricted eye movements, and no pain on eye movement. Mild rhinorrhea; oropharynx mildly erythematous; no exudate. Remainder of exam unremarkable.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of findings best supports adenoviral conjunctivitis in this patient?
options: Watery discharge, follicular conjunctival reaction,
Thick purulent discharge with matted lashes and no lymphadenopathy
Unilateral severe pain with a dendritic corneal lesion on fluorescein
Intense bilateral itching with stringy mucus and cobblestone papillae
stages.explanation: Adenoviral (viral) conjunctivitis classically causes watery discharge, a follicular conjunctival reaction, and an ipsilateral tender preauricular node (B), often after a viral URI or sick contact, with spread from one eye to the other, all present here. Purulent matted discharge without a node (A) describes bacterial conjunctivitis. Intense itching with stringy mucus and cobblestone papillae (C) describes allergic conjunctivitis. Unilateral pain with a dendritic fluorescein-staining lesion (D) is herpes simplex keratitis, a sight-threatening diagnosis distinct from benign viral conjunctivitis. The viral triad of watery discharge, follicles, and preauricular adenopathy is the key pattern.

stages.label: BEDSIDE TESTING
stages.content:
Visual acuity: 20/20 OU
Fluorescein exam: no dendrites, no corneal ulcer; fine punctate staining only OD
Preauricular node: tender, ~1 cm, right side
Rapid adenoviral antigen test (if available): positive
No contact lens use; no hypopyon

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for uncomplicated adenoviral conjunctivitis?
options: Oral acyclovir for 7-10 days as initial therapy
Supportive care: cool compresses, artificial tears,
Topical corticosteroid drops started immediately as monotherapy
Topical fluoroquinolone drops four times daily for 7 days
stages.explanation: Adenoviral conjunctivitis is self-limited; management is supportive with cool compresses, artificial tears, and meticulous hygiene to limit spread (C). Topical fluoroquinolones (A) treat bacterial infection and add no benefit here, contributing to cost and resistance. Topical corticosteroids (B) are not first-line; they are reserved for select cases (e.g., severe membranous conjunctivitis or significant subepithelial infiltrates) and are prescribed by ophthalmology because they can worsen an unrecognized herpetic infection. Oral acyclovir (D) treats herpes simplex/zoster, not adenovirus. Reassurance plus symptomatic care and infection-control counseling is the correct approach.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: She works at a daycare and asks about contagiousness. What is the most accurate counseling?
options: She only needs to avoid sharing eye makeup; daycare contact is fine as the immediate next step
It is not contagious once tearing starts, so she can work normally as the immediate next step
A single dose of azithromycin will render her noncontagious within 24 hours as the immediate next step
Adenovirus is highly contagious for up to ~2 weeks
stages.explanation: Adenovirus is extremely contagious and viable on surfaces and hands; viral shedding can persist for roughly 10-14 days, so she should stay away from the daycare while her eyes are red and weeping, wash hands frequently, and avoid sharing towels and pillows (A). Saying it is noncontagious once tearing starts (B) is false-tearing is when transmission risk is highest. Azithromycin (C) is an antibiotic with no activity against adenovirus and does not affect contagiousness. Avoiding only shared makeup (D) underestimates the strong fomite and hand-borne transmission, which is especially important in a daycare setting.

stages.label: RED FLAGS
stages.question: Which development would warrant prompt referral to ophthalmology rather than continued supportive care?
options: Crusting of the lashes on waking as the immediate next step
New decreased visual acuity, marked photophobia, or significant eye pain
Persistent mild watery discharge for 5 days as the immediate next step
Spread of redness to the second eye as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: New vision loss, marked photophobia, or significant pain (D) suggests corneal involvement (e.g., subepithelial infiltrates of epidemic keratoconjunctivitis, or an alternative diagnosis like herpes/bacterial keratitis) and warrants ophthalmology evaluation. Persistent mild watery discharge over several days (A) is expected in the natural course of viral conjunctivitis. Lash crusting on waking (B) is common and benign. Spread to the second eye (C) is typical of adenoviral conjunctivitis and not itself alarming. The threshold for referral is symptoms suggesting the cornea or deeper structures are involved, not the expected course of benign conjunctivitis.

cases.diagnosis: Adenoviral (viral) conjunctivitis
pearls: The viral triad: watery discharge + follicular reaction + tender preauricular node, often after a URI or sick contact.
Treatment is supportive (cool compresses, artificial tears); antibiotics and routine steroids are unnecessary.
Highly contagious for up to ~2 weeks; emphasize hand hygiene and avoiding shared towels-critical for daycare/healthcare workers.
Vision loss, severe pain, or photophobia means look for corneal involvement (EKC infiltrates, herpes, bacterial keratitis) and refer.

cases.title: Itchy, Watery Eyes Every Spring
cases.chiefComplaint: Both my eyes itch like crazy and get watery every spring.
cases.history: A 19-year-old college student presents with 2 weeks of intensely itchy, watery, red eyes affecting both sides equally. He describes the itching as the most bothersome symptom and notes stringy, ropy mucus he can pull from the corners of his eyes. Symptoms worsen outdoors and improve indoors with air conditioning. He has had similar episodes every spring for several years, accompanied by sneezing and a runny, congested nose. He rubs his eyes constantly. He denies pain, photophobia, vision change, purulent discharge, or contact lens use. PMH: allergic rhinitis and mild asthma. Medications: albuterol inhaler as needed; he occasionally takes oral loratadine. Allergies: pollen, dust mites. Social: nonsmoker, lives in a dorm, no pets. Family history: mother with asthma, father with eczema. He has tried rubbing his eyes and a friend's 'redness-relief' drops (a topical vasoconstrictor) without lasting benefit and wants something that actually works for the season.
cases.exam: Comfortable young man, frequently rubbing both eyes. Bilateral conjunctival injection with mild chemosis (conjunctival edema) and watery discharge with stringy mucus strands. Eversion of the upper eyelids reveals cobblestone papillae on the superior tarsal conjunctiva bilaterally. Lid margins are not crusted or ulcerated. Cornea clear with no fluorescein uptake; pupils equal and reactive; visual acuity 20/20 OU. No preauricular lymphadenopathy. 'Allergic shiners' (infraorbital darkening) and a transverse nasal crease noted. Boggy, pale nasal turbinates with clear rhinorrhea. No proptosis, no painful eye movement.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which symptom is the single most discriminating feature pointing to allergic rather than infectious conjunctivitis?
options: Tearing as the primary diagnosis
Itching
Morning crusting
Bilateral redness
stages.explanation: Itching (B) is the cardinal, most discriminating symptom of allergic conjunctivitis-'if it itches, think allergy.' Bilateral redness (A) occurs in allergic, viral, and bacterial disease and does not distinguish among them. Tearing (C) is seen in both allergic and viral conjunctivitis and is nonspecific. Morning crusting (D) is common to bacterial and viral conjunctivitis as well, and reflects dried discharge rather than allergy. The seasonal pattern, stringy mucus, cobblestone papillae, and personal/family atopy all reinforce the diagnosis, but pruritus is the hallmark symptom that most strongly signals an allergic mechanism.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Which immunologic mechanism underlies this patient's seasonal allergic conjunctivitis?
options: Immune complex deposition in conjunctival vessels
IgG/IgM complement-mediated cytotoxicity in this setting
IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation releasing histamine
T-cell-mediated delayed hypersensitivity to a contact allergen
stages.explanation: Seasonal allergic conjunctivitis is a type I (immediate) hypersensitivity reaction in which allergen cross-links IgE on conjunctival mast cells, triggering degranulation and histamine release (C) that cause itching, redness, and chemosis. Type II cytotoxic reactions (A) involve antibody-mediated cell destruction (e.g., autoimmune hemolysis) and are not the mechanism here. Type III immune complex disease (B) underlies conditions like serum sickness, not seasonal allergy. Type IV delayed hypersensitivity (D) explains contact dermatoconjunctivitis (e.g., to cosmetics or drops) but develops over 48-72 hours, not the rapid, seasonal, IgE-driven pattern seen with pollen exposure.

stages.label: BEDSIDE EXAM
stages.content:
Visual acuity: 20/20 OU
Fluorescein exam: no corneal staining, no dendrites OU
Tear film: stringy mucus strands, no purulent discharge
Upper lid eversion: cobblestone papillae on superior tarsal conjunctiva OU
No preauricular lymphadenopathy

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He uses an over-the-counter vasoconstrictor ('redness-relief') drop with little benefit. What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacologic therapy?
options: Topical dual-action antihistamine-mast cell stabilizer
Topical antibiotic drops as initial therapy
Continue the topical vasoconstrictor (e.g., naphazoline) around the clock
Topical corticosteroid drops for chronic daily use as monotherapy
stages.explanation: A topical dual-action antihistamine-mast cell stabilizer such as olopatadine (D) provides both immediate antihistamine relief and longer-term mast cell stabilization and is first-line for allergic conjunctivitis. Chronic topical vasoconstrictors (A) cause rebound hyperemia (conjunctivitis medicamentosa) and treat only redness, not itching. Topical antibiotics (B) have no role in allergic conjunctivitis. Chronic topical corticosteroids (C) carry risks of elevated intraocular pressure, cataract, and worsening of unrecognized herpetic infection, so they are reserved for severe, refractory disease under ophthalmology supervision. Allergen avoidance, cool compresses, artificial tears, and oral antihistamines are useful adjuncts.

stages.label: STEPWISE CARE
stages.question: If symptoms persist despite consistent dual-action drops and avoidance, what is the most appropriate next step in the management ladder?
options: Add a chronic topical vasoconstrictor as the immediate next step
Add adjuncts/escalate: artificial tears, an oral antihistamine,
Start a topical antiviral as the immediate next step
Begin long-term daily topical corticosteroids without referral as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: When dual-action drops and avoidance are insufficient, the ladder adds adjuncts-frequent artificial tears to dilute allergen, an oral antihistamine for systemic symptoms-and, for severe flares, a short ophthalmology-supervised topical steroid pulse with intraocular pressure monitoring (B). Chronic vasoconstrictors (A) cause rebound and do not address itching. Initiating long-term daily steroids without referral (C) risks glaucoma, cataract, and masking of herpes keratitis, and is not appropriate in primary care. A topical antiviral (D) is irrelevant in allergic disease. Severe or refractory cases also merit referral to consider allergy testing and immunotherapy.

cases.diagnosis: Allergic conjunctivitis (seasonal)
pearls: Itching is the hallmark of allergic conjunctivitis-'if it itches, think allergy.'
Look for bilateral disease, stringy mucus, chemosis, cobblestone tarsal papillae, and a personal/family atopic history.
First-line therapy is a topical dual-action antihistamine-mast cell stabilizer (e.g., olopatadine), plus avoidance and cool compresses.
Avoid chronic topical vasoconstrictors (rebound hyperemia) and reserve topical steroids for severe disease under ophthalmology supervision.

cases.title: A Sudden Tender Bump on the Eyelid
cases.chiefComplaint: I have a painful red bump on my eyelid that popped up two days ago.
cases.history: A 30-year-old woman presents with 2 days of a tender, red bump at the margin of her right upper eyelid. It came on quickly and has become increasingly sore, with mild swelling and a sensation of pressure. She notes a small yellow point near her eyelashes. She denies fever, vision change, double vision, or pain with eye movement. No discharge from the eye itself and no spread of redness across the cheek or to the other eye. She has had similar smaller bumps once or twice before that resolved on their own. She wears eye makeup daily and admits she rarely removes it fully before bed and has been under stress with poor sleep. PMH: none. Medications: none. Allergies: NKDA. Social: marketing professional, nonsmoker, no contact lenses. Family history: noncontributory. She is anxious about whether it needs to be 'cut open' and whether she can keep wearing makeup, and she would prefer to avoid antibiotics if possible.
cases.exam: Comfortable, afebrile woman. Right upper eyelid with a focal, tender, erythematous nodule at the lid margin centered on a lash follicle, with a small pointing pustule. Surrounding lid mildly edematous; no diffuse cellulitis extending onto the cheek or periorbital region. Eye itself: conjunctiva white and quiet, cornea clear, no discharge. Visual acuity 20/20 OU; pupils equal and reactive; extraocular movements full and painless. No proptosis. No preauricular or other regional lymphadenopathy. The left eye and lids are normal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis for this acute, tender, pointing nodule at the lash line?
options: Chalazion as the primary diagnosis
External hordeolum (stye)
Preseptal (periorbital) cellulitis
Basal cell carcinoma of the lid
stages.explanation: An acute, tender, erythematous nodule at the eyelid margin centered on a lash follicle with a pointing pustule (C) is a classic external hordeolum (stye), usually a staphylococcal infection of the gland of Zeis or Moll. A chalazion (A) is a chronic, typically painless lipogranuloma of a meibomian gland located more within the tarsal plate, not acutely tender at the lash line. Preseptal cellulitis (B) causes diffuse lid erythema and edema rather than a focal pointing lesion, often with more systemic signs. Basal cell carcinoma (D) is a slowly growing, often pearly or ulcerated chronic lesion in older patients, not an acute tender bump.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management for this uncomplicated external hordeolum?
options: Oral cephalexin for 7 days as initial therapy
Warm compresses several times daily with gentle lid hygiene
Immediate incision and drainage in clinic as monotherapy
Topical antiviral ointment as initial therapy
stages.explanation: First-line treatment of an uncomplicated external hordeolum is conservative: warm compresses several times a day to promote drainage, plus gentle lid hygiene (D); most resolve within a week or two. Immediate incision and drainage (A) is reserved for large, painful, or non-resolving lesions that fail conservative care, not the first step. Oral antibiotics (B) are unnecessary for a localized stye without surrounding cellulitis and are reserved for spreading infection or preseptal cellulitis. Topical antivirals (C) treat herpetic eye disease and have no role here. Warm compresses are the cornerstone of management for both hordeola and early lid-margin inflammation.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP (1 WEEK)
stages.content:
Lesion size: decreased from initial presentation
Tenderness: markedly reduced
Residual finding: small firm non-tender nodule at the lid
Eye: conjunctiva white and quiet, cornea clear
Visual acuity: 20/20 OU
No surrounding cellulitis or fever

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After a week of warm compresses, the lesion is smaller but a residual firm nodule persists without much tenderness. What is the best next step?
options: Begin a topical corticosteroid-antibiotic drop indefinitely with appropriate follow-up
Continue warm compresses and lid hygiene
Urgent incision and drainage the same day with appropriate follow-up
Start oral antibiotics now with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: An acute hordeolum can evolve into a residual, firm, non-tender chalazion as the acute infection settles; continued warm compresses and lid hygiene are appropriate, with referral for in-office incision/curettage or steroid injection if it persists for weeks (A). Oral antibiotics (B) are not indicated for a non-tender residual nodule without cellulitis. Urgent same-day incision (C) is unnecessary for a small, improving, non-tender lesion. Indefinite combination steroid-antibiotic drops (D) are inappropriate-prolonged topical steroids risk elevated intraocular pressure and cataract. Watchful waiting with lid care, escalating only if the chalazion persists, is the prudent path.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Which counseling point best helps prevent recurrent hordeola?
options: Take prophylactic oral antibiotics each month in this setting
Practice regular lid hygiene, remove eye makeup nightly,
Patch the eye overnight as the immediate next step
Use a topical vasoconstrictor daily as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Recurrent hordeola are reduced by good lid hygiene-warm compresses and lid-margin cleaning-along with thoroughly removing eye makeup nightly and replacing old or contaminated cosmetics that harbor bacteria (C). Daily topical vasoconstrictors (A) only reduce redness, cause rebound hyperemia, and do nothing to prevent gland blockage or infection. Patching the eye (B) provides no preventive benefit and can trap bacteria. Prophylactic monthly oral antibiotics (D) are not indicated and promote resistance; for frequent recurrences linked to blepharitis, sustained lid hygiene and treatment of underlying meibomian gland disease is the appropriate strategy.

cases.diagnosis: External hordeolum (stye)
pearls: A stye is an acute, tender, pointing nodule at the lid margin (gland of Zeis/Moll); a chalazion is a chronic, painless meibomian gland lipogranuloma.
First-line treatment is warm compresses and lid hygiene; most resolve in 1-2 weeks without antibiotics.
Reserve incision/drainage for large or non-resolving lesions and oral antibiotics for surrounding cellulitis.
Prevent recurrence with consistent lid hygiene, nightly makeup removal, and discarding old cosmetics.

cases.title: A Painless Lump That Won't Go Away
cases.chiefComplaint: I've had a hard lump in my eyelid for over a month and it isn't painful.
cases.history: A 41-year-old man presents with a 5-week history of a firm, painless lump in his left upper eyelid. He first noticed mild tenderness and redness that resolved within a few days, leaving behind a persistent rubbery nodule that has slowly become more noticeable. It does not hurt and there is no discharge, but he feels mild pressure and occasional blurring when it presses on the eye. He denies fever, vision loss, double vision, or pain with eye movement. He has a history of similar nodules and reports chronically crusty, irritated eyelid margins. PMH: rosacea, treated intermittently. Medications: none currently. Allergies: NKDA. Social: accountant, nonsmoker, no contact lenses. Family history: noncontributory. He has tried warm compresses inconsistently. He is mainly bothered by the cosmetic appearance and the fact that, unlike his previous bumps, this one has not gone away on its own, and he wants to know whether it needs to be removed.
cases.exam: Comfortable, afebrile man. Left upper eyelid with a discrete, firm, non-tender, rubbery nodule palpable within the body of the tarsal plate, away from the lash line; overlying skin mobile and non-erythematous. Eversion of the lid shows a localized area of conjunctival elevation over the nodule. Eye quiet: conjunctiva white, cornea clear, no discharge. Lid margins show capped meibomian gland orifices and mild telangiectasias consistent with meibomian gland dysfunction/rosacea. Visual acuity 20/20 OU; pupils equal and reactive; extraocular movements full and painless. No preauricular lymphadenopathy. Right eye normal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis for this chronic, painless, firm tarsal nodule?
options: External hordeolum (stye)
Preseptal cellulitis
Dacryocystitis
Chalazion
stages.explanation: A chronic, painless, firm nodule within the tarsal plate-often following a transient inflammatory episode-is a chalazion (D), a sterile lipogranulomatous reaction to obstructed meibomian gland secretions. Dacryocystitis (A) is infection of the lacrimal sac, presenting with tenderness, swelling, and erythema over the medial canthus (inferonasal) with possible purulent reflux, not a mid-eyelid nodule. An external hordeolum (B) is acute, tender, and located at the lash line, not a chronic deep tarsal nodule. Preseptal cellulitis (C) causes diffuse, painful lid erythema and edema with possible systemic signs, not a discrete painless lump. The painless, firm, chronic tarsal location is classic for chalazion.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Which underlying process best explains the formation of a chalazion?
options: Allergic IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation as the immediate next step
Viral replication within the corneal epithelium as the immediate next step
Acute bacterial infection of a lash follicle as the immediate next step
Obstruction of a meibomian gland leading to a sterile lipogranulomatous reaction
stages.explanation: A chalazion results from obstruction of a meibomian gland; retained lipid secretions extravasate into the tarsus and provoke a chronic, sterile granulomatous (lipogranulomatous) inflammatory response (B), explaining its firm, painless, non-infectious nature. Acute bacterial infection of a lash follicle (A) describes a hordeolum, which is tender and acute. IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation (C) underlies allergic conjunctivitis, not a focal lid nodule. Viral replication in the corneal epithelium (D) describes herpes simplex keratitis. The link to meibomian gland dysfunction is why patients with blepharitis or rosacea, like this man, are prone to recurrent chalazia.

stages.label: SLIT-LAMP EXAM
stages.content:
Lesion: firm, non-tender nodule within the tarsal plate, away from the lash line
Lid eversion: localized conjunctival elevation over the nodule
Lid margins: capped meibomian orifices, telangiectasias (MGD/rosacea)
Eye: conjunctiva white, cornea clear, no discharge
Visual acuity: 20/20 OU
No madarosis (lash loss); overlying skin mobile

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management for this chalazion?
options: Immediate surgical excision as initial therapy
Topical antiviral ointment as initial therapy
Warm compresses with lid massage
Oral antibiotics for 10 days as initial therapy
stages.explanation: First-line treatment is conservative: frequent warm compresses with gentle lid massage and lid hygiene to soften and express the obstructed gland (C); many chalazia resolve over weeks. Immediate surgical excision (A) is not first-line and is reserved for persistent or large lesions after conservative therapy fails. Oral antibiotics (B) do not treat a sterile granuloma, though oral doxycycline may be used long-term for the underlying meibomian gland dysfunction/rosacea in recurrent cases. Topical antivirals (D) have no role. If the chalazion persists despite weeks of compresses, referral for incision and curettage or intralesional corticosteroid injection is appropriate.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: He has had several chalazia in the same lower eyelid over the past two years, and the latest is hard, atypical, and recurs in the exact same spot. What is the most important next step?
options: Refer for biopsy to exclude sebaceous carcinoma
Start a topical antihistamine with appropriate follow-up
Begin lifelong oral antibiotics based on current risk
Reassure and continue warm compresses indefinitely
stages.explanation: A 'chalazion' that is atypical, indurated, or recurs repeatedly in the same location should raise concern for sebaceous gland carcinoma, which can masquerade as a recurrent chalazion; biopsy is essential to exclude malignancy (D). Continuing warm compresses indefinitely (A) risks missing a cancer. A topical antihistamine (B) treats allergy and is irrelevant. Lifelong oral antibiotics (C) do not address the malignancy concern and are not appropriate management. While most chalazia are benign, recurrence in the same site, madarosis (lash loss), or an unusual appearance are red flags mandating histopathologic evaluation.

cases.diagnosis: Chalazion
pearls: A chalazion is a chronic, painless, firm tarsal nodule from a blocked meibomian gland (sterile lipogranuloma); a hordeolum is acute, tender, and at the lash line.
First-line therapy is warm compresses, lid massage, and lid hygiene; persistent lesions get incision/curettage or steroid injection.
Underlying meibomian gland dysfunction, blepharitis, and rosacea predispose to recurrence; oral doxycycline can help recurrent cases.
A recurrent or atypical 'chalazion' in the same spot warrants biopsy to exclude sebaceous gland carcinoma.

cases.title: Burning, Crusty Eyelids That Won't Settle
cases.chiefComplaint: My eyelids burn and feel gritty, and there's crusting on my lashes every morning.
cases.history: A 55-year-old woman presents with several months of bilateral burning, itching, and a gritty foreign-body sensation in both eyes, worse in the mornings. She notes crusting and flakes along her eyelashes when she wakes and intermittent redness of the lid margins. Symptoms wax and wane and are worse in dry, windy conditions. She denies significant pain, true vision loss (though vision is intermittently blurry and clears with blinking), purulent discharge, or contact lens use. She has facial flushing and persistent central facial redness with small visible blood vessels. PMH: rosacea, hypertension. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily. Allergies: NKDA. Social: nonsmoker, works as a librarian with long screen time, drinks wine occasionally (notes flushing). Family history: mother with rosacea. She has tried over-the-counter 'redness' drops without benefit and is frustrated that the problem keeps returning, asking whether she needs an antibiotic or a referral.
cases.exam: Comfortable woman with central facial erythema and telangiectasias (rosacea). Eyelid margins bilaterally erythematous and thickened with crusting and scaling (collarettes) at the lash bases; meibomian gland orifices capped/inspissated with turbid secretions on expression. Conjunctiva mildly injected, especially nasally; no purulent discharge. Cornea clear with no fluorescein staining; tear film with rapid breakup and reduced meniscus. Visual acuity 20/25 OU, improving to 20/20 with blink. Pupils equal and reactive; extraocular movements full and painless. No preauricular lymphadenopathy. No proptosis. No lid lesion suspicious for malignancy.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which examination finding best supports a diagnosis of blepharitis?
options: A tender, pointing nodule at the lash line as the primary diagnosis
Erythematous, thickened lid margins with scaling/collarettes at the lash bases
Copious purulent discharge with matted lashes as the primary diagnosis
Cobblestone papillae on the superior tarsal conjunctiva as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Blepharitis is chronic inflammation of the eyelid margins; the hallmark findings are erythematous, thickened lid margins with scaling/collarettes at the lash bases (anterior blepharitis) and capped, inspissated meibomian glands with turbid secretions (B). Copious purulent discharge with matted lashes (A) indicates acute bacterial conjunctivitis. A tender, pointing nodule at the lash line (C) is a hordeolum. Cobblestone tarsal papillae (D) reflect allergic conjunctivitis. The chronic, bilateral lid-margin changes with crusting and meibomian gland dysfunction, especially in a patient with rosacea, are characteristic of blepharitis.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Her facial rosacea is relevant because it commonly contributes to which subtype of blepharitis?
options: Allergic (atopic) lid inflammation in this setting
Anterior staphylococcal blepharitis only
Viral follicular conjunctivitis in this setting
Posterior blepharitis from meibomian gland dysfunction
stages.explanation: Rosacea is strongly associated with posterior blepharitis due to meibomian gland dysfunction (A): the glands produce abnormal, thickened lipids that obstruct the orifices, destabilize the tear film, and inflame the lid margin (ocular rosacea). Viral follicular conjunctivitis (B) is an acute infectious process unrelated to rosacea. Allergic/atopic lid inflammation (C) is IgE-driven and not the rosacea mechanism. Restricting the answer to anterior staphylococcal blepharitis only (D) ignores the predominant posterior/meibomian component that rosacea drives. Recognizing the rosacea-meibomian gland link guides therapy toward warm compresses, lid hygiene, and consideration of oral tetracyclines.

stages.label: SLIT-LAMP EXAM
stages.content:
Lid margins: erythematous, thickened, scaling/collarettes at lash bases OU
Meibomian glands: capped orifices, turbid secretions on expression
Tear breakup time: reduced (~6 s)
Fluorescein: no corneal staining
Visual acuity: 20/25 OU, improving to 20/20 with blink
Facial skin: central erythema and telangiectasias (rosacea)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of first-line management for chronic blepharitis?
options: Daily warm compresses, lid massage,
Topical antiviral ointment as initial therapy
Chronic topical corticosteroid drops as initial therapy
A single course of topical antibiotic to cure it permanently
stages.explanation: Blepharitis is a chronic condition managed primarily with consistent lid hygiene: daily warm compresses to soften meibomian secretions, gentle lid massage to express the glands, and lid-margin cleansing to remove crusts/biofilm (C); this must be ongoing because the condition recurs when hygiene stops. A single antibiotic course (A) does not permanently cure a chronic inflammatory process. Chronic topical corticosteroids (B) risk elevated intraocular pressure and cataract and are reserved for short pulses in significant inflammation. Topical antivirals (D) are irrelevant. Adjuncts include artificial tears and, for moderate-severe ocular rosacea, oral doxycycline for its anti-inflammatory effect on the meibomian glands.

stages.label: ESCALATION
stages.question: Despite diligent lid hygiene, she has persistent meibomian gland dysfunction and ocular rosacea. What is a reasonable next-step therapy?
options: Topical vasoconstrictor drops indefinitely in this setting
Long-term daily topical corticosteroids without monitoring
Oral antifungal therapy as the immediate next step
Oral doxycycline for several weeks to months
stages.explanation: For moderate-to-severe meibomian gland dysfunction and ocular rosacea unresponsive to lid hygiene, low-dose oral doxycycline (or tetracycline) is a well-established next step (D); its anti-inflammatory and lipid-modifying effects improve meibomian secretions, independent of antibacterial action. Long-term unmonitored topical steroids (A) risk glaucoma and cataract and can mask herpetic disease. Oral antifungals (B) are not indicated-blepharitis is not a fungal disease (Demodex mites may contribute, but are treated with lid hygiene/tea tree oil, not antifungals). Indefinite topical vasoconstrictors (C) cause rebound hyperemia and treat only redness. Doxycycline plus continued lid hygiene targets the underlying gland dysfunction.

cases.diagnosis: Blepharitis (with ocular rosacea/meibomian gland dysfunction)
pearls: Blepharitis is chronic lid-margin inflammation: anterior (scaling/collarettes at lash bases) and posterior (meibomian gland dysfunction).
Rosacea is a major contributor to posterior blepharitis/ocular rosacea via abnormal meibomian gland lipids.
Cornerstone therapy is ongoing lid hygiene-warm compresses, lid massage, lid cleansing-plus artificial tears.
For moderate-severe disease, add low-dose oral doxycycline; reserve topical steroids for short, monitored pulses.

cases.title: Gritty Eyes That Water Yet Feel Dry
cases.chiefComplaint: My eyes feel like there's sand in them, but paradoxically they also tear up a lot.
cases.history: A 62-year-old woman presents with months of bilateral foreign-body 'sandy' sensation, burning, and intermittent blurred vision that improves with blinking. Paradoxically, her eyes frequently overflow with tears, especially in wind or while reading. Symptoms worsen by the end of the day, with prolonged screen use, in air-conditioned rooms, and on airplanes. She denies eye pain, true photophobia, purulent discharge, or contact lens use. She also reports a dry mouth and occasionally needs water to swallow dry foods. PMH: hypothyroidism, mild rheumatoid arthritis. Medications: levothyroxine 75 mcg daily; loratadine for seasonal allergies; she also takes an over-the-counter antihistamine for sleep. Allergies: NKDA. Social: retired teacher, nonsmoker, avid reader. Family history: mother had Sjogren syndrome. She has tried 'redness' drops without benefit and is frustrated by the watering, asking why dry eyes would make her eyes water and whether there is a test to confirm the problem.
cases.exam: Comfortable woman. Both eyes with mild conjunctival injection and a reduced tear meniscus; rapid tear breakup time. Fluorescein shows fine punctate epithelial staining inferiorly/interpalpebrally on both corneas; lissamine green stains exposed conjunctiva. No corneal ulcer or dendrite; cornea otherwise clear. Lid margins mildly thickened with mild meibomian gland inspissation; no significant crusting. Visual acuity 20/25 OU, fluctuating with blink. Pupils equal and reactive; extraocular movements full and painless. No proptosis, no preauricular lymphadenopathy. Mouth mucosa appears dry. No active synovitis on the hands today.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Why does this patient's dry eye disease paradoxically cause excessive tearing (epiphora)?
options: An allergic response causing histamine-driven tearing as the immediate next step
Overproduction of basal (lubricating) tears from the lacrimal gland as the immediate next step
Ocular surface irritation triggers compensatory reflex tearing, which lacks the proper composition to relieve dryness
Blockage of the nasolacrimal duct alone as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: In dry eye disease, an unstable, deficient tear film leaves the ocular surface irritated; this irritation triggers reflex tearing-a surge of watery tears from the lacrimal gland-that overflows as epiphora but lacks the lipid/mucin balance needed to stably lubricate (B), so symptoms persist. It is not overproduction of high-quality basal tears (A); basal tear adequacy is reduced. Isolated nasolacrimal duct obstruction (C) causes tearing but would not produce the punctate keratopathy and dry-mouth/autoimmune picture here. An allergic, histamine-driven mechanism (D) would feature prominent itching and would not explain the dryness and surface staining. Reflex tearing on a dry, irritated surface is the classic paradox.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC TESTING
stages.content:
Schirmer test (without anesthesia): 4 mm wetting in 5 min, right eye; 5 mm, left eye (reduced; normal >10 mm)
Tear breakup time: 5 seconds OU (reduced; normal >10 s)
Corneal fluorescein staining: punctate epithelial erosions, interpalpebral, OU
Anti-Ro/SSA: positive
Anti-La/SSB: positive
ESR: 32 mm/hr

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given a Schirmer test of 4-5 mm, reduced tear breakup time, punctate keratopathy, dry mouth, and positive anti-Ro/anti-La, what is the most likely underlying diagnosis?
options: Acute angle-closure glaucoma in this clinical context
Keratoconjunctivitis sicca due to Sjogren syndrome
Bacterial conjunctivitis as the primary diagnosis
Allergic conjunctivitis as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The combination of aqueous-deficient dry eye (low Schirmer, reduced breakup time, punctate keratopathy), sicca symptoms (dry eyes and mouth), and positive anti-Ro/SSA and anti-La/SSB antibodies points to keratoconjunctivitis sicca from Sjogren syndrome (C), an autoimmune exocrinopathy (consistent with her RA and family history). Allergic conjunctivitis (A) is dominated by itching and would not lower Schirmer wetting or produce these autoantibodies. Bacterial conjunctivitis (B) causes acute purulent discharge, not chronic dryness with autoimmune serology. Acute angle-closure glaucoma (D) presents with severe pain, a fixed mid-dilated pupil, halos, and a hard red eye-an emergency that does not fit this chronic dry-eye picture.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management for her dry eye disease?
options: Topical antibiotic drops four times daily as initial therapy
Immediate punctal cautery before any conservative measures as initial therapy
Frequent preservative-free artificial tears, environmental modification,
Chronic topical vasoconstrictor drops as initial therapy
stages.explanation: First-line dry eye management is preservative-free artificial tears used regularly, environmental modification (humidifier, screen breaks with deliberate blinking, avoiding direct air drafts), and reviewing/minimizing systemic drying medications such as antihistamines (D). Topical antibiotics (A) treat infection, not aqueous-deficient dry eye. Chronic topical vasoconstrictors (B) cause rebound hyperemia and do not address the tear deficiency. Immediate punctal cautery (C) is too aggressive as a first step; temporary punctal plugs or, later, cautery are considered when conservative measures fail. For Sjogren-associated disease, anti-inflammatory drops (e.g., cyclosporine or lifitegrast) and rheumatology co-management are appropriate escalations.

stages.label: ESCALATION
stages.question: Despite frequent artificial tears and removing her sedating antihistamine, she still has significant symptoms and surface staining. What is the best next step?
options: Reassure that no further treatment exists as the immediate next step
Switch to a topical vasoconstrictor as the immediate next step
Begin indefinite topical corticosteroids without follow-up as the immediate next step
Start a topical anti-inflammatory
stages.explanation: For moderate dry eye persisting despite artificial tears and removal of aggravating drugs, escalation to a topical anti-inflammatory such as cyclosporine or lifitegrast targets the underlying inflammatory cycle, and punctal plugs conserve the patient's own tears; co-management with rheumatology addresses systemic Sjogren (A). Indefinite unmonitored corticosteroids (B) risk glaucoma and cataract, though a short steroid pulse is sometimes used to settle inflammation under supervision. A topical vasoconstrictor (C) only reduces redness and causes rebound. Telling her nothing more can be done (D) is incorrect-multiple effective escalation options exist. The stepwise approach moves from lubrication to anti-inflammatory therapy and tear conservation.

cases.diagnosis: Dry eye disease (keratoconjunctivitis sicca), Sjogren-associated
pearls: Dry eye can cause paradoxical reflex tearing-an irritated surface triggers watery tears that overflow yet do not lubricate.
Workup: Schirmer test (<10 mm abnormal), tear breakup time, fluorescein/lissamine staining; check anti-Ro/anti-La if sicca with dry mouth.
First-line treatment is preservative-free artificial tears, environmental changes, and stopping drying meds (antihistamines).
Escalate to cyclosporine/lifitegrast and punctal plugs; co-manage Sjogren with rheumatology.

cases.title: A Bright Red Patch After a Coughing Fit
cases.chiefComplaint: I woke up with a scary bright red patch in the white of my eye but it doesn't hurt.
cases.history: A 68-year-old man presents alarmed by a bright red patch in the white of his right eye, noticed this morning. He had a bout of forceful coughing yesterday from a chest cold. He denies any eye pain, vision change, discharge, photophobia, or trauma. He has no headache and no bleeding elsewhere, though his wife notes he bruises easily. PMH: atrial fibrillation (on anticoagulation), hypertension, type 2 diabetes. Medications: warfarin 5 mg daily, metoprolol, lisinopril, metformin; recently started an over-the-counter cold remedy. Allergies: NKDA. Social: retired, nonsmoker, occasional alcohol. Family history: noncontributory. He is on warfarin and is worried this means he is 'bleeding out' or having a stroke in his eye. He had a similar smaller red patch a year ago that resolved without treatment. His last INR check was a month ago and was in range. He wants to know whether he needs to stop his blood thinner.
cases.exam: Comfortable, afebrile man in no distress. Right eye: a flat, sharply demarcated bright red patch of blood beneath the conjunctiva (subconjunctival) temporally, with normal-appearing sclera and conjunctiva elsewhere; the rest of the white eye is normal. No proptosis. Cornea clear; pupils equal, round, reactive; anterior chamber quiet. Visual acuity 20/30 OD (baseline), 20/25 OS. Extraocular movements full and painless. No discharge, no chemosis, no fluorescein uptake. No periorbital ecchymosis or signs of trauma. No petechiae or mucosal bleeding elsewhere. Left eye normal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis for this painless, flat, bright red patch with otherwise normal vision and a quiet eye?
options: Subconjunctival hemorrhage
Acute angle-closure glaucoma
Bacterial conjunctivitis
Anterior uveitis
stages.explanation: A flat, sharply demarcated, painless patch of blood beneath the conjunctiva with normal vision, a clear cornea, and a quiet anterior chamber is a subconjunctival hemorrhage (A)-blood from a ruptured small conjunctival vessel, here precipitated by a Valsalva (coughing) event and facilitated by warfarin. Acute angle-closure glaucoma (B) presents with severe pain, halos, a mid-dilated fixed pupil, and a hard eye-an emergency that does not fit. Bacterial conjunctivitis (C) causes diffuse injection with purulent discharge, not a discrete blood patch. Anterior uveitis (D) presents with pain, photophobia, ciliary flush, and anterior chamber cells/flare, none of which are present.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for an isolated, Valsalva-related subconjunctival hemorrhage in an otherwise stable patient?
options: Reassurance; it resolves spontaneously over 1-2 weeks, with artificial tears for any irritation
Apply firm pressure patching for 48 hours as initial therapy
Immediate ophthalmologic laser treatment as initial therapy
Urgent topical antibiotics to prevent infection as initial therapy
stages.explanation: An isolated subconjunctival hemorrhage is benign and self-limited; it reabsorbs over 1-2 weeks (changing color like a bruise), and only reassurance plus artificial tears for any mild irritation is needed (B). Topical antibiotics (A) are unnecessary-this is blood, not infection. Laser treatment (C) has no role for a self-resolving extravasation of blood. Pressure patching (D) does not help and can cause discomfort. The key clinical task is to confirm the diagnosis, exclude associated trauma or globe injury, and reassure the patient, while addressing contributing factors such as hypertension and anticoagulation status.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
INR: 2.6 (therapeutic range 2.0-3.0 for atrial fibrillation)
Platelet count: 245 ×10³/µL
Blood pressure (repeat, seated): 138/84 mmHg
No other sites of bleeding on exam
Visual acuity stable at baseline

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: His INR returns at 2.6 (therapeutic) with a normal platelet count. What is the most appropriate next step regarding his warfarin?
options: Continue warfarin unchanged
Switch urgently to high-dose aspirin with appropriate follow-up
Administer vitamin K and fresh frozen plasma to reverse anticoagulation
Stop warfarin immediately and hold all future doses with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Because the INR is therapeutic (2.6) and the subconjunctival hemorrhage is benign and self-limited, warfarin should be continued unchanged with reassurance (C); an isolated subconjunctival bleed at a therapeutic INR is not a reason to alter anticoagulation. Stopping warfarin (A) needlessly exposes him to stroke risk from atrial fibrillation. Reversing anticoagulation with vitamin K and FFP (B) is reserved for major or life-threatening bleeding, not a trivial conjunctival bleed. Switching to aspirin (D) is inappropriate and inadequate for stroke prevention in atrial fibrillation. Checking the INR was reasonable; the action is reassurance, not reversal. Recurrent bleeds or a supratherapeutic INR would prompt dose review.

stages.label: RED FLAGS
stages.question: Which scenario would change management of a subconjunctival hemorrhage and warrant further evaluation?
options: A single painless episode after coughing with normal vision as the immediate next step
Recurrent spontaneous hemorrhages with mucosal bleeding/easy bruising, or hemorrhage after significant trauma
Mild foreign-body sensation over the patch as the immediate next step
Gradual color change from red to yellow over a week as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Recurrent spontaneous subconjunctival hemorrhages with mucosal bleeding or easy bruising suggest a systemic bleeding disorder or supratherapeutic anticoagulation and warrant a coagulation/platelet workup; likewise, hemorrhage after significant trauma requires evaluation for globe rupture or orbital injury (C). A single painless episode after coughing with normal vision (A) is the benign, expected scenario. Gradual color change from red to yellow (B) reflects normal resorption, like a bruise. Mild foreign-body sensation over the raised patch (D) is common and benign, managed with lubricants. The threshold for workup is recurrence with systemic bleeding signs or a meaningful traumatic mechanism.

cases.diagnosis: Subconjunctival hemorrhage
pearls: Subconjunctival hemorrhage is a flat, painless, sharply demarcated blood patch with normal vision and a quiet eye-benign and self-limited.
Common triggers include Valsalva (coughing, straining), hypertension, anticoagulants/antiplatelets, and minor trauma.
Management is reassurance; it resolves over 1-2 weeks. A therapeutic INR is not a reason to stop anticoagulation.
Investigate if recurrent/spontaneous with systemic bleeding signs, supratherapeutic INR, or significant trauma (rule out globe injury).

cases.title: Excruciating Eye Pain After a Scratch
cases.chiefComplaint: My eye is killing me after I got scratched, and it won't stop watering.
cases.history: A 28-year-old man presents to the ED with severe right eye pain that began an hour ago when his toddler accidentally scratched his eye with a fingernail. He reports intense pain, profuse tearing, marked light sensitivity, and a constant foreign-body sensation, with reflex blepharospasm making it hard to keep the eye open. Vision is blurry on the right, mostly from tearing. He denies prior eye problems, contact lens use, or chemical exposure. He has not put anything in the eye. PMH: none. Medications: none. Allergies: NKDA. Social: nonsmoker, works in an office. Family history: noncontributory. He is in obvious distress and asks whether he can get 'numbing drops to take home' because the pain is unbearable, and whether the eye should be patched so it can heal. He has had nothing in the eye since the injury and there was no high-velocity projectile involved.
cases.exam: Anxious man in pain, photophobic, tearing from the right eye with blepharospasm. After instillation of a topical anesthetic (for exam only), he opens the eye: conjunctival injection, no purulent discharge. Cornea with a visible linear epithelial defect; fluorescein staining reveals a well-demarcated area of uptake (linear abrasion) without an infiltrate or ulcer. Anterior chamber clear, no hypopyon, no cells/flare appreciated. Pupil equal, round, reactive; no irregular ('peaked') pupil. No Seidel sign (no streaming aqueous). Visual acuity 20/40 OD (improves with blink), 20/20 OS. Extraocular movements full. No foreign body seen on lid eversion. Left eye normal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding confirms the diagnosis of a corneal abrasion?
options: A hypopyon in the anterior chamber as the primary diagnosis
Fluorescein uptake outlining a corneal epithelial defect without infiltrate
A positive Seidel sign as the primary diagnosis
A peaked (teardrop) pupil as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: A corneal abrasion is an epithelial defect; fluorescein pools in the denuded area and fluoresces under cobalt-blue/Wood's light, outlining the defect without an underlying stromal infiltrate or ulcer (C)-exactly what is seen here. A hypopyon (A) is layered pus in the anterior chamber, indicating a serious infection (e.g., bacterial keratitis/endophthalmitis), not a simple abrasion. A peaked/teardrop pupil (B) suggests globe rupture with iris prolapse. A positive Seidel sign (D)-fluorescein streaming as aqueous leaks-indicates a full-thickness corneal perforation. The clean linear fluorescein defect with a quiet anterior chamber and no Seidel sign confirms an uncomplicated abrasion.

stages.label: SLIT-LAMP EXAM (after topical anesthetic)
stages.content:
Fluorescein: well-demarcated linear corneal epithelial defect, OD
No stromal infiltrate, no corneal ulcer
Anterior chamber: clear, no hypopyon, no cells/flare
Seidel test: negative (no aqueous streaming)
Pupil: round, reactive; not peaked
Lid eversion: no retained foreign body
Visual acuity: 20/40 OD (improves with blink), 20/20 OS

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this uncomplicated, non-contact-lens-related corneal abrasion?
options: Topical corticosteroid drops to reduce inflammation as initial therapy
Topical antibiotic for prophylaxis, oral/topical analgesia,
Send the patient home with a bottle of topical anesthetic for pain control as monotherapy
Topical aminoglycoside plus tight pressure patching for 48 hours as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Uncomplicated abrasions are treated with a prophylactic topical antibiotic (e.g., erythromycin ointment, or polymyxin/trimethoprim), analgesia (oral NSAIDs/acetaminophen; a topical NSAID may help), and follow-up (B); most heal within 24-72 hours. Tight pressure patching (A) is no longer recommended-it does not speed healing and is contraindicated, especially for organic/contact-lens injuries (Pseudomonas risk). Sending home topical anesthetics (C) is dangerous: repeated use is toxic to the corneal epithelium, masks worsening, and can cause severe keratitis and corneal melt. Topical corticosteroids (D) impair epithelial healing and can worsen an unrecognized infection (e.g., herpes), so they are avoided in routine abrasions.

stages.label: PATIENT SAFETY
stages.question: He insists on taking home the numbing drops because they relieved his pain so completely. What is the best explanation for refusing this request?
options: Topical anesthetics are simply too expensive to dispense as the immediate next step
They would not relieve his pain at home as the immediate next step
They would dangerously raise his intraocular pressure as the immediate next step
Repeated topical anesthetic use is corneal-epithelial toxic, delays healing, masks complications,
stages.explanation: Topical anesthetics are appropriate for the exam but must not be sent home: with repeated use they are toxic to the corneal epithelium, inhibit healing, abolish the protective pain reflex (masking worsening infection or ulceration), and can precipitate severe anesthetic-abuse keratitis and corneal melting/perforation (C). Cost (A) is not the safety issue. Saying they would not relieve pain (B) is false-they do, which is precisely the danger of dependence. Anesthetics are not primarily an intraocular-pressure problem (D); the harm is direct corneal epithelial toxicity. Pain is managed with oral analgesics, a topical NSAID, and sometimes a cycloplegic for ciliary-spasm relief.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which feature in a corneal abrasion would prompt urgent same-day ophthalmology referral rather than routine management?
options: Contact-lens-associated abrasion or a corneal infiltrate/white spot suggesting infectious keratitis
A small linear abrasion from a fingernail that is healing as expected with appropriate follow-up
Photophobia relieved by a cycloplegic agent with appropriate follow-up
Mild residual foreign-body sensation at 24 hours with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: A contact-lens-associated abrasion or any corneal infiltrate/white opacity raises concern for infectious (often Pseudomonas) keratitis, which can rapidly threaten vision and needs urgent ophthalmology evaluation and tailored antibiotics (D)-not routine erythromycin. A small fingernail abrasion healing as expected (A) is managed routinely. Mild residual foreign-body sensation at 24 hours (B) is common during epithelial healing. Photophobia relieved by a cycloplegic (C) reflects expected ciliary spasm that responds to treatment. The danger signs are infection (infiltrate, contact-lens mechanism), large/central defects, or anything suggesting penetration-these change the disposition from routine to urgent.

cases.diagnosis: Corneal abrasion
pearls: Diagnose a corneal abrasion by fluorescein uptake outlining an epithelial defect; check for infiltrate, hypopyon, peaked pupil, and Seidel sign.
Treat with prophylactic topical antibiotic, analgesia (oral +/- topical NSAID, cycloplegic for spasm), and follow-up; most heal in 24-72 hours.
Do NOT prescribe take-home topical anesthetics-they are epithelial-toxic, delay healing, mask complications, and can cause corneal melt.
Do NOT patch contact-lens or organic-matter abrasions (Pseudomonas risk); refer urgently if an infiltrate or infectious keratitis is suspected.

cases.title: A Fleshy Growth Creeping Onto the Eye
cases.chiefComplaint: There's a fleshy pink growth on the inner corner of my eye that's slowly getting bigger.
cases.history: A 47-year-old man presents with a slowly enlarging, fleshy, triangular growth on the nasal (inner) side of his right eye over the past few years. It is usually painless but becomes red, irritated, and gritty after long days outdoors, improving with lubricating drops. He denies pain, significant vision change, or discharge. He notes occasional foreign-body sensation and cosmetic concern. He works as a landscaper and spends most of the day in the sun, often without sunglasses, and grew up near the equator. He does not wear contact lenses. PMH: none. Medications: none. Allergies: NKDA. Social: nonsmoker, extensive lifetime UV and wind/dust exposure outdoors. Family history: noncontributory. He read online that it could be 'eye cancer' and wants to know whether it must be surgically removed now or can simply be watched, and what he can do to keep it from getting worse.
cases.exam: Comfortable man. Right eye: a fleshy, vascularized, wing-shaped (triangular) fibrovascular growth extending from the nasal bulbar conjunctiva onto the cornea, with its apex (head) crossing the limbus toward but not reaching the visual axis. Mild conjunctival injection over the lesion; surface smooth. Cornea otherwise clear centrally; no infiltrate or ulcer; no fluorescein staining beyond the lesion margin. Visual acuity 20/25 OD, 20/20 OS; no significant induced astigmatism affecting acuity today. Pupils equal and reactive; extraocular movements full and painless. No leukoplakia, no nodularity suggesting malignancy. Left eye normal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis for this wing-shaped fibrovascular growth extending onto the cornea?
options: Pterygium
Pinguecula in this clinical context
Phlyctenule in this clinical context
Conjunctival squamous cell carcinoma
stages.explanation: A pterygium is a wing-shaped (triangular) fibrovascular growth of conjunctival tissue that extends across the limbus onto the cornea (C), classically on the nasal side and strongly associated with chronic UV/sun, wind, and dust exposure-fitting this outdoor worker. A pinguecula (A) is a similar UV-related yellowish conjunctival deposit but remains on the conjunctiva and does NOT cross onto the cornea. Conjunctival squamous cell carcinoma (B) tends to appear gelatinous/leukoplakic, nodular, or with abnormal feeder vessels and atypical features-warranting biopsy-rather than the smooth, classic wing shape. A phlyctenule (D) is a small inflammatory nodule (often hypersensitivity-related), not a progressive corneal-encroaching wing.

stages.label: RISK FACTORS
stages.question: Which is the most important risk factor for the development and progression of a pterygium?
options: Contact lens overwear in this setting
Chronic warfarin use in this setting
Chronic ultraviolet exposure
Uncontrolled diabetes mellitus
stages.explanation: Chronic ultraviolet light exposure (A) is the principal risk factor for pterygium, which is why it is common in outdoor workers and people living near the equator; wind, dust, and dryness contribute. Contact lens overwear (B) predisposes to microbial keratitis and giant papillary conjunctivitis, not pterygium. Uncontrolled diabetes (C) affects the retina and lens (retinopathy, cataract) but is not a recognized driver of pterygium. Chronic warfarin use (D) predisposes to subconjunctival hemorrhage, not pterygium formation. Counseling therefore centers on UV protection-sunglasses and brimmed hats-to slow growth and reduce recurrence.

stages.label: SLIT-LAMP EXAM
stages.content:
Lesion: wing-shaped fibrovascular growth, nasal bulbar conjunctiva crossing limbus onto cornea, OD
Apex (head): approaching but not reaching the visual axis
Surface: smooth, vascularized; no leukoplakia or nodularity
Cornea: clear centrally, no infiltrate or ulcer
Refraction: no significant vision-limiting astigmatism today
Visual acuity: 20/25 OD, 20/20 OS

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given that the lesion is not encroaching on the visual axis and acuity is preserved, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Topical antibiotic drops indefinitely as initial therapy
Conservative care-UV protection , lubricating drops for irritation,
Urgent excisional surgery this week as initial therapy
Immediate radiotherapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: An asymptomatic or mildly symptomatic pterygium that does not threaten the visual axis is managed conservatively: UV protection (sunglasses, hats), artificial tears/lubricants for irritation, and periodic monitoring of size and effect on the cornea (C). Urgent surgery (A) is not warranted now; surgery is elective and reserved for specific indications. Indefinite topical antibiotics (B) have no role-this is not an infection. Radiotherapy (D) is not first-line management; adjunctive measures to reduce recurrence after surgery (e.g., conjunctival autograft, sometimes mitomycin C) are surgeon-directed. The mainstay for a quiet, non-encroaching pterygium is surveillance plus symptom control and sun protection.

stages.label: INDICATIONS FOR SURGERY
stages.question: Which development would most clearly justify surgical removal of the pterygium?
options: Encroachment on the visual axis or induced astigmatism reducing vision
Stable size over many years without symptoms as the immediate next step
A single episode of foreign-body sensation as the immediate next step
Mild redness after a windy day as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Surgery is indicated when the pterygium threatens or reaches the visual axis, induces clinically significant astigmatism that degrades vision, causes persistent intolerable irritation despite lubricants, or for major cosmetic concern (B). Mild redness after wind (A) is managed with lubricants and UV protection. A pterygium stable over years without symptoms (C) needs only surveillance, not surgery. A single episode of foreign-body sensation (D) is not an operative indication. Importantly, patients should be counseled that pterygia can recur after excision, so surgeons use techniques like conjunctival autografting (and occasionally mitomycin C) to lower recurrence rates.

cases.diagnosis: Pterygium
pearls: A pterygium is a wing-shaped fibrovascular growth that crosses the limbus onto the cornea (usually nasal); a pinguecula stays on the conjunctiva.
Chronic UV exposure is the key risk factor-counsel sunglasses and brimmed hats to slow progression and reduce recurrence.
Manage quiet, non-encroaching lesions conservatively: lubricants and surveillance.
Operate for visual-axis encroachment, vision-degrading astigmatism, refractory irritation, or cosmesis-and warn about recurrence (use conjunctival autograft +/- mitomycin C).

cases.title: Bulging Red Eye and a Whooshing Sound After a Fall
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive bulging, redness, and a constant whooshing noise in her left eye for three weeks.
cases.history: A 58-year-old woman presents with three weeks of a gradually protruding, red left eye accompanied by a continuous 'whooshing' sound she hears synchronized with her pulse, worse when lying down. She fell down a flight of stairs six weeks ago, striking the left side of her head, and had a brief loss of consciousness; a CT head at that time showed no intracranial hemorrhage. Since then she notes intermittent horizontal double vision, a dull retro-orbital ache, and mild left eyelid swelling. She denies fever, recent infection, or sinus symptoms. PMH: hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, atorvastatin 20 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: retired accountant, drinks wine socially, never smoked. Family history: noncontributory. She is most distressed by the noise, which interferes with sleep, and worried the eye is 'pushing out.' She has not had any visual blurring at distance but reports occasional transient dimming. No jaw claudication or scalp tenderness.
cases.exam: Left eye: axial and slightly inferolateral proptosis, measured 4 mm greater than the right by exophthalmometry, with visible pulsation. Conjunctiva shows dilated, tortuous 'corkscrew' episcleral vessels extending to the limbus and chemosis. Auscultation over the closed globe reveals a soft continuous bruit that diminishes with ipsilateral carotid compression. Intraocular pressure is 26 mmHg OS, 15 mmHg OD. Extraocular motility shows mild abduction limitation OS. Pupils equal, no RAPD. Visual acuity 20/25 OS, 20/20 OD. Dilated fundus exam: mildly dilated retinal veins, no disc edema. Right eye normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature on this examination most strongly distinguishes this presentation from thyroid eye disease or an orbital cellulitis?
options: Limitation of abduction as the first step today
An orbital bruit with pulsatile proptosis
Elevated intraocular pressure as the first step today
Proptosis with eyelid swelling as the first step today
stages.explanation: An audible orbital bruit combined with pulsatile proptosis and arterialized (corkscrew) conjunctival/episcleral vessels is the classic triad pointing to an abnormal arteriovenous communication—a carotid-cavernous fistula. These three together are not features of thyroid eye disease or cellulitis. Proptosis with lid swelling (wrong) is nonspecific and occurs in both thyroid eye disease and cellulitis. Elevated IOP (wrong) can occur in thyroid eye disease and many orbital processes from raised episcleral venous pressure and is not discriminating alone. Abduction limitation (wrong) reflects sixth-nerve or restrictive involvement and is seen in numerous orbital and cavernous sinus conditions. The bruit, pulsation, and arterialized vessels specifically reflect high-flow arterial blood entering the venous orbital outflow, the hallmark of a fistula.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the prior trauma and high-flow features, which lesion is most likely and what is its typical mechanism?
options: An orbital varix that distends with Valsalva as the primary diagnosis
A direct high-flow fistula between the intracavernous internal carotid artery
Cavernous sinus thrombosis from septic emboli as the primary diagnosis
A spontaneous low-flow dural shunt from meningeal branches in an older patient
stages.explanation: A direct (high-flow) carotid-cavernous fistula results from a tear in the wall of the intracavernous internal carotid artery, often after head trauma, allowing arterial blood to flow directly into the cavernous sinus. This patient's significant head trauma and florid high-flow signs (loud bruit, marked pulsatile proptosis, very dilated vessels) fit a direct fistula. A spontaneous dural shunt (wrong) is low-flow, typically in older or hypertensive women, with subtler 'red eye' signs and no clear trauma. Cavernous sinus thrombosis (wrong) is usually septic, febrile, painful, and rapidly progressive with multiple cranial neuropathies. An orbital varix (wrong) intermittently distends with Valsalva and does not produce a continuous bruit or arterialized vessels.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
CT angiography head/orbits: Enlarged left superior ophthalmic vein
Bulging (convex) lateral wall of the left cavernous sinus
Proptosis of the left globe with enlarged extraocular muscles
MRI/MRA: Flow voids within an engorged left cavernous sinus
Digital subtraction angiography: Early opacification of the cavernous sinus during the arterial phase via a direct communication with the cavernous internal carotid artery
Dilated superior ophthalmic vein with reversed (anterograde to orbit) flow

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which imaging study is the gold standard for confirming the diagnosis and planning treatment?
options: B-scan ocular ultrasonography
Non-contrast CT of the orbits
Digital subtraction angiography
Orbital color Doppler ultrasound alone
stages.explanation: Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) is the gold standard: it demonstrates early arterial-phase filling of the cavernous sinus, defines whether the fistula is direct or dural, identifies feeding vessels and venous drainage patterns, and guides endovascular treatment. CT/CTA and MRI/MRA (helpful) show suggestive signs—enlarged superior ophthalmic vein, convex cavernous sinus—but cannot fully characterize flow dynamics for treatment planning. B-scan ultrasonography (wrong) may show a dilated superior ophthalmic vein but is nonspecific and cannot define the arterial supply. Non-contrast CT (wrong) lacks vascular detail. Color Doppler alone (wrong) can suggest arterialized, reversed flow in the superior ophthalmic vein but is operator-dependent and insufficient for definitive characterization or intervention planning.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate definitive treatment for this symptomatic high-flow fistula?
options: Systemic anticoagulation with heparin as initial therapy
High-dose intravenous corticosteroids as initial therapy
Endovascular transarterial or transvenous embolization
Observation, as most direct fistulas close spontaneously as monotherapy
stages.explanation: Symptomatic direct (high-flow) carotid-cavernous fistulas are treated with endovascular embolization—transarterial or transvenous occlusion using detachable coils, balloons, or covered stents—to obliterate the abnormal communication while preserving carotid flow. This relieves proptosis, bruit, and elevated IOP and prevents vision loss. Anticoagulation (wrong) does not close an arterial fistula and risks hemorrhage. Observation (wrong) is reasonable for many low-flow dural shunts, which can thrombose spontaneously, but high-flow direct fistulas with progressive proptosis, raised IOP, and risk of vision loss require intervention. Corticosteroids (wrong) treat inflammatory orbital disease, not a structural arteriovenous fistula. Urgency increases with cortical venous drainage, corneal exposure, or markedly elevated IOP threatening the optic nerve.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which feature would most strongly indicate an EMERGENT need for treatment to prevent permanent vision loss?
options: Cortical venous drainage or markedly elevated IOP with optic nerve compromise / severe corneal exposure
Mild chemosis and conjunctival injection as the leading concern
A soft bruit audible only on auscultation as the leading concern
Intermittent horizontal diplopia as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Emergent intervention is driven by threats to vision and brain: retrograde cortical venous drainage (risk of venous hypertension and intracranial hemorrhage), markedly elevated intraocular pressure causing optic neuropathy, or severe corneal exposure from proptosis. These mandate urgent endovascular closure. Mild chemosis/injection (wrong) is uncomfortable but not vision-threatening. A soft bruit (wrong) is a diagnostic sign, not an urgency marker. Intermittent diplopia (wrong) from a cranial neuropathy is troubling but not an emergency by itself. Recognizing the 'aggressive' features—cortical venous drainage, refractory glaucoma, exposure keratopathy, and rapidly worsening vision—separates fistulas needing prompt treatment from indolent low-flow shunts that can be observed.

cases.diagnosis: Direct (high-flow) carotid-cavernous fistula, post-traumatic, left eye
pearls: The classic triad of a carotid-cavernous fistula is pulsatile proptosis, an orbital bruit, and arterialized 'corkscrew' conjunctival vessels.
Direct (high-flow) fistulas typically follow trauma and arise from the intracavernous internal carotid artery; dural (low-flow) shunts are spontaneous, often in older hypertensive women.
An enlarged superior ophthalmic vein on CT/MRI is a key radiologic clue; digital subtraction angiography is the diagnostic and treatment-planning gold standard.
Elevated intraocular pressure from raised episcleral venous pressure and cortical venous drainage are danger signs; symptomatic high-flow fistulas are treated with endovascular embolization.

cases.title: Double Vision and a Numb Cheek After a Punch
cases.chiefComplaint: Double vision when looking up and numbness of the cheek after being struck in the eye.
