cases.history: A 29-year-old man presents to the emergency department two hours after being punched in the right eye during a bar altercation. He reports immediate pain, swelling, and vertical double vision that worsens when he looks upward. He also notes numbness over his right cheek, upper lip, and upper teeth. He denies loss of consciousness, neck pain, rhinorrhea, or loss of vision, though the eye 'feels pushed in.' He blew his nose afterward and noticed sudden swelling around the eye. PMH: none. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: occasional alcohol, vapes nicotine, no illicit drugs. Family history: noncontributory. He has had no prior eye surgery or trauma and wears no corrective lenses. Tetanus is up to date. He is anxious about the persistent double vision and the strange numbness in his face but otherwise feels well, with no headache, nausea, or vomiting since arrival.
cases.exam: Right periorbital ecchymosis and edema with mild enophthalmos. Visual acuity 20/20 OU; pupils equal and reactive, no RAPD. Extraocular motility: marked limitation of upgaze in the right eye with diplopia in upgaze; horizontal gaze full. Forced duction not yet performed. Hypoesthesia over the right infraorbital nerve distribution (cheek, upper lip, upper gingiva). Subcutaneous crepitus of the right lower lid (orbital emphysema). Conjunctiva mildly injected without laceration; cornea clear, anterior chamber deep and quiet. Intraocular pressure 16 mmHg OD. Fundus exam normal with no retinal detachment or commotio. No step-off of the orbital rim palpable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The combination of restricted upgaze, infraorbital numbness, and orbital emphysema after blunt trauma most strongly suggests injury to which structure?
options: The orbital floor (maxillary roof)
The medial orbital wall (lamina papyracea)
The orbital roof (frontal bone)
The zygomaticofrontal suture only
stages.explanation: Restricted upgaze (from inferior rectus/orbital fat entrapment), infraorbital nerve hypoesthesia (the nerve runs in the orbital floor), and orbital emphysema (communication with the maxillary sinus, worsened by nose-blowing) localize the fracture to the orbital floor—a classic blowout fracture. Medial wall fractures (partly correct as a co-occurrence) also cause emphysema but typically restrict horizontal gaze (medial rectus) and do not cause infraorbital numbness. Roof fractures (wrong) are rarer, associated with frontal trauma and possible CSF leak, and do not affect the infraorbital nerve. An isolated zygomaticofrontal suture injury (wrong) is part of a zygomaticomaxillary complex fracture pattern and does not explain infraorbital hypoesthesia or upgaze restriction from floor entrapment.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the best initial imaging study to confirm the fracture and assess for tissue entrapment?
options: Ocular B-scan ultrasonography in this clinical context
MRI of the orbits as the primary diagnosis
Non-contrast CT of the orbits with thin coronal cuts
Plain orbital radiographs (Waters view)
stages.explanation: Non-contrast CT of the orbits with thin axial and coronal reconstructions is the imaging study of choice for orbital fractures: it precisely defines floor defects, herniated orbital contents, and muscle entrapment, and the coronal view best shows inferior rectus position. Plain radiographs (wrong) may show a 'teardrop' sign or air-fluid level but are insensitive and have been largely replaced by CT. MRI (wrong) is not first-line acutely—slower, less available, and inferior for bony detail, though useful for soft tissue in select cases. B-scan ultrasonography (wrong) evaluates the globe (retinal detachment, vitreous hemorrhage) but cannot characterize the bony orbit or entrapment.

stages.label: CT ORBITS
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT orbits (coronal/axial): Fracture of the right orbital floor with a 'trapdoor' bony fragment
Herniation of inferior orbital fat into the right maxillary sinus
Inferior rectus muscle displaced inferiorly toward the fracture, partially entrapped
Air-fluid level in the right maxillary sinus; orbital emphysema
No orbital roof or medial wall fracture; globe intact, no retrobulbar hematoma
Infraorbital canal involved by the fracture line

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: This is an isolated floor fracture in an adult with non-resolving diplopia and a small amount of soft tissue entrapment. Which factor would most strongly favor URGENT (within 24–48 h) surgical repair rather than observation?
options: Orbital emphysema on CT as initial therapy
A trapdoor fracture with true muscle entrapment causing a non-resolving oculocardiac reflex
Infraorbital nerve hypoesthesia as initial therapy
Periorbital ecchymosis with 2 mm of enophthalmos as initial therapy
stages.explanation: True muscle entrapment in a trapdoor-type fracture—especially in children, whose elastic bone snaps back and traps the muscle ('white-eyed blowout')—causing a persistent oculocardiac reflex (nausea, bradycardia, syncope) is a surgical emergency requiring release within 24–48 hours to prevent muscle ischemia and permanent restriction. Infraorbital hypoesthesia (wrong) is common and often improves spontaneously; it is not an urgent operative indication. Orbital emphysema (wrong) usually resolves with conservative measures (avoid nose-blowing, antibiotics if sinus involvement). Mild enophthalmos with ecchymosis (wrong) can be observed initially; surgery for cosmetically significant enophthalmos (>2 mm) or large defects is typically planned after 1–2 weeks once swelling subsides, not urgently.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The patient has improving diplopia, no bradycardia or vomiting, and a small floor defect. What is the most appropriate disposition and counseling?
options: Admit for intravenous mannitol and corticosteroids to reduce orbital pressure with appropriate follow-up
Immediate operative repair tonight regardless of symptoms with appropriate follow-up
Discharge with sinus precautions, oral antibiotics, nasal decongestants, ice,
Discharge with no follow-up since vision is 20/20 with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: An adult with a small floor fracture, no entrapment-related oculocardiac reflex, and improving diplopia can be managed conservatively: avoid nose-blowing/Valsalva (sinus precautions), nasal decongestants, ice, and often a short course of oral antibiotics when the maxillary sinus is involved, with oculoplastics follow-up at 1–2 weeks. Surgery is then decided based on persistent diplopia in primary/downgaze or cosmetically significant enophthalmos. Immediate surgery tonight (wrong) is reserved for true entrapment with oculocardiac reflex. No follow-up (wrong) is unsafe—delayed enophthalmos and diplopia must be reassessed. Mannitol/steroids (wrong) treat retrobulbar hemorrhage/optic nerve compromise, which this patient does not have (IOP normal, no RAPD, no proptosis).

cases.diagnosis: Right orbital floor (blowout) fracture with inferior rectus entrapment and infraorbital nerve injury
pearls: The blowout fracture triad is restricted vertical gaze (diplopia), infraorbital nerve hypoesthesia, and enophthalmos; orbital emphysema worsens with nose-blowing.
Thin-cut coronal CT of the orbits is the imaging standard for defining the floor defect and inferior rectus entrapment.
The 'white-eyed blowout' / trapdoor fracture in children traps muscle with little external bruising and triggers the oculocardiac reflex—an emergency requiring repair within 24–48 hours.
Most adult floor fractures are observed first; surgery is for persistent diplopia in functional gaze or significant enophthalmos, usually after swelling subsides.

cases.title: Sudden Vision Loss With a Normal-Looking Eye After Head Trauma
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe loss of vision in one eye after a bicycle crash, with an eye that looks normal.
cases.history: A 24-year-old man presents after a high-speed bicycle crash in which he was thrown over the handlebars and struck the right side of his forehead and brow on the pavement; he wore no helmet. He had a brief dazed period but no sustained loss of consciousness. He now reports that vision in his right eye 'went dark' almost immediately and has not recovered. He denies eye pain, double vision, or flashes, but notes a right frontal headache and a scalp laceration over the brow. PMH: none. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: graduate student, occasional alcohol, no tobacco or drugs. Family history: noncontributory. He is alert and oriented, with no neck pain on log-roll precautions. He has not had prior eye disease and previously had normal vision in both eyes. He is frightened because the affected eye looks essentially normal to him in the mirror, yet he cannot see out of it.
cases.exam: Right eye: visual acuity counting fingers at 1 foot; a brisk relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) is present. Color vision (Ishihara) markedly reduced OD. Anterior segment normal—cornea clear, anterior chamber deep and quiet, no hyphema. Intraocular pressure 15 mmHg OD, 16 mmHg OS. Dilated fundus exam: optic disc appears normal and pink with sharp margins; macula and vessels normal; no retinal whitening, hemorrhage, or detachment. Right eye otherwise quiet. Left eye: 20/20, normal pupil, normal fundus. Periorbital and supraorbital ecchymosis with a brow laceration. No proptosis; extraocular movements full.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A patient with profound monocular vision loss, an RAPD, and a normal-appearing fundus after blunt brow trauma localizes the lesion to which structure?
options: The lens as the first step today
The retina as the first step today
The visual cortex
The optic nerve
stages.explanation: A relative afferent pupillary defect with severe vision loss but a normal fundus indicates an optic nerve lesion—here, traumatic optic neuropathy. The 'patient sees nothing, doctor sees nothing' pattern (normal disc acutely) is characteristic because the injury is typically retrobulbar. A retinal cause (wrong) would show fundus abnormalities (commotio, detachment, central retinal artery occlusion with a cherry-red spot). A lens cause such as traumatic cataract or dislocation (wrong) would impair the view and acuity but not produce an RAPD. A cortical lesion (wrong) causes homonymous field loss, not a monocular RAPD, because pupillary fibers leave before the geniculate. The RAPD is the single most important objective sign confirming an optic nerve (or extensive retinal) afferent defect.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Why does the optic disc typically appear NORMAL on the initial exam in this condition?
options: Acute swelling masks any abnormality on the same day as the primary diagnosis
Blunt trauma cannot injure the optic nerve, so the disc stays normal as the primary diagnosis
The macula compensates for the optic nerve injury as the primary diagnosis
The injury most often occurs in the intracanalicular/posterior portion of the nerve, so disc pallor takes 3–6 weeks to develop
stages.explanation: Most traumatic optic neuropathy is indirect and posterior—commonly at the intracanalicular segment where the nerve is tethered—so the visible optic disc looks normal acutely. Optic atrophy (disc pallor) emerges only after retrograde degeneration, typically over 3–6 weeks. Stating blunt trauma cannot injure the nerve (wrong) is false; deceleration forces transmit to the canal. 'Acute swelling masks abnormality' (wrong) is incorrect—anterior swelling would actually be visible; the disc is normal because the injury is behind the globe. The macula compensating (wrong) is not a real mechanism. Recognizing that a normal disc does not exclude severe optic nerve injury is the high-yield teaching point; the RAPD and acuity loss carry the diagnosis.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head/orbits (fine cuts): No intracranial hemorrhage
Fracture through the right optic canal with a small bony fragment near the canal
No retrobulbar hematoma; globe intact, no open-globe signs
No orbital floor or medial wall fracture
Visual evoked potentials (later): reduced amplitude and prolonged latency OD
Fundus photos: normal optic disc OD on day of injury

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which immediate bedside step is essential to exclude a surgically reversible cause of acute traumatic vision loss?
options: Measure intraocular pressure
Obtain fluorescein angiography as the immediate next step
Start intravenous mannitol empirically as the immediate next step
Patch the eye and reassess in 24 hours as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Before attributing vision loss purely to indirect optic neuropathy, you must exclude an orbital compartment syndrome from retrobulbar hemorrhage—a true emergency. Signs include proptosis, markedly elevated IOP, a tense orbit, and resistance to retropulsion; treatment is immediate lateral canthotomy and cantholysis. This patient has normal IOP and no proptosis, making compartment syndrome unlikely, but checking is essential. Empiric mannitol (wrong) is not the first action and does not address a compartment syndrome. Patching and waiting 24 hours (wrong) risks missing a reversible cause and permanent loss. Fluorescein angiography (wrong) evaluates retinal vasculature and is irrelevant to an optic nerve injury with a normal fundus.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Compartment syndrome is excluded. According to current evidence, what is the most appropriate management of indirect traumatic optic neuropathy?
options: Emergent optic canal decompression in every case as initial therapy
Individualized decision
Mandatory high-dose IV megadose corticosteroids for all patients as initial therapy
Immediate intravitreal anti-VEGF injection as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Current evidence (informed by the CRASH trial showing harm from high-dose steroids in head injury and the lack of proven benefit in the International Optic Nerve Trauma Study) supports an individualized approach to indirect traumatic optic neuropathy: observation is a legitimate option, and many cases are managed expectantly. Mandatory megadose corticosteroids (wrong) are not supported and may increase mortality in associated head trauma. Routine emergent canal decompression (wrong) lacks proven benefit and is reserved for selected cases (e.g., a compressive bony fragment with deteriorating vision) after multidisciplinary discussion. Intravitreal anti-VEGF (wrong) treats neovascular retinal disease, not optic nerve trauma. The prognosis depends on initial acuity and is guarded.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Which factor is the strongest predictor of final visual outcome in traumatic optic neuropathy?
options: Whether a brow laceration was sutured
The patient's age in this setting
The initial visual acuity
Presence of periorbital ecchymosis
stages.explanation: Initial visual acuity is the strongest prognostic factor in traumatic optic neuropathy—better presenting vision predicts better recovery, while no light perception at presentation portends a poor outcome. This guides counseling and shared decision-making. Age (wrong) has limited prognostic weight compared with initial acuity. Periorbital ecchymosis (wrong) reflects soft-tissue injury severity but does not predict optic nerve recovery. Whether a brow laceration was sutured (wrong) is irrelevant to optic nerve prognosis. Because no intervention has clearly proven benefit, presenting acuity remains the key data point for setting expectations, and serial exams track the development of optic atrophy over weeks.

cases.diagnosis: Indirect traumatic optic neuropathy, right eye (with right optic canal fracture)
pearls: Traumatic optic neuropathy is the classic 'patient sees nothing, doctor sees nothing'—severe vision loss with an RAPD but an acutely normal-appearing disc.
Disc pallor (optic atrophy) develops only after weeks because the indirect injury is usually posterior/intracanalicular.
Always exclude retrobulbar hemorrhage / orbital compartment syndrome (proptosis, high IOP, tense orbit) — treat with immediate lateral canthotomy and cantholysis.
High-dose corticosteroids and routine surgical decompression lack proven benefit and steroids may be harmful; management of indirect TON is individualized, often observation.

cases.title: Blurry Spot After a Paintball to the Eye
cases.chiefComplaint: Blurred central vision after being hit in the eye by a paintball.
cases.history: A 19-year-old man presents two hours after being struck directly in the left eye by a paintball during a game; he was wearing a mask that slipped. He reports immediate pain and blurred vision, particularly a dull spot in his central vision, but denies flashes, floaters, or a curtain over his vision. He has no double vision and no eye discharge. PMH: mild asthma. Medications: albuterol inhaler PRN. Allergies: none. Social: college student, social alcohol, vapes occasionally, no other drugs. Family history: noncontributory. He has never had eye surgery, wears glasses for mild myopia, and had normal vision before the impact. He is worried because the central blur has not improved over the last two hours. He denies headache, nausea, or loss of consciousness, and there was no other facial trauma. Tetanus status is current. He is otherwise healthy and active.
cases.exam: Left eye: visual acuity 20/60, improving to 20/50 with pinhole. Pupil round and reactive, no RAPD. Mild lid edema; conjunctiva slightly injected; cornea clear; anterior chamber deep with no hyphema; intraocular pressure 17 mmHg. Lens clear, no phacodonesis. Dilated fundus exam: a confluent area of gray-white retinal opacification (whitening) involving the posterior pole and extending to the macula, with the underlying choroidal pattern obscured; no retinal break, no detachment, no subretinal or vitreous hemorrhage. Right eye: 20/20, normal exam. No orbital rim tenderness or step-off; extraocular movements full.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The gray-white retinal opacification at the posterior pole after blunt ocular trauma represents which entity?
options: Subretinal hemorrhage
Commotio retinae (Berlin edema)
Retinal detachment
Central retinal artery occlusion
stages.explanation: Transient gray-white opacification of the outer retina after blunt trauma is commotio retinae (Berlin edema when it involves the macula). It results from disruption/damage to the photoreceptor outer segments and retinal pigment epithelium from the contrecoup shock wave—not true intracellular edema despite the historical name. Central retinal artery occlusion (wrong) causes whitening with a cherry-red spot and is typically painless, embolic, in older patients, with a profound RAPD. Retinal detachment (wrong) shows an elevated, mobile retina, often with a tear and a 'curtain' field defect. Subretinal hemorrhage (wrong) appears as a dark/red lesion under the retina. The diffuse outer-retinal whitening here without elevation, cherry-red spot, or hemorrhage is classic commotio.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Beyond identifying the retinal whitening, what is the most important next step in evaluating this trauma patient?
options: Start systemic corticosteroids as the immediate next step
Intravitreal anti-VEGF injection as the immediate next step
Immediate scleral buckle surgery as the immediate next step
A thorough dilated exam to rule out associated injuries
stages.explanation: Commotio retinae itself is usually self-limited, so the key step is a meticulous dilated examination (including scleral depression of the periphery) to detect concurrent vision-threatening injuries—retinal tears/dialysis, detachment, choroidal rupture, traumatic macular hole, hyphema, angle recession, or lens subluxation—which change management. Scleral buckle (wrong) is only for retinal detachment, not isolated commotio. Systemic corticosteroids (wrong) have no proven benefit and are not standard for commotio. Intravitreal anti-VEGF (wrong) treats neovascular disease and is not indicated. Documenting the full extent of trauma and arranging appropriate follow-up is essential because the prognosis depends largely on associated injuries rather than the whitening itself.

stages.label: ANCILLARY TESTING
stages.content:
Optical coherence tomography (OCT) macula OD-affected eye: Hyperreflectivity of the outer retina with disruption of the ellipsoid zone (photoreceptor inner/outer segment junction) at the fovea
No subretinal fluid, no full-thickness macular hole
Scleral-depressed peripheral exam: no retinal tear, dialysis, or detachment
No choroidal rupture identified
Intraocular pressure stable at 17 mmHg; no angle recession on gonioscopy

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the OCT showing foveal ellipsoid-zone disruption, what determines whether this patient recovers vision and what is the best management plan?
options: Permanent blindness is certain; refer for low-vision services only as the immediate next step
Recovery is generally good when peripheral retina is involved
Urgent vitrectomy is required to clear the whitening as the immediate next step
Vision always returns to normal regardless of macular involvement; no follow-up needed as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Commotio retinae confined to the peripheral retina typically resolves within days to weeks with full visual recovery. When the macula/fovea is involved (Berlin edema) and OCT shows persistent ellipsoid-zone (photoreceptor) disruption, the prognosis is more guarded—some patients are left with permanent reduced acuity, a macular pigmentary scar, or even a macular hole. Because the condition is self-limited and has no specific therapy, management is supportive: observation, protective eyewear, and scheduled re-examination to detect delayed complications (hole, detachment). Saying vision always normalizes with no follow-up (wrong) ignores macular cases. Certain permanent blindness (wrong) is overly pessimistic. Vitrectomy (wrong) does not treat commotio and is reserved for specific complications such as a macular hole or detachment.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate disposition for this patient?
options: No follow-up necessary as the injury is benign with appropriate follow-up
Admit for intravenous steroids with appropriate follow-up
Schedule immediate laser photocoagulation of the macula with appropriate follow-up
Discharge with eye protection, return precautions for flashes/floaters/curtain,
stages.explanation: Isolated commotio retinae is managed as an outpatient: discharge with protective eyewear, clear return precautions for new flashes, floaters, or a curtain/shadow (which would suggest a delayed retinal tear or detachment), and ophthalmology follow-up in roughly 1–2 weeks for a repeat dilated, scleral-depressed exam. Admission for IV steroids (wrong) is unnecessary and unproven. Laser to the macula (wrong) would damage central vision and is not a treatment for commotio. No follow-up (wrong) is unsafe because delayed retinal detachment and macular complications can develop after blunt trauma, and macular involvement here warrants monitoring of visual recovery.

cases.diagnosis: Commotio retinae (Berlin edema) with foveal involvement, left eye, after blunt trauma
pearls: Commotio retinae is transient outer-retinal whitening after blunt trauma from photoreceptor/RPE disruption (contrecoup), not true edema; macular involvement is termed Berlin edema.
Distinguish it from central retinal artery occlusion (cherry-red spot, painless, older, embolic) — commotio follows trauma and lacks a cherry-red spot.
Always perform a scleral-depressed peripheral exam to exclude associated retinal breaks, detachment, choroidal rupture, and anterior-segment trauma.
Peripheral commotio usually resolves fully; persistent foveal ellipsoid-zone disruption on OCT predicts a guarded visual prognosis.

cases.title: Sudden Loss of Central Vision in One Eye
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden painless loss of central vision in the right eye that started yesterday.
cases.history: A 71-year-old woman presents with sudden, painless loss of central vision in her right eye noticed when she woke up yesterday; she sees a dark central blob. She denies flashes, a curtain, eye pain, or headache, and has no jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, or proximal muscle aches. PMH: long-standing poorly controlled hypertension, type 2 diabetes (last HbA1c 7.4%), and hyperlipidemia. Medications: lisinopril 20 mg daily, amlodipine 10 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, aspirin 81 mg daily. Allergies: sulfa (rash). Social: former smoker (30 pack-years, quit 5 years ago), no alcohol. Family history: mother had a stroke; father had glaucoma. She lives alone and is anxious about losing independence. She reports her blood pressure readings at home are 'often high, 160s.' She has had no recent trauma, no prior episodes of vision loss, and her last eye exam two years ago was unremarkable aside from mild hypertensive changes.
cases.exam: Right eye: visual acuity 20/200; pupil reactive with a trace RAPD. Anterior segment normal; intraocular pressure 16 mmHg. Dilated fundus: a focal, round, saccular dilation (~250 µm) of a second-order branch of the superior temporal retinal artery, surrounded by a multilevel macular hemorrhage—preretinal, intraretinal, and subretinal components—with adjacent lipid (hard) exudate and a partial hemorrhagic ring. Scattered arteriolar narrowing, arteriovenous nicking, and a few flame hemorrhages elsewhere (hypertensive retinopathy). No disc edema, no neovascularization, no retinal detachment. Left eye: 20/25, similar hypertensive arteriolar changes, no aneurysm or hemorrhage.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A focal saccular dilation of a retinal arteriole with surrounding multilevel macular hemorrhage in a hypertensive older woman is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Wet age-related macular degeneration
Retinal arterial macroaneurysm
Proliferative diabetic retinopathy with neovascularization
Branch retinal vein occlusion in this clinical context
stages.explanation: A focal, saccular outpouching of a retinal arteriole (usually a first- to third-order branch, often superotemporal) with hemorrhage at multiple levels—preretinal, intraretinal, and subretinal—plus surrounding lipid exudate is the hallmark of a retinal arterial macroaneurysm, strongly associated with systemic hypertension in older women. Proliferative diabetic retinopathy (wrong) features neovascularization and is bilateral/diffuse, not a single saccular arteriolar dilation. Branch retinal vein occlusion (wrong) follows a venous distribution with sectoral flame hemorrhages and a dilated tortuous vein at an arteriovenous crossing, not an arterial saccule. Wet AMD (wrong) shows a choroidal neovascular membrane with subretinal fluid/hemorrhage and drusen, typically without a visible arteriolar aneurysm; it is not tied to a single arteriole.

stages.label: RISK FACTOR
stages.question: Which systemic condition is most strongly associated with this lesion and should be optimized?
options: Iron-deficiency anemia
Hypothyroidism in this setting
Hyperthyroidism in this setting
Systemic hypertension
stages.explanation: Retinal arterial macroaneurysms are most strongly associated with chronic systemic hypertension (and arteriosclerosis), typically in women in their 60s–70s—exactly this patient, whose BP is 172/96 with established hypertensive retinopathy. Optimizing blood pressure is central to management and reducing the risk to the fellow eye. Hypothyroidism (wrong) is not linked to macroaneurysm formation. Iron-deficiency anemia (wrong) can cause retinal hemorrhages in severe cases but is not the characteristic association with macroaneurysms. Hyperthyroidism (wrong) is unrelated. Identifying and treating hypertension (often co-managing with the primary physician) is both diagnostically supportive and therapeutically important.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Fluorescein angiography: Focal saccular dilation of a superotemporal arteriole with early uniform filling and late leakage
Surrounding blockage (hypofluorescence) from multilevel hemorrhage
No retinal or disc neovascularization; no capillary nonperfusion typical of vein occlusion
OCT macula: Subretinal and intraretinal fluid with hyperreflective hemorrhage; foveal contour disrupted
Blood pressure recheck in clinic: 168/94 mmHg
Carotid duplex (ordered): pending

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The macroaneurysm is leaking with macular hemorrhage and edema threatening the fovea. Which management approach is most appropriate?
options: Immediate panretinal photocoagulation of the entire retina as initial therapy
Blood pressure optimization plus targeted treatment of the exudative complication
Observation alone with no systemic workup as initial therapy
Emergent enucleation as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Management combines systemic blood pressure control with treatment tailored to the complication. Many macroaneurysms involute and thrombose spontaneously, so observation is appropriate when the fovea is not threatened. When there is sight-threatening exudation or macular edema, options include focal laser photocoagulation to the aneurysm or feeding vessel and intravitreal anti-VEGF for associated edema; submacular or dense preretinal hemorrhage may need additional measures. Panretinal photocoagulation (wrong) treats ischemic neovascular disease (proliferative diabetic retinopathy, ischemic vein occlusion), not a focal arterial aneurysm. Observation with no workup (wrong) misses critical hypertension management. Enucleation (wrong) is never indicated for this condition. Co-management of blood pressure is essential to protect the fellow eye.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If this patient instead presented with a dense, dome-shaped collection of blood beneath the macula obscuring the fovea, which complication and consideration is most relevant?
options: Endophthalmitis requiring intravitreal antibiotics as the leading concern
Rhegmatogenous retinal detachment requiring scleral buckle as the leading concern
Submacular hemorrhage, where displacement may be considered to protect the fovea
Vitreous hemorrhage requiring immediate panretinal photocoagulation as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Rupture of a retinal arterial macroaneurysm can produce a dense submacular (subretinal) hemorrhage that, if it persists under the fovea, causes photoreceptor toxicity and poor visual outcomes. Options to protect the fovea include pneumatic displacement with intravitreal gas (often combined with tissue plasminogen activator) or vitreoretinal surgery in selected cases. Vitreous hemorrhage with PRP (wrong) conflates a different bleed type with a treatment for neovascular disease; PRP is not indicated for a macroaneurysm. Endophthalmitis (wrong) is an infection with pain, hypopyon, and rapid decline—not a hemorrhagic complication of a macroaneurysm. Rhegmatogenous detachment with scleral buckle (wrong) arises from retinal breaks, not from arteriolar hemorrhage. Recognizing and addressing submacular hemorrhage is key to preserving central vision.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which imaging modality best confirms the saccular arteriolar dilation and characterizes its leakage?
options: Fluorescein angiography
B-scan ultrasonography in this setting
Fundus autofluorescence in this setting
Optical coherence tomography angiography only
stages.explanation: Fluorescein angiography best confirms a retinal arterial macroaneurysm: it shows early, uniform filling of the saccular arteriolar dilation with late leakage, and surrounding blockage from hemorrhage, while excluding neovascularization and the capillary nonperfusion of vein occlusion. OCT angiography (partly useful) can show flow within the aneurysm but may be obscured by overlying hemorrhage and is adjunctive. B-scan ultrasonography (wrong) is for media opacity/posterior segment evaluation when the view is poor, not for characterizing a small arteriolar aneurysm. Fundus autofluorescence (wrong) maps RPE/lipofuscin changes and does not demonstrate the vascular lesion or its leakage. Combined with OCT for macular fluid, fluorescein angiography secures the diagnosis and guides whether focal laser or anti-VEGF is needed.

cases.diagnosis: Retinal arterial macroaneurysm with multilevel macular hemorrhage, right eye (in a hypertensive patient)
pearls: A retinal arterial macroaneurysm is a focal saccular arteriolar dilation, classically superotemporal, in older hypertensive women, causing multilevel (pre-, intra-, subretinal) macular hemorrhage.
Systemic hypertension is the key association—blood pressure optimization is part of management and protects the fellow eye.
Many macroaneurysms thrombose and involute spontaneously; treat (focal laser, anti-VEGF) when exudation or macular edema threatens the fovea.
Dense submacular hemorrhage is a vision-threatening complication that may warrant pneumatic displacement (gas ± tPA) or surgery to protect the fovea.

cases.title: A White Pupil in a Young Boy's Eye
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents noticed a white reflection in one eye in photographs and that he seems to bump into things on that side.
cases.history: A 6-year-old boy is brought in because his parents have repeatedly seen a white-yellow glow in his right eye in flash photographs over the past few months, and he has started closing one eye and bumping into objects on his right. He has no eye pain or redness. He was a full-term healthy child meeting developmental milestones. He has had no fever, weight loss, or systemic symptoms. PMH: unremarkable; no prematurity or oxygen therapy. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Family history: no retinoblastoma, no childhood eye cancers, no known inherited eye disease; parents are nonconsanguineous. Social: in first grade, lives with both parents and a younger sibling. The parents are very worried after reading online that a white pupil can mean eye cancer. He has not had prior eye exams beyond newborn screening. Vision in the left eye seems normal to the family. There is no history of trauma. He has no strabismus that the parents had noticed before this.
cases.exam: Right eye: leukocoria (white pupillary reflex); visual acuity reduced to counting fingers. A sensory exotropia of the right eye is noted. Anterior segment is quiet; no rubeosis at this stage. Dilated fundus exam: yellowish subretinal exudation with prominent retinal telangiectatic, dilated, 'light-bulb' aneurysmal vessels in the inferotemporal/temporal periphery, massive lipid (hard) exudate, and an exudative (non-rhegmatogenous) retinal elevation; no discrete calcified white mass. Left eye: 20/20, normal fundus with no telangiectasia or exudate. No proptosis. No lymphadenopathy. The remainder of the pediatric exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Leukocoria in a child is most urgently concerning for which diagnosis that MUST be excluded first?
options: Congenital cataract
Persistent fetal vasculature
Retinoblastoma
Coats disease in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Any child with leukocoria must have retinoblastoma—an intraocular malignancy that is life-threatening if missed—excluded first. Although this child's findings (telangiectatic vessels, massive lipid exudation, exudative detachment, unilateral, boys) point toward Coats disease, the workup is driven by the imperative to rule out retinoblastoma. Congenital cataract (wrong) causes leukocoria but the lens here is clear and the white reflex is from subretinal exudate. Coats disease (correct as the eventual diagnosis, but not the entity that must be excluded first) is a key differential and retinoblastoma mimic. Persistent fetal vasculature (wrong) presents in infancy, often with microphthalmia and a retrolental stalk, not telangiectatic peripheral vessels with lipid. Distinguishing Coats from retinoblastoma is the central diagnostic challenge.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the telangiectatic 'light-bulb' vessels, massive lipid exudation, and unilateral involvement in a young boy, which diagnosis best fits?
options: Retinoblastoma in this clinical context
Coats disease
Retinopathy of prematurity
Familial exudative vitreoretinopathy
stages.explanation: Coats disease is an idiopathic, typically unilateral retinal vascular disorder of young boys characterized by telangiectatic and aneurysmal ('light-bulb') retinal vessels with abundant intraretinal/subretinal lipid exudation and exudative retinal detachment—exactly this picture. Familial exudative vitreoretinopathy (wrong) is bilateral and inherited with peripheral avascularity and dragging of vessels, usually with a family history. Retinopathy of prematurity (wrong) requires prematurity/oxygen exposure, which this full-term child lacks, and is bilateral. Retinoblastoma (wrong) typically shows a solid mass, often with calcification on imaging, rather than diffuse telangiectasia with lipid; it remains the must-exclude mimic. The combination of unilaterality, male sex, telangiectasia, and lipid exudate is classic for Coats disease.

stages.label: IMAGING & ANCILLARY TESTS
stages.content:
B-scan ultrasonography: exudative retinal detachment with subretinal echoes; NO intraocular calcification
Fluorescein angiography: focal telangiectasia, 'light-bulb' aneurysmal dilations, capillary nonperfusion, and leakage in the temporal periphery
MRI orbits: subretinal exudate; no enhancing solid mass, no extraocular extension; findings not typical of retinoblastoma
CBC and exam: no leukocoria-associated systemic disease; no lymphadenopathy
Subretinal fluid (if sampled): cholesterol crystals and lipid-laden macrophages

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which finding most reliably helps DISTINGUISH this condition from retinoblastoma on imaging?
options: Presence of an exudative retinal detachment as the immediate next step
Unilateral involvement as the immediate next step
Leukocoria on external exam as the immediate next step
Absence of intraocular calcification on B-scan/CT, with no enhancing solid mass on MRI
stages.explanation: Retinoblastoma characteristically contains intraocular calcification (seen on B-scan ultrasound or CT) and forms an enhancing solid mass; Coats disease lacks calcification and shows subretinal exudate without a solid enhancing tumor on MRI. This imaging distinction is the most reliable separator. An exudative retinal detachment (wrong) occurs in both Coats disease and advanced (exophytic) retinoblastoma, so it does not discriminate. Leukocoria (wrong) is the shared presenting sign that prompts the workup, not a differentiator. Unilateral involvement (wrong) is typical of Coats but also common in sporadic unilateral retinoblastoma, so it cannot reliably distinguish them. When imaging is equivocal, examination under anesthesia by an ocular oncologist is essential.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Retinoblastoma is excluded. The eye has telangiectasia, exudation, and a localized exudative detachment with retained visual potential. What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Systemic chemotherapy as for retinoblastoma as initial therapy
Observation only, as the disease never progresses as initial therapy
Immediate enucleation as initial therapy
Ablation of the abnormal telangiectatic vessels with laser photocoagulation and/or cryotherapy, with anti-VEGF
stages.explanation: Coats disease is treated by ablating the leaking telangiectatic vessels—laser photocoagulation for less extensive disease and cryotherapy for peripheral or more exudative lesions—to halt exudation and allow resorption of subretinal fluid. Intravitreal anti-VEGF can be an adjunct for significant exudation/edema, and vitreoretinal surgery (with possible drainage) is reserved for advanced total exudative detachment. Enucleation (wrong) is reserved for blind, painful eyes or when retinoblastoma cannot be excluded—not when visual potential remains. Systemic chemotherapy (wrong) treats retinoblastoma, not Coats disease. Observation alone (wrong) is inappropriate because Coats is progressive and can lead to total detachment, neovascular glaucoma, and a blind painful eye if untreated. Early intervention preserves vision and the globe.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Which factor at presentation most strongly predicts a poor visual outcome in this disease?
options: The presence of a few peripheral telangiectasias only
Advanced stage with total exudative retinal detachment
A normal fellow eye as the immediate next step
Male sex as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The strongest predictor of poor visual outcome in Coats disease is advanced stage at presentation—particularly total exudative retinal detachment and progression to neovascular glaucoma, which can lead to a blind, painful eye and even enucleation. Earlier stages with limited telangiectasia treated promptly have far better outcomes. A few peripheral telangiectasias (wrong) represent early, more treatable disease. Male sex (wrong) is a demographic association, not an outcome predictor within the disease. A normal fellow eye (wrong) is expected (Coats is typically unilateral) and does not worsen the affected eye's prognosis. Staging (Shields classification) thus drives both treatment intensity and prognostic counseling, underscoring the value of early detection.

cases.diagnosis: Coats disease, right eye (idiopathic retinal telangiectasia with exudation)
pearls: Leukocoria in a child mandates urgent exclusion of retinoblastoma before any other diagnosis is accepted.
Coats disease is classically unilateral, in young boys, with telangiectatic 'light-bulb' retinal vessels and massive lipid exudation causing exudative (non-rhegmatogenous) detachment.
Absence of intraocular calcification and of an enhancing solid mass on imaging helps separate Coats disease from retinoblastoma.
Treatment ablates the leaking telangiectasias with laser and/or cryotherapy (± anti-VEGF/surgery) to preserve vision and avoid total detachment.

cases.title: Fading Vision in One Eye, Then the Other, in a Young Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Painless blurring and a darkening central spot in the left eye, now starting in the right eye.
cases.history: A 23-year-old man reports that over the past three weeks the central vision in his left eye became progressively blurred, with a dark smudge in the middle of his sight; colors look 'washed out.' In the last few days the right eye has begun to do the same. There is no eye pain, no pain with eye movement, no flashes, and no headache. PMH: healthy. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: he smokes about a pack of cigarettes a day and drinks alcohol heavily on weekends. Family history: a maternal uncle and his mother's brother went 'legally blind' in their 20s for unclear reasons, and his mother has mild visual complaints; his father's side is unaffected. He works as a bartender. He denies recent illness, weight loss, numbness, weakness, or bladder symptoms. He is alarmed that this is following the same pattern his uncle described, and that it is now affecting both eyes sequentially over a short period.
cases.exam: Visual acuity 20/200 OS and 20/60 OD, both declining. Central scotomas (cecocentral) on confrontation and formal fields. Marked dyschromatopsia (red-green) bilaterally. Pupils: sluggish; no significant RAPD because involvement is becoming symmetric/bilateral. Anterior segments normal; IOP normal. Dilated fundus: in the acute phase, circumpapillary telangiectatic microangiopathy, hyperemia and pseudoedema of the optic disc, and tortuous retinal vessels, WITHOUT true leakage. No vitritis. No pallor yet, but early temporal disc changes. Neurologic exam otherwise normal—no other cranial nerve deficits, normal strength, sensation, gait, and reflexes.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Sequential, painless, bilateral central vision loss with cecocentral scotomas and dyschromatopsia in a young man points to which localization and pattern?
options: Bilateral optic neuropathy
Acute angle-closure glaucoma
Occipital (cortical) stroke
Bilateral retinal detachment
stages.explanation: Central/cecocentral scotomas, prominent dyschromatopsia, and reduced acuity with relatively normal-appearing or hyperemic discs indicate optic nerve (papillomacular bundle) dysfunction—a bilateral optic neuropathy. The sequential, painless, young-male pattern with a maternal-line family history is highly suggestive of Leber hereditary optic neuropathy. Bilateral retinal detachment (wrong) would cause field defects with an elevated mobile retina and is not painless-symmetric in this way. Acute angle-closure glaucoma (wrong) is painful with a red eye, high IOP, mid-dilated pupil, and halos—absent here (IOP normal, no pain). Occipital stroke (wrong) causes homonymous field defects with preserved central acuity if the macula is spared and does not produce cecocentral scotomas or dyschromatopsia from optic nerve disease; it is also unusual in a healthy 23-year-old.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The fundus shows circumpapillary telangiectasia and disc pseudoedema that does NOT leak on angiography. Together with the family history, which diagnosis and inheritance pattern is most likely?
options: Leber hereditary optic neuropathy with mitochondrial inheritance
Multiple sclerosis–associated optic neuritis in this clinical context
Nutritional (toxic) optic neuropathy, X-linked
Autosomal dominant optic atrophy (Kjer) as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Leber hereditary optic neuropathy (LHON) is a mitochondrial disorder (maternally inherited; common point mutations at mtDNA positions 11778, 14484, 3460) causing subacute, sequential, painless bilateral central vision loss in young men. The classic acute fundus triad is circumpapillary telangiectatic microangiopathy, disc pseudoedema, and absence of leakage on fluorescein angiography—exactly described. The maternal-line family history (affected uncle, mother's relatives) fits maternal inheritance. Dominant optic atrophy (wrong) is autosomal dominant, insidious from childhood, and milder. MS-associated optic neuritis (wrong) is usually unilateral, PAINFUL with eye movement, and shows true disc/RAPD findings with response to steroids. 'Nutritional/toxic, X-linked' (wrong) misstates inheritance—toxic/nutritional optic neuropathy is acquired and not X-linked, though tobacco/alcohol can worsen LHON expression.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC WORKUP
stages.content:
Fluorescein angiography: NO disc leakage despite pseudoedema (distinguishes from true papillitis/papilledema)
Mitochondrial DNA testing: pathogenic m.11778G>A (MT-ND4) mutation detected
MRI brain/orbits with contrast: optic nerves without enhancement; no demyelinating white-matter lesions
B12, folate, methylmalonic acid: normal
OCT (retinal nerve fiber layer): swelling of the RNFL acutely, expected to progress to atrophy
Visual evoked potentials: prolonged latency and reduced amplitude bilaterally

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which test most definitively confirms the diagnosis?
options: Mitochondrial DNA testing for the common LHON point mutations
Temporal artery biopsy as the immediate next step
Lumbar puncture with oligoclonal bands in this setting
Serum aquaporin-4 (NMO) antibody as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Mitochondrial DNA testing for the three common LHON point mutations (m.11778G>A, m.14484T>C, m.3460G>A) is definitive—identifying a pathogenic mutation confirms the diagnosis, as in this patient's m.11778G>A result. Lumbar puncture with oligoclonal bands (wrong) evaluates multiple sclerosis/optic neuritis, which the painless, bilateral, non-enhancing presentation argues against. Temporal artery biopsy (wrong) is for giant cell arteritis in older patients with systemic symptoms and elevated inflammatory markers. Serum aquaporin-4 antibody (wrong) tests for neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder, which typically causes severe painful optic neuritis often with myelitis, not this maternally inherited fundus picture. Genetic confirmation also guides counseling for maternal relatives.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management and counseling for this patient?
options: Urgent neurosurgical optic nerve decompression as initial therapy
Plasma exchange as initial therapy
High-dose IV corticosteroids as for optic neuritis as initial therapy
Idebenone may be offered, strongly advise smoking
stages.explanation: LHON management is largely supportive plus modifiable-risk reduction: idebenone (a coenzyme Q10 analog) is the agent with the best evidence and may improve outcomes, especially if started early; patients should be strongly counseled to stop smoking and limit alcohol (these and other mitochondrial toxins worsen expression/penetrance), and to avoid drugs toxic to mitochondria. Low-vision rehabilitation and genetic counseling for maternal relatives are essential. High-dose steroids (wrong) treat inflammatory optic neuritis and do not help LHON. Plasma exchange (wrong) is used in NMO/severe demyelinating disease, not LHON. Optic nerve decompression (wrong) has no role in this mitochondrial neuropathy. Prognosis varies by mutation—14484 has the best chance of recovery, 11778 the worst.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: A maternally related female cousin asks about her risk. Which statement about inheritance and penetrance is most accurate?
options: Carriers are guaranteed to lose vision as the immediate next step
Only maternal relatives inherit the mutation
Affected men transmit the mutation to all their children as the immediate next step
The condition is autosomal dominant with full penetrance as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: LHON is mitochondrial, so the mutation passes only through the maternal line—maternal relatives can inherit it, but affected MEN do not transmit it to any of their children. Penetrance is incomplete: many carriers never lose vision, and males are affected several-fold more often than females, with additional modifiers (smoking, alcohol, hormonal/genetic factors). Saying affected men transmit to all children (wrong) describes autosomal dominant, not mitochondrial, inheritance. Calling it autosomal dominant with full penetrance (wrong) is incorrect on both counts. Stating carriers are guaranteed to lose vision (wrong) overstates risk given incomplete penetrance. Accurate counseling reassures carriers while emphasizing modifiable risks like tobacco and alcohol avoidance.

cases.diagnosis: Leber hereditary optic neuropathy (m.11778G>A), bilateral sequential
pearls: LHON causes subacute, painless, sequential bilateral central vision loss in young men, with cecocentral scotomas and dyschromatopsia.
The acute fundus triad is circumpapillary telangiectatic microangiopathy, disc pseudoedema, and NO leakage on fluorescein angiography.
It is maternally (mitochondrial) inherited—common mutations 11778, 14484, 3460; genetic testing confirms the diagnosis and guides family counseling.
Management: idebenone, avoidance of tobacco/alcohol and mitochondrial toxins, low-vision support; 14484 carries the best visual prognosis and 11778 the worst.

cases.title: A Teenager Who Can't See the Board Anymore
cases.chiefComplaint: Gradual blurring of central vision in both eyes over the past year, worse for reading and the classroom board.
cases.history: A 14-year-old girl is brought in for about a year of slowly worsening central vision in both eyes; she struggles to read the board, holds books close, and has trouble recognizing faces across a room, though her side vision feels normal. She also notices difficulty seeing in bright light and takes longer to adjust between light and dark. There is no eye pain, redness, flashes, or floaters. PMH: healthy, full-term, no prematurity. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: 9th grader, no smoking/alcohol, no toxic exposures, no medications associated with retinal toxicity. Family history: her parents have normal vision, but a younger brother is reported to have 'weak eyes'; the parents are first cousins (consanguineous). She has had glasses prescribed twice in the past year without much improvement, which prompted referral. She denies night blindness as a prominent early symptom. She is frustrated that new glasses have not helped and is worried about driving in the future.
cases.exam: Best-corrected visual acuity 20/80 OD and 20/100 OS; refraction does not improve acuity. Pupils equal, reactive, no RAPD. Anterior segments normal; IOP normal. Color vision mildly reduced. Dilated fundus exam: bilateral, symmetric, ill-defined macular atrophy with a beaten-bronze appearance and yellow-white pisciform (fish-tail) flecks scattered at the level of the retinal pigment epithelium in the posterior pole, extending beyond the arcades; peripheral retina relatively normal; vessels and optic disc normal; no bone-spicule pigmentation. The exam appears far worse functionally than one might expect from a casual look, with subtle early macular change.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Bilateral central vision loss with yellow-white pisciform flecks at the level of the RPE and macular atrophy in a teenager suggests which category of disease?
options: Diabetic macular edema
Bilateral central retinal vein occlusion
Bilateral optic neuritis
An inherited macular dystrophy
stages.explanation: Symmetric, bilateral central vision loss in a teenager with macular atrophy and characteristic yellow-white pisciform (fish-tail) flecks at the RPE level is the hallmark of an inherited macular dystrophy—most likely Stargardt disease. The young age, symmetry, flecks, and family clues (consanguinity, affected sibling) all fit a hereditary dystrophy. Bilateral optic neuritis (wrong) is typically painful, often with disc/RAPD changes and a more acute course, without RPE flecks. Diabetic macular edema (wrong) would require diabetes and shows microaneurysms, hard exudate, and edema, not pisciform flecks in a healthy 14-year-old. Bilateral central retinal vein occlusion (wrong) is extremely rare at this age and produces diffuse hemorrhages and dilated tortuous veins, not RPE-level flecks.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis and inheritance pattern best fits, and what is the underlying gene?
options: Best vitelliform dystrophy, autosomal dominant (BEST1)
Cone-rod dystrophy, mitochondrial
Retinitis pigmentosa, X-linked (RPGR)
Stargardt disease, autosomal recessive (ABCA4)
stages.explanation: Stargardt disease is the most common inherited macular dystrophy, usually autosomal recessive due to mutations in ABCA4, which encodes a photoreceptor transporter; impaired clearance leads to lipofuscin (A2E) accumulation in the RPE, producing pisciform flecks and central atrophy in the first two decades. The consanguineous parents support recessive inheritance. Best disease (wrong) is autosomal dominant (BEST1) with a characteristic egg-yolk (vitelliform) macular lesion and an abnormal EOG, not pisciform flecks. X-linked RP (wrong) presents with night blindness, peripheral bone-spicule pigment, and an attenuated arterioles—not isolated macular flecks early on. Cone-rod dystrophy can overlap clinically, but the classic flecks-plus-macular-atrophy picture from ABCA4 is the best fit here, and it is not mitochondrial.

stages.label: IMAGING & ELECTROPHYSIOLOGY
stages.content:
Fluorescein angiography: a 'dark (silent) choroid' — masking of choroidal fluorescence by lipofuscin in the RPE
Fundus autofluorescence: hyperautofluorescent flecks with central hypoautofluorescent atrophy
OCT macula: loss of the ellipsoid zone and outer retinal/photoreceptor atrophy at the fovea
Genetic testing: biallelic ABCA4 pathogenic variants confirmed
Electroretinogram: variable; often normal early (macular disease) with later cone (± rod) dysfunction
Color vision: red-green and mild blue-yellow defects

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which classic angiographic finding strongly supports this diagnosis?
options: Branch-distribution capillary nonperfusion
Disc hyperfluorescence with late leakage
A 'dark choroid' on fluorescein angiography
Macular leakage in a petaloid pattern
stages.explanation: A 'dark choroid' (silent choroid) on fluorescein angiography—where excessive lipofuscin in the RPE masks the normal background choroidal fluorescence—is a classic, strongly supportive sign of Stargardt disease, present in a large proportion of cases. Petaloid macular leakage (wrong) describes cystoid macular edema, not Stargardt. Disc hyperfluorescence with late leakage (wrong) indicates optic nerve head inflammation/edema (e.g., papillitis), unrelated to this dystrophy. Branch-distribution capillary nonperfusion (wrong) is a feature of vascular occlusive disease such as vein occlusion, not a hereditary RPE/photoreceptor dystrophy. Together with fundus autofluorescence, OCT photoreceptor loss, and ABCA4 genetic confirmation, the dark choroid helps secure the diagnosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management and counseling for this patient and family?
options: Immediate macular laser photocoagulation as initial therapy
High-dose vitamin A supplementation as initial therapy
Systemic corticosteroids to halt progression as initial therapy
No specific cure exists
stages.explanation: There is no approved cure for Stargardt disease; management is supportive and protective. Patients should use UV-blocking sunglasses and avoid bright light/sun exposure that may accelerate RPE damage, and—importantly—AVOID high-dose vitamin A, which can increase toxic lipofuscin (A2E) accumulation in ABCA4 disease. Low-vision rehabilitation (magnifiers, assistive technology), educational accommodations, and genetic counseling (autosomal recessive; sibling at risk) are key, and emerging gene/pharmacologic therapies may be available through clinical trials. High-dose vitamin A (wrong) is specifically discouraged and may worsen the disease. Macular laser (wrong) would damage central vision and has no role. Systemic corticosteroids (wrong) do not affect this non-inflammatory dystrophy. Realistic counseling about progression and driving is appropriate.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: The parents ask about prognosis and risk to the younger sibling. Which counseling is most accurate?
options: Vision typically stabilizes at near-normal levels and siblings are not at risk as the immediate next step
Central vision usually declines progressively with preserved peripheral vision
The disease is X-linked, so only sons are at risk as the immediate next step
Blindness including loss of peripheral vision is inevitable within a year as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Stargardt disease usually causes progressive loss of central vision—commonly declining toward 20/200 over time—while peripheral vision and navigation are typically preserved (it is a macular dystrophy). As an autosomal recessive (ABCA4) condition, two carrier parents confer a 25% recurrence risk for each subsequent child, so the younger sibling should be evaluated. Saying vision stabilizes near normal with no sibling risk (wrong) is overly optimistic and ignores recessive inheritance. Calling it X-linked (wrong) misstates the genetics. Predicting total blindness including peripheral loss within a year (wrong) is inaccurate—peripheral vision is generally retained and progression is gradual. Honest counseling supports planning, low-vision services, and family genetic testing.

cases.diagnosis: Stargardt disease (ABCA4-related macular dystrophy), bilateral
pearls: Stargardt disease is the most common inherited macular dystrophy—autosomal recessive ABCA4 mutations causing lipofuscin accumulation in the RPE.
Look for bilateral central vision loss in youth, yellow-white pisciform (fish-tail) flecks, beaten-bronze macular atrophy, and a 'dark/silent choroid' on fluorescein angiography.
OCT shows foveal photoreceptor (ellipsoid zone) loss; fundus autofluorescence highlights flecks and atrophy; ABCA4 testing confirms the diagnosis.
There is no cure—counsel on UV protection, AVOID high-dose vitamin A (worsens A2E accumulation), and provide low-vision and genetic support.

cases.title: A Boy With Intensely Itchy, Watery Eyes Every Spring
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe, recurrent eye itching, redness, and stringy discharge that flares each spring.
cases.history: An 11-year-old boy presents with several weeks of intensely itchy, red, watery eyes with a thick, ropy (stringy) discharge and marked light sensitivity. His mother reports this happens every spring and summer and improves in winter, and that he constantly rubs his eyes and blinks. He feels like there is something gritty under his upper lids. PMH: atopic dermatitis and seasonal allergic rhinitis; he had asthma as a younger child. Medications: cetirizine PRN, topical emollients for eczema. Allergies: dust mites, grass pollen. Social: lives in a warm, dry climate; loves outdoor soccer. Family history: mother and sister have allergic rhinitis; father has asthma. He has missed school days due to photophobia. He has used over-the-counter 'redness-relief' drops without lasting benefit. There is no purulent green discharge, no fever, and no recent sick contacts with conjunctivitis. He denies trauma and contact lens use. The itching is the most prominent and distressing symptom.
cases.exam: Both eyes: conjunctival hyperemia with thick, ropy mucoid discharge and photophobia. Eversion of the upper eyelids reveals giant 'cobblestone' papillae (>1 mm) on the superior tarsal conjunctiva. At the limbus there are gelatinous infiltrates with chalky-white Horner-Trantas dots (collections of eosinophils). Cornea: mild superficial punctate keratopathy superiorly; no shield ulcer at this time, and the visual axis is clear. Visual acuity 20/20 OU; pupils normal; no RAPD. No preauricular lymphadenopathy. No follicles or membranes typical of viral or chlamydial disease. Periocular skin shows mild eczematous changes.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Seasonal, intensely itchy conjunctivitis with giant cobblestone tarsal papillae and limbal Horner-Trantas dots in an atopic boy is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Vernal keratoconjunctivitis
Bacterial conjunctivitis
Viral (adenoviral) conjunctivitis
Acute angle-closure glaucoma
stages.explanation: Vernal keratoconjunctivitis (VKC) is a chronic, bilateral, seasonal (spring/summer) allergic ocular surface disease of young, often atopic boys, marked by intense itching, ropy mucus, giant cobblestone papillae on the superior tarsus, and limbal Horner-Trantas dots (eosinophil aggregates)—exactly this presentation. Bacterial conjunctivitis (wrong) features purulent discharge and a burning/gritty sensation rather than dominant itching, without giant papillae or Trantas dots. Viral conjunctivitis (wrong) gives watery discharge, follicles, and preauricular lymphadenopathy, often after a recent cold, not seasonal cobblestone papillae. Acute angle-closure glaucoma (wrong) presents with a painful red eye, halos, a mid-dilated pupil, high IOP, and nausea—none of which are present (vision 20/20, normal pupils). Itching is the cardinal symptom pointing to allergy.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Which immune mechanism predominantly drives this condition?
options: Purely neutrophilic bacterial infection in this setting
Immune-complex (type III) deposition as the immediate next step
IgG-mediated cytotoxic (type II) reaction in this setting
IgE-mediated hypersensitivity with eosinophilic/Th2 inflammation
stages.explanation: VKC is driven by IgE-mediated (type I) hypersensitivity together with a strong Th2/eosinophilic and chronic (delayed, type IV) component—mast cell degranulation and eosinophil-rich inflammation produce the itching, papillae, and Trantas dots (which are literally eosinophil collections). This fits the patient's atopic background. Immune-complex (type III) deposition (wrong) underlies conditions like serum sickness or vasculitis, not VKC. IgG-mediated cytotoxic (type II) reactions (wrong) describe processes such as autoimmune hemolytic anemia, not allergic conjunctivitis. A purely neutrophilic bacterial infection (wrong) would cause purulent discharge without the eosinophilic, itch-dominant allergic picture. Understanding the allergic, eosinophilic mechanism explains why mast cell stabilizers, antihistamines, and anti-inflammatory therapy work.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management for moderate vernal keratoconjunctivitis?
options: Oral corticosteroids indefinitely as initial therapy
Allergen avoidance, cool compresses,
Topical antibiotic drops as initial therapy
Long-term continuous topical corticosteroids as monotherapy
stages.explanation: First-line VKC management combines trigger/allergen avoidance and supportive measures (cool compresses, lubricants) with topical dual-action antihistamine/mast-cell stabilizers such as olopatadine for ongoing control; short, judicious pulses of topical corticosteroids are added for acute flares, and topical calcineurin inhibitors (cyclosporine or tacrolimus) are useful steroid-sparing agents for chronic/severe disease. Topical antibiotics (wrong) do not treat an allergic process. Continuous corticosteroid monotherapy (wrong) risks steroid-induced glaucoma, cataract, and infection, so steroids are limited to pulses with monitoring. Indefinite oral corticosteroids (wrong) carry unacceptable systemic toxicity and are not standard. Severe VKC warrants ophthalmology co-management, especially to detect and treat corneal shield ulcers.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which sight-threatening corneal complication of this disease must be monitored for, and what causes it?
options: Acanthamoeba keratitis from tap-water rinsing as the leading concern
Fungal keratitis from soil exposure as the leading concern
Corneal neovascularization from contact lens overwear as the leading concern
A shield ulcer — a sterile epithelial defect over the superior cornea from inflammatory mediators
stages.explanation: A shield (vernal) ulcer is the key sight-threatening complication of VKC: a sterile, oval epithelial defect/plaque, typically in the superior cornea, caused by toxic eosinophil-derived inflammatory mediators and mechanical abrasion from the giant tarsal papillae rubbing the cornea. It can scar the visual axis and requires aggressive anti-inflammatory treatment (and sometimes debridement). Contact lens–related neovascularization (wrong) is unrelated—this patient does not wear lenses. Fungal keratitis (wrong) follows vegetative/soil trauma and is infectious, not the characteristic VKC complication. Acanthamoeba keratitis (wrong) is linked to contact lenses and water exposure with severe pain out of proportion, again not the hallmark VKC complication. Recognizing and promptly treating a shield ulcer preserves vision.

cases.diagnosis: Vernal keratoconjunctivitis (bilateral, seasonal)
pearls: VKC is a chronic, seasonal, intensely itchy allergic conjunctivitis of young atopic boys with giant cobblestone superior tarsal papillae and limbal Horner-Trantas dots (eosinophils).
It is driven by IgE-mediated (type I) plus Th2/eosinophilic inflammation—itching is the cardinal symptom.
First-line therapy: allergen avoidance, dual-action antihistamine/mast-cell stabilizers, with short steroid pulses for flares and topical cyclosporine/tacrolimus for steroid-sparing control.
Watch for a shield (vernal) ulcer—a sterile superior corneal ulcer from inflammatory mediators and papillary trauma that can threaten vision.

cases.title: Routine Eye Screening on a Long-Term Lupus Medication
cases.chiefComplaint: Referred for a scheduled eye screening; she has noticed subtle trouble reading and faint paracentral 'gaps' in her vision.
cases.history: A 60-year-old woman is referred for a routine ophthalmologic screening visit. She has been on hydroxychloroquine for systemic lupus erythematosus for 9 years. She mentions that over the past several months reading has become subtly harder and she occasionally notices small missing patches just off-center, especially in dim light; she denies sudden vision loss, pain, flashes, or floaters. PMH: SLE (on therapy), hypertension, and chronic kidney disease (stage 3). Medications: hydroxychloroquine 400 mg daily (she weighs 62 kg), lisinopril 10 mg daily, low-dose prednisone 5 mg daily. Allergies: penicillin (hives). Social: never smoked, rare alcohol, retired teacher. Family history: mother had macular degeneration. She is concerned because her rheumatologist mentioned the medication 'can affect the eyes.' She has had only intermittent eye exams in the past. Her renal function has declined gradually. She takes the hydroxychloroquine reliably and has never had a dose reduction despite her weight and kidney disease.
cases.exam: Best-corrected visual acuity 20/25 OD and 20/30 OS. Pupils equal, reactive, no RAPD. Anterior segments unremarkable; IOP normal; lenses with mild nuclear sclerosis. Dilated fundus: subtle bilateral parafoveal retinal pigment epithelial changes with early loss of the foveal reflex; in the more advanced eye there is a ring of parafoveal depigmentation around a preserved central island, beginning to form a 'bull's-eye' pattern. No drusen typical of AMD, no hemorrhage, no vascular occlusion. Amsler grid: faint paracentral scotomas. The peripheral retina is normal. Findings are symmetric and concentrated in the macula, sparing the very center early on.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given her 9-year hydroxychloroquine use and parafoveal changes, which factor in her history MOST increases her risk of retinal toxicity?
options: Her family history of age-related macular degeneration
High daily dose relative to body weight plus duration
Her hypertension as the first step today
Her prednisone use as the first step today
stages.explanation: The major risk factors for hydroxychloroquine retinopathy are a high daily dose relative to actual body weight (current guidance: keep ≤5 mg/kg/day), long duration of use (>5 years markedly raises risk), and renal disease (reduced clearance increases drug levels)—plus concurrent tamoxifen and pre-existing retinal/macular disease. This patient is on 400 mg/day at 62 kg (≈6.4 mg/kg/day, above the safe threshold), for 9 years, with stage 3 CKD: a high-risk combination. Hypertension (wrong) is not a recognized risk factor for this toxicity. Prednisone (wrong) does not cause this retinopathy. A family history of AMD (wrong) is a separate condition; while pre-existing maculopathy can complicate screening, AMD family history is not the principal driver of hydroxychloroquine toxicity here. Dose, duration, and renal function are the key modifiable risks.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The parafoveal ring of depigmentation around a preserved center is described as which classic pattern, and what does it represent?
options: A 'sea-fan' neovascular frond as the primary diagnosis
A 'sunburst' from sickle retinopathy as the primary diagnosis
'Bull's-eye maculopathy' from parafoveal photoreceptor/RPE damage with foveal sparing
A 'cherry-red spot' from inner retinal ischemia as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Hydroxychloroquine retinopathy classically produces a 'bull's-eye maculopathy': a ring of parafoveal retinal pigment epithelium and photoreceptor damage that spares the central fovea, creating concentric rings resembling a target. It corresponds to the parafoveal scotomas she describes. A cherry-red spot (wrong) reflects inner retinal ischemia (e.g., central retinal artery occlusion or some storage diseases), not chloroquine toxicity. A 'sunburst' lesion (wrong) is a chorioretinal scar seen in sickle cell retinopathy. A 'sea-fan' (wrong) is a peripheral neovascular frond, also in sickle cell disease. Notably, in more pigmented (Asian) patients toxicity can be more pericentral/extramacular rather than purely parafoveal, which screening must account for. The bull's-eye pattern is the classic late sign.

stages.label: SCREENING TESTS
stages.content:
Automated visual field (10-2): bilateral parafoveal scotomas
Spectral-domain OCT: parafoveal thinning of the outer retina with disruption of the ellipsoid zone and a preserved subfoveal region ('flying saucer' sign)
Fundus autofluorescence: parafoveal ring of increased autofluorescence (early) with areas of decreased autofluorescence where RPE is lost
Multifocal ERG: reduced parafoveal amplitudes
Calculated dose: 400 mg/day ÷ 62 kg ≈ 6.4 mg/kg/day (exceeds ≤5 mg/kg/day)
Renal function: eGFR 48 mL/min/1.73 m² (stage 3 CKD)

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which combination of tests is recommended for screening and detecting early hydroxychloroquine retinopathy?
options: Fluorescein angiography alone as the immediate next step
Automated 10-2 visual fields PLUS spectral-domain OCT
Intraocular pressure measurement and dilated exam only in this setting
Color vision testing and gonioscopy as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Current screening guidelines recommend automated 10-2 visual fields combined with spectral-domain OCT as the primary modalities, supplemented when needed by fundus autofluorescence (FAF) and/or multifocal ERG. These objective tests detect early parafoveal (or, in some patients, pericentral) damage before irreversible fundus changes—OCT may show parafoveal ellipsoid-zone loss with foveal preservation (the 'flying saucer' sign). Color vision and gonioscopy (wrong) are not sensitive screening tools for this toxicity. Fluorescein angiography alone (wrong) is not the recommended screening test and is unnecessary for routine surveillance. IOP and a dilated exam only (wrong) are insufficient because early toxicity precedes visible fundus changes. A baseline exam at initiation and annual screening after 5 years (sooner with risk factors) is advised.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Toxicity is confirmed. What is the single most important management step?
options: Increase the hydroxychloroquine dose to suppress lupus and protect the eyes as initial therapy
Begin laser photocoagulation of the macula as initial therapy
Promptly discontinue hydroxychloroquine in coordination with her rheumatologist, since the retinopathy can progress even after stopping
Start intravitreal anti-VEGF injections as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of management is to stop the offending drug—promptly discontinue hydroxychloroquine in coordination with the prescribing rheumatologist (who will choose an alternative for her lupus)—because there is no treatment to reverse the retinopathy, and damage can continue to progress for a time even after cessation. Early detection and stopping the drug limit further loss. Increasing the dose (wrong) would worsen the toxicity. Intravitreal anti-VEGF (wrong) treats neovascular/exudative disease, not a toxic maculopathy. Macular laser (wrong) would damage central vision and has no role. This case also highlights prevention: dosing ≤5 mg/kg/day of actual weight and dose adjustment for renal impairment, with guideline-based screening to catch toxicity early.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: To minimize future risk in patients on this drug, which dosing and monitoring strategy is correct?
options: No screening is needed if visual acuity is normal as the immediate next step
Keep the dose ≤5 mg/kg of ACTUAL body weight per day, reduce for renal impairment, obtain a baseline exam,
Dose by ideal body weight at up to 6.5 mg/kg/day with screening only if symptoms occur as the immediate next step
Screen only every 10 years regardless of risk as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Current guidance is to keep hydroxychloroquine ≤5 mg/kg of ACTUAL body weight per day, lower the dose in renal impairment (reduced clearance raises drug levels), perform a baseline ophthalmologic exam near initiation, and screen annually beginning after 5 years of use—earlier and more often when risk factors (high dose, renal disease, tamoxifen, pre-existing maculopathy) are present. Dosing by ideal weight at 6.5 mg/kg with symptom-only screening (wrong) reflects outdated, higher-risk practice. Screening every 10 years (wrong) is far too infrequent. Relying on normal acuity (wrong) is unsafe because early toxicity is detected by 10-2 fields and SD-OCT before central acuity drops. Prevention plus early objective screening averts irreversible vision loss.

cases.diagnosis: Hydroxychloroquine (toxic) retinopathy — bull's-eye maculopathy, bilateral
pearls: Key risk factors for hydroxychloroquine retinopathy: daily dose >5 mg/kg actual weight, duration >5 years, renal impairment, concurrent tamoxifen, and pre-existing maculopathy.
The classic late sign is bull's-eye maculopathy (parafoveal RPE/photoreceptor loss with foveal sparing); OCT may show the 'flying saucer' sign.
Screen with 10-2 automated visual fields plus SD-OCT (± FAF/mfERG); baseline at start and annually after 5 years, sooner if risk factors—remember pericentral patterns in more pigmented patients.
Management is to stop the drug with the rheumatologist; toxicity is irreversible and may progress even after discontinuation, so prevention and early detection are essential.

cases.title: Severe Eye Pain, Nausea, and Halos Around Lights
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe pain in the right eye with blurry vision, rainbow halos around lights, and vomiting that began this evening.
cases.history: A 62-year-old woman presents to the ED at 9 PM with 4 hours of excruciating right-sided eye and brow pain that started while she was watching a movie in a dim theater. She reports profound blurring of vision in that eye, colored halos around lights, and three episodes of non-bloody vomiting. She initially assumed it was 'another migraine' and took ibuprofen without relief. She denies trauma, discharge, or contact-lens use. PMH: hyperopia (she wears reading glasses and 'thick' distance glasses), well-controlled hypertension, and seasonal allergies for which she took an over-the-counter diphenhydramine tablet before the movie. Medications: lisinopril 10 mg daily, cetirizine as needed. Allergies: none. Social: retired teacher, never-smoker, rare alcohol. Family history: a sister had 'sudden blindness' requiring emergency eye surgery in her 60s. Review of systems is positive for headache and photophobia but negative for fever, neck stiffness, focal weakness, jaw claudication, and scalp tenderness.
cases.exam: Patient is in obvious distress, holding the right side of her face. Right eye: visual acuity hand-motion only; conjunctival injection with ciliary flush; cornea hazy/steamy (microcystic edema); pupil mid-dilated (~5 mm) and fixed, nonreactive to light; anterior chamber appears shallow on penlight side-illumination; globe feels rock-hard on gentle palpation. Left eye: acuity 20/25, clear cornea, round reactive pupil, shallow but quiet anterior chamber. No proptosis, no relative afferent pupillary defect on the left, extraocular movements intact bilaterally. No preauricular lymphadenopathy. Neuro grossly nonfocal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which bedside test most rapidly confirms the suspected diagnosis in this patient?
options: Lumbar puncture to exclude subarachnoid hemorrhage
Tonometry to measure intraocular pressure
Fluorescein staining to look for a dendritic corneal ulcer
