Dilated fundoscopic exam to evaluate the optic disc
stages.explanation: The constellation of a fixed mid-dilated pupil, steamy cornea, rock-hard globe, halos, and vomiting is classic for acute angle-closure glaucoma; tonometry rapidly confirms a markedly elevated IOP (often 40-80 mmHg) and is the single most useful bedside test. Fluorescein is wrong because there is no history of dendrite, abrasion, or contact-lens use, and corneal haze here is from edema, not epithelial defect. Fundoscopy is difficult through a hazy cornea and does not establish the diagnosis acutely. Lumbar puncture targets subarachnoid hemorrhage, but a unilateral red, hard, painful eye with halos points to the eye, not the meninges; LP would dangerously delay sight-saving treatment.

stages.label: BEDSIDE TESTING
stages.content:
Tonometry (right eye): IOP 58 mmHg
Tonometry (left eye): IOP 19 mmHg
Gonioscopy (left eye): appositionally narrow, occludable angle
Slit lamp (right): corneal microcystic edema, shallow anterior chamber, mid-dilated nonreactive pupil
Fundus (right): hazy view, disc not well visualized

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: What is the underlying mechanism of this patient's acute presentation?
options: Trabecular meshwork clogged by inflammatory cells and protein before discharge planning
Neovascular fibrovascular membrane contracting over the angle before discharge planning
Pupillary block causing iris bombe
Overproduction of aqueous humor by the ciliary body as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: In acute primary angle closure, the mid-dilated pupil maximizes iris-lens contact, blocking aqueous flow through the pupil (pupillary block). Aqueous accumulates behind the iris, bowing it forward (iris bombe), so the peripheral iris closes the already-narrow angle and obstructs trabecular outflow, spiking IOP. Her hyperopia (short eye, crowded anterior segment), dim-light pupil dilation, and an anticholinergic antihistamine were the perfect triggers. Inflammatory-cell clogging describes uveitic/inflammatory glaucoma. A contracting fibrovascular membrane describes neovascular glaucoma (e.g., post-CRVO/diabetes). Aqueous overproduction is not a recognized mechanism of acute glaucoma; the problem is outflow obstruction, not inflow excess.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which is the most appropriate initial medical regimen to lower this patient's IOP?
options: Immediate dilation with phenylephrine to break the attack as initial therapy
Topical timolol, apraclonidine,
Topical antibiotic drops and an eye patch as initial therapy
Topical atropine plus warm compresses as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Acute angle closure is treated with multimodal IOP lowering: topical beta-blocker (timolol) and alpha-agonist (apraclonidine) reduce aqueous production, a topical prostaglandin/pilocarpine plus systemic acetazolamide further lower pressure, and IV mannitol osmotically draws fluid out when IOP is very high. Pilocarpine constricts the pupil to pull the iris off the angle once pressure begins to fall. Atropine is exactly wrong-it dilates and worsens block. Antibiotics and patching treat infection/abrasion, not glaucoma. Phenylephrine dilates the pupil and would intensify pupillary block, precipitating disaster. Definitive treatment is laser peripheral iridotomy after the cornea clears, with prophylactic iridotomy of the fellow eye.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After IOP is medically controlled and the cornea clears, what is the definitive treatment and the key step for the fellow (left) eye?
options: Trabeculectomy to the affected eye only with appropriate follow-up
Observation alone, since the attack has resolved with appropriate follow-up
Laser peripheral iridotomy to the affected eye,
Lifelong topical timolol to both eyes with no procedure needed based on current risk
stages.explanation: Laser peripheral iridotomy (LPI) creates an alternative aqueous pathway, relieving pupillary block, and is the definitive treatment for the affected eye once the cornea is clear enough. Crucially, the fellow eye shares the same anatomically narrow, occludable angle (confirmed on gonioscopy here) and carries a high risk of a future attack, so it receives prophylactic LPI. Lifelong timolol alone leaves the anatomic block unaddressed and risks recurrence. Trabeculectomy is reserved for refractory chronic glaucoma, not first-line for acute angle closure. Observation alone ignores the imminent threat to both eyes; untreated angle closure causes permanent optic nerve damage and irreversible vision loss.

cases.diagnosis: Acute primary angle-closure glaucoma (right eye)
pearls: A fixed mid-dilated pupil with a steamy cornea and a rock-hard globe is acute angle closure until proven otherwise-check IOP immediately.
Hyperopia, dim lighting, and anticholinergic/sympathomimetic drugs (e.g., diphenhydramine, decongestants) are classic precipitants.
Never dilate or give atropine; pilocarpine and IOP-lowering drops/systemics are used first, then definitive laser peripheral iridotomy.
The fellow eye is anatomically at risk and warrants prophylactic peripheral iridotomy.

cases.title: Routine Screening Reveals Optic Disc Changes
cases.chiefComplaint: Asymptomatic patient referred after a routine optometry visit found high eye pressure and an abnormal-looking optic nerve.
cases.history: A 68-year-old Black man presents to the ophthalmology clinic after his optometrist noted elevated intraocular pressure and 'large cup-to-disc ratios' during a routine exam for new reading glasses. He has no eye pain, redness, halos, or noticeable vision loss and reads and drives without difficulty. He has never had eye surgery or trauma. PMH: type 2 diabetes mellitus (last A1c 6.9%), hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. Medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, amlodipine 10 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg nightly. Allergies: none. Social: retired postal worker, former smoker (quit 15 years ago), occasional alcohol. Family history is notable: his father went blind 'from glaucoma' in his 70s. Review of systems is otherwise negative; he specifically denies sudden vision loss, flashes, floaters, eye pain, and headache. He is concerned because of his family history and wants to know whether he will lose his sight.
cases.exam: Visual acuity 20/20 right eye and 20/25 left eye with correction. Pupils equal and reactive, no relative afferent pupillary defect. Intraocular pressure 27 mmHg right and 29 mmHg left by applanation. Slit-lamp anterior segment is unremarkable, conjunctivae white and quiet, corneas clear, anterior chambers deep and quiet. Gonioscopy shows open, normal-appearing angles bilaterally. Dilated fundus exam reveals increased cup-to-disc ratios (0.7 right, 0.8 left) with thinning of the inferior neuroretinal rim and a notch; no disc hemorrhage today. Maculae and peripheral retinae are normal. No neovascularization.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the open angles, elevated IOP, and disc cupping, which test best characterizes the functional damage and guides monitoring?
options: Standard automated visual field perimetry
B-scan ocular ultrasound
Corneal topography as the first step today
Fluorescein angiography
stages.explanation: Open-angle glaucoma is a chronic optic neuropathy; standard automated perimetry (Humphrey visual field) maps the characteristic functional defects and is the cornerstone of diagnosis and longitudinal monitoring alongside OCT of the retinal nerve fiber layer. Fluorescein angiography evaluates retinal/choroidal vasculature (e.g., diabetic retinopathy, CNV), not glaucomatous field loss. B-scan ultrasound images the globe when media are opaque (e.g., dense cataract, vitreous hemorrhage)-unnecessary here with clear media. Corneal topography maps corneal shape for keratoconus or refractive surgery, irrelevant to optic nerve function (though central corneal thickness pachymetry is useful for interpreting IOP). Perimetry directly answers whether-and where-vision is being lost.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC TESTING
stages.content:
Humphrey visual field (right): superior arcuate (Bjerrum) scotoma with nasal step; central fixation preserved
Humphrey visual field (left): denser superior and inferior arcuate defects, nasal step
OCT RNFL: inferior and superior rim thinning, both eyes (left worse)
Central corneal thickness: 505 µm right, 510 µm left (thin)
IOP on repeat: 28 mmHg right, 30 mmHg left

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature most strongly supports primary open-angle glaucoma rather than another optic neuropathy?
options: Arcuate scotoma with a nasal step
Bitemporal hemianopia respecting the vertical meridian as the primary diagnosis
A central scotoma with metamorphopsia and subretinal fluid as the primary diagnosis
Sudden, painful central vision loss with an afferent pupillary defect as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Glaucomatous damage targets the arcuate retinal nerve fiber bundles, producing arcuate/Bjerrum scotomas and nasal steps that respect the horizontal meridian, with central acuity spared until late-exactly this patient's pattern, paired with open angles, elevated IOP, cupping, and rim thinning. Sudden painful loss with an RAPD suggests optic neuritis or arteritic ischemic optic neuropathy, not chronic glaucoma. Bitemporal hemianopia respecting the vertical meridian localizes to the optic chiasm (e.g., pituitary tumor). A central scotoma with metamorphopsia and subretinal fluid points to macular disease such as neovascular AMD, not optic nerve disease. The combination here is textbook POAG.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment to reduce his risk of progressive vision loss?
options: Oral acetazolamide indefinitely as initial therapy
Immediate trabeculectomy in both eyes as initial therapy
Reassurance and re-evaluation in 2 years as initial therapy
Topical prostaglandin analog to lower IOP, or selective laser trabeculoplasty as an equivalent first-line option
stages.explanation: Lowering IOP is the only proven way to slow glaucomatous progression. A topical prostaglandin analog (latanoprost) is first-line: once-daily, potent, and well tolerated, working by increasing uveoscleral outflow. Selective laser trabeculoplasty is now an accepted equivalent first-line option and avoids adherence problems. Chronic systemic acetazolamide causes paresthesias, metabolic acidosis, and renal stones and is reserved for short-term or refractory use, not maintenance. Trabeculectomy is an invasive surgery reserved for inadequate response to medical/laser therapy. Reassurance with 2-year follow-up ignores documented field loss and high-risk features (Black race, family history, thin corneas, IOP near 30)-this patient needs treatment now.

stages.label: RISK FACTORS
stages.question: Which combination of this patient's features places him at highest risk for progression and justifies aggressive control?
options: Black race, positive family history, elevated IOP,
Hyperopia, female sex, and a shallow anterior chamber in this setting
Myopia, light iris color, and recent cataract surgery in this setting
Migraine history, contact-lens use, and dry eye in this setting
stages.explanation: Black race, a first-degree family history of glaucoma (his blind father), elevated IOP, older age, and thin central corneas are all established, additive risk factors for open-angle glaucoma onset and progression-thin corneas both raise true IOP risk and cause applanation to underestimate pressure. Hyperopia, female sex, and shallow chambers are risk factors for angle-closure, a different disease. Myopia is a POAG risk factor, but light iris color and recent cataract surgery are not the relevant drivers here. Migraine, contact-lens use, and dry eye are unrelated to glaucomatous optic neuropathy. Recognizing his high-risk profile justifies an aggressive IOP target and close monitoring.

cases.diagnosis: Primary open-angle glaucoma (bilateral)
pearls: POAG is typically asymptomatic with preserved central acuity until late-screening and disc/IOP findings, not symptoms, make the diagnosis.
Classic field loss is arcuate (Bjerrum) scotomas with nasal steps respecting the horizontal meridian; OCT RNFL complements perimetry.
Black race, family history, older age, high IOP, and thin central corneas raise risk; thin corneas cause applanation to underestimate IOP.
Lowering IOP is the only proven intervention; prostaglandin analogs or selective laser trabeculoplasty are first-line.

cases.title: Sudden Painless Loss of Vision in One Eye
cases.chiefComplaint: Abrupt, painless, near-complete loss of vision in the left eye that occurred 70 minutes ago.
cases.history: A 71-year-old man is brought to the ED by his wife after his vision in the left eye 'went dark like a curtain' while reading the paper about 70 minutes ago. The loss was instantaneous, painless, and persistent. He denies flashes, floaters, eye pain, redness, headache, jaw pain with chewing, scalp tenderness, or recent weight loss. He had a brief similar episode last month that resolved in minutes ('like a shade coming down'), which he ignored. PMH: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes, and atrial fibrillation for which he admits he 'stopped taking the blood thinner' months ago because of cost. Medications (as prescribed): amlodipine 10 mg daily, atorvastatin 80 mg nightly, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, apixaban 5 mg twice daily (non-adherent). Allergies: none. Social: 40-pack-year smoking history, social alcohol. Family history: father had a stroke. ROS otherwise negative, specifically no contralateral symptoms, weakness, dysarthria, or facial droop.
cases.exam: Left eye: visual acuity counting-fingers at best, with a dense relative afferent pupillary defect (Marcus Gunn pupil). Anterior segment normal, cornea clear, IOP 16 mmHg, eye white and quiet, no pain on movement. Dilated fundus exam of the left eye: diffuse retinal whitening/opacification of the posterior pole with a cherry-red spot at the fovea; arterial narrowing with segmented 'box-carring' of the blood column. Right eye: acuity 20/30, normal disc, no APD. Carotid auscultation reveals a left-sided bruit. Cardiac exam irregularly irregular. No temporal artery tenderness or nodularity; temporal arteries pulsatile. Neuro exam nonfocal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Sudden painless monocular vision loss with a cherry-red spot and box-carring is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Vitreous hemorrhage
Central retinal artery occlusion
Central retinal vein occlusion
Acute angle-closure glaucoma
stages.explanation: Acute, painless, severe monocular vision loss with a pale edematous retina, a cherry-red spot (intact choroidal perfusion showing through at the thin foveola), arterial box-carring, and a dense RAPD is the classic picture of central retinal artery occlusion. CRVO causes the opposite fundus appearance-diffuse retinal hemorrhages, tortuous dilated veins ('blood-and-thunder')-not a pale retina with box-carring. Vitreous hemorrhage obscures the fundus view and is associated with floaters, often in diabetics, without a cherry-red spot. Acute angle closure is painful, with a red eye, hazy cornea, mid-dilated pupil, and very high IOP-none of which are present in this white, soft, painless eye.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: CRAO is considered a stroke equivalent. What is the most appropriate immediate disposition?
options: Discharge with outpatient ophthalmology follow-up in 1 week with appropriate follow-up
Activate an emergent stroke pathway: urgent neuroimaging
Start topical antibiotics and patch the eye with appropriate follow-up
Admit to ophthalmology for elective laser photocoagulation with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: CRAO is a central nervous system infarction of retinal tissue and a stroke equivalent. Current guidance is to manage these patients like acute ischemic stroke: emergent transfer/evaluation at a stroke center with urgent brain imaging, because they carry a high short-term risk of concurrent or subsequent cerebral stroke and need expedited etiologic workup. Discharge with routine follow-up is dangerous and squanders any treatment window. Laser photocoagulation treats neovascular complications, not acute arterial occlusion, and is not emergent here. Topical antibiotics and patching treat surface/infectious problems-this is an ischemic, intraocular vascular emergency, not an ocular surface disease.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ESR 14 mm/hr
CRP 4 mg/L
Platelets 240 ×10³/µL
INR 1.1
Finger-stick glucose 142 mg/dL
ECG: atrial fibrillation, rate 106
Noncontrast CT head: no hemorrhage, no established infarct
Carotid duplex: 80% stenosis of the left internal carotid artery
Transthoracic echo: left atrial enlargement, no visible thrombus

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The patient presented within a few hours of onset. Regarding acute treatment options, which statement is most accurate?
options: Hyperbaric oxygen is contraindicated in all cases of CRAO as initial therapy
Intravenous thrombolysis given within an early time window is the most promising acute therapy
Intravitreal anti-VEGF injection is the standard acute treatment for CRAO as initial therapy
There is robust, guideline-mandated evidence that ocular massage and anterior chamber paracentesis restore vision in most patients as monotherapy
stages.explanation: Because CRAO is a retinal infarct, the most promising acute intervention is IV thrombolysis delivered within an early window (commonly cited as up to roughly 4.5 hours from onset), administered through stroke-center pathways-this is the central time-window dilemma and why rapid recognition matters. Conservative maneuvers (ocular massage, paracentesis, breathing into a bag, IOP-lowering drops) are commonly attempted but have weak, unproven efficacy; they are not guideline-mandated cures. Hyperbaric oxygen is actually an adjunct used at some centers, not universally contraindicated. Anti-VEGF treats later neovascularization (e.g., in ischemic vein occlusion or diabetic disease), not the acute arterial blockage. Time is retina.

stages.label: ETIOLOGY
stages.question: Two embolic sources are evident on workup. Which combination best explains this patient's CRAO and prior transient episode?
options: Retinal vasospasm from migraine and dehydration as the immediate next step
Carotid artery atherosclerosis
Open-angle glaucoma and diabetic macular edema as the immediate next step
Giant cell arteritis and central vein thrombosis as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: His prior brief monocular shade-down was amaurosis fugax-a warning TIA-equivalent from carotid disease, and duplex confirms an 80% left ICA stenosis ipsilateral to the affected eye. He also has untreated atrial fibrillation off apixaban, a potent cardioembolic source. Together these explain both the herald event and today's CRAO, and they direct secondary prevention (carotid revascularization evaluation plus anticoagulation). Giant cell arteritis must always be excluded in older patients, but his ESR/CRP are normal and there are no GCA symptoms, making arteritic disease unlikely. Glaucoma and diabetic macular edema do not cause acute arterial occlusion. Migraine vasospasm is a diagnosis of exclusion and does not fit a 71-year-old with structural carotid and cardiac emboli.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Beyond acute treatment, which secondary-prevention plan best addresses both embolic sources identified in this patient?
options: Antiplatelet monotherapy alone, with no further vascular evaluation as the immediate next step
Resume/initiate oral anticoagulation for atrial fibrillation, refer for carotid revascularization of the symptomatic high-grade ICA stenosis,
Long-term systemic corticosteroids to prevent recurrence as the immediate next step
Daily ocular massage indefinitely to keep the artery patent as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Both embolic sources demand targeted prevention. His atrial fibrillation requires oral anticoagulation (e.g., resuming apixaban with attention to the cost barrier that caused non-adherence), which is superior to antiplatelet therapy for cardioembolic prevention. His symptomatic 80% ipsilateral ICA stenosis meets criteria for carotid revascularization (endarterectomy or stenting), ideally done early after the ischemic event. Aggressive risk-factor control-high-intensity statin, blood pressure, glycemic management, and smoking cessation-rounds out prevention. Antiplatelet monotherapy alone undertreats his atrial fibrillation and ignores the operable carotid lesion. Systemic corticosteroids are for giant cell arteritis, which was excluded. Ongoing ocular massage has no role in secondary prevention and does not address the embolic sources.

cases.diagnosis: Central retinal artery occlusion (left eye), stroke equivalent, embolic from carotid stenosis and atrial fibrillation
pearls: Sudden painless monocular vision loss with a cherry-red spot, retinal whitening, box-carring, and a dense RAPD = CRAO.
CRAO is a stroke equivalent: activate stroke protocols, image the brain, and pursue urgent etiologic workup at a stroke center.
Always check ESR/CRP to exclude giant cell arteritis in older patients with retinal/optic ischemia.
Amaurosis fugax is a herald TIA-evaluate carotids and cardiac sources to prevent the next event.

cases.title: Gradual Darkening of Vision in One Eye
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive blurring and dimming of vision in the right eye over the past two weeks.
cases.history: A 66-year-old man presents to clinic with two weeks of painless, progressively worsening vision in his right eye, which now seems 'dim and smeared,' worse on waking. He denies flashes, a curtain, eye pain, redness, or headache. He recalls no sudden moment of loss; it has crept up. PMH: poorly controlled hypertension, hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes, and obstructive sleep apnea (poorly adherent to CPAP). He also has open-angle glaucoma in both eyes. Medications: hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, atorvastatin 20 mg nightly, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, latanoprost drops nightly to both eyes. Allergies: none. Social: former heavy smoker (quit 2 years ago), minimal alcohol. Family history: hypertension and stroke. ROS: denies jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, weight loss, limb weakness, or speech difficulty. He admits his blood pressure is 'usually high' and that he misses CPAP most nights.
cases.exam: Right eye: visual acuity 20/200, with a relative afferent pupillary defect. Anterior segment quiet, cornea clear, IOP 18 mmHg; importantly, the iris shows no new vessels today and the angle is open. Dilated fundus exam (right): optic disc swelling, dilated and tortuous retinal veins in all four quadrants, diffuse intraretinal hemorrhages (dot-blot and flame-shaped) extending to the periphery, scattered cotton-wool spots, and macular edema-a 'blood-and-thunder' fundus. Left eye: acuity 20/30, mild hypertensive arteriolar changes, no hemorrhages, IOP 17 mmHg. No proptosis, no carotid bruit appreciated.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The 'blood-and-thunder' fundus with four-quadrant hemorrhages, dilated tortuous veins, and disc edema is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Proliferative diabetic retinopathy without occlusion
Central retinal artery occlusion
Central retinal vein occlusion
Hypertensive emergency with papilledema only
stages.explanation: Diffuse hemorrhages in all four quadrants with dilated, tortuous veins, disc edema, cotton-wool spots, and macular edema-the 'blood-and-thunder' fundus-define central retinal vein occlusion. CRAO instead shows a pale, whitened retina with a cherry-red spot and box-carring, not widespread hemorrhage. Hypertensive emergency can cause disc edema and some hemorrhages but is typically bilateral and lacks the unilateral venous engorgement and dense four-quadrant hemorrhage of CRVO. Proliferative diabetic retinopathy features neovascularization and is usually bilateral and more symmetric; while this patient is diabetic, the acute, unilateral, venous-pattern picture is occlusive CRVO. His hypertension, diabetes, glaucoma, and OSA are all CRVO risk factors.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC TESTING
stages.content:
Visual acuity right eye: 20/200
Relative afferent pupillary defect: present (right)
OCT macula (right): cystoid macular edema, central subfield thickness 540 µm
Fluorescein angiography (right): >10 disc areas of capillary nonperfusion, delayed venous filling
Electroretinogram (right): reduced b:a wave ratio
No iris or angle neovascularization on gonioscopy today

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which set of findings classifies this as the ISCHEMIC (rather than non-ischemic) subtype of CRVO?
options: Visual acuity 20/200 or worse, a relative afferent pupillary defect, extensive capillary nonperfusion on angiography,
Visual acuity 20/40, no RAPD, minimal hemorrhages, and a normal ERG as the primary diagnosis
Bilateral disc edema with preserved acuity as the primary diagnosis
A cherry-red spot with retinal whitening as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Ischemic CRVO is defined by markers of severe retinal hypoxia: poor visual acuity (often 20/200 or worse), a relative afferent pupillary defect, large areas (>10 disc areas) of capillary nonperfusion on fluorescein angiography, numerous cotton-wool spots, and an abnormal ERG (reduced b:a ratio)-all present here. Good acuity, no RAPD, scant hemorrhages, and a normal ERG describe the non-ischemic subtype, which has a far lower neovascular risk. A cherry-red spot with whitening is CRAO, not CRVO. Bilateral disc edema with preserved acuity suggests papilledema from raised intracranial pressure. Correctly identifying the ischemic subtype is critical because it predicts neovascular complications.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which dreaded 90-day complication of ischemic CRVO must be actively monitored for, and how is it screened?
options: Retinal detachment, screened by visual field testing as the leading concern
Neovascular glaucoma from iris/angle neovascularization , screened by serial gonioscopy
Cataract, screened by slit-lamp lens grading as the leading concern
Optic neuritis, screened by MRI of the orbits as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Severe retinal ischemia drives VEGF release, causing neovascularization of the iris (rubeosis) and the angle; the fibrovascular tissue blocks aqueous outflow and produces neovascular glaucoma, classically around 90 days-hence '90-day glaucoma.' Screening requires frequent (often monthly for the first months) undilated iris exams, gonioscopy of the angle, and IOP measurement to catch rubeosis early. Retinal detachment is not the characteristic CRVO complication and isn't screened by visual fields. Cataract is unrelated to the acute ischemic process. Optic neuritis is an inflammatory/demyelinating disease evaluated by MRI, not a complication of vein occlusion. Early detection of neovascularization allows timely treatment before pressure becomes uncontrollable.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management approach for this ischemic CRVO with macular edema and high neovascular risk?
options: Immediate enucleation of the affected eye as initial therapy
Systemic anticoagulation with warfarin to lyse the venous clot as initial therapy
Topical prostaglandin drops alone, with annual follow-up as initial therapy
Intravitreal anti-VEGF injections for macular edema, panretinal photocoagulation if neovascularization develops,
stages.explanation: Management of ischemic CRVO centers on intravitreal anti-VEGF (and sometimes steroid) injections to treat vision-limiting macular edema, panretinal photocoagulation when neovascularization of the iris/angle/retina appears, and optimizing systemic risk factors-blood pressure, diabetes, lipids, and OSA-which underlie the occlusion. Close monitoring (gonioscopy, IOP, iris exam) catches neovascular glaucoma early. Systemic anticoagulation does not reverse CRVO and adds bleeding risk without proven benefit. Enucleation is reserved for a blind, painful eye, not initial management. Topical drops with annual follow-up grossly under-treats a high-risk ischemic eye and would miss the rapidly developing neovascular complications this patient is prone to.

cases.diagnosis: Ischemic central retinal vein occlusion (right eye)
pearls: A unilateral 'blood-and-thunder' fundus-four-quadrant hemorrhages, dilated tortuous veins, disc edema-is CRVO.
Ischemic subtype markers: acuity ≤20/200, RAPD, extensive capillary nonperfusion (>10 disc areas), and reduced ERG b:a ratio.
Ischemic CRVO risks '90-day' neovascular glaucoma; monitor with serial iris exam, gonioscopy, and IOP.
Treat macular edema with anti-VEGF, add PRP for neovascularization, and aggressively control HTN, diabetes, and OSA.

cases.title: Flashes, Floaters, and a Shadow in the Vision
cases.chiefComplaint: A day of flashing lights and floaters in the left eye, now with a dark curtain descending from the upper field.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man presents to the ED reporting that yesterday he began seeing brief 'lightning flashes' in the periphery of his left eye, followed by a shower of new floaters 'like a swarm of gnats.' This morning he noticed a dark shadow at the top of his vision that has been slowly descending like a curtain over the day. He denies pain, redness, or discharge. He is highly myopic (-9.00 diopters) and wears thick glasses; he has not had eye surgery or recent trauma, though he did some heavy yard work two days ago. PMH: myopia, well-controlled hypertension. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: accountant, nonsmoker, social alcohol. Family history: a brother with a 'detached retina.' ROS: denies headache, jaw pain, scalp tenderness, diplopia, and contralateral symptoms. He is anxious because the shadow is growing and worries it is reaching the center of his sight.
cases.exam: Left eye: visual acuity 20/25 centrally but with a definite inferior-to-superior field defect corresponding to the patient's described curtain; pupils equal, no relative afferent pupillary defect; anterior segment quiet, IOP 11 mmHg (slightly lower than fellow eye). Dilated fundus exam (left): pigmented cells in the anterior vitreous ('tobacco dust'/Shafer sign); a superior-temporal horseshoe retinal tear with an associated bullous, billowing, detached retina; the macula appears attached on exam. Right eye: acuity 20/20, lattice degeneration noted in the periphery, retina otherwise attached, IOP 14 mmHg. No proptosis or ptosis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Flashes, a shower of new floaters, and a descending curtain in a high myope are most concerning for which diagnosis?
options: Vitreous hemorrhage from diabetic retinopathy
Migraine with visual aura in this clinical context
Posterior vitreous detachment without a tear
Rhegmatogenous retinal detachment
stages.explanation: New flashes (photopsias from vitreoretinal traction), a sudden shower of floaters, and a progressive curtain/shadow in the visual field are the hallmark warning sequence of a rhegmatogenous retinal detachment, especially in a high myope with lattice degeneration and a family history. A simple posterior vitreous detachment causes flashes and floaters but no field-cutting curtain or detached retina-though it can precipitate a tear. Diabetic vitreous hemorrhage causes floaters but this patient is not diabetic and has a visible tear with detachment, not blood. Migraine aura is a transient, often bilateral scintillating scotoma lasting minutes with full recovery, not a persistent monocular curtain with a fundus lesion.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: What is the immediate mechanism producing this type of retinal detachment?
options: Tractional fibrovascular membranes pulling the retina forward as the immediate next step
Exudative fluid from a choroidal tumor lifting the retina as the immediate next step
Inflammatory serous fluid from posterior scleritis as the immediate next step
A full-thickness retinal break allowing liquefied vitreous to pass beneath
stages.explanation: Rhegmatogenous detachment ('rhegma' = break) occurs when a full-thickness retinal tear-here a horseshoe tear from vitreous traction-lets liquefied vitreous flow through the break into the subretinal space, peeling the neurosensory retina off the retinal pigment epithelium. The 'tobacco dust' (Shafer sign) reflects pigment released through the break. Exudative detachment comes from sub-retinal fluid leakage (e.g., tumor, inflammation) with no break. Tractional detachment results from contracting fibrovascular membranes (e.g., proliferative diabetic retinopathy) mechanically lifting the retina, again without a break. Posterior scleritis can cause serous (exudative) fluid, not a rhegmatogenous break. Identifying the break is what defines and directs repair of this subtype.

stages.label: IMAGING & EXAM
stages.content:
Slit-lamp: pigment granules in anterior vitreous (Shafer sign), left eye
Indirect ophthalmoscopy (left): superotemporal horseshoe tear, bullous detachment; macula attached (macula-on)
B-scan ultrasound (left): mobile, undulating membrane consistent with retinal detachment; fovea not involved
IOP: 11 mmHg left, 14 mmHg right
Fellow eye: peripheral lattice degeneration, retina attached

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The macula is currently attached (macula-on). Why does this finding drive the urgency of surgical repair?
options: Macula-on detachments never progress, so repair can be scheduled in several weeks with appropriate follow-up
Macula-off detachments require no surgery because vision is already lost with appropriate follow-up
Repairing a macula-on detachment urgently preserves central vision before the fovea detaches, whereas once the macula detaches the central visual prognosis drops sharply
The distinction is irrelevant; all detachments have the same prognosis and timing with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Central visual outcome hinges on whether the fovea is still attached. A macula-on detachment is a surgical urgency-prompt repair (commonly within about 24 hours) aims to fix the retina before the detachment extends to the fovea, preserving good central acuity. Once the macula detaches (macula-off), photoreceptor damage means central vision often does not fully recover even after successful reattachment, so the same hour-to-hour urgency is reduced (repair still indicated, but central prognosis is already compromised). Saying macula-on detachments never progress is false-this one is bullous and advancing. Macula-off eyes still need surgery to salvage peripheral/remaining vision and reattach the retina. The macula-on/off status is the central prognostic and timing variable.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which is an appropriate definitive treatment for this macula-on rhegmatogenous retinal detachment?
options: Topical steroid drops and oral acetazolamide as initial therapy
Observation with serial fundus photos as initial therapy
Intravitreal anti-VEGF injection as initial therapy
Pars plana vitrectomy, scleral buckle, or pneumatic retinopexy to reattach the retina
stages.explanation: Definitive repair restores retinal apposition and seals the break: options include pars plana vitrectomy, scleral buckle, and pneumatic retinopexy, combined with laser or cryotherapy retinopexy to wall off the tear; the choice depends on the break location, extent, and lens status. Anti-VEGF treats neovascular/exudative macular disease, not a mechanical detachment. Observation is inappropriate for a progressing bullous detachment threatening the macula-delay risks foveal involvement and permanent central vision loss. Topical steroids and acetazolamide treat inflammation and lower IOP but do nothing to reattach a detached retina. The fellow eye's lattice degeneration should also be evaluated for prophylactic laser given the high risk in this myopic patient.

cases.diagnosis: Macula-on rhegmatogenous retinal detachment (left eye)
pearls: Flashes + new shower of floaters + a progressing curtain = rhegmatogenous retinal detachment until proven otherwise; high myopia and lattice are key risks.
Shafer sign ('tobacco dust') in the anterior vitreous signals a retinal break.
Macula-on is a surgical urgency (repair ~within 24 h) to save central vision; macula-off carries a worse central prognosis.
Definitive options are vitrectomy, scleral buckle, or pneumatic retinopexy with laser/cryo retinopexy; evaluate the fellow eye.

cases.title: Wavy, Distorted Central Vision in One Eye
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden distortion of central vision in the right eye, with straight lines appearing wavy over the past several days.
cases.history: A 78-year-old woman presents to clinic reporting that over the past several days the straight lines of her kitchen tiles and door frames look 'bent and wavy' through her right eye, and there is a smudge in the very center of her vision. She first noticed it on her home Amsler grid, which her eye doctor had given her to check daily. She denies pain, redness, flashes, a curtain, or floaters. PMH: dry (non-exudative) age-related macular degeneration in both eyes followed for years, hypertension, and osteoporosis. Medications: AREDS2 vitamin supplement daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily, alendronate weekly. Allergies: sulfa (rash). Social: lifelong nonsmoker now, but smoked one pack/day for 30 years (quit at 60); lives independently. Family history: mother had 'macular degeneration' and lost central vision. ROS: denies headache, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, and diplopia. She is worried because reading and recognizing faces have suddenly become difficult.
cases.exam: Right eye: visual acuity 20/80 (down from a documented 20/30 three months ago); central metamorphopsia and a central scotoma on Amsler grid testing; pupils equal and reactive, no relative afferent pupillary defect; anterior segment normal, IOP 15 mmHg. Dilated fundus exam (right): macular drusen plus a grayish-green subretinal lesion at the fovea with overlying subretinal fluid and a small subretinal hemorrhage. Left eye: acuity 20/25, numerous soft macular drusen and pigmentary changes without subretinal fluid or hemorrhage, IOP 14 mmHg. No vitreous cells, no peripheral retinal breaks. Optic discs normal, no cupping.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Acute central metamorphopsia and a central scotoma with subretinal fluid and hemorrhage in a patient with known dry AMD most strongly suggest which development?
options: Conversion to neovascular age-related macular degeneration
A new posterior vitreous detachment in this clinical context
Open-angle glaucoma as the first step today
Progression to geographic atrophy (advanced dry AMD)
stages.explanation: A patient with pre-existing dry AMD who suddenly develops metamorphopsia (wavy lines), a central scotoma, and exam findings of subretinal fluid and hemorrhage has most likely converted to neovascular (wet) AMD, in which a choroidal neovascular membrane leaks and bleeds beneath the macula. Geographic atrophy is advanced dry AMD causing slow, painless central vision loss without fluid or hemorrhage-not this acute leaking picture. A posterior vitreous detachment causes flashes and floaters, not central metamorphopsia with subretinal fluid. Open-angle glaucoma causes peripheral arcuate field loss with cupping and elevated IOP, sparing central acuity until late-incompatible with her acute central distortion, fluid, and normal disc.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC TESTING
stages.content:
OCT macula (right): subretinal and intraretinal fluid; pigment epithelial detachment; disruption of the outer retina at the fovea
Fluorescein angiography (right): early hyperfluorescence with late leakage consistent with a choroidal neovascular membrane
OCT angiography (right): abnormal choroidal neovascular network beneath the fovea
Visual acuity (right): 20/80
Amsler grid: central distortion and scotoma

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: What is the underlying lesion responsible for the subretinal fluid and hemorrhage in wet AMD?
options: A choroidal neovascular membrane-abnormal new vessels growing from the choroid through Bruch's membrane, leaking fluid
A full-thickness retinal tear as the immediate next step
A retinal arteriolar embolus as the immediate next step
Inflammatory infiltrate from infectious endophthalmitis as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Wet AMD is driven by a choroidal neovascular membrane: VEGF-mediated abnormal new vessels grow from the choroid through breaks in Bruch's membrane into the sub-retinal pigment epithelium and subretinal space, where they leak serous fluid and blood, producing the gray-green lesion, fluid, hemorrhage, and metamorphopsia seen here. A retinal arteriolar embolus causes ischemic CRAO (pale retina, cherry-red spot), not exudative macular leakage. A full-thickness retinal tear underlies rhegmatogenous detachment with flashes/curtain, not a foveal neovascular membrane. Infectious endophthalmitis presents with pain, redness, hypopyon, and vitritis-usually after surgery or trauma-none of which are present in this quiet, painless eye. The CNV is the therapeutic target.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the first-line treatment for neovascular AMD with an active choroidal neovascular membrane?
options: Increasing the AREDS2 supplement dose as initial therapy
Panretinal photocoagulation as initial therapy
High-dose oral corticosteroids as initial therapy
Intravitreal anti-VEGF injections
stages.explanation: Intravitreal anti-VEGF therapy (bevacizumab, ranibizumab, aflibercept, and newer agents) is the first-line, vision-preserving treatment for neovascular AMD; by blocking VEGF it causes regression of the choroidal neovascular membrane, dries subretinal/intraretinal fluid, and can stabilize or improve acuity, given as a series of injections with ongoing monitoring. Systemic corticosteroids are not standard therapy for CNV and carry systemic risks. Panretinal photocoagulation treats peripheral ischemia/neovascularization in diabetic retinopathy or ischemic vein occlusion, not subfoveal CNV. AREDS2 supplements reduce progression risk in intermediate dry AMD but do not treat an active neovascular membrane-once wet AMD develops, anti-VEGF, not more vitamins, is required.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate counseling and monitoring for her fellow (left) eye, which has intermediate dry AMD?
options: Begin prophylactic anti-VEGF injections to the healthy fellow eye as the immediate next step
Continue AREDS2 supplementation, encourage daily home Amsler grid testing, advise smoking avoidance,
No monitoring is needed because only one eye is affected as the immediate next step
Start topical glaucoma drops to the fellow eye as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The fellow eye with intermediate dry AMD is at substantial risk of also converting to wet AMD, so management is risk reduction and early detection: AREDS2 supplements (shown to slow progression in intermediate/advanced AMD), daily Amsler grid self-monitoring, smoking avoidance (her major modifiable risk), and prompt evaluation at the first sign of new metamorphopsia or central scotoma-because early anti-VEGF preserves more vision. No monitoring ignores a high-risk eye and her family history. Prophylactic anti-VEGF to an eye without active CNV is not indicated and exposes it to injection risks. Topical glaucoma drops treat elevated IOP/glaucoma, which she does not have. Early detection is the key to saving the second eye.

cases.diagnosis: Neovascular (wet) age-related macular degeneration (right eye)
pearls: New metamorphopsia or a central scotoma in a dry-AMD patient signals conversion to wet AMD-image with OCT urgently.
Wet AMD is caused by a choroidal neovascular membrane leaking fluid and blood beneath the macula.
First-line treatment is intravitreal anti-VEGF (bevacizumab, ranibizumab, aflibercept).
Home Amsler grid testing, AREDS2 supplements, and smoking cessation protect the fellow eye; act fast on new distortion.

cases.title: Rapidly Worsening Painful Red Eye in a Contact-Lens Wearer
cases.chiefComplaint: Two days of a severely painful, red, light-sensitive right eye with blurry vision and discharge in a contact-lens wearer.
cases.history: A 23-year-old woman presents to clinic with two days of rapidly worsening right eye pain, redness, marked light sensitivity, blurry vision, and thick discharge. She is a daily soft-contact-lens wearer who admits she frequently sleeps in her lenses, swims in them, and 'tops off' old solution rather than using fresh disinfectant; she napped in her lenses two nights ago. She denies trauma. The pain and a white spot on her eye have grown noticeably worse just since yesterday. PMH: myopia, otherwise healthy. Medications: oral contraceptive pill. Allergies: none. Social: college student, occasional alcohol, nonsmoker. Family history: noncontributory. ROS: denies fever, joint pains, oral/genital ulcers, and contralateral symptoms. She has been wearing her glasses since removing the lens and reports the eye is now too painful and photophobic to tolerate light, with a foreign-body sensation and reflex tearing.
cases.exam: Right eye: visual acuity 20/100 (reduced), marked conjunctival injection with ciliary flush, and copious mucopurulent discharge. Slit lamp shows a 3 mm central round corneal epithelial defect with a dense underlying stromal infiltrate and indistinct, feathery 'melting' edges; the cornea around it is hazy/edematous. A 1.2 mm hypopyon (layered white cells) is present in the anterior chamber, with significant cell and flare. The pupil is reactive but the view is partly obscured. IOP is soft/normal. Left eye: acuity 20/20, white and quiet, cornea clear. No proptosis, no preauricular node, extraocular movements intact and painless.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A contact-lens wearer with a central corneal ulcer, stromal infiltrate, and hypopyon-what is the most important immediate step?
options: Obtain corneal scrapings for Gram stain
Patch the eye and recheck in one week as the first step today
Prescribe a topical corticosteroid to reduce inflammation in this clinical context
Reassure and continue contact-lens wear in the other eye in this clinical context
stages.explanation: This is bacterial (microbial) keratitis with a hypopyon-a vision-threatening emergency. The standard of care for a significant central ulcer is to scrape the cornea for Gram stain and culture to identify the organism and guide therapy, then start aggressive empiric topical antibiotics immediately. Topical corticosteroids alone are contraindicated initially-they can worsen infection and accelerate corneal melting before the organism is controlled. Patching is contraindicated in suspected infectious keratitis because it creates a warm, closed environment that promotes bacterial growth (and is especially dangerous with Pseudomonas). Reassurance and continued lens wear ignore a sight-threatening infection and a clear modifiable risk factor-she must stop all lens wear.

stages.label: LABS & MICROBIOLOGY
stages.content:
Corneal scraping Gram stain: Gram-negative rods
Culture (blood and chocolate agar): Pseudomonas aeruginosa, oxidase-positive
Antibiotic sensitivities: susceptible to fluoroquinolones and fortified aminoglycosides
Contact-lens case culture: Pseudomonas aeruginosa
Visual acuity (right): 20/100

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The culture grows Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Which feature of this organism explains the rapidly enlarging ulcer and corneal 'melting'?
options: Slow-growing satellite lesions with branching septate hyphae as the primary diagnosis
Formation of dendritic epithelial lesions with terminal bulbs as the primary diagnosis
Ring infiltrate with severe pain out of proportion from a free-living amoeba
Production of proteases
stages.explanation: Pseudomonas aeruginosa elaborates potent proteases (including elastase) and exotoxins that rapidly digest corneal collagen, producing the fulminant, fast-enlarging ulcer with feathery 'melting' edges that can perforate within days-classic for contact-lens-related Pseudomonas keratitis. Dendritic lesions with terminal bulbs are characteristic of herpes simplex keratitis, a viral process. Satellite lesions with branching septate hyphae describe fungal keratitis (e.g., Fusarium/Aspergillus), often after vegetative trauma, with a more indolent course. A ring infiltrate with pain out of proportion suggests Acanthamoeba, also linked to contact lenses/water but distinct. The Gram-negative rod with melting stroma and hypopyon points squarely to Pseudomonas.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate antibiotic regimen for this Pseudomonas corneal ulcer?
options: Topical antiviral (trifluridine) drops as initial therapy
A single intravitreal vancomycin injection as initial therapy
Oral amoxicillin once daily for 7 days as initial therapy
Intensive topical fluoroquinolone, or fortified topical antibiotics , with close daily follow-up
stages.explanation: Severe bacterial keratitis is treated with intensive topical antibiotics-broad-spectrum fluoroquinolone monotherapy given around the clock (often hourly initially), or fortified topical antibiotics such as tobramycin (Gram-negative coverage) combined with a fortified cephalosporin/vancomycin (Gram-positive coverage)-with daily review to confirm improvement and adjust per culture. Topical drops achieve far higher corneal concentrations than oral drugs; once-daily oral amoxicillin is grossly inadequate and lacks reliable Pseudomonas coverage. Topical antivirals treat herpes simplex keratitis, not a bacterial ulcer. Intravitreal vancomycin treats endophthalmitis (intraocular infection) and does not address a corneal stromal infection; this hypopyon is a sterile reactive inflammatory response, not intraocular infection.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Which counseling point is most important to prevent recurrence once this ulcer heals?
options: Avoid overnight lens wear
Use lenses only in the affected eye to 'rest' the other eye as the immediate next step
It is safe to sleep in lenses as long as they are rinsed with tap water as the immediate next step
Switch to colored decorative lenses purchased without a prescription as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Recurrent microbial keratitis is prevented by eliminating the behaviors that caused it: no overnight wear (sleeping in lenses is the single biggest modifiable risk), no swimming or showering in lenses (water harbors Pseudomonas and Acanthamoeba), always use fresh disinfecting solution (never tap water, never 'topping off'), regularly replace the lens case, and practice rigorous hand and lens hygiene with proper replacement schedules. Tap water rinsing is dangerous-it introduces the very organisms that cause keratitis. Unregulated decorative lenses substantially increase infection risk and are not safer. Wearing a lens in the affected eye is contraindicated while it heals. Behavior change is the cornerstone of prevention.

cases.diagnosis: Bacterial (contact-lens-related Pseudomonas) keratitis with hypopyon (right eye)
pearls: A contact-lens wearer with a painful central corneal ulcer, stromal infiltrate, and hypopyon has microbial keratitis until proven otherwise-scrape and culture before treating.
Pseudomonas causes rapid, melting Gram-negative ulcers via proteases/exotoxins and can perforate within days.
Treat with intensive topical fluoroquinolone or fortified antibiotics around the clock; avoid initial steroids and never patch a suspected infectious ulcer.
Overnight wear, swimming/showering in lenses, and 'topping off' solution are key preventable risk factors.

cases.title: Blood in the Eye After a Paintball Injury
cases.chiefComplaint: Blunt trauma to the right eye from a paintball, now with pain, blurry vision, and visible blood in the front of the eye.
cases.history: A 17-year-old boy is brought to the ED after being struck in the right eye by a paintball at close range about an hour ago; his protective mask had slipped. He reports right eye pain, light sensitivity, blurry vision, and notes the front of his eye 'has blood in it.' He denies loss of consciousness, double vision, or nausea. PMH: known sickle cell trait (HbAS), otherwise healthy; he plays sports without prior eye problems. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: high-school student, denies alcohol, tobacco, or drug use. Family history: mother has sickle cell trait, an uncle has sickle cell disease. ROS: denies headache, neck pain, weakness, and contralateral eye symptoms. The injury was a single direct blow to the globe; he removed his mask afterward and has been holding a shirt to the eye. He is anxious and asks whether he will lose vision in the eye.
cases.exam: Right eye: visual acuity 20/60; periorbital ecchymosis without obvious globe rupture or full-thickness laceration. Slit lamp shows layered red blood in the inferior anterior chamber occupying roughly one-third of its height (a grade I-II hyphema) with a visible fluid level; the cornea is clear, the pupil is round and reactive though sluggish, and there is mild photophobia. IOP is 22 mmHg. No Seidel sign (no aqueous leak), no obvious iris dialysis today, and the lens appears clear. Posterior segment view is limited but no obvious retinal detachment. Left eye: acuity 20/20, normal, IOP 14 mmHg. Extraocular movements intact, no proptosis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Layered blood in the anterior chamber after blunt trauma defines a hyphema. Besides documenting the grade, what must be urgently excluded first?
options: Dry eye disease as the first step today
Allergic conjunctivitis as the first step today
Globe rupture/open-globe injury
A refractive error requiring new glasses as the first step today
stages.explanation: After significant blunt ocular trauma, the first priority is to exclude an open-globe (rupture) injury, because management diverges sharply: a ruptured globe requires a protective shield, no pressure or pressure-lowering drops that could expel contents, antiemetics, tetanus prophylaxis, antibiotics, and emergent surgery. A negative Seidel test (no streaming aqueous), a formed clear chamber depth, normal/elevated IOP, and absence of a full-thickness laceration help exclude rupture here. Refractive error, allergic conjunctivitis, and dry eye are trivial concerns that do not explain traumatic intraocular blood and would dangerously distract from the real emergency. Only after rupture is excluded do you proceed with hyphema-specific management.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this traumatic hyphema, which initial management plan is most appropriate?
options: Vigorous eye irrigation and bed rest in a flat, fully supine position as initial therapy
Immediate anterior chamber washout for all hyphemas regardless of size or pressure as initial therapy
Aspirin for pain and prompt discharge without follow-up as initial therapy
Limit activity with head-of-bed elevation, a rigid eye shield, daily IOP checks
stages.explanation: Standard hyphema management is supportive and protective: restrict activity, elevate the head of the bed (so blood settles inferiorly and clears the visual axis), place a rigid shield (not a patch) to prevent further injury, check IOP and examine daily during the high-risk rebleed window, and use a topical cycloplegic for comfort and often a topical steroid to reduce inflammation. Lying flat supine lets blood disperse across the visual axis, and vigorous irrigation does not treat intraocular blood. Routine washout is reserved for specific indications (uncontrolled IOP, large/total clot, corneal blood staining), not all hyphemas. Aspirin and other antiplatelet/anticoagulant agents must be AVOIDED-they increase rebleeding risk-and discharge without close follow-up is unsafe.

stages.label: LABS & TESTING
stages.content:
Hemoglobin electrophoresis (confirmatory): HbAS (sickle cell trait)
IOP (right eye), serial: 22 → 31 mmHg over 24 hours
Gonioscopy (deferred acutely to avoid rebleed)
CT orbit: no globe rupture, no fracture, intraocular blood without foreign body
Visual acuity (right): 20/60

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Why does this patient's sickle cell trait critically complicate his hyphema and its management?
options: Sickle trait protects the optic nerve from any pressure-related damage as the leading concern
Sickle trait only matters if the patient has full sickle cell disease as the leading concern
Sickle trait makes the blood clear faster, so no special monitoring is needed as the leading concern
Sickled red cells in the anterior chamber clog the trabecular meshwork
stages.explanation: In sickle patients (trait or disease), red cells in the anterior chamber sickle in the hypoxic, acidotic, hypertonic aqueous and become rigid, obstructing the trabecular meshwork far more than normal cells and driving sharp IOP rises (as seen here, 22→31). The sickle-prone microvasculature also makes the optic nerve vulnerable to ischemic damage at pressures normally considered tolerable, so the IOP threshold for intervention is lower. Importantly, systemic carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (e.g., acetazolamide) can lower aqueous pH and promote sickling, so they are generally avoided or used cautiously; methazolamide is sometimes preferred. Sickle trait absolutely matters-it does not protect the nerve, does not clear blood faster, and is relevant even without full disease.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: His IOP rises to 31 mmHg despite topical therapy. Which step is most appropriate given his sickle cell trait?
options: Escalate IOP control with sickle-safe options
Start high-dose systemic acetazolamide as the preferred first agent with appropriate follow-up
Apply firm digital ocular massage to lower the pressure with appropriate follow-up
Reassure and recheck pressure in one month with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: With a refractory IOP spike in a sickle patient, lower the pressure using sickle-safe strategies: topical aqueous suppressants (beta-blocker, alpha-agonist) and cautious systemic therapy favoring methazolamide over acetazolamide, while avoiding agents that acidify aqueous and worsen sickling. Because these patients tolerate high IOP poorly and risk optic nerve ischemia, the threshold for surgical anterior chamber washout (to remove sickled cells obstructing outflow) is lower-washout is considered for persistently uncontrolled pressure. High-dose acetazolamide as first agent is wrong here because it can precipitate sickling. Waiting a month risks irreversible optic nerve damage and misses the rebleed window. Digital ocular massage applies pressure to a recently traumatized eye and can provoke rebleeding-contraindicated.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: During the first week, the rebleed risk peaks. Which counseling point and finding should most concern the team about a secondary hemorrhage?
options: Rebleeding only occurs after the eye is completely healed at 6 weeks as the leading concern
Rebleeding typically occurs between days 2
Rebleeding is most likely on day 1 and is always harmless as the leading concern
Rebleeding is prevented entirely by lying flat and resuming sports immediately as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Secondary hemorrhage (rebleed) classically occurs between days 2 and 5 as the original clot retracts and lyses, and the rebleed is often larger than the initial hyphema-driving dangerous IOP elevation, corneal blood staining, and worse outcomes, a particular threat in a sickle patient who tolerates pressure poorly. This is why activity restriction, head elevation, a rigid shield, and avoidance of aspirin/NSAIDs and anticoagulants are emphasized through this window, with close daily monitoring. Saying rebleeding is harmless or only happens on day 1 understates the real day 2-5 peak. It does not wait until full healing at 6 weeks. Lying flat and resuming sports immediately would increase, not prevent, rebleeding-the opposite of correct advice.

cases.diagnosis: Traumatic hyphema with elevated IOP and sickle cell trait, at risk for rebleed (right eye)
pearls: After blunt ocular trauma, exclude open-globe rupture first (Seidel test, shield, no pressure) before hyphema-specific care.
Hyphema management: head-of-bed elevation, rigid shield, daily IOP/exam, cycloplegic ± topical steroid; avoid aspirin/NSAIDs and anticoagulants.
Sickle cell trait causes RBCs to sickle in aqueous, clogging outflow and risking optic nerve ischemia at lower IOPs.
Avoid acetazolamide (acidifies aqueous, promotes sickling); favor methazolamide and have a lower threshold for anterior chamber washout.

cases.title: Double Vision and Eye Pain After a Fist to the Face
cases.chiefComplaint: After being punched in the left eye, the patient has double vision when looking up, pain on eye movement, and episodes of nausea and a slow heartbeat.
cases.history: A 29-year-old man presents to the ED after being punched in the left eye during an altercation about 90 minutes ago. He reports vertical double vision that is worst when he looks up, pain with eye movement, and a feeling that his eye is 'stuck.' He has had two episodes of nausea and felt lightheaded; a nurse noted his pulse dropped during one episode. He denies vision loss in the affected eye itself. PMH: healthy. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: works in construction, social alcohol, nonsmoker. Family history: noncontributory. ROS: denies neck pain, numbness, rhinorrhea, and contralateral symptoms, but notes numbness of the left cheek and upper lip. Of note, despite the significant mechanism, the eye looks surprisingly white and uninflamed externally ('white-eyed'). He is bothered most by the diplopia and the recurrent waves of nausea whenever he tries to look upward.
cases.exam: Left eye: visual acuity 20/20 (vision in the eye preserved); the eye is white and quiet with minimal external bruising despite the mechanism ('white-eyed blowout'). There is marked restriction of upgaze (supraduction) of the left eye with vertical diplopia on attempted up-gaze; forced duction would be positive (restrictive). Mild enophthalmos. Infraorbital nerve distribution shows decreased sensation over the left cheek and upper lip. Pupils equal and reactive, no relative afferent pupillary defect, IOP 15 mmHg, cornea clear, no hyphema, no Seidel. When the patient attempts upgaze, he becomes nauseated and bradycardic. Right eye normal. No subcutaneous emphysema noted.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Restricted upgaze, vertical diplopia, infraorbital numbness, and a 'white' eye after blunt trauma suggest which diagnosis, and what is the best confirmatory imaging?
options: Orbital floor fracture with inferior rectus entrapment
Isolated cranial nerve III palsy; obtain MRI brain as the first step today
Retrobulbar hemorrhage; obtain plain orbital radiographs as the first step today
Carotid-cavernous fistula; obtain carotid ultrasound as the first step today
stages.explanation: Blunt force raises orbital pressure and fractures the thin orbital floor; the inferior rectus/orbital tissue can herniate and become entrapped, causing restricted upgaze, vertical diplopia, infraorbital (V2) hypesthesia, and sometimes enophthalmos-the picture here-best confirmed with thin-cut coronal and axial CT of the orbits. A pure CN III palsy causes a 'down-and-out' eye with ptosis and a dilated pupil, not isolated restricted upgaze with a positive forced duction. A carotid-cavernous fistula causes a pulsatile proptotic red eye with a bruit, not a white eye with mechanical restriction. Retrobulbar hemorrhage causes proptosis, raised IOP, and an APD (orbital compartment syndrome); plain films are inadequate for orbital fracture detail.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The patient becomes bradycardic and nauseated when looking up. Which reflex is responsible, and what does it imply?
options: Vasovagal syncope from pain, of no surgical significance as the leading concern
Cushing reflex, implying raised intracranial pressure as the leading concern
Bezold-Jarisch reflex from myocardial ischemia as the leading concern
Oculocardiac reflex : traction on entrapped extraocular muscle triggers bradycardia, nausea,
stages.explanation: Traction on an entrapped extraocular muscle stimulates afferent trigeminal fibers that synapse on the vagal nucleus, producing the oculocardiac reflex-bradycardia, nausea, and vomiting on eye movement. Its presence signals genuine muscle entrapment with ischemia and is a red-flag indication for urgent surgical release, especially in tight 'trapdoor' fractures. The Cushing reflex (hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respirations) reflects raised intracranial pressure, not orbital entrapment. Dismissing it as simple vasovagal syncope ('no surgical significance') is dangerous-here it specifically marks entrapment. The Bezold-Jarisch reflex relates to cardiac mechanoreceptors in ischemia/inferior MI, not to ocular muscle traction. Recognizing the oculocardiac reflex changes the surgical timeline from elective to urgent.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
CT orbits (coronal, thin cut): left orbital floor fracture with herniation and entrapment of the inferior rectus muscle into the maxillary sinus
No globe rupture, no retrobulbar hematoma
Minimal soft-tissue swelling externally ('trapdoor'/white-eyed pattern)
Infraorbital canal involved
Heart rate during upgaze attempts: 48-52 bpm

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given entrapment with a positive oculocardiac reflex in a 'white-eyed/trapdoor' pattern, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Observation for 2 weeks to allow swelling to resolve, then reassess as initial therapy
Immediate enucleation of the affected eye as initial therapy
Discharge with outpatient ENT follow-up in 2 weeks for elective repair as initial therapy
Urgent surgical exploration
stages.explanation: True muscle entrapment with an active oculocardiac reflex-classically the 'white-eyed blowout/trapdoor' pattern (more typical in younger, elastic bone)-is a surgical urgency: the trapped muscle is ischemic, and delay leads to permanent fibrosis, restriction, and persistent diplopia, so it is released within roughly 24-48 hours (sooner with severe oculocardiac symptoms). Discharge for elective repair in 2 weeks or watchful waiting is appropriate only for fractures WITHOUT entrapment and with tolerable diplopia, where swelling may settle-not for this entrapped, symptomatic patient. Enucleation is never indicated here-the globe is intact and vision is 20/20. The deceptively normal-looking external eye must not lull the team into delay.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which additional instruction is important while arranging urgent operative repair?
options: Advise against nose-blowing
Encourage vigorous nose-blowing to clear the sinus with appropriate follow-up
Apply firm pressure to the globe to reduce the herniation with appropriate follow-up
Give aspirin to prevent clot formation with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: With a fracture connecting the orbit to the maxillary/paranasal sinus, the patient must be told NOT to blow his nose: the Valsalva from nose-blowing can push sinus air through the fracture into the orbit, causing orbital emphysema and a sudden rise in orbital pressure that can compromise the optic nerve. Nasal precautions and, in many protocols, prophylactic antibiotics (given the sinus communication) are advised. Encouraging nose-blowing is exactly the opposite and dangerous. Applying firm pressure to the globe risks worsening injury and can raise IOP/compromise perfusion. Aspirin increases bleeding risk perioperatively and confers no benefit here. These supportive measures protect the eye while definitive surgical release is arranged.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: While the surgical team is mobilized and the patient remains intermittently bradycardic on attempted upgaze, what is the most appropriate immediate intervention for symptomatic oculocardiac reflex episodes?
options: Administer a beta-blocker to slow the heart further as the immediate next step
Stop the provoking eye movement, monitor on telemetry,
Discharge home and have him return only if he faints as the immediate next step
Repeatedly provoke upgaze to 'stretch out' the entrapped muscle as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The oculocardiac (trigeminovagal) reflex causes vagally mediated bradycardia; the immediate management is to halt the provoking stimulus (stop forcing upgaze), place the patient on cardiac monitoring, and treat symptomatic or hemodynamically significant bradycardia with IV atropine (an anticholinergic that blocks the vagal efferent) while urgently arranging surgical release of the entrapped muscle-the definitive fix. Repeatedly provoking upgaze worsens the reflex and prolongs muscle ischemia. A beta-blocker would deepen the bradycardia-exactly wrong. Discharging a patient with an active oculocardiac reflex and true entrapment risks permanent muscle damage and dangerous bradyarrhythmia. Definitive treatment remains prompt operative release; atropine and monitoring are the bridging measures.

cases.diagnosis: Orbital floor (blowout) fracture with inferior rectus entrapment-'white-eyed/trapdoor' pattern (left orbit)
pearls: Restricted upgaze + vertical diplopia + infraorbital (V2) numbness after blunt trauma = orbital floor blowout; confirm with thin-cut coronal CT.
A bradycardia/nausea (oculocardiac reflex) on eye movement signals true entrapment and mandates urgent surgical release.
'White-eyed' trapdoor blowouts look deceptively benign externally but threaten permanent muscle ischemia-repair within ~24-48 h.
Tell patients NOT to blow the nose (orbital emphysema risk) and consider prophylactic antibiotics due to sinus communication.

cases.title: White Pupil and a Wandering Eye in a Young Child
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents noticed a white reflection in the child's right pupil in photographs and that the right eye drifts outward.
cases.history: A 6-year-old boy is brought to clinic by his parents, who noticed that in recent flash photographs his right pupil glows white instead of red, and that the right eye sometimes drifts outward. They also feel his vision in that eye is poor-he sits very close to the television and seems not to use the right eye. There is no eye pain, redness, or trauma. He was born full term with an uncomplicated perinatal course. PMH: healthy, fully vaccinated, normal development. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: attends first grade; lives with both parents and a younger sibling. Family history: no known eye cancers, no family history of retinoblastoma or childhood eye tumors. ROS: no fever, weight loss, headache, vomiting, or bone pain; no prior eye surgeries. The parents are alarmed because an online search of 'white pupil in photos' suggested it could be an eye tumor.
cases.exam: Right eye: leukocoria (white pupillary reflex) confirmed; visual acuity markedly reduced (fix-and-follow poor); an exotropic (outward) deviation is present. No proptosis, no eye redness, no pain. Dilated fundus exam (right): yellowish subretinal exudation with prominent, dilated, tortuous, telangiectatic ('light-bulb') retinal vessels and areas of lipid (hard) exudate; an associated exudative retinal detachment in the inferior periphery; NO discrete chalky-white calcified intraocular mass. Left eye: normal red reflex, acuity age-appropriate, normal fundus. The child is otherwise well-appearing with no lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Leukocoria in a child is a red-flag finding. What is the single most important diagnosis that must be urgently excluded?
options: Simple refractive error (needs glasses)
Retinoblastoma-a life-threatening intraocular malignancy
Allergic conjunctivitis as the first step today
Benign congenital ptosis as the first step today
stages.explanation: Any child with leukocoria (a white pupillary reflex) must be urgently evaluated to exclude retinoblastoma, the most common primary intraocular malignancy of childhood, which is life-threatening if missed but highly curable when caught early. Other causes of leukocoria include Coats disease, persistent fetal vasculature, congenital cataract, toxocariasis, and ROP-but retinoblastoma is the can't-miss diagnosis that drives the urgency. A refractive error does not cause leukocoria. Allergic conjunctivitis causes itching, redness, and tearing, not a white reflex. Congenital ptosis is a droopy lid, unrelated to the pupillary reflex. Leukocoria plus strabismus (the two most common presenting signs of retinoblastoma) warrants immediate referral to a pediatric ophthalmologist/ocular oncologist.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which fundus features most strongly favor Coats disease over retinoblastoma in this child?
options: A pale retina with a cherry-red spot as the primary diagnosis
Multiple white satellite lesions with branching hyphae as the primary diagnosis
A discrete chalky-white mass with intralesional calcification and bilateral involvement as the primary diagnosis
Yellowish subretinal lipid exudation with dilated, tortuous, telangiectatic retinal vessels
stages.explanation: Coats disease is an idiopathic retinal vascular disorder, classically in young boys and unilateral, defined by abnormal telangiectatic, dilated, tortuous ('light-bulb') retinal vessels that leak, producing massive yellowish lipid (lipoproteinaceous) subretinal exudation and exudative retinal detachment-exactly this child's findings-without an intraocular mass. Retinoblastoma instead forms a discrete white/cream retinal mass that characteristically shows calcification on imaging and can be bilateral/familial. Satellite lesions with branching hyphae indicate fungal infection, not Coats. A pale retina with a cherry-red spot is CRAO, a vascular occlusion of a completely different age group and mechanism. The presence of telangiectasia/exudation and the absence of a calcified mass point to Coats.

stages.label: IMAGING & WORKUP
stages.content:
B-scan ocular ultrasound (right): subretinal fluid and exudate; NO intraocular calcification
MRI orbits/brain with contrast: exudative retinal detachment without a discrete enhancing calcified mass; no optic nerve invasion; no pinealoblastoma
Fluorescein angiography (right): retinal telangiectasias with 'light-bulb' aneurysmal dilatations and leakage; areas of capillary nonperfusion
Family history: negative for retinoblastoma

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which imaging finding is most useful in distinguishing retinoblastoma from Coats disease and supports the diagnosis here?
options: Bitemporal hemianopia on visual fields as the primary diagnosis
Intraocular calcification on B-scan/CT favors retinoblastoma
Presence of macular drusen on OCT as the primary diagnosis
Elevated intraocular pressure on tonometry as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Calcification is the key discriminator: retinoblastoma characteristically contains intraocular calcium, readily detected on B-scan ultrasound (and CT), whereas Coats disease shows subretinal exudate and telangiectatic vessels WITHOUT a calcified mass. In this child, B-scan and MRI show exudative detachment and telangiectasia but no calcification, strongly supporting Coats and arguing against retinoblastoma; MRI also helps exclude optic nerve invasion and associated pinealoblastoma. Macular drusen are a feature of AMD in older adults, irrelevant here. Bitemporal hemianopia localizes to the optic chiasm (e.g., pituitary lesion), not an intraocular tumor distinction. Intraocular pressure does not differentiate these two; both can secondarily raise pressure late. Calcification status is the single most useful imaging clue.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Once Coats disease is confirmed, what is the appropriate management approach for this exudative stage?
options: Enucleation of the eye, as for advanced retinoblastoma as initial therapy
Reassurance with annual eye exams and no intervention as initial therapy
Ablate the leaking telangiectatic vessels with laser photocoagulation and/or cryotherapy to halt exudation
Systemic chemotherapy identical to retinoblastoma protocols as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Coats disease is treated by destroying the abnormal leaking vessels: laser photocoagulation and/or cryotherapy ablate the telangiectasias to stop exudation, with anti-VEGF injections and surgical drainage/repair reserved for advanced exudative retinal detachment, all aimed at halting progression and salvaging vision-followed by close monitoring because it can recur. Enucleation is the treatment for an eye with non-salvageable advanced retinoblastoma, not for Coats (though end-stage painful/blind Coats eyes can rarely require it). Systemic chemotherapy follows retinoblastoma protocols and is not the standard treatment for Coats. Reassurance with annual exams alone under-treats an actively exudating eye and risks progression to total exudative detachment and blindness. Early vascular ablation offers the best visual outcome.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Beyond treating the eye, which point about Coats disease versus retinoblastoma is most important for counseling this family and planning follow-up?
options: Coats disease is a hereditary cancer requiring genetic testing and screening of siblings, like retinoblastoma with appropriate follow-up
Coats disease is a non-hereditary, non-malignant vascular disorder , but the eye needs ongoing follow-up because exudation can recur
Coats disease always resolves on its own and needs no follow-up with appropriate follow-up
Both conditions carry the same risk of a second intracranial (pineal) tumor with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Coats disease is a sporadic, non-hereditary, non-malignant retinal vascular disorder-so, unlike retinoblastoma, there is no cancer risk, no need for germline genetic testing, and no need to screen siblings. Counseling should reassure the family on these points while emphasizing that the affected eye requires sustained follow-up because exudation can recur after treatment, and that amblyopia management (and refractive correction/occlusion as needed) is essential in a 6-year-old to maximize visual development. Retinoblastoma, by contrast, can be heritable (RB1) warranting genetic counseling and sibling screening, and bilateral/heritable cases carry a risk of trilateral disease with a pineal (intracranial) tumor-features that do NOT apply to Coats. Saying Coats self-resolves or shares retinoblastoma's pineal risk is incorrect.

cases.diagnosis: Coats disease (exudative stage) presenting with leukocoria (right eye)
pearls: Leukocoria in a child = urgent workup to exclude retinoblastoma; leukocoria and strabismus are its two commonest presenting signs.
Coats disease: unilateral, usually in young boys, with telangiectatic 'light-bulb' vessels, lipid exudation, and exudative detachment-no calcified mass.
Intraocular calcification on B-scan/CT favors retinoblastoma; its absence (plus telangiectasia/exudation) favors Coats.
Treat Coats by ablating leaking vessels (laser/cryotherapy), with anti-VEGF and drainage for advanced exudative detachment.
