cases.title: Painful Rash and a Red Eye on One Side of the Face
cases.chiefComplaint: A painful blistering rash on his right forehead and scalp for four days, now with a red, watery right eye.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man presents with a 4-day history of a painful, burning rash over his right forehead, upper eyelid, and the tip of his nose. The pain preceded the rash by about two days and is described as deep and stabbing. Over the past day the right eye has become red, watery, and mildly light-sensitive, and his vision feels slightly hazy. He denies trauma, contact lens use, or prior similar episodes. Past medical history includes hypertension treated with amlodipine 5 mg daily and type 2 diabetes managed with metformin 1000 mg twice daily; his last HbA1c was 7.8%. He had chickenpox as a child and has not received the recombinant zoster vaccine. He has no known drug allergies. He is a retired accountant, lives with his wife, drinks alcohol socially, and quit smoking 15 years ago. Family history is notable for coronary artery disease. He reports feeling generally run-down with mild malaise but no fever. He has not taken any antiviral medication.
cases.exam: A grouped vesiculopustular rash on an erythematous base covers the right forehead and upper eyelid, strictly respecting the midline. Vesicles are present on the tip and side of the right nose. The right eye shows conjunctival injection and tearing; visual acuity is 20/40 right, 20/20 left. There is mild eyelid edema. Pupils are equal and reactive without a relative afferent defect. Extraocular movements are full and painless. The left eye and left face are entirely normal. No proptosis, no preauricular lymphadenopathy. Cranial nerve exam is otherwise intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The rash respects the midline and involves the forehead, upper lid, and tip of the nose. Which clinical sign most strongly predicts that the eye itself will be involved?
options: Conjunctival injection with tearing
Vesicles on the tip and side of the nose
Eyelid edema on the affected side
Pain preceding the appearance of the rash
stages.explanation: Hutchinson sign — vesicles on the tip/side of the nose — reflects involvement of the nasociliary branch of V1, which also supplies the globe; its presence markedly raises the risk of ocular involvement and warrants careful slit-lamp evaluation. Eyelid edema is nonspecific and common with any periocular inflammation. Conjunctival injection indicates surface irritation but does not specifically predict intraocular or corneal disease. Pain preceding the rash (pre-herpetic neuralgia) is characteristic of zoster but tells you nothing about whether the eye is affected. Recognizing Hutchinson sign should prompt urgent ophthalmologic referral even before complications are visible.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Herpes zoster ophthalmicus
Primary herpes simplex blepharoconjunctivitis
Contact dermatitis of the eyelid
Bacterial preseptal cellulitis
stages.explanation: A painful, dermatomal vesicular rash respecting the midline in the V1 distribution of an older adult is classic for herpes zoster ophthalmicus (reactivation of varicella-zoster in the ophthalmic division of the trigeminal nerve). Preseptal cellulitis produces diffuse lid erythema and edema but not grouped vesicles in a dermatome. Contact dermatitis is itchy rather than painful, lacks a dermatomal pattern, and does not respect the midline so sharply. Primary HSV blepharoconjunctivitis tends to occur in younger patients, is less strictly dermatomal, and usually lacks the pre-eruptive neuralgia and unilateral forehead involvement seen here.

stages.label: SLIT-LAMP EXAMINATION
stages.content:
Visual acuity: 20/40 OD, 20/20 OS
Conjunctiva: 2+ injection OD
Cornea: fine punctate epithelial keratitis with a small dendriform (pseudodendrite) lesion staining faintly with fluorescein
Anterior chamber: 1+ cell, 1+ flare OD
Intraocular pressure: 22 mmHg OD, 14 mmHg OS
Fundus: normal disc and macula OD

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the presentation and slit-lamp findings, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Oral acyclovir 800 mg five times daily plus ophthalmology referral
Topical corticosteroid drops started immediately without antiviral coverage
Topical antibiotic ointment alone and reassurance as initial therapy
Oral prednisone alone to suppress the inflammation as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Herpes zoster ophthalmicus with ocular involvement requires systemic antiviral therapy — high-dose oral acyclovir or valacyclovir — ideally started within 72 hours of rash onset to reduce viral replication and ocular complications, together with ophthalmology referral. Topical antibiotic alone ignores the viral etiology and intraocular inflammation. Topical or systemic corticosteroids without antiviral cover can worsen viral keratitis and are only used adjunctively (e.g., for stromal keratitis or uveitis) under ophthalmologic supervision once antivirals are on board. Steroids alone are therefore inappropriate as initial therapy. Antiviral therapy reduces the incidence and severity of ocular complications such as keratitis, uveitis, and episcleritis, and oral analgesia plus close ophthalmologic follow-up should accompany treatment.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Several weeks later he reports persistent burning, aching facial pain in the same distribution despite the rash having healed. What is this complication?
options: Trigeminal neuralgia (tic douloureux)
Postherpetic neuralgia
Secondary bacterial superinfection
Recurrent corneal erosion
stages.explanation: Postherpetic neuralgia — pain persisting beyond the healing of the rash (typically >90 days) in the affected dermatome — is the most common complication of zoster and is more frequent in older patients. It is managed with agents such as gabapentin, pregabalin, tricyclic antidepressants, or topical lidocaine. Recurrent corneal erosion causes sharp ocular pain on waking, not diffuse dermatomal facial pain. Secondary bacterial superinfection would present with renewed purulence and erythema, not isolated neuropathic pain. Trigeminal neuralgia produces brief, electric, trigger-evoked paroxysms rather than the constant burning ache of postherpetic neuralgia and is unrelated to a preceding zoster eruption.

cases.diagnosis: Herpes zoster ophthalmicus (V1) with associated keratitis and anterior uveitis
pearls: Hutchinson sign (vesicles on the nose tip) signals nasociliary nerve involvement and high risk of ocular disease.
Start systemic antivirals (acyclovir/valacyclovir) ideally within 72 hours of rash onset; refer to ophthalmology if the eye is involved.
Corticosteroids are only adjunctive and never used without antiviral coverage in zoster keratitis.
The recombinant zoster vaccine is recommended for adults 50 and older to prevent zoster and its complications.

cases.title: Aching, Light-Sensitive Red Eye in a Young Man
cases.chiefComplaint: A deep, aching pain and marked light sensitivity in the left eye for three days with redness around the colored part of the eye.
cases.history: A 34-year-old man presents with three days of a dull, aching pain in the left eye, severe photophobia, and tearing. He notes the eye is red, especially in a ring around the iris, and his vision is mildly blurred. There is no purulent discharge and no itching. He denies trauma, contact lens use, or foreign-body sensation. On review of systems he reports several months of low-back pain and morning stiffness lasting over an hour that improves with exercise, which he attributed to his desk job. He has had two similar but milder eye episodes in the past, each resolving over a couple of weeks. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable; he takes no regular medications and uses ibuprofen occasionally for his back. He has no known drug allergies. He works in IT, drinks alcohol on weekends, does not smoke, and is sexually active with one partner. Family history is notable for a father with chronic back stiffness and a maternal uncle with inflammatory bowel disease. He denies fever, urinary symptoms, joint swelling, or recent diarrhea.
cases.exam: Visual acuity is 20/20 right, 20/30 left. The left eye shows ciliary (perilimbal) injection that is most intense at the limbus. The left pupil is smaller than the right and reacts sluggishly; consensual photophobia is present (shining light in the right eye causes left eye pain). There is no discharge and the lids are normal. Cornea is clear without fluorescein uptake. The right eye is entirely normal. No proptosis, normal extraocular movements without pain. Spinal exam reveals reduced lumbar flexion and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature best distinguishes this presentation from acute bacterial conjunctivitis?
options: Mild blurring of vision as the first step today
Deep aching pain with photophobia
Presence of tearing and a red eye in this clinical context
A history of recurrent episodes as the first step today
stages.explanation: Deep aching pain, photophobia, and ciliary (perilimbal) injection point to intraocular inflammation (anterior uveitis) rather than a surface process. Bacterial conjunctivitis causes diffuse bulbar redness, a gritty sensation, and purulent discharge, with little true pain or photophobia and normal vision. Tearing and a red eye are nonspecific and occur in both. Mild blurring can occur in many conditions and is not discriminating on its own. Recurrent episodes are suggestive of uveitis but are not a presenting examination finding and could occur with allergic or other recurrent surface disease, so the pain/photophobia/ciliary-flush triad is the key distinguishing feature.

stages.label: SLIT-LAMP EXAMINATION
stages.content:
Visual acuity: 20/20 OS uncorrected pinhole improves to 20/25
Conjunctiva: ciliary flush OS, white OD
Cornea: clear, no fluorescein staining; fine keratic precipitates on inferior endothelium OS
Anterior chamber: 3+ cells and 2+ flare OS; no hypopyon
Pupil: miotic and slightly irregular OS (early posterior synechiae)
Intraocular pressure: 12 mmHg OS, 15 mmHg OD
Fundus: normal OU

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The slit-lamp shows 3+ cells and 2+ flare with keratic precipitates and early synechiae. What is the diagnosis?
options: Viral keratoconjunctivitis
Acute anterior uveitis
Episcleritis in this clinical context
Acute angle-closure glaucoma
stages.explanation: Cells and flare in the anterior chamber, keratic precipitates, ciliary flush, a miotic pupil, and posterior synechiae define acute anterior uveitis (iritis). Acute angle-closure glaucoma causes a fixed mid-dilated pupil, a steamy cornea, very high intraocular pressure, and severe pain with nausea — here the pupil is miotic and the pressure is normal. Episcleritis produces sectoral redness with minimal pain, no photophobia, and no anterior-chamber reaction. Viral keratoconjunctivitis shows follicular conjunctivitis, watery discharge, preauricular nodes, and corneal staining rather than anterior-chamber cells and flare. The combination of cells, flare, keratic precipitates, and synechiae localizes the inflammation to the anterior uvea and distinguishes iritis from these surface or angle-related mimics.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Given his chronic inflammatory back pain, morning stiffness, and sacroiliac tenderness, which test is most likely to be associated with his condition?
options: Serum ACE level in this setting
Rheumatoid factor in this setting
HLA-B27
Antinuclear antibody (ANA)
stages.explanation: Recurrent acute anterior uveitis in a young man with inflammatory back pain and sacroiliitis strongly suggests an HLA-B27-associated spondyloarthropathy, most likely ankylosing spondylitis; HLA-B27 is the relevant association. ANA is useful in juvenile idiopathic arthritis-associated chronic anterior uveitis (typically young girls) and in lupus, not this picture. Rheumatoid factor relates to rheumatoid arthritis, which classically spares the axial skeleton and is associated with scleritis rather than acute anterior uveitis. Serum ACE supports sarcoidosis, which more often causes granulomatous or posterior uveitis; the acute, recurrent, unilateral pattern with sacroiliitis fits HLA-B27 disease best.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial treatment for the eye?
options: Oral acyclovir and an eye patch as initial therapy
Topical antibiotic drops four times daily as initial therapy
Topical antihistamine/mast-cell stabilizer drops as monotherapy
Topical corticosteroid plus a cycloplegic agent
stages.explanation: Acute anterior uveitis is treated with topical corticosteroids to suppress intraocular inflammation, combined with a cycloplegic/mydriatic agent (e.g., cyclopentolate) to relieve ciliary-spasm pain and prevent or break posterior synechiae. Topical antibiotics treat bacterial surface infection, which is not present here. Oral acyclovir is for herpetic disease; there are no vesicles, dendrites, or herpetic features. Antihistamine/mast-cell stabilizers treat allergic conjunctivitis, which causes itching and papillae, not anterior-chamber cells and flare. Intraocular pressure and response should be monitored on follow-up, the steroid tapered as inflammation settles, and any underlying systemic disease such as spondyloarthropathy addressed with rheumatology to reduce recurrence.

cases.diagnosis: Acute anterior uveitis (iritis), HLA-B27-associated with likely ankylosing spondylitis
pearls: Anterior uveitis presents with deep aching pain, photophobia, ciliary flush, a miotic pupil, and anterior-chamber cells/flare.
Recurrent acute anterior uveitis in a young man with inflammatory back pain points to HLA-B27 spondyloarthropathy.
Treat with topical steroids plus a cycloplegic to control inflammation and prevent posterior synechiae.
Consensual photophobia (light in the unaffected eye causes pain in the affected eye) is a hallmark of true iritis.

cases.title: A Swollen, Bulging Eye and Fever in a Child
cases.chiefComplaint: Two days of worsening right eye swelling, redness, and pain that now hurts when he moves the eye, with fever.
cases.history: A 9-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with two days of progressive right eyelid swelling, redness, and pain. Today his parents noted the eye looks like it is bulging, and he complains of pain when he moves it and double vision when looking to the side. He has had a runny nose and nasal congestion for about a week following a cold, and developed a fever yesterday. He has been less active and is not eating well. There is no history of trauma, insect bite, or foreign body. Past medical history is unremarkable; immunizations are up to date, including Haemophilus influenzae type b. He takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. He lives with his parents and a younger sister, attends fourth grade, and there are no sick contacts with similar eye problems. Family history is noncontributory. On further questioning the parents note the swelling and redness have clearly worsened despite acetaminophen, and he is more lethargic than this morning.
cases.exam: The right upper and lower eyelids are markedly swollen, erythematous, and tender, making the eye difficult to open. There is proptosis of the right globe and conjunctival chemosis. Extraocular movements of the right eye are restricted and painful in all directions; the left eye moves fully and painlessly. There is pain on attempted eye movement. Visual acuity is 20/25 right (limited by swelling) and 20/20 left. The right pupil is reactive without a relative afferent pupillary defect at present. The left eye and lids are normal. Nasal mucosa is congested with purulent discharge. No neck stiffness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which findings most clearly distinguish a postseptal (orbital) process from a preseptal one in this child?
options: Eyelid erythema and tenderness
A preceding upper respiratory infection
Proptosis with painful, restricted eye movements
Fever and elevated white count
stages.explanation: Proptosis, pain on eye movement, restricted ocular motility (ophthalmoplegia), and chemosis indicate involvement of the orbit behind the orbital septum — i.e., orbital (postseptal) cellulitis. Eyelid erythema and tenderness occur in both preseptal and orbital cellulitis and cannot distinguish them. Fever and leukocytosis are more common with orbital disease but also occur in preseptal infection and are nonspecific. A preceding upper respiratory infection (here, sinusitis) is a risk factor for orbital cellulitis but is not itself a localizing sign. The orbital signs — proptosis and painful, limited motility — are what move the diagnosis behind the septum.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely source and diagnosis?
options: Preseptal cellulitis from local skin infection
Allergic periorbital edema as the primary diagnosis
Orbital cellulitis from contiguous ethmoid sinusitis
Cavernous sinus thrombosis from facial furuncle
stages.explanation: Orbital cellulitis in children most commonly arises from contiguous spread from the ethmoid sinuses, separated from the orbit only by the thin lamina papyracea; his recent URI and purulent rhinorrhea fit this source. Preseptal cellulitis would lack proptosis and painful ophthalmoplegia. Cavernous sinus thrombosis is a feared extension but usually presents with bilateral findings, multiple cranial neuropathies, and a toxic, deteriorating course; it is a complication rather than the initial diagnosis here. Allergic periorbital edema is typically bilateral, itchy, nontender, and afebrile, without proptosis or restricted movements. The recent viral upper respiratory illness with purulent rhinorrhea and the localizing orbital signs together point firmly to ethmoid sinusitis spreading into the orbit as the source.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: CT of the orbits and sinuses with contrast
Plain skull radiographs in this setting
Observation with repeat exam in 24 hours
Lumbar puncture as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Contrast-enhanced CT of the orbits and paranasal sinuses is the imaging of choice to confirm orbital cellulitis, define sinus involvement, and detect a subperiosteal or orbital abscess that may require drainage. Plain radiographs lack the sensitivity to assess the orbit and abscess formation. Lumbar puncture is not indicated without meningeal signs and could be hazardous if intracranial extension or raised pressure exists. Observation alone is unsafe given proptosis, painful ophthalmoplegia, and high fever, which signal true orbital involvement and risk of vision-threatening or intracranial complications; imaging and intravenous antibiotics should not be delayed.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 16.8 ×10³/µL (84% neutrophils)
CRP 92 mg/L
Blood cultures: pending
CT orbits/sinuses with contrast: opacification of the right ethmoid air cells with inflammatory stranding of the medial orbital fat and a small medial subperiosteal collection; globe and optic nerve intact

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Based on the CT findings, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate as an outpatient with next-day follow-up as monotherapy
Immediate orbital exenteration as initial therapy
Topical antibiotic drops and warm compresses as initial therapy
Admit for IV broad-spectrum antibiotics with ophthalmology
stages.explanation: Orbital cellulitis with a subperiosteal collection requires hospital admission and prompt intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics covering MRSA, streptococci, and respiratory/sinus flora (e.g., vancomycin plus ceftriaxone or ampicillin-sulbactam), with ophthalmology and ENT involvement and serial vision/motility checks; surgical drainage is considered for large abscesses or clinical deterioration. Oral antibiotics as an outpatient are appropriate only for uncomplicated preseptal cellulitis, not postseptal disease with proptosis. Topical drops and compresses do not treat a deep orbital infection. Exenteration is a disfiguring last resort reserved for life-threatening invasive disease (e.g., mucormycosis), not standard bacterial orbital cellulitis.

cases.diagnosis: Orbital (postseptal) cellulitis with subperiosteal abscess secondary to ethmoid sinusitis
pearls: Proptosis, painful and restricted eye movements, and chemosis distinguish orbital from preseptal cellulitis.
In children, orbital cellulitis most often spreads from the ethmoid sinuses through the lamina papyracea.
Contrast CT of the orbits and sinuses identifies abscess; admit for IV antibiotics with ophthalmology/ENT.
Watch for vision loss, a developing RAPD, and intracranial extension such as cavernous sinus thrombosis.

cases.title: A Red, Puffy Eyelid After a Bug Bite
cases.chiefComplaint: One day of redness and swelling of the right upper eyelid after a bug bite, with the eye otherwise feeling fine.
cases.history: A 5-year-old girl is brought to the pediatric clinic with one day of redness and swelling of the right upper eyelid. Her mother recalls a small insect bite near the eyebrow at a picnic the day before, with a tiny central punctum. Overnight the lid became pink, warm, and puffy. The girl is acting normally, playing, eating, and not complaining of eye pain; she denies double vision and her vision seems normal to her parents. There is mild itching at the site. She has had no fever, no nasal congestion, and no recent cold. There is no history of trauma beyond the bite and no contact lens use. Past medical history is unremarkable and immunizations are up to date. She takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. She lives with her parents and an older brother, attends kindergarten, and has no sick contacts. Family history is noncontributory. The mother specifically reports the eye opens normally and the girl moves it freely without discomfort.
cases.exam: The right upper eyelid is erythematous, mildly edematous, and warm, with a small central papule (bite site). The girl opens the eye spontaneously. Conjunctiva is white and quiet; there is no discharge. Visual acuity is age-appropriate and equal. Extraocular movements are full and painless in both eyes, with no pain on eye movement. There is no proptosis and the globe is not displaced. Pupils are equal and reactive with no relative afferent pupillary defect. The left eye and lids are normal. No tenderness over the sinuses and no cervical or preauricular lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which set of findings reassures you that this is a preseptal rather than an orbital process?
options: Full painless eye movements, no proptosis, normal vision,
Mild itching at the bite site as the first step today
A history of an insect bite as the first step today
Erythema and warmth of the eyelid as the first step today
stages.explanation: Full, painless extraocular movements, absence of proptosis, normal visual acuity, and no relative afferent pupillary defect indicate the infection is confined anterior to the orbital septum (preseptal). Eyelid erythema and warmth are present in both preseptal and orbital cellulitis and do not localize the process. An insect bite is a plausible portal of entry but does not by itself tell you the depth of infection. Itching suggests a local inflammatory or allergic component but again is not localizing. The key reassuring features are the normal orbital signs — movements, globe position, vision, and pupils.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Dacryocystitis
Allergic conjunctivitis
Orbital cellulitis
Preseptal cellulitis
stages.explanation: Localized lid erythema, warmth, and swelling following a break in the skin (insect bite) with entirely normal orbital function is classic preseptal (periorbital) cellulitis. Orbital cellulitis would show proptosis, painful or restricted eye movements, and possibly reduced vision — all absent here. Allergic conjunctivitis causes bilateral itchy, watery eyes with conjunctival involvement rather than a unilateral warm, tender lid with a bite punctum. Dacryocystitis produces tenderness and swelling over the lacrimal sac at the medial canthus, often with expressible pus from the puncta, not diffuse upper-lid cellulitis at an insect bite.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this well-appearing child?
options: Oral antibiotics as an outpatient
Oral antihistamines alone as initial therapy
Admit for IV vancomycin and ceftriaxone as initial therapy
Topical antibiotic drops only as initial therapy
stages.explanation: A well-appearing child with mild, uncomplicated preseptal cellulitis and normal orbital findings can be managed as an outpatient with oral antibiotics such as amoxicillin-clavulanate (covering skin flora including S. aureus and streptococci), with clear return precautions and close follow-up. IV antibiotics and admission are reserved for orbital cellulitis, very young or toxic-appearing children, or failure of outpatient therapy. Topical antibiotic drops treat conjunctival surface infection, not lid/soft-tissue cellulitis. Oral antihistamines address allergy but would not treat a bacterial cellulitis; itching here is from the bite, not an allergic eye condition.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which development should prompt the parents to return immediately for re-evaluation?
options: A small bruise developing at the bite site with appropriate follow-up
New pain with eye movement, double vision, or the eye appearing to bulge
Mild residual itching at the bite site with appropriate follow-up
The bite papule slowly fading over several days based on current risk
stages.explanation: New pain on eye movement, diplopia, or proptosis would signal progression to orbital (postseptal) cellulitis and demand urgent re-evaluation with imaging and IV antibiotics. Mild residual itching is expected from the insect bite and is not concerning. A fading bite papule indicates improvement. A small bruise at the bite site is benign local trauma. Parents should be counseled on these specific red-flag orbital signs — plus worsening swelling, high fever, lethargy, or vision change — so they can seek care promptly if the infection extends behind the septum, since orbital cellulitis requires imaging, admission, and intravenous antibiotics rather than outpatient oral therapy.

cases.diagnosis: Preseptal (periorbital) cellulitis following an insect bite
pearls: Preseptal cellulitis has normal vision, full painless eye movements, no proptosis, and no RAPD.
A break in the skin (insect bite, abrasion) is a common portal of entry for preseptal cellulitis.
Well-appearing children with uncomplicated preseptal cellulitis can be treated with oral antibiotics outpatient.
Teach return precautions: pain on eye movement, diplopia, or proptosis suggests progression to orbital cellulitis.

cases.title: Painful Vision Loss in One Eye in a Young Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Several days of blurred, dim vision in the left eye with pain when she moves it.
cases.history: A 29-year-old woman presents with four days of progressively blurred and dim vision in the left eye, accompanied by an aching pain behind the eye that worsens when she looks from side to side. She notes that colors, especially reds, appear washed out and less vivid in that eye. There is no redness, discharge, or itching, and no trauma or contact lens use. On review of systems she recalls an episode last year of transient numbness and tingling in her right leg lasting about a week that resolved without treatment, which she did not report at the time. She has had occasional bouts of fatigue. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable; she takes only a combined oral contraceptive pill and a daily multivitamin. She has no known drug allergies. She works as a graphic designer, drinks alcohol rarely, does not smoke, and has no recreational drug use. Family history includes an aunt with an unspecified neurological condition. She denies headache, jaw pain, scalp tenderness, weight loss, or systemic symptoms.
cases.exam: Visual acuity is 20/20 right and 20/80 left. There is a left relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) on swinging-flashlight testing. Color vision (Ishihara) is markedly reduced in the left eye (3/14 plates) compared with the right (14/14). The left eye is white and quiet with no anterior-chamber reaction; there is pain on attempted eye movements. Confrontation fields suggest a central scotoma on the left. Fundoscopy shows a normal-appearing optic disc on the left (no swelling) and a normal right disc. The remainder of the cranial nerve and neurologic exam is normal, with no other focal deficits today.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The classic triad here is unilateral vision loss, pain on eye movement, and dyschromatopsia with a RAPD. What does a relative afferent pupillary defect indicate?
options: A problem with the lens or media of the affected eye as the first step today
A defect in the efferent (motor) pupillary pathway of the affected eye
Bilateral, symmetric optic nerve disease as the first step today
An asymmetric defect in the afferent visual pathway, here the left optic nerve
stages.explanation: A RAPD reflects an asymmetric lesion of the afferent visual pathway — most often the optic nerve — so that less light signal is conducted from the affected eye; on the swinging-flashlight test the affected pupil paradoxically dilates when the light moves to it. An efferent (motor) defect would cause an unequal pupil size or poor constriction in one eye regardless of which eye is illuminated, not a relative afferent defect. Media opacities like cataract rarely cause a true RAPD. Bilateral symmetric optic neuropathy would not produce a relative (asymmetric) defect because both sides conduct equally poorly.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute angle-closure glaucoma
Optic neuritis (retrobulbar)
Anterior ischemic optic neuropathy
Central retinal artery occlusion
stages.explanation: A young woman with subacute unilateral vision loss, pain worsened by eye movement, dyschromatopsia, a central scotoma, a RAPD, and a normal-appearing disc has retrobulbar optic neuritis ("the patient sees nothing and the doctor sees nothing"). Acute angle-closure glaucoma causes a red, painful eye with a mid-dilated pupil, corneal edema, and high pressure, not a quiet white eye. Central retinal artery occlusion produces sudden, painless, profound vision loss with a pale retina and cherry-red spot. Anterior ischemic optic neuropathy occurs in older patients with vasculopathic risk factors and shows disc swelling, typically without pain on eye movement.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which imaging study is most appropriate to evaluate the cause and prognosis?
options: Orbital ultrasound (B-scan)
Non-contrast CT head in this setting
Carotid duplex ultrasound in this setting
MRI of the brain and orbits with contrast
stages.explanation: MRI of the brain and orbits with contrast is the study of choice: it can show enhancement of the affected optic nerve and, importantly, identify periventricular white-matter demyelinating lesions that establish the risk of progression to multiple sclerosis. Non-contrast CT is insensitive for demyelination and optic nerve inflammation. Carotid duplex evaluates embolic/ischemic retinal disease such as retinal artery occlusion, which is not the mechanism here. Orbital B-scan ultrasound assesses ocular structures and masses but cannot evaluate the intracranial white matter that governs prognosis in optic neuritis. The number and location of demyelinating lesions on MRI directly informs the risk of conversion to multiple sclerosis and the decision to start disease-modifying therapy.

stages.label: MRI BRAIN & ORBITS WITH CONTRAST
stages.content:
Left optic nerve: T2 hyperintensity with gadolinium enhancement of the retrobulbar segment
Brain: multiple ovoid periventricular and juxtacortical T2/FLAIR hyperintensities, several oriented perpendicular to the ventricles (Dawson fingers)
No mass lesion, no hemorrhage
Findings consistent with demyelination

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the MRI findings, what is the most appropriate acute treatment for the optic neuritis?
options: Oral prednisone alone (standard tapering dose)
No treatment; observe as vision will recover regardless
Intravenous methylprednisolone followed by an oral taper
Therapeutic plasma exchange as first-line therapy
stages.explanation: Per the Optic Neuritis Treatment Trial, high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone (followed by an oral taper) speeds visual recovery in acute optic neuritis and is the standard acute treatment, especially when MRI shows demyelinating lesions. Critically, oral prednisone alone (standard doses) is contraindicated as initial therapy because it was associated with a higher rate of recurrence. While vision often recovers spontaneously over weeks, IV steroids hasten recovery and the MS-risk lesions warrant neurology referral for disease-modifying therapy. Simply observing without treatment forgoes the proven benefit of faster recovery. Plasma exchange is reserved for severe, steroid-refractory cases and is not first-line treatment for typical acute optic neuritis.

cases.diagnosis: Optic neuritis (retrobulbar) as a clinically isolated syndrome heralding multiple sclerosis
pearls: Optic neuritis classically presents with painful (worse on eye movement) unilateral vision loss, dyschromatopsia, and a RAPD.
In retrobulbar optic neuritis the disc looks normal — 'the patient sees nothing and the doctor sees nothing.'
MRI brain/orbits with contrast detects demyelinating lesions and stratifies the risk of conversion to multiple sclerosis.
Treat with IV methylprednisolone, not standard-dose oral prednisone alone (which raised recurrence in the ONTT).

cases.title: Sudden Vision Loss with Headache and Jaw Pain in an Older Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden loss of vision in the right eye this morning, on a background of weeks of headache and jaw pain while chewing.
cases.history: A 74-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden, painless loss of vision in her right eye that began this morning. For the past three weeks she has had a new, persistent right-sided temporal headache and tenderness of her scalp, noting pain when she combs her hair. She also describes an aching fatigue in her jaw muscles after a few minutes of chewing that forces her to stop. She reports general malaise, low-grade fevers, and unintentional weight loss of about 4 kg over two months, with aching and stiffness in her shoulders and hips, worse in the mornings. Past medical history includes hypertension on lisinopril 10 mg daily and hyperlipidemia on atorvastatin 20 mg daily. She has no known drug allergies. She is a retired teacher, lives alone, does not smoke, and rarely drinks alcohol. Family history is noncontributory. She denies eye redness, discharge, trauma, prior similar episodes, or chest pain. Before the vision loss she recalls a brief episode of transient graying of vision in the same eye two days ago that resolved.
cases.exam: Visual acuity is counting fingers in the right eye and 20/25 in the left. There is a right relative afferent pupillary defect. The right temporal artery is prominent, tender, hardened, and pulseless to palpation; the left is normal. Scalp tenderness is present over the right temple. The right eye is white and quiet without injection. Fundoscopy of the right eye shows a swollen, pale ("chalky white") optic disc with blurred margins; the left disc is normal. Extraocular movements are full. There is tenderness and reduced range of motion at the shoulders. The remainder of the cranial nerve and neurologic exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which combination of features makes this an ophthalmic emergency requiring immediate action?
options: Headache and weight loss alone as the first step today
Bilateral shoulder stiffness and morning aching as the first step today
Acute monocular vision loss with jaw claudication, scalp tenderness,
A red, painful eye with discharge as the first step today
stages.explanation: Acute monocular vision loss combined with jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, and a tender, pulseless temporal artery in a woman over 50 is the classic picture of giant cell (temporal) arteritis threatening the optic nerve — a true emergency because the fellow eye can be lost within days without treatment. Headache and weight loss alone are nonspecific constitutional symptoms. Shoulder stiffness suggests associated polymyalgia rheumatica but is not itself sight-threatening. A red, painful eye with discharge describes a surface infection, not the quiet white eye with a pale swollen disc seen in arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The fundus shows a pale, chalky-white swollen disc. What is the most likely mechanism of her vision loss?
options: Non-arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (NAION)
Arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy
Retinal detachment as the primary diagnosis
Central retinal vein occlusion
stages.explanation: A pallid ("chalky white") swollen optic disc with severe vision loss in this clinical context indicates arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy from giant cell arteritis, due to inflammatory occlusion of the posterior ciliary arteries. Central retinal vein occlusion shows diffuse retinal hemorrhages and a 'blood and thunder' fundus, not a pale disc. Non-arteritic AION causes a hyperemic (not pallid) disc swelling, typically in patients with a 'disc at risk' and vasculopathic factors, with less severe loss and without jaw claudication or constitutional symptoms. Retinal detachment produces a curtain-like field defect and visible detached retina, not optic disc pallor.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the single most important next step in management?
options: Order a temporal artery biopsy and withhold treatment until results return
Start high-dose systemic corticosteroids immediately, before biopsy
Begin aspirin alone and arrange outpatient rheumatology follow-up
Obtain an MRI of the brain before any treatment as initial therapy
stages.explanation: When giant cell arteritis is suspected with visual loss, high-dose systemic corticosteroids (e.g., IV methylprednisolone or high-dose oral prednisone) must be started immediately to protect the fellow eye — treatment should NOT be delayed for the biopsy, since steroids do not significantly alter biopsy findings for one to two weeks. Withholding steroids pending biopsy risks irreversible bilateral blindness. Aspirin is adjunctive but inadequate alone. MRI does not establish the diagnosis and should not delay sight-saving therapy. The diagnostic workup (ESR, CRP, and temporal artery biopsy) proceeds in parallel with, not before, treatment, and biopsy remains informative for one to two weeks after steroids are begun, so there is no justification for withholding corticosteroids in a patient with threatened vision.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
ESR 96 mm/hr
CRP 84 mg/L
Hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL (normocytic)
Platelets 512 ×10³/µL
ALP mildly elevated
Temporal artery biopsy scheduled within 1 week

stages.label: CONFIRMATION
stages.question: What is the gold-standard test to confirm the diagnosis, and what is its key finding?
options: Carotid ultrasound showing plaque as the immediate next step
Lumbar puncture showing elevated opening pressure as the immediate next step
MRA showing temporal artery stenosis as the immediate next step
Temporal artery biopsy showing granulomatous inflammation with giant cells
stages.explanation: Temporal artery biopsy is the gold standard; it shows transmural granulomatous inflammation with multinucleated giant cells and fragmentation of the internal elastic lamina. Because involvement is segmental ('skip lesions'), an adequate-length specimen is needed and a negative biopsy does not fully exclude the disease. MRA and ultrasound (which may show a 'halo sign') can support the diagnosis but are not the confirmatory gold standard. Carotid ultrasound assesses atherosclerotic plaque, unrelated to this vasculitis. Elevated CSF opening pressure characterizes idiopathic intracranial hypertension, a different cause of disc swelling in a different patient population.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If untreated, what is the most feared immediate complication of giant cell arteritis?
options: Cataract formation as the leading concern
Vision loss in the contralateral eye
Chronic dry eye syndrome
Retinal vein occlusion in the affected eye
stages.explanation: The most feared immediate complication is vision loss in the fellow eye, which can occur within days if corticosteroids are not promptly started — hence the urgency of empiric treatment. Giant cell arteritis can also cause stroke and aortic aneurysm/dissection from large-vessel involvement, but bilateral blindness is the classic acute ophthalmic threat. Chronic dry eye and cataract are unrelated to this vasculitic process. Retinal vein occlusion is not the typical mechanism of arteritic visual loss, which involves the posterior ciliary (arterial) supply to the optic nerve head. This risk to the second eye is precisely why empiric high-dose corticosteroids are started immediately on clinical suspicion rather than awaiting biopsy confirmation.

cases.diagnosis: Giant cell (temporal) arteritis with arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy
pearls: Suspect GCA in patients over 50 with new headache, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, and a tender/pulseless temporal artery.
Arteritic AION shows a pallid (chalky-white) swollen disc; ESR and CRP are markedly elevated.
Start high-dose corticosteroids immediately — do NOT wait for the temporal artery biopsy — to protect the fellow eye.
Biopsy can stay positive for 1–2 weeks after steroids; watch for skip lesions and associated polymyalgia rheumatica.

cases.title: Daily Headaches and Brief Graying of Vision in a Young Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Weeks of daily headaches with brief episodes of vision graying out in both eyes and a whooshing sound in her ears.
cases.history: A 31-year-old woman presents with six weeks of daily, dull headaches that are worse in the mornings and when she lies down, bends over, or strains. She describes brief episodes, lasting a few seconds, where the vision in both eyes 'grays out' or blacks out completely, often when she stands up, then returns to normal. She also reports a pulsatile 'whooshing' sound in her ears and intermittent horizontal double vision. There is no weakness, numbness, or speech difficulty. Past medical history is notable for obesity; she has gained about 14 kg over the past year. She started a course of oral isotretinoin for acne three months ago and takes a combined oral contraceptive. She has no known drug allergies. She works as a receptionist, does not smoke, and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is noncontributory. She denies fever, neck stiffness, recent infection, or trauma. She is worried because the brief vision episodes are becoming more frequent, and she now notices her side vision feels slightly narrowed.
cases.exam: BMI is 36 kg/m². Visual acuity is 20/20 in both eyes. Pupils are equal and reactive with no relative afferent pupillary defect. Fundoscopy shows bilateral optic disc swelling (papilledema) with blurred disc margins and obscuration of vessels at the disc; no hemorrhages. There is a mild left abducens (CN VI) palsy with limited left lateral gaze and horizontal diplopia. Visual fields show an enlarged blind spot bilaterally with early nasal constriction. The remainder of the cranial nerve exam is normal, and there are no other focal neurologic deficits. Neck is supple without meningismus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Bilateral disc swelling (papilledema) raises concern for raised intracranial pressure. What is the most appropriate first diagnostic step?
options: Neuroimaging to exclude a mass or venous sinus thrombosis
CT of the orbits only as the first step today
Immediate lumbar puncture without imaging as the first step today
Empiric acetazolamide and discharge as the first step today
stages.explanation: Papilledema reflects raised intracranial pressure, and before a lumbar puncture you must exclude a space-occupying lesion and cerebral venous sinus thrombosis with neuroimaging — MRI of the brain, ideally with MR venography. Performing an LP before imaging risks herniation if a mass lesion is present and misses venous thrombosis, a key mimic of idiopathic intracranial hypertension. Empiric acetazolamide with discharge skips the essential exclusion of dangerous secondary causes. CT of the orbits alone does not evaluate the brain parenchyma or venous sinuses adequately. Imaging first, then LP, is the correct sequence.

stages.label: NEUROIMAGING & LUMBAR PUNCTURE
stages.content:
MRI brain: no mass, hydrocephalus, or structural lesion; partially empty sella; flattening of the posterior globes and distension of the optic nerve sheaths
MR venography: patent dural venous sinuses (no thrombosis)
Lumbar puncture: opening pressure 32 cm H2O (normal ≤25)
CSF analysis: normal cell count, protein, and glucose

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the clinical picture and results, what is the diagnosis?
options: Brain tumor with mass effect in this clinical context
Idiopathic intracranial hypertension
Malignant hypertension as the primary diagnosis
Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis
stages.explanation: An obese young woman with headache, transient visual obscurations, pulsatile tinnitus, a CN VI palsy, papilledema, elevated CSF opening pressure, normal CSF composition, and normal neuroimaging (aside from signs of raised pressure) meets the criteria for idiopathic intracranial hypertension. Venous sinus thrombosis was excluded by patent sinuses on MR venography. A brain tumor is excluded by the absence of a mass on MRI. Malignant hypertension would feature severely elevated blood pressure and end-organ damage; her blood pressure is normal. The combination of raised pressure with normal CSF and no structural cause defines IIH.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Besides weight loss counseling, what is the first-line medical therapy?
options: Therapeutic anticoagulation
Topiramate as monotherapy first-line
Oral prednisone long term
Acetazolamide
stages.explanation: Acetazolamide, a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor that reduces CSF production, is the first-line medical therapy for idiopathic intracranial hypertension, combined with weight management. The patient should also stop contributing agents such as isotretinoin (vitamin A derivative) and tetracyclines. Long-term prednisone is not used and can worsen weight gain and cause rebound on withdrawal. Topiramate is sometimes used adjunctively (and aids weight loss) but acetazolamide is the standard first choice. Anticoagulation treats venous sinus thrombosis, which was excluded here. Serial visual fields are monitored, and surgery (optic nerve sheath fenestration or CSF shunting) is reserved for progressive vision loss.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: What is the most important reason to monitor and treat this condition aggressively?
options: Risk of hearing loss from the tinnitus as the leading concern
Risk of permanent, progressive vision loss from chronic papilledema
Risk of developing chronic migraine as the leading concern
Risk of the abducens palsy becoming permanent and cosmetically obvious
stages.explanation: The most important reason for vigilant monitoring (serial visual fields and acuity) and aggressive treatment is to prevent permanent, progressive vision loss from sustained papilledema, which can lead to optic atrophy and irreversible blindness if untreated. Headache is a major symptom but is rarely the sight-threatening issue. The pulsatile tinnitus typically resolves with treatment and does not cause true hearing loss. The CN VI palsy is a false localizing sign of raised pressure and usually resolves once pressure is controlled. Protecting vision is the central goal of IIH management, which is why serial visual fields and acuity guide escalation to surgical options such as optic nerve sheath fenestration or CSF shunting when medical therapy fails.

cases.diagnosis: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (pseudotumor cerebri)
pearls: Suspect IIH in obese young women with headache, transient visual obscurations, pulsatile tinnitus, and papilledema.
Always obtain neuroimaging (with MR venography) before LP to exclude a mass and venous sinus thrombosis.
Diagnosis requires elevated CSF opening pressure with normal CSF composition and no structural cause.
Treat with weight loss and acetazolamide, stop offending drugs (vitamin A/tetracyclines), and monitor fields to prevent blindness.

cases.title: Gradual Blurring and Glare While Driving at Night
cases.chiefComplaint: Slowly worsening, painless blurring of vision over a year or two with bothersome glare from oncoming headlights at night.
cases.history: A 71-year-old man presents with a gradual, painless decline in his vision over the past 18 months. He describes everything looking 'foggy' or 'dimmer,' as if looking through a dirty windshield, and complains of significant glare and halos around oncoming headlights when driving at night, which has made him avoid night driving. He notes colors seem less vivid and more yellowed. Interestingly, he recently found he can read the newspaper without his reading glasses, which surprised him. There is no eye pain, redness, discharge, flashes, floaters, or curtain over his vision, and no sudden change. Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes on metformin 1000 mg twice daily and hypertension on amlodipine. He used inhaled and occasional oral corticosteroids years ago for asthma. He has no known drug allergies. He is a retired carpenter, a former smoker (quit 10 years ago), and drinks alcohol occasionally. He spent many years working outdoors in the sun. Family history includes age-related cataracts in both parents.
cases.exam: Best-corrected visual acuity is 20/60 right and 20/50 left, improving only slightly with pinhole. The red reflex is dulled and irregular bilaterally. On slit-lamp examination there is central yellow-brown opacification (nuclear sclerosis) of the lens in both eyes, denser on the right. The conjunctiva and cornea are clear; the anterior chambers are deep and quiet. Pupils are equal and reactive with no relative afferent pupillary defect. Intraocular pressures are 15 mmHg right and 16 mmHg left. Dilated fundus exam shows a view partially obscured by the lens but no diabetic retinopathy, normal optic discs, and flat maculae without hemorrhage or edema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: He reports being able to read without his reading glasses recently ('second sight'). What explains this phenomenon?
options: Onset of macular degeneration improving near vision
Improvement of presbyopia with age as the first step today
A myopic shift caused by increasing nuclear sclerosis of the lens
Spontaneous correction of his astigmatism as the first step today
stages.explanation: 'Second sight' is a myopic shift produced by increasing refractive index of the hardening, sclerotic nucleus of the lens, temporarily improving near vision so a presbyopic patient can read without glasses. Presbyopia does not spontaneously improve with age — it worsens. Macular degeneration impairs central vision rather than improving near reading. Astigmatism does not spontaneously correct from lens nuclear sclerosis. Recognizing this myopic shift, along with painless gradual blur, glare, halos at night, and color desaturation, points toward a nuclear sclerotic cataract; the phenomenon is temporary because continued lens opacification eventually outweighs any refractive benefit and overall vision declines.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the dulled red reflex and central lens opacity, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Diabetic macular edema
Age-related nuclear sclerotic cataract
Open-angle glaucoma
Primary angle-closure glaucoma
stages.explanation: Painless, gradual blurring with glare, halos at night, color desaturation, a myopic shift, a dulled red reflex, and central yellow-brown lens opacity are diagnostic of an age-related nuclear sclerotic cataract. Open-angle glaucoma is typically asymptomatic until late and causes peripheral field loss with optic disc cupping, not lens opacity or a dull red reflex. Diabetic macular edema would show retinal thickening and exudates on exam — his macula is flat without retinopathy. Primary angle-closure glaucoma presents acutely with a painful red eye, halos, a mid-dilated pupil, and high pressure, none of which are present.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His vision now interferes with daily activities like driving. What is the definitive treatment?
options: Laser peripheral iridotomy as initial therapy
Phacoemulsification cataract extraction with intraocular lens implantation
Stronger eyeglasses and observation only as initial therapy
Topical glaucoma drops as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The definitive treatment for a visually significant cataract is surgical removal — phacoemulsification with implantation of an intraocular lens — indicated when the cataract impairs function such as driving, reading, or daily activities. Updating glasses may help early, but once the cataract limits function and pinhole no longer corrects the deficit, surgery is appropriate. Topical glaucoma drops treat elevated intraocular pressure, which he does not have. Laser peripheral iridotomy treats narrow/closed angles to prevent angle-closure glaucoma and has no role in treating a cataract. Cataract surgery is one of the most common and successful operations performed.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Months after successful cataract surgery he returns with gradual blurring again. What is the most likely cause?
options: Posterior capsule opacification
Retinal detachment as the leading concern
Recurrence of the cataract
Acute endophthalmitis as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Posterior capsule opacification, often called a 'secondary cataract,' is the most common late complication after cataract surgery, caused by proliferation of residual lens epithelial cells on the posterior capsule; it produces gradual painless blurring months to years later and is easily treated with a YAG laser capsulotomy. A true cataract cannot recur because the natural lens has been removed. Acute endophthalmitis presents days after surgery with pain, redness, hypopyon, and rapid vision loss — not gradual late blurring. Retinal detachment causes flashes, floaters, and a curtain-like field defect, not painless slowly progressive haze.

cases.diagnosis: Age-related nuclear sclerotic cataract
pearls: Cataract causes gradual, painless blurring with glare, halos at night, and color desaturation.
Nuclear sclerosis can cause a myopic shift ('second sight'), temporarily improving near vision.
A dulled or irregular red reflex with central lens opacity supports the diagnosis.
Definitive treatment is phacoemulsification with IOL when vision impairs daily function; PCO is treated with YAG capsulotomy.

cases.title: Severe Eye Pain and Vision Loss After Hammering Metal
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe pain and loss of vision in the right eye immediately after a piece of metal flew into it while hammering.
cases.history: A 42-year-old man presents after he was hammering on a metal bolt without eye protection when he felt something strike his right eye, followed immediately by severe pain, tearing, and marked loss of vision. He reports the eye feels 'wet' and that there is something running down his cheek. He cannot see well out of that eye now. He denies any prior eye problems, surgery, or contact lens use. Past medical history is unremarkable; he takes no regular medications and has no known drug allergies. His last tetanus booster was more than 10 years ago. He works in an auto repair shop, is right-handed, drinks alcohol on weekends, and is a current smoker. Family history is noncontributory. He has not eaten in several hours. He is anxious and in significant pain, holding a tissue over the eye. He denies head injury, loss of consciousness, neck pain, or other injuries, and there is no nausea or vomiting.
cases.exam: The patient is in pain with the right eye partly closed. There is conjunctival hemorrhage and a small amount of clear fluid leaking from the cornea. The right pupil is teardrop-shaped (peaked) and pointing toward a corneal laceration; there appears to be a small amount of dark uveal tissue at the wound. Visual acuity in the right eye is limited to hand motions; the left eye is 20/20. The anterior chamber of the right eye appears shallow. The left eye is entirely normal. Because of concern for globe integrity, intraocular pressure is NOT measured and no further manipulation is performed. A fluorescein test of the cornea was about to be performed.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A peaked (teardrop) pupil and clear fluid leaking from the cornea after a high-velocity injury are most concerning for what?
options: Open globe
Subconjunctival hemorrhage alone
Traumatic iritis
Corneal abrasion
stages.explanation: A peaked or teardrop-shaped pupil (often pointing to the wound), extruded dark uveal tissue, a shallow anterior chamber, and leaking aqueous after a high-velocity metal-on-metal injury are hallmark signs of an open globe. A corneal abrasion causes pain and fluorescein uptake but no full-thickness wound, pupil distortion, or leaking aqueous. Traumatic iritis produces photophobia and anterior-chamber cells without globe penetration or a peaked pupil. An isolated subconjunctival hemorrhage is painless, does not affect the pupil, and does not threaten globe integrity. The constellation here mandates treating this as an open globe until proven otherwise.

stages.label: EXAMINATION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate action regarding the examination?
options: Pry the lids open forcefully to fully assess the wound in this setting
Stop examining and manipulating the eye, and place a rigid eye shield
Irrigate the eye copiously to flush out the metal before discharge planning
Apply gentle pressure to check intraocular pressure and instill fluorescein for a Seidel test
stages.explanation: With a suspected open globe, you must immediately stop examination and any manipulation and protect the eye with a rigid shield (not a patch) to prevent pressure that could extrude intraocular contents. Checking intraocular pressure with applanation/pressure is contraindicated because external pressure can expel uveal tissue or vitreous; a Seidel test confirming leaking aqueous can be done with great care but pressure must never be applied. Copious irrigation and forceful lid opening apply pressure to the globe and risk extruding contents — irrigation is for chemical burns, not penetrating trauma. Minimize manipulation entirely.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
CT orbits (thin-cut, axial and coronal): a small radiopaque intraocular foreign body in the posterior segment of the right globe
Globe contour irregularity with anterior chamber shallowing consistent with rupture
No orbital wall fracture
MRI deferred due to suspected metallic foreign body

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Besides placing a shield, which set of measures is appropriate in the emergency department before surgery?
options: Topical anesthetic drops every hour for pain control at home as initial therapy
IV broad-spectrum antibiotics, antiemetics, analgesia, tetanus prophylaxis, NPO status,
Atropine drops and a tight pressure patch over the eye as initial therapy
Topical antibiotic ointment, topical steroids, and discharge with follow-up as initial therapy
stages.explanation: An open globe requires IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (to prevent endophthalmitis), antiemetics (vomiting raises intraocular pressure and can extrude contents), adequate analgesia, tetanus prophylaxis, keeping the patient NPO in anticipation of urgent surgical repair, and immediate ophthalmology consultation — along with the protective shield. Topical antibiotics/steroids with discharge ignore the surgical emergency. A tight pressure patch is dangerous because it applies pressure to the globe. Repeated topical anesthetic use is toxic to the cornea and inappropriate, and home management is unsafe for an open globe, which needs prompt operative repair. Cycloplegics, eye drops, and any maneuver that raises intraocular pressure should be avoided, and the patient kept calm and NPO until the surgical team can take over.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the definitive next step for this confirmed open globe with a retained intraocular foreign body?
options: Discharge home with oral antibiotics and outpatient ophthalmology follow-up in one week
Observe overnight and reassess vision in the morning before deciding based on current risk
Remove the foreign body at the bedside in the emergency department with appropriate follow-up
Urgent surgical exploration
stages.explanation: A confirmed open globe with a retained intraocular foreign body requires urgent surgical exploration and primary repair in the operating room, typically within 24 hours, with removal of the foreign body and closure of the wound to restore globe integrity and reduce the risk of endophthalmitis. Discharge with oral antibiotics abandons a surgical emergency and invites infection and further extrusion. Bedside removal applies pressure and lacks the controlled, sterile, microsurgical conditions required, risking extrusion of intraocular contents. Overnight observation needlessly delays definitive repair; while the patient is stabilized (shield, IV antibiotics, antiemetics, NPO), the eye still needs prompt operative management.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Weeks after repair, the uninjured left eye becomes red, photophobic, and inflamed. What is this rare complication?
options: Allergic reaction to eye drops
Bacterial conjunctivitis
Sympathetic ophthalmia
Acute angle-closure glaucoma
stages.explanation: Sympathetic ophthalmia is a rare bilateral granulomatous uveitis in which the uninjured ('sympathizing') eye becomes inflamed weeks to months after penetrating trauma or surgery to the other eye, due to an autoimmune reaction against shared ocular antigens. Bacterial conjunctivitis causes purulent discharge in a single eye unrelated to prior contralateral trauma. An allergic drop reaction would cause itching and lid swelling, typically in the treated eye, without granulomatous uveitis. Acute angle-closure glaucoma presents with a fixed mid-dilated pupil, high pressure, and a hazy cornea, not a post-traumatic bilateral autoimmune uveitis. Early treatment and, when appropriate, enucleation of a blind injured eye can reduce risk.

cases.diagnosis: Open globe rupture with intraocular metallic foreign body
pearls: A peaked/teardrop pupil, extruded uveal tissue, and leaking aqueous (positive Seidel) signal an open globe.
Do not measure IOP or apply pressure — place a rigid shield and stop manipulating the eye.
Give IV antibiotics, antiemetics, analgesia, tetanus prophylaxis, keep NPO, and consult ophthalmology urgently.
Sympathetic ophthalmia is a rare bilateral uveitis of the fellow eye weeks to months after penetrating trauma.

cases.title: Burning, Whitened Eye After a Chemical Splash
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe burning, tearing, and blurred vision in the left eye immediately after liquid drain cleaner splashed into it.
cases.history: A 36-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after liquid drain cleaner splashed into his left eye while he was unclogging a sink. He immediately felt intense burning, with tearing, redness, and blurred vision, and reflexively shut the eye. He arrives in severe pain, unable to keep the eye open. He is not sure exactly what was in the product but the bottle warned it was caustic. He did not have eye protection and did not rinse the eye well at home. There is no other injury. Past medical history is unremarkable; he takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. He works as a building superintendent, is a nonsmoker, and drinks socially. Tetanus status is up to date. He is otherwise healthy and in obvious distress, clutching the eye. The triage nurse notes the product label indicates a strongly alkaline (lye-based) cleaner.
cases.exam: The patient is in severe pain with blepharospasm of the left eye. After topical anesthetic is instilled to allow examination, the left conjunctiva shows areas of blanching (whitening) at the limbus with surrounding injection. The cornea is hazy and partly opacified, and there is perilimbal ischemia. Visual acuity in the left eye is reduced to counting fingers; the right eye is 20/20 and normal. There is no globe laceration, no peaked pupil, and no foreign body embedded. The eyelid skin shows mild erythema. The right eye and adnexa are entirely normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A patient arrives with a known alkali chemical splash to the eye. What must be done FIRST, even before taking a full history?
options: Order a CT scan of the orbit as the first step today
Measure visual acuity and intraocular pressure in this clinical context
Obtain a detailed history of the exact chemical and timeline
Begin immediate, copious irrigation of the eye with saline or water
stages.explanation: Ocular chemical burns are a true emergency where immediate, copious irrigation takes priority over everything else — including history-taking — because every second of contact, especially with alkali, causes ongoing tissue damage. Irrigation (with saline, lactated Ringer's, or water) should start at once and continue until the ocular surface pH normalizes (around 7.0–7.4). Taking a detailed history first wastes critical time. Measuring acuity and intraocular pressure, while important, comes after irrigation has begun. CT imaging is irrelevant in the acute phase of a surface chemical burn and would dangerously delay treatment. Treatment precedes diagnosis here.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Why are alkali burns generally more damaging than acid burns to the eye?
options: Alkalis cause liquefactive necrosis
Alkalis only affect the eyelids and spare the globe as the immediate next step
Alkalis cause coagulative necrosis that limits penetration as the immediate next step
Acids penetrate faster because they dissolve the cornea instantly in this setting
stages.explanation: Alkalis cause liquefactive necrosis: they saponify the fatty acids of cell membranes and penetrate rapidly and deeply into the cornea and anterior chamber, causing extensive, ongoing damage. By contrast, acids cause coagulative necrosis, where precipitated proteins form a barrier that limits deeper penetration, so acid burns are typically less severe. It is therefore incorrect that alkalis cause coagulative necrosis or that acids penetrate faster. Alkalis certainly do not spare the globe — perilimbal ischemia and corneal opacification, as seen here, reflect deep alkali injury. This deep penetration is why alkali burns carry a worse prognosis.

stages.label: MONITORING
stages.question: During and after irrigation, what is the key endpoint that tells you irrigation can be slowed or stopped?
options: When the ocular surface pH returns to and stays neutral
When the patient's pain has completely resolved in this setting
When the conjunctival redness disappears in this setting
After exactly 5 minutes of irrigation regardless of pH
stages.explanation: Irrigation should continue until the ocular surface pH is checked with litmus paper and remains neutral (approximately 7.0–7.4) on repeat measurement after a brief pause; this confirms the offending chemical has been adequately diluted and cleared. Pain resolution is unreliable, especially after topical anesthetic, and does not indicate the chemical is gone. A fixed short time (e.g., 5 minutes) is inadequate, as alkali burns often require prolonged irrigation with large volumes and rechecking pH. Conjunctival redness can persist long after the pH normalizes and is not a valid endpoint. pH normalization is the objective target.

stages.label: POST-IRRIGATION ASSESSMENT
stages.content:
Ocular surface pH: 7.2 after 2 L of irrigation (initially 11)
Visual acuity: counting fingers OS
Fluorescein: large epithelial defect over the cornea OS
Limbus: approximately 4 clock-hours of perilimbal (limbal) ischemia / blanching OS
Intraocular pressure: 24 mmHg OS

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Which examination finding is the most important predictor of poor prognosis after an ocular alkali burn?
options: The amount of conjunctival injection
The patient's initial pain score
The degree of eyelid skin erythema
The extent of limbal (perilimbal) ischemia
stages.explanation: The extent of limbal (perilimbal) ischemia is the key prognostic indicator, because the limbus houses the corneal epithelial stem cells; greater ischemia means worse stem-cell loss, poorer corneal healing, risk of corneal neovascularization, conjunctivalization, and scarring (e.g., the Roper-Hall and Dua classifications grade severity by limbal/conjunctival ischemia). Eyelid skin erythema reflects superficial cutaneous injury and does not predict corneal outcome. Conjunctival injection is nonspecific and can be marked even in milder burns. The initial pain score does not correlate with tissue damage, particularly since anesthetic alters it. Limbal ischemia drives the long-term visual prognosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After irrigation and pH normalization, which set of treatments is most appropriate for this moderate-to-severe alkali burn?
options: A tight pressure patch and discharge with oral antibiotics as initial therapy
Topical antibiotic, topical steroid, cycloplegic, lubrication,
Immediate corneal transplant in the emergency department as initial therapy
Topical anesthetic drops for home use until healed as initial therapy
stages.explanation: After irrigation, management of a moderate-to-severe alkali burn includes a topical antibiotic (prophylaxis over the epithelial defect), a topical corticosteroid (to limit inflammation in the early phase, under ophthalmology guidance), a cycloplegic agent (for pain from ciliary spasm), aggressive lubrication, and often agents promoting healing (e.g., ascorbate/citrate), with urgent ophthalmology referral and IOP monitoring. A tight pressure patch and discharge are inadequate for a sight-threatening burn. Take-home topical anesthetics are corneotoxic and contraindicated. A corneal transplant is not an acute emergency-department procedure; surgical reconstruction is considered later if scarring impairs vision.

cases.diagnosis: Ocular alkali (chemical) burn with limbal ischemia
pearls: Chemical eye burns demand immediate copious irrigation BEFORE history; continue until surface pH is neutral (7.0–7.4).
Alkalis cause liquefactive necrosis and penetrate deeply, making them more damaging than acids (coagulative necrosis).
The extent of limbal ischemia predicts prognosis because the limbus contains corneal epithelial stem cells.
Never give patients topical anesthetics for home use — they are corneotoxic and impair healing.

cases.title: Painful Red Eye After Cataract Surgery
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe pain and worsening vision in the right eye four days after cataract surgery.
cases.history: A 77-year-old woman presents with a two-day history of progressively severe, deep aching pain in the right eye accompanied by marked redness and a dramatic drop in vision. She underwent uncomplicated phacoemulsification with intraocular lens implantation in the right eye four days ago. The first postoperative day she saw well, but yesterday her vision became foggy and is now reduced to perceiving only hand motions. She reports increasing photophobia and a sensation of pressure. She denies trauma, eye rubbing, or contact with contaminated water. Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 8.4%), hypertension, and blepharitis. Medications include metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, and her postoperative regimen of prednisolone acetate 1% and moxifloxacin 0.5% drops, which she admits to using irregularly. She has no known drug allergies. She lives alone, is a lifelong nonsmoker, and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history is unremarkable. She is anxious and frightened about losing her sight.
cases.exam: Right eye visual acuity is hand motions; left eye 20/30. The right lid is mildly swollen with conjunctival injection and chemosis. The cornea shows mild edema. There is a 1.5 mm layered hypopyon in the anterior chamber with dense cell and flare and a fibrinous pupillary membrane. The red reflex is markedly diminished and the fundus is obscured by vitritis. Intraocular pressure is 24 mmHg. The surgical wound is intact without leak (negative Seidel). The left eye is unremarkable with a clear lens periphery. No proptosis, no restriction of extraocular movements, and no relative afferent pupillary defect could be reliably assessed given media opacity.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains this presentation four days after cataract surgery?
options: Toxic anterior segment syndrome (TASS)
Acute postoperative bacterial endophthalmitis
Sterile uveitis from retained lens fragments
Acute angle-closure glaucoma
stages.explanation: Acute postoperative endophthalmitis classically presents 3-7 days after intraocular surgery with pain, decreasing vision, hypopyon, and vitritis—exactly this picture. TASS is incorrect because it occurs within 12-48 hours, is typically pain-light, spares the vitreous, and improves rapidly with steroids. Sterile uveitis from retained lens fragments can mimic inflammation but lacks the rapid devastating vision loss and dense vitritis and usually appears later. Acute angle-closure glaucoma causes a mid-dilated fixed pupil, a rock-hard eye with very high IOP, and a cloudy cornea, but not a hypopyon with vitritis; her IOP of 24 is only mildly elevated. The combination of timing, pain, hypopyon, and vitritis makes infectious endophthalmitis the urgent working diagnosis until proven otherwise.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the single most important next step to confirm the diagnosis and guide therapy?
options: Send blood cultures and start systemic vancomycin
Order orbital CT to exclude orbital cellulitis
Perform a vitreous tap for Gram stain and culture
Obtain B-scan ultrasonography of the right eye
stages.explanation: A vitreous tap (and aqueous sample) for Gram stain and culture is the definitive diagnostic step and is performed at the same sitting as intravitreal antibiotic injection. The vitreous is the highest-yield site for isolating the organism, which is most often coagulase-negative Staphylococcus. B-scan is useful when the fundus view is obscured to assess for retinal detachment or dense vitreous involvement, but it does not identify the organism. Blood cultures and systemic vancomycin are low-yield in acute postoperative (exogenous) endophthalmitis, where the infection is local; the EVS showed no benefit from systemic antibiotics in this setting. Orbital CT is reserved for suspected orbital cellulitis, which would show proptosis and motility restriction—both absent here.

stages.label: MICROBIOLOGY & IMAGING
stages.content:
Vitreous tap Gram stain: Gram-positive cocci in clusters
Vitreous culture (pending, preliminary): coagulase-negative Staphylococcus
Aqueous culture: no growth at 24 h
B-scan ultrasound: moderate vitreous opacities, retina attached, no choroidal mass
Intraocular pressure: 24 mmHg
Blood glucose: 184 mg/dL
WBC: 9.8 ×10³/µL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Based on the Endophthalmitis Vitrectomy Study and her presentation, what is the most appropriate immediate treatment?
options: Topical fortified antibiotics alone with close follow-up
Systemic intravenous vancomycin and ceftazidime only
Immediate pars plana vitrectomy regardless of acuity
Intravitreal vancomycin plus ceftazidime injection
stages.explanation: Intravitreal injection of vancomycin (covering gram-positives including MRSA) plus ceftazidime (covering gram-negatives) is the cornerstone of treatment for acute postoperative endophthalmitis and delivers high drug concentrations directly to the infection. The EVS demonstrated that immediate vitrectomy benefits only patients presenting with light-perception-only vision; for hand-motions or better, tap-and-inject yields equivalent outcomes, so routine immediate vitrectomy is not indicated here (her vision is hand motions—borderline, but tap/inject is standard initial therapy with vitrectomy reserved for deterioration or LP vision). Topical antibiotics alone cannot achieve therapeutic intravitreal levels and would be inadequate. Systemic antibiotics alone were shown by EVS to add no benefit in this setting and penetrate the vitreous poorly.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Forty-eight hours after tap-and-inject her vision has dropped to light perception with worsening vitritis. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Add high-dose oral corticosteroids and reassess in one week
Proceed with pars plana vitrectomy
Repeat intravitreal antibiotics and continue observation
Switch to systemic linezolid and topical steroids
stages.explanation: Clinical deterioration to light-perception vision with worsening vitritis despite intravitreal antibiotics is an indication for pars plana vitrectomy, which debulks the infectious and inflammatory load, clears the visual axis, and obtains a larger specimen. The EVS supports vitrectomy for patients with light-perception vision and is appropriate when there is failure to improve after tap-and-inject. Repeating intravitreal antibiotics with continued observation risks irreversible retinal damage in a worsening eye. Oral corticosteroids may be adjunctive but do not address the persistent infectious burden and should never be the sole escalation. Switching antibiotics to systemic linezolid does not overcome poor vitreous penetration and delays definitive surgical control. Prompt vitrectomy offers the best chance of salvaging useful vision.

cases.diagnosis: Acute postoperative bacterial endophthalmitis (coagulase-negative Staphylococcus)
pearls: Acute postoperative endophthalmitis presents 3-7 days after intraocular surgery with pain, vision loss, hypopyon, and vitritis—coagulase-negative Staphylococcus is the most common organism.
Tap-and-inject (vitreous sample plus intravitreal vancomycin and ceftazidime) is first-line; the EVS reserved immediate vitrectomy for light-perception vision.
TASS is the key mimic—it appears within 12-48 hours, is relatively painless, spares the vitreous, and responds rapidly to steroids.
Systemic antibiotics add no proven benefit in acute postoperative endophthalmitis due to poor vitreous penetration.

cases.title: Sudden Droopy Eyelid and Double Vision
