cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden drooping of the right eyelid with double vision and a dull headache.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man presents to the emergency department with the abrupt onset of right eyelid drooping and binocular horizontal and vertical double vision that began three hours ago. He describes an associated dull, deep right-sided headache that came on around the same time, unlike any headache he has had before. He denies fever, neck stiffness, weakness, slurred speech, or limb numbness. He has not had recent trauma. Past medical history includes hypertension and a 40-pack-year smoking history. Medications include amlodipine 10 mg daily; he admits to poor adherence. He has no known drug allergies. He drinks two to three beers most evenings and works as a long-haul truck driver. Family history is notable for a brother with a cerebral aneurysm and an aunt with polycystic kidney disease. He is alert and appropriately concerned, asking whether he is having a stroke. He notes the eyelid is so droopy he cannot see out of the eye unless he lifts it manually.
cases.exam: Alert, oriented man in mild distress from headache. The right upper lid shows complete ptosis. When the lid is elevated, the right eye is deviated down and out at rest. There is marked limitation of adduction, elevation, and depression of the right eye; abduction is intact. The right pupil is 6 mm and minimally reactive to light; the left pupil is 3 mm and briskly reactive. Visual acuity is 20/25 in each eye when the lid is held up. The remainder of the cranial nerve exam is normal, facial sensation is symmetric, and there is no facial droop. Motor strength, coordination, and gait are normal. Neck is supple without meningismus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which cranial nerve deficit best explains the down-and-out eye, complete ptosis, and dilated pupil?
options: Third nerve palsy with pupil involvement
Horner syndrome with internuclear ophthalmoplegia
Sixth (abducens) nerve palsy in this clinical context
Fourth (trochlear) nerve palsy
stages.explanation: A complete third (oculomotor) nerve palsy produces ptosis (levator palpebrae), a down-and-out eye (unopposed lateral rectus and superior oblique), and—when the pupillomotor fibers are involved—a fixed, dilated pupil. This patient has all three, indicating a pupil-involving third nerve palsy. A fourth nerve palsy causes only vertical/torsional diplopia with a hypertropia and head tilt, not ptosis or a dilated pupil. A sixth nerve palsy causes isolated abduction deficit with esotropia; his abduction is intact. Horner syndrome causes ptosis with a constricted (miotic) pupil and would not cause an adduction/elevation deficit or mydriasis. The dilated pupil is the critical finding pointing toward an external compressive lesion.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Why is the dilated, poorly reactive pupil the most alarming feature in this man?
options: It localizes the lesion to the cavernous sinus from venous thrombosis as the primary diagnosis
It suggests external compression, most concerning for a posterior communicating artery aneurysm
It indicates a benign microvascular ischemic palsy from his hypertension in this clinical context
It confirms myasthenia gravis affecting the iris sphincter as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Pupillomotor parasympathetic fibers run on the outer surface of the oculomotor nerve, where they are vulnerable to external compression. A compressive lesion—most classically a posterior communicating artery aneurysm—produces a 'pupil-involving' third nerve palsy, a neurosurgical emergency because of impending rupture. By contrast, microvascular ischemic palsies (diabetes, hypertension) typically spare the pupil because the central fibers infarct while the peripheral pupillary fibers retain their blood supply ('pupil-sparing'). Cavernous sinus pathology usually involves multiple nerves (III, IV, V1, VI) and is not signaled specifically by pupil dilation. Myasthenia gravis never affects the pupil because it is a neuromuscular junction disorder of striated muscle only. His positive family history of aneurysm heightens the concern.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate imaging study?
options: MRI orbits with contrast as the immediate next step
Carotid duplex ultrasound before discharge planning
Non-contrast head CT alone in this setting
CT angiography of the head to evaluate for aneurysm
stages.explanation: A pupil-involving third nerve palsy requires urgent vascular imaging to exclude an aneurysm. CT angiography (or MRA, often with conventional catheter angiography as the gold standard) reliably detects posterior communicating artery aneurysms 3 mm or larger and can be obtained rapidly. Non-contrast head CT alone is insufficient because it can miss an unruptured aneurysm, though it is useful to detect subarachnoid hemorrhage if rupture has occurred. MRI orbits would not adequately evaluate the circle of Willis and delays diagnosis. Carotid duplex evaluates extracranial carotid disease and has no role in detecting an intracranial aneurysm. Time is critical because an enlarging aneurysm can rupture catastrophically.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
CT angiography head: 7 mm saccular aneurysm at the junction of the right internal carotid and posterior communicating artery
Non-contrast head CT: no acute subarachnoid hemorrhage
MRI brain: no infarct, no mass lesion
Finger-stick glucose: 104 mg/dL
HbA1c: 5.6%

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Imaging confirms a 7 mm posterior communicating artery aneurysm without hemorrhage. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Discharge with outpatient neurology follow-up in two weeks as monotherapy
Urgent neurosurgical/neurointerventional consultation for aneurysm treatment
Begin IV heparin to prevent thromboembolic complications as initial therapy
Start aspirin and observe with serial imaging as initial therapy
stages.explanation: An unruptured but symptomatic posterior communicating artery aneurysm causing an acute third nerve palsy is at high risk of rupture and warrants urgent neurosurgical/neurointerventional consultation for definitive treatment by surgical clipping or endovascular coiling. Symptomatic aneurysms and those producing cranial neuropathy carry a markedly elevated short-term rupture risk, so prompt securing of the aneurysm is indicated, and early treatment also improves recovery of the nerve palsy. Outpatient follow-up dangerously delays care. Aspirin and observation are inappropriate for a symptomatic compressive aneurysm and aspirin could worsen bleeding if rupture occurs. IV heparin is contraindicated because anticoagulation increases the risk and severity of hemorrhage from an unsecured aneurysm.

cases.diagnosis: Pupil-involving third (oculomotor) nerve palsy from a posterior communicating artery aneurysm
pearls: A pupil-involving third nerve palsy is a posterior communicating artery aneurysm until proven otherwise and demands urgent vascular imaging.
Pupillomotor fibers travel on the surface of CN III, so compressive lesions dilate the pupil while ischemic (diabetic/hypertensive) palsies are typically pupil-sparing.
The eye sits 'down and out' from unopposed lateral rectus (CN VI) and superior oblique (CN IV) action.
Definitive treatment is urgent surgical clipping or endovascular coiling; the palsy often improves once the aneurysm is secured.

cases.title: Horizontal Double Vision Worse Far Away
cases.chiefComplaint: Horizontal double vision that is worse when looking at distant objects.
cases.history: A 54-year-old man presents with three weeks of horizontal double vision that resolves when he covers either eye. The images are side by side and the separation is worst when he looks far into the distance or to the left, and improves at near. He has also developed a constant, dull, generalized headache over the past month that is worse in the morning and when he lies flat, sometimes accompanied by transient graying of vision when he bends over. He reports mild nausea but no vomiting, weakness, or numbness. Past medical history includes obesity and obstructive sleep apnea. He takes no regular medications and has no known drug allergies. He recently started a medication course of isotretinoin prescribed by a dermatologist for severe acne. He does not smoke and drinks socially. He works as an accountant. Family history is unremarkable. On further questioning he notes pulsatile tinnitus described as a 'whooshing' sound in both ears, and his wife says he has seemed more sluggish lately.
cases.exam: Alert, obese man in no acute distress. Visual acuity is 20/20 in each eye. There is an esotropia in primary gaze that increases on attempted left gaze, with limited abduction of the left eye; the right eye abducts fully. The deviation is greater at distance than at near. Pupils are equal, round, and reactive with no afferent defect. Color vision is normal. Dilated fundus exam reveals bilateral optic disc edema with blurred disc margins and absent spontaneous venous pulsations; the maculae and periphery are normal. Facial sensation and strength are intact, and the remainder of the cranial nerve exam is normal. Motor, sensory, coordination, and gait examinations are unremarkable. There is no proptosis or lid retraction.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which nerve palsy explains the abduction deficit and esotropia worse at distance and on left gaze?
options: Left internuclear ophthalmoplegia
Left fourth (trochlear) nerve palsy
Left sixth (abducens) nerve palsy
Left third (oculomotor) nerve palsy
stages.explanation: The sixth (abducens) nerve innervates the lateral rectus, which abducts the eye. A sixth nerve palsy causes an abduction deficit with esotropia that is worse at distance and worsens on gaze toward the affected side (here, left gaze)—precisely this pattern. A third nerve palsy would cause ptosis, a down-and-out eye, and impaired adduction/elevation, not isolated abduction loss. A fourth nerve palsy causes vertical, not horizontal, diplopia. Internuclear ophthalmoplegia causes an adduction deficit on the affected side with abducting nystagmus of the other eye and results from a medial longitudinal fasciculus lesion; this patient's deficit is abduction, not adduction. The horizontal, side-by-side images worse at distance are classic for an abducens palsy.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the bilateral disc edema, headache worse when lying flat, pulsatile tinnitus, and transient visual obscurations, why is the sixth nerve palsy considered a 'false-localizing' sign?
options: Because it reflects a brainstem stroke rather than nerve compression as the primary diagnosis
Because raised intracranial pressure stretches the long intracranial course of CN VI, mimicking a focal lesion
Because the palsy is actually caused by orbital myositis as the primary diagnosis
Because the deficit will localize incorrectly to the cavernous sinus as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The abducens nerve has a long intracranial course and bends sharply over the petrous ridge, making it vulnerable to stretching when intracranial pressure rises. Thus a sixth nerve palsy can occur from globally elevated ICP without any lesion at the nerve itself—a 'false-localizing sign' because it falsely implies focal pathology along the nerve. The clinical picture (papilledema, postural headache, pulsatile tinnitus, transient visual obscurations, obesity, and an ICP-raising agent like isotretinoin/vitamin A derivatives) strongly suggests idiopathic intracranial hypertension. It is not a brainstem stroke, which would produce additional crossed signs. Orbital myositis would cause pain and restriction with proptosis. The deficit does not point to the cavernous sinus, where multiple nerves would be affected.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate sequence of investigations to confirm the underlying cause?
options: Neuroimaging followed by lumbar puncture with opening pressure
Lumbar puncture first, then imaging if abnormal as the immediate next step
Tensilon test and acetylcholine receptor antibodies before discharge planning
Empiric acetazolamide and clinical follow-up without further testing
stages.explanation: When papilledema and suspected raised ICP are present, neuroimaging must come first to exclude a mass lesion or venous sinus thrombosis; MRI brain with MR venography is preferred and also evaluates for cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, a key mimic. Only after imaging excludes a space-occupying lesion is lumbar puncture performed to measure opening pressure and analyze CSF—an elevated opening pressure with normal CSF composition supports idiopathic intracranial hypertension. Performing lumbar puncture before imaging risks herniation if an undetected mass is present. Empiric treatment without confirming the diagnosis is unsafe given the differential. A Tensilon test and acetylcholine receptor antibodies evaluate myasthenia gravis, which does not cause papilledema or a true nerve palsy.

stages.label: IMAGING & LP
stages.content:
MRI brain with contrast: no mass, partially empty sella, flattening of posterior globes
MR venography: patent dural venous sinuses, no thrombosis
Lumbar puncture opening pressure: 34 cm H2O (normal <25)
CSF analysis: glucose 62 mg/dL, protein 22 mg/dL, WBC 1/µL, normal cytology
BMI: 36 kg/m²

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Imaging is normal, MRV shows no thrombosis, and opening pressure is 34 cm H2O with normal CSF. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Discontinue isotretinoin, start acetazolamide,
Proceed directly to optic nerve sheath fenestration as monotherapy
Place a ventriculoperitoneal shunt urgently as initial therapy
Begin systemic corticosteroids as long-term therapy as monotherapy
stages.explanation: The diagnosis is idiopathic intracranial hypertension. Management combines removing the precipitant (isotretinoin, a vitamin A derivative known to raise ICP), starting acetazolamide to reduce CSF production, and instituting weight loss, which is the most effective long-term intervention. This addresses the headache and protects vision. Long-term systemic corticosteroids are avoided because they promote weight gain and cause rebound elevation on withdrawal. Optic nerve sheath fenestration and CSF shunting are surgical options reserved for fulminant disease or progressive vision loss despite maximal medical therapy, not first-line for this patient with preserved acuity. Removing the offending drug and starting acetazolamide with weight management is the appropriate, guideline-concordant first step, with close monitoring of visual fields and discs.

cases.diagnosis: Sixth (abducens) nerve palsy as a false-localizing sign of idiopathic intracranial hypertension
pearls: A sixth nerve palsy causes horizontal diplopia and esotropia worse at distance and worse on gaze toward the affected side.
Because of its long intracranial course over the petrous ridge, CN VI is the classic 'false-localizing' palsy of raised intracranial pressure.
In suspected raised ICP, image (MRI + MRV) before lumbar puncture, and confirm with an elevated opening pressure and normal CSF.
First-line IIH therapy is weight loss plus acetazolamide and removal of precipitants such as vitamin A derivatives, tetracyclines, and excess vitamin A.

cases.title: Vertical Double Vision and a Tilted Head
cases.chiefComplaint: Vertical double vision and difficulty reading or going down stairs.
cases.history: A 45-year-old man presents with six weeks of vertical double vision that is most bothersome when he reads, walks down stairs, or looks down and to one side. He says the two images are stacked one above the other and tilted relative to each other, and that he can fuse them if he tilts his head. His wife has noticed he now habitually tilts his head toward the right shoulder. He recalls a minor motor vehicle collision two months ago in which he struck his forehead on the steering wheel and was briefly dazed but did not lose consciousness. He had no diplopia immediately afterward, but it appeared gradually over the following weeks. Past medical history is unremarkable; he takes only occasional ibuprofen and has no known drug allergies. He does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. He works as a carpenter and reports the diplopia is jeopardizing his ability to work safely. Family history is noncontributory. He brings old photographs that, on review, show a longstanding subtle right head tilt predating the accident.
cases.exam: Alert man with a compensatory head tilt toward the right shoulder. Visual acuity is 20/20 in each eye. He has a right hypertropia in primary gaze. The right hypertropia increases on left gaze and on right head tilt, and decreases on right gaze and left head tilt. There is mild overaction of the right inferior oblique. Extraocular movements are otherwise full, with no abduction or adduction deficit. Pupils are equal and reactive without an afferent defect. There is no ptosis. Lids and orbits are normal without proptosis. The optic discs are sharp with normal color vision, and the remainder of the cranial nerve, motor, and sensory examinations is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The pattern of vertical diplopia with a head tilt is best evaluated with which bedside test?
options: Ice-pack test as the first step today
Cover-uncover test for horizontal deviation only
Hess screen for thyroid eye disease
Parks-Bielschowsky three-step test
stages.explanation: The Parks-Bielschowsky three-step test is designed to isolate the paretic cyclovertical muscle in vertical diplopia, especially when a fourth nerve palsy is suspected. Step 1 identifies which eye is higher (hypertropic) in primary gaze; step 2 determines whether the hypertropia worsens on right or left gaze; step 3 (the Bielschowsky head-tilt test) determines whether it worsens on right or left head tilt. The simple horizontal cover-uncover test does not localize a vertical muscle. The ice-pack test screens for myasthenic ptosis, which is absent here. The Hess screen maps ocular deviations generally but is not the specific localizing maneuver for a suspected trochlear palsy. The three-step test is the high-yield tool for this presentation.

stages.label: THREE-STEP TEST
stages.content:
Step 1 (primary gaze): right hypertropia
Step 2 (gaze): right hypertropia worse on LEFT gaze
Step 3 (Bielschowsky head tilt): right hypertropia worse on RIGHT head tilt
Double Maddox rod: excyclotorsion of the right eye
Forced ductions: free, no restriction

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The three-step test shows a right hypertropia worse on left gaze and right head tilt, with excyclotorsion. Which muscle/nerve is paretic?
options: Left superior oblique (fourth nerve)
Right superior oblique (fourth nerve)
Right superior rectus (third nerve)
Left inferior rectus (third nerve)
stages.explanation: Applying the three-step test: a right hypertropia (step 1) that worsens on left gaze (step 2) and worsens on right head tilt (step 3) localizes to the right superior oblique muscle, innervated by the right fourth (trochlear) nerve. The superior oblique normally depresses, intorts, and abducts the eye; its paralysis produces a hypertropia worst when the eye is adducted (left gaze for the right eye) and on ipsilateral head tilt, with excyclotorsion confirmed by double Maddox rod. The left inferior rectus and right superior rectus produce different three-step patterns. A left superior oblique palsy would give a left hypertropia worse on right gaze and left head tilt—the mirror image. Free forced ductions exclude a restrictive cause such as thyroid eye disease.

stages.label: ETIOLOGY
stages.question: The old photographs show a longstanding head tilt and his vertical fusional amplitudes are large. What does this suggest about the etiology?
options: An acute aneurysmal compression of the trochlear nerve in this setting
A decompensated congenital fourth nerve palsy unmasked by minor trauma
Thyroid eye disease with inferior rectus restriction in this setting
Myasthenia gravis with fatigable diplopia as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Large vertical fusional amplitudes and a longstanding head tilt visible in old photographs are hallmarks of a congenital fourth nerve palsy that has been compensated for years and then decompensated—often precipitated by minor head trauma, fatigue, or aging. The fourth nerve, with its long slender course and dorsal exit from the brainstem, is particularly susceptible to trauma. Aneurysmal compression of CN IV is rare and would present acutely without prior compensation. Myasthenia causes variable, fatigable diplopia without a fixed three-step pattern, torsion, or chronic head tilt. Thyroid eye disease produces restriction with positive forced ductions and lid signs; this patient has free forced ductions and no orbital signs. The history of head trauma uncovering a previously latent congenital palsy fits best.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management for his symptomatic but stable vertical diplopia?
options: Start pyridostigmine empirically as initial therapy
Immediate strabismus surgery on the inferior oblique as monotherapy
Orbital decompression surgery as initial therapy
Prism correction in spectacles with observation,
stages.explanation: For a decompensated congenital (or stable acquired) fourth nerve palsy, the initial approach is conservative: ground-in or press-on prisms to neutralize the vertical deviation and restore single vision, with observation since some palsies stabilize or improve. Strabismus surgery (such as inferior oblique weakening or superior oblique tucking) is offered when the deviation is large, incomitant, or disabling and is not adequately controlled by prisms—typically after a period of stability rather than immediately. Pyridostigmine treats myasthenia, which is not the diagnosis. Orbital decompression treats compressive thyroid eye disease, which is excluded by the free forced ductions and absence of orbital signs. Prisms first, surgery for persistent disability, is the standard stepwise management.

cases.diagnosis: Decompensated congenital right fourth (trochlear) nerve palsy
pearls: The Parks-Bielschowsky three-step test localizes the paretic cyclovertical muscle: a superior oblique palsy gives hypertropia worse on contralateral gaze and ipsilateral head tilt.
Trochlear palsy causes vertical and torsional (excyclotorsion) diplopia worst on downgaze—patients struggle reading and descending stairs.
Large vertical fusional amplitudes and a head tilt in old photographs point to a longstanding congenital palsy that decompensated, often after minor trauma.
Manage symptomatic stable trochlear palsy with prisms first; reserve strabismus surgery for persistent or disabling diplopia.

cases.title: One Small Pupil and a Drooping Lid
cases.chiefComplaint: Friends noticed his right eyelid is slightly drooping and the pupil looks smaller.
cases.history: A 52-year-old man is referred after coworkers commented that his right upper eyelid has been slightly droopy for two months and the right pupil looks smaller than the left, more noticeable in dim light. He denies double vision, eye pain, or vision change. On review of systems he reports a six-week history of a nagging right shoulder and arm ache and an intermittent dry cough, and he has lost about 12 pounds without trying. He also notes that the right side of his face does not seem to sweat as much as the left. Past medical history is notable for COPD. Medications include a tiotropium inhaler and albuterol as needed; he has no known drug allergies. He has a 45-pack-year smoking history and continues to smoke. He drinks beer on weekends and worked for years in construction with possible asbestos exposure. Family history is unremarkable. He appears thin and chronically ill but is in no acute distress and is worried the eye changes signal something serious.
cases.exam: Thin man in no distress. Visual acuity is 20/20 in each eye. The right upper lid shows mild ptosis (2 mm) and there is subtle elevation of the right lower lid ('upside-down ptosis'). The right pupil measures 3 mm and the left 5 mm in dim light; in bright light they measure 2.5 mm and 3 mm. The anisocoria is therefore greater in darkness, and there is dilation lag of the right pupil. Both pupils react normally to light and near. Extraocular movements are full and there is no proptosis. The right side of the face is noted to be drier. Chest auscultation reveals decreased breath sounds at the right apex. The remainder of the cranial nerve and neurologic examination is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The triad of ptosis, miosis with anisocoria worse in dark, and facial anhidrosis indicates which condition?
options: Third nerve palsy
Horner syndrome
Adie tonic pupil
Physiologic anisocoria
stages.explanation: Horner syndrome is the classic triad of ptosis, miosis, and anhidrosis caused by interruption of the oculosympathetic pathway. The miotic pupil dilates poorly in darkness, so the anisocoria is greater in dim light, and there is dilation lag—both present here. The ptosis is mild (Müller's muscle) and may be accompanied by 'upside-down ptosis' of the lower lid. A third nerve palsy causes a dilated pupil with ptosis and ophthalmoplegia, the opposite pupil findings. An Adie tonic pupil is large with light-near dissociation and would show anisocoria worse in bright light. Physiologic anisocoria is equal in light and dark and lacks ptosis and anhidrosis. The accompanying systemic features (smoking, weight loss, apical findings) raise concern for a serious cause.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which pharmacologic test best confirms Horner syndrome at the bedside?
options: Topical atropine to block constriction
Dilute (0.125%) pilocarpine constriction
Topical apraclonidine, looking for reversal of anisocoria
Topical phenylephrine 10% dilation in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Topical apraclonidine, a weak alpha agonist, confirms Horner syndrome by exploiting denervation supersensitivity of the sympathetically denervated iris dilator: the affected miotic pupil dilates (and the ptosis may improve) while the normal pupil shows little change, producing a 'reversal' of the anisocoria. Cocaine drops were the traditional test (no dilation of the Horner pupil), but apraclonidine is now widely used. Dilute pilocarpine 0.125% tests for an Adie tonic pupil (cholinergic supersensitivity), not Horner. Phenylephrine 10% and atropine are not standard confirmatory tests for Horner syndrome. Apraclonidine reversal is the practical confirmatory test, after which hydroxyamphetamine or imaging is used to localize the lesion.

stages.label: PHARMACOLOGIC & IMAGING
stages.content:
Topical apraclonidine 0.5%: right (miotic) pupil dilates, ptosis improves; left pupil unchanged — confirms Horner syndrome
Chest X-ray: right apical opacity with possible rib involvement
CT chest: 4.5 cm right apical (superior sulcus) mass invading the brachial plexus region
Weight: down 12 lb over 2 months

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: Given the apical lung mass, shoulder/arm pain, and anhidrosis of the face, where is the lesion localized along the sympathetic pathway?
options: Neuromuscular junction of the iris dilator in this setting
First-order (central) neuron in the hypothalamospinal tract
Third-order (postganglionic) neuron at the internal carotid
Second-order neuron, compressed by a Pancoast tumor
stages.explanation: The oculosympathetic pathway has three neurons. The second-order (preganglionic) neuron exits the spinal cord at T1, travels over the lung apex, and synapses in the superior cervical ganglion. A superior sulcus (Pancoast) tumor at the lung apex classically compresses this preganglionic segment, producing Horner syndrome plus brachial plexus involvement (shoulder/arm pain, hand weakness). Anhidrosis of the face also fits a preganglionic/central lesion. A first-order central lesion (brainstem/cord) would usually have other neurologic signs and broader anhidrosis. A third-order postganglionic lesion at the carotid causes Horner with limited or no facial anhidrosis and may have headache. There is no neuromuscular junction in autonomic pupillary control as a relevant localization. The apical mass pinpoints a preganglionic Pancoast lesion.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Refer urgently for tissue biopsy
Reassure and observe with repeat exam in six months as monotherapy
Prescribe topical brimonidine to treat the ptosis as initial therapy
Start empiric antibiotics for presumed apical pneumonia
stages.explanation: A newly diagnosed apical (superior sulcus) mass causing Horner syndrome in a heavy smoker with weight loss is a non-small cell lung cancer until proven otherwise and requires urgent tissue biopsy and multidisciplinary staging (often including PET and brain MRI) to guide treatment, which for Pancoast tumors frequently involves neoadjuvant chemoradiation followed by surgery. Observation dangerously delays diagnosis of a potentially curable malignancy. Empiric antibiotics are inappropriate because the CT shows an invasive mass, not a consolidative pneumonia. Topical brimonidine could cosmetically raise the lid but ignores the life-threatening underlying tumor. Recognizing Horner syndrome as the presenting sign of a Pancoast tumor and triaging the patient for oncologic workup is the priority.

cases.diagnosis: Right Horner syndrome (preganglionic) from a Pancoast (superior sulcus) lung tumor
pearls: Horner syndrome = ptosis + miosis + anhidrosis; the miosis causes anisocoria worse in darkness with dilation lag.
Apraclonidine confirms Horner syndrome via denervation supersensitivity, reversing the anisocoria.
A preganglionic (second-order) lesion over the lung apex with arm pain points to a Pancoast tumor—pursue urgent chest imaging in smokers.
Painful or rapidly progressive Horner syndrome, or one with systemic red flags, mandates imaging of the entire sympathetic pathway from hypothalamus to orbit.

cases.title: A Large Pupil That Will Not Constrict Quickly
cases.chiefComplaint: Noticed one pupil is much larger than the other and her near vision is blurry.
cases.history: A 33-year-old woman presents because she noticed in a selfie that her right pupil is much larger than the left, and over the past two weeks she has had blurry vision when reading and trouble adjusting from far to near, with mild light sensitivity. She denies eye pain, headache, droopy lid, weakness, or numbness. She had a viral upper respiratory illness about a month ago. She has no significant past medical history and takes only a combined oral contraceptive pill and occasional loratadine for allergies. She denies using eye drops, scopolamine patches, or handling plants such as jimson weed, and has not been exposed to nebulized ipratropium. She has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke, drinks wine socially, and works as a graphic designer. Family history is unremarkable. On focused questioning she reports that her knee and ankle reflexes have seemed diminished when checked at a recent physical, and she sometimes feels lightheaded standing up. She is otherwise healthy and active and is anxious that the pupil difference signals a brain problem.
cases.exam: Healthy-appearing woman in no distress. Visual acuity is 20/20 distance in each eye; near vision is blurred in the right eye uncorrected. The right pupil is 6 mm and the left 3.5 mm in room light; the anisocoria is greater in bright light. The right pupil reacts poorly and sluggishly to light but constricts slowly and more completely to a sustained near target (light-near dissociation), then redilates slowly. Slit-lamp exam of the right iris shows segmental, vermiform (worm-like) movements of the iris border. There is no ptosis and extraocular movements are full without diplopia. The optic discs are normal with no afferent pupillary defect. Deep tendon reflexes are diminished at the knees and ankles bilaterally. The remainder of the neurologic exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A dilated pupil that reacts poorly to light but constricts to near, with segmental iris movements and anisocoria worse in light, suggests which diagnosis?
options: Third nerve palsy in this clinical context
Argyll Robertson pupil
Adie (tonic) pupil
Pharmacologic mydriasis from an anticholinergic agent
stages.explanation: An Adie tonic pupil is a large pupil with a poor light reaction but a slow, tonic constriction to a near target (light-near dissociation), slow redilation, and segmental/vermiform iris movements from damage to the ciliary ganglion/short ciliary nerves. The anisocoria is worse in bright light because the affected pupil cannot constrict. A third nerve palsy would include ptosis and ophthalmoplegia, which are absent. Pharmacologic mydriasis produces a widely dilated pupil that does not respond to dilute or even standard pilocarpine and lacks tonic near response and vermiform movements; she denies anticholinergic exposure. Argyll Robertson pupils are small, bilateral, and irregular with light-near dissociation (syphilis), not a large unilateral tonic pupil. The vermiform iris movement is highly specific for Adie pupil.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which pharmacologic test confirms an Adie tonic pupil?
options: Topical apraclonidine, expecting reversal of anisocoria
Standard pilocarpine 1%, expecting no constriction in this clinical context
Topical cocaine 4%, expecting no dilation as the primary diagnosis
Dilute pilocarpine 0.125%, expecting constriction of the affected pupil
stages.explanation: Because the postganglionic parasympathetic denervation of the ciliary ganglion produces cholinergic denervation supersensitivity, the Adie pupil constricts to dilute (0.1-0.125%) pilocarpine that has little effect on a normal pupil—this asymmetric constriction confirms the diagnosis. Topical cocaine tests for Horner syndrome (sympathetic), not Adie. Standard pilocarpine 1% would constrict both a normal and an Adie pupil and is used instead to distinguish pharmacologic mydriasis (which fails to constrict even to 1%). Apraclonidine reversal confirms Horner syndrome, not Adie. Demonstrating supersensitivity to dilute pilocarpine is the classic confirmatory test for a tonic pupil.

stages.label: PHARMACOLOGIC TESTING
stages.content:
Dilute pilocarpine 0.125% both eyes: right (large) pupil constricts briskly; left pupil minimal change — confirms cholinergic supersensitivity
Standard pilocarpine not required
Deep tendon reflexes: 1+ knees and ankles bilaterally
RPR/VDRL: nonreactive
Blood pressure lying 118/74, standing 104/70 mmHg

stages.label: ASSOCIATION
stages.question: The diminished deep tendon reflexes combined with the tonic pupil define which named syndrome?
options: Holmes-Adie syndrome
Wallenberg syndrome
Parinaud syndrome in this setting
Miller Fisher syndrome
stages.explanation: Holmes-Adie syndrome is the combination of a tonic (Adie) pupil with generalized hyporeflexia or areflexia, sometimes accompanied by autonomic features such as orthostatic blood pressure changes and segmental anhidrosis—matching this young woman's diminished reflexes and mild orthostasis. Miller Fisher syndrome is a Guillain-Barré variant with ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, and areflexia (anti-GQ1b antibodies), but it features external ophthalmoplegia rather than an isolated tonic pupil. Parinaud (dorsal midbrain) syndrome causes light-near dissociation but with upgaze palsy and convergence-retraction nystagmus, not vermiform iris movements or dilute-pilocarpine supersensitivity. Wallenberg (lateral medullary) syndrome causes crossed sensory loss, ataxia, and Horner syndrome, not a tonic dilated pupil. The pupil-plus-hyporeflexia pairing is the defining feature of Holmes-Adie.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After confirming a benign Adie tonic pupil with hyporeflexia and a negative syphilis screen, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Reassurance, reading correction or dilute pilocarpine for symptoms,
Lifelong systemic anticholinergic therapy as initial therapy
High-dose intravenous corticosteroids as initial therapy
Urgent MRI brain and neurosurgical referral as initial therapy
stages.explanation: An idiopathic Adie tonic pupil with hyporeflexia (Holmes-Adie syndrome) is a benign condition; once compressive and infectious causes are excluded, management is reassurance plus symptomatic treatment. Near-vision blur can be helped with reading glasses or low-dose (dilute) pilocarpine to aid accommodation/constriction, and photophobia tends to improve over time as the pupil becomes more miotic. Urgent MRI and neurosurgery are unnecessary because the pupil findings are postganglionic and benign with no ophthalmoplegia or other red flags. Intravenous corticosteroids have no role in this non-inflammatory ciliary ganglion process. Systemic anticholinergics would worsen, not help, the condition. Reassurance with symptomatic care and observation is the correct, conservative approach.

cases.diagnosis: Adie (tonic) pupil with hyporeflexia (Holmes-Adie syndrome)
pearls: An Adie tonic pupil is large with light-near dissociation, slow tonic near constriction, slow redilation, and segmental vermiform iris movements.
Anisocoria from an Adie pupil is worse in bright light (the affected pupil cannot constrict), the opposite of Horner syndrome.
Dilute pilocarpine 0.125% confirms the diagnosis via cholinergic denervation supersensitivity; standard pilocarpine 1% distinguishes pharmacologic mydriasis (no constriction).
Tonic pupil plus diminished deep tendon reflexes defines Holmes-Adie syndrome, a benign condition managed with reassurance and symptomatic care.

cases.title: Abnormal Findings on a Premature Infant Eye Screening
cases.chiefComplaint: Routine screening eye examination flags abnormal retinal blood vessels in a premature infant.
cases.history: A 6-week-old infant is examined in the neonatal intensive care unit during a scheduled retinal screening. The infant was born at 25 weeks' gestation with a birth weight of 620 g. The NICU course has been complicated by respiratory distress syndrome requiring intubation, prolonged supplemental oxygen with fluctuating saturations, two episodes of culture-positive sepsis, a patent ductus arteriosus, and several blood transfusions. The infant is now at 31 weeks' postmenstrual age and is on nasal CPAP and gavage feeds. There is no family history of congenital eye disease or childhood blindness. The mother received antenatal steroids and magnesium. There were no maternal infections identified on prenatal screening. The neonatology team requests ophthalmology evaluation per protocol given the gestational age, low birth weight, and oxygen exposure. The parents are anxious and have been counseled that premature infants require eye screening. The infant is hemodynamically stable on the day of the exam with no acute deterioration.
cases.exam: Small preterm infant in an isolette, stable on CPAP. Anterior segments are clear with reactive pupils after mydriatic drops. Dilated indirect ophthalmoscopy with scleral depression shows the retinal vasculature has developed only to zone II with a sharp demarcation line and a raised ridge at the junction of vascularized and avascular retina. There is early extraretinal fibrovascular (neovascular) tissue arising from the ridge in several clock hours. The posterior pole vessels show dilation and tortuosity (arterial tortuosity and venous dilation) in two quadrants. There is no retinal detachment, no vitreous hemorrhage, and no leukocoria. The findings are symmetric between the two eyes. The optic nerves appear normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: These findings in a 25-week, 620 g infant with oxygen exposure are most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Congenital cytomegalovirus retinitis
Familial exudative vitreoretinopathy
Persistent fetal vasculature
Retinopathy of prematurity
stages.explanation: Retinopathy of prematurity (ROP) is a vasoproliferative retinopathy of premature, low-birth-weight infants exposed to supplemental oxygen, characterized by incomplete retinal vascularization with a demarcation line, ridge, and extraretinal neovascularization—exactly these findings. The strongest risk factors are low gestational age, low birth weight, and oxygen therapy, all present here. Persistent fetal vasculature is typically unilateral with a retrolental stalk and microphthalmos, not a peripheral ridge. Familial exudative vitreoretinopathy looks similar but occurs in full-term infants with a positive family history, which is absent. Congenital CMV causes a necrotizing retinitis with pigmentary scarring and systemic features, not a peripheral fibrovascular ridge. The prematurity and oxygen exposure make ROP the diagnosis.

stages.label: STAGING
stages.question: A raised ridge with extraretinal fibrovascular proliferation arising from it corresponds to which ROP stage?
options: Stage 2 (ridge) as the immediate next step
Stage 1 (demarcation line) in this setting
Stage 5 (total retinal detachment)
Stage 3
stages.explanation: ROP is staged by the appearance at the vascular-avascular junction. Stage 1 is a flat demarcation line; stage 2 is a raised ridge; stage 3 adds extraretinal fibrovascular (neovascular) proliferation extending from the ridge into the vitreous—this infant's finding. Stage 4 is partial retinal detachment (4A extrafoveal, 4B involving the fovea) and stage 5 is total detachment, neither of which is present given the attached retina. Identifying stage 3 is critical because, combined with plus disease, it can reach a treatment threshold. The presence of a ridge plus neovascular tufts defines stage 3, advancing the infant's risk relative to stages 1-2.

stages.label: EXAM CLASSIFICATION
stages.content:
Zone: II
Stage: 3 (extraretinal fibrovascular proliferation) in 6 contiguous clock hours
Plus disease: present (dilation and tortuosity of posterior pole vessels in ≥2 quadrants)
Laterality: symmetric, both eyes
No retinal detachment, no vitreous hemorrhage

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Zone II, stage 3 with plus disease meets what designation under current classification, guiding the urgency of treatment?
options: Immature retina requiring no follow-up
Type 2 ROP (observe closely)
Type 1 ROP (treatment-warranted)
Regressed (involutional) ROP
stages.explanation: Under the Early Treatment for ROP (ETROP) classification, Type 1 ROP—warranting treatment—includes zone I any stage with plus disease, zone I stage 3 without plus, and zone II stage 2 or 3 WITH plus disease. This infant has zone II stage 3 with plus disease, which is Type 1 ROP and requires prompt treatment, generally within 48-72 hours. Immature retina with no follow-up is clearly wrong given active stage 3 plus disease. Type 2 ROP (zone I stage 1-2 without plus, or zone II stage 3 without plus) is observed closely rather than treated. Regressed ROP describes involution of disease, which has not occurred. The presence of plus disease at the threshold is the key driver mandating treatment.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment for this Type 1 (threshold) ROP?
options: Systemic corticosteroids and oxygen restriction alone as monotherapy
Laser photocoagulation of the avascular peripheral retina
Immediate scleral buckle surgery as initial therapy
Observation with weekly exams until term as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Type 1 (treatment-warranted) ROP is treated by ablating the avascular peripheral retina with laser photocoagulation, which destroys the ischemic, VEGF-producing retina and halts neovascular drive; intravitreal anti-VEGF injection (e.g., bevacizumab) is an accepted alternative, particularly for posterior zone I disease, though it requires prolonged surveillance for recurrence. Continued observation is unsafe once Type 1 thresholds are met because progression to retinal detachment can be rapid. Scleral buckle surgery is reserved for later-stage tractional detachment (stage 4-5), not threshold stage 3. Adjusting oxygen and steroids does not reverse established threshold disease. Timely retinal ablation (laser) or anti-VEGF is the standard of care to prevent blindness.

cases.diagnosis: Retinopathy of prematurity, zone II stage 3 with plus disease (Type 1, treatment-warranted)
pearls: ROP risk is driven by low gestational age, low birth weight, and supplemental oxygen exposure; screening is mandatory for qualifying preterm infants.
Plus disease (posterior pole venous dilation and arterial tortuosity) signals activity and pushes disease to a treatment threshold.
Type 1 ROP (e.g., zone II stage 2-3 with plus, or zone I disease) warrants treatment, generally within 48-72 hours.
Treatment is laser photocoagulation of avascular retina or intravitreal anti-VEGF; tractional detachment (stage 4-5) requires vitreoretinal surgery.

cases.title: A White Glow in a Toddler's Eye on Photos
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents notice a white reflection in their daughter's eye in photographs and that her eye sometimes turns inward.
cases.history: An 18-month-old girl is brought in because her parents have repeatedly seen a white glow in her right eye in flash photographs over the past two months, instead of the normal red-eye reflex. They also think her right eye sometimes drifts inward. She is otherwise well, feeding and growing normally, with no fevers, eye pain, redness, or discharge. She was born full-term after an uncomplicated pregnancy and delivery, met her developmental milestones, and has no prior eye problems. She takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. There is no history of trauma. Family history is notable for a maternal cousin who reportedly had an eye removed in early childhood for a tumor; the parents are unsure of details. The parents are first-time parents and are very worried. The child has had no exposure to toxins, and there is no consanguinity. Immunizations are up to date. On the day of the visit she is playful and interactive, tracking toys with the left eye more readily than the right.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, interactive toddler. There is leukocoria (a white pupillary reflex) of the right eye, with an absent red reflex on that side; the left red reflex is normal. There is an esotropia of the right eye. The right eye fixes and follows poorly, while the left fixes and follows well, suggesting reduced vision on the right. The conjunctiva is white and quiet without injection, and the cornea is clear with a normal-sized globe (no buphthalmos or microphthalmos). Dilated fundus examination of the right eye reveals a creamy-white, elevated, vascularized retinal mass with overlying calcific foci and surrounding subretinal fluid. The left fundus is normal. There is no proptosis and no palpable lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Leukocoria with a calcified retinal mass and strabismus in an 18-month-old is most concerning for which diagnosis?
options: Congenital cataract
Toxocara endophthalmitis
Retinoblastoma
Coats disease in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Retinoblastoma is the most common intraocular malignancy of childhood and classically presents with leukocoria and strabismus in a child under 2-3 years; a white, elevated, vascularized retinal mass with intralesional calcification is highly characteristic. A congenital cataract causes leukocoria but is a lens opacity without a retinal mass or calcification. Coats disease causes leukocoria from exudative retinal detachment with telangiectatic vessels and yellowish subretinal exudate but typically lacks calcification and tends to occur in slightly older boys. Toxocara causes a granuloma with vitritis and an inflamed eye, not a calcified mass with a quiet eye. The combination of leukocoria, strabismus, and a calcified retinal mass strongly indicates retinoblastoma, and the family history raises concern for a heritable form.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Incisional biopsy of the retinal mass for histology
Observation with repeat photographs in three months
Examination under anesthesia with dilated fundoscopy
Fine-needle aspiration of the vitreous in this setting
stages.explanation: Suspected retinoblastoma is evaluated by an examination under anesthesia with meticulous bilateral dilated fundoscopy (to map all tumors and assess the fellow eye) plus orbital and brain MRI to assess optic nerve and extraocular extension and to screen for associated intracranial (pineal) tumor. Crucially, biopsy and any procedure that breaches the globe are contraindicated because they risk extraocular tumor seeding and worse prognosis—this rules out incisional biopsy and vitreous fine-needle aspiration. CT is generally avoided in these children to limit radiation, especially in heritable disease. Observation is unacceptable for a vision- and life-threatening malignancy. The diagnosis is made clinically/by imaging under anesthesia, not by tissue sampling.

stages.label: IMAGING & GENETICS
stages.content:
Exam under anesthesia: solitary calcified intraretinal mass right eye, 12 mm base; left eye normal
Orbital/brain MRI: intraocular mass without optic nerve or extraocular extension; pineal region normal
B-scan ultrasound: high internal reflectivity with calcification and acoustic shadowing
RB1 germline testing: pending
No evidence of metastatic disease on systemic workup

stages.label: GENETICS
stages.question: Which statement about RB1 genetics and trilateral retinoblastoma is correct and most important to convey to this family?
options: Bilateral or familial disease implies a germline RB1 mutation with risk of a pineal tumor
Germline RB1 mutations reduce the risk of other malignancies later in life as the immediate next step
Trilateral retinoblastoma refers to tumors in three quadrants of one eye as the immediate next step
Retinoblastoma is never inheritable and the family history is coincidental as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Retinoblastoma results from biallelic inactivation of the RB1 tumor suppressor (Knudson two-hit hypothesis). Germline (heritable) RB1 mutations—suggested by bilateral, multifocal, or familial disease and by the positive family history here—carry autosomal dominant inheritance with high penetrance, an increased risk of a midline intracranial (usually pineal) tumor termed 'trilateral retinoblastoma,' and a lifetime risk of second primary cancers such as osteosarcoma. Saying it is never inheritable is false and clinically dangerous. Germline RB1 mutations increase, not decrease, the risk of other malignancies. Trilateral retinoblastoma refers to bilateral ocular tumors plus an intracranial midline tumor, not three quadrants of one eye. Germline testing guides surveillance and genetic counseling.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this unilateral, intraocular tumor without metastatic spread, what is the most appropriate management goal and modality?
options: External beam radiotherapy as first-line for all cases as initial therapy
Globe- and vision-preserving treatment when feasible, reserving enucleation for advanced eyes
Primary enucleation only, with no role for eye-preserving therapy as initial therapy
Topical chemotherapy drops alone as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Modern retinoblastoma management aims to save life first, then preserve the eye and vision when feasible. For intraocular disease without high-risk features, eye-preserving approaches—systemic or intra-arterial chemotherapy (chemoreduction) combined with focal consolidation (laser, cryotherapy, or plaque brachytherapy)—can control the tumor while retaining the globe. Enucleation remains appropriate for advanced eyes with no visual potential or when globe salvage would compromise survival, but it is not the only option here. External beam radiotherapy is largely avoided, especially in germline disease, because it increases the risk of radiation-induced second cancers. Topical drops alone cannot treat an intraocular solid tumor. A multidisciplinary, vision-sparing strategy is preferred when oncologically safe.

cases.diagnosis: Retinoblastoma (unilateral, intraocular) with possible germline RB1 mutation
pearls: Leukocoria and strabismus in a child under 3 are retinoblastoma until proven otherwise—check the red reflex at every well-child visit.
Diagnosis is by exam under anesthesia plus MRI; biopsy is contraindicated because it risks extraocular tumor seeding.
Bilateral, multifocal, or familial disease implies a germline RB1 mutation with risk of trilateral (pineal) tumor and second primary cancers like osteosarcoma.
Treatment prioritizes survival, then globe and vision preservation; external beam radiation is avoided in germline disease to limit second cancers.

cases.title: A Baby's Watery, Crusty Eye Since Birth
cases.chiefComplaint: Persistent tearing and crusting of one eye since the first weeks of life.
cases.history: A 4-month-old infant is brought in by parents for chronic watering and mattering of the left eye that has been present almost since birth. They describe tears welling up and spilling onto the cheek and a daily film of yellowish crust on the lashes, especially after sleep, that they wipe away with warm water. The eye itself looks white and the baby does not seem to be in pain or rub the eye excessively. The symptoms have been steady, occasionally a bit worse with upper respiratory infections, but there has been no fever, no eyelid swelling, and no spreading redness. The infant was born full-term by vaginal delivery without complications and received routine erythromycin ointment at birth. She is feeding well, gaining weight, and developing normally. She takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. There is no family history of congenital eye disorders. The parents have been cleaning the lashes but have not been taught any massage technique. They are reassured the baby is otherwise thriving but want to know if treatment is needed.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, content infant. The left eye shows an increased tear lake with overflow tearing (epiphora) and mucoid debris at the medial canthus and on the lashes. Gentle pressure over the lacrimal sac region expresses a small amount of mucoid material from the puncta, but the overlying skin is not red, warm, swollen, or tender. The conjunctiva is white and quiet without significant injection, and the cornea is clear with a normal, symmetric red reflex bilaterally. The eyelids are normal in position without entropion or trichiasis. Corneal diameters are normal with no signs of buphthalmos. Intraocular structures and the right eye are unremarkable. There is no photophobia or blepharospasm.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Chronic tearing and mattering since birth in an otherwise white, quiet eye is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Congenital glaucoma in this clinical context
Congenital nasolacrimal duct obstruction
Bacterial conjunctivitis
Congenital dacryocystocele with infection
stages.explanation: Congenital nasolacrimal duct obstruction (CNLDO) is the most common cause of persistent tearing and mucoid discharge from birth, due to a membranous obstruction (usually at the valve of Hasner) preventing tear drainage; the eye remains white and quiet with mucoid reflux on sac pressure—exactly this picture. Congenital glaucoma also causes tearing but is accompanied by photophobia, blepharospasm, corneal clouding/enlargement (buphthalmos), and elevated pressure, all absent here. Bacterial conjunctivitis causes a red, injected eye with purulent discharge, not a chronically white eye with epiphora since birth. A dacryocystocele presents as a bluish swelling below the medial canthus at birth and, if infected, with overlying erythema and tenderness, which are not present. CNLDO is the clear diagnosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management for this 4-month-old?
options: Dacryocystorhinostomy as initial therapy
Lacrimal sac massage
Long-term oral antibiotics as initial therapy
Immediate nasolacrimal duct probing under anesthesia
stages.explanation: First-line management of uncomplicated CNLDO is conservative because the majority resolve spontaneously within the first year of life. Caregivers are taught Crigler hydrostatic massage—firm downward strokes over the lacrimal sac to generate pressure that can rupture the membranous obstruction—along with lid hygiene and topical antibiotics only for episodes of mucopurulent discharge. Probing is reserved for cases that persist beyond about 12 months or that fail conservative therapy, not as an initial step at 4 months. Dacryocystorhinostomy is a surgical bypass reserved for refractory or anatomic obstruction in older children/adults, not first-line in infants. Long-term oral antibiotics are inappropriate for a noninfected, simple obstruction. Massage with observation is the evidence-based first step.

stages.label: TECHNIQUE
stages.question: How should the parents be instructed to perform Crigler lacrimal sac massage correctly?
options: Press on the globe itself to express tears as the immediate next step
Rub the eyelashes outward toward the temple after each feed in this setting
Apply firm downward pressure over the lacrimal sac toward the nose 2-3 times daily
Massage upward from the sac toward the eyebrow several times daily in this setting
stages.explanation: Crigler massage is performed by placing a finger over the lacrimal sac (just below the medial canthal tendon) and applying firm, downward pressure directed toward the nose, repeated several times (typically 2-3 times daily). This generates hydrostatic pressure within the sac that helps rupture the membranous obstruction at the valve of Hasner. Massaging upward toward the eyebrow pushes contents back toward the puncta and is ineffective. Rubbing the lashes outward is simple lid hygiene, not therapeutic sac massage. Pressing on the globe is incorrect and potentially harmful, with no effect on the drainage system. Proper downward, nose-directed sac compression is the key teaching point for parents.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which finding during follow-up would warrant escalation rather than continued conservative care?
options: Mucoid reflux on sac pressure with a quiet eye with appropriate follow-up
Occasional crusting after naps with appropriate follow-up
Development of a red, tender, swollen mass over the lacrimal sac with fever
Persistent mild tearing at the next routine visit based on current risk
stages.explanation: Development of a red, tender, swollen mass over the lacrimal sac with fever indicates acute dacryocystitis, an infection of the obstructed sac requiring prompt systemic antibiotics and urgent referral—an escalation beyond conservative massage. Persistent mild tearing, occasional crusting after naps, and mucoid reflux with a quiet eye are all expected features of uncomplicated CNLDO that continue to be managed with massage and observation, with probing considered if symptoms persist past about a year. Recognizing the transition from a simple obstruction to an infected, inflamed sac is essential, because acute dacryocystitis should not be probed during the acute infection and needs antibiotics first. The inflammatory mass with fever is the red-flag finding mandating escalation.

cases.diagnosis: Congenital nasolacrimal duct obstruction
pearls: CNLDO is the most common cause of infant epiphora and mattering; the eye stays white and quiet with mucoid reflux on sac pressure.
Most cases resolve spontaneously within the first year—first-line care is Crigler massage (firm downward, nose-directed sac pressure) and lid hygiene.
Reserve nasolacrimal probing for obstruction persisting beyond about 12 months or failing conservative therapy.
Photophobia, blepharospasm, and corneal enlargement point to congenital glaucoma, not CNLDO—escalate immediately.

cases.title: Painful Red Swelling Beside the Nose
cases.chiefComplaint: Painful red swelling at the inner corner of the eye with pus.
cases.history: A 59-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with two days of increasing pain, redness, and swelling at the inner corner of her right eye, just below the inner eyelid near the nose. She says the area is tender and warm, and this morning she noticed pus coming from the corner of her eye when she pressed on the swelling. For several months before this she had intermittent tearing and occasional mattering of the same eye. She denies double vision, pain with eye movements, decreased vision, or proptosis. She reports feeling feverish with chills. Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus and prior chronic tearing of the right eye. Medications include metformin 1000 mg twice daily and lisinopril 10 mg daily; she has no known drug allergies. She is a nonsmoker and drinks alcohol rarely. She lives with her husband and works as a librarian. Family history is noncontributory. She is uncomfortable but not toxic-appearing, and she is concerned because the swelling has grown noticeably since yesterday.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable woman, not toxic. There is a tender, erythematous, warm, fluctuant swelling over the lacrimal sac region just below and medial to the right medial canthus. Gentle pressure over the sac expresses purulent material (mucopurulent reflux) from the punctum. Visual acuity is 20/25 in each eye. Extraocular movements are full and painless, and there is no proptosis. The pupils are equal and reactive without an afferent defect, and the conjunctiva of the globe is only mildly injected medially. The cornea is clear. There is no chemosis, no resistance to retropulsion, and no orbital signs. Periauricular and submandibular nodes are not significantly enlarged. The left eye and adnexa are normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A tender, erythematous swelling below the medial canthus with purulent reflux from the punctum is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Preseptal cellulitis from a skin abrasion
Hordeolum (stye)
Acute dacryocystitis
Orbital cellulitis
stages.explanation: Acute dacryocystitis is infection of the lacrimal sac, almost always arising from underlying nasolacrimal duct obstruction with stasis; it presents as a tender, red, warm swelling located below and medial to the medial canthus, often with purulent reflux through the punctum when the sac is pressed—precisely this presentation, with her preceding chronic tearing supporting outflow obstruction. Orbital cellulitis causes proptosis, painful or restricted eye movements, and possible vision change, none present here. A hordeolum is a focal tender lump on the eyelid margin from a blocked gland, not a medial canthal sac swelling. Preseptal cellulitis is diffuse lid erythema/edema without the localized sac mass and punctal pus. The medial canthal location with punctal reflux is pathognomonic for dacryocystitis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate management of this acute dacryocystitis?
options: Immediate nasolacrimal duct probing to relieve the obstruction
Topical antibiotic drops alone as initial therapy
Systemic antibiotics
Urgent dacryocystorhinostomy that same day as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Acute dacryocystitis is treated first with systemic (oral or, if systemically ill/diabetic, intravenous) antibiotics covering common organisms such as Staphylococcus and Streptococcus, plus warm compresses; a pointing abscess can be incised and drained. Crucially, probing and irrigation are NOT performed during the acute infection because instrumentation can spread infection and cause cellulitis or fistula. Dacryocystorhinostomy (DCR) is the definitive procedure to bypass the obstruction but is performed electively after the acute infection resolves, not emergently the same day. Topical drops alone are inadequate for an infection within the sac and soft tissues. Antibiotics with supportive care now, and deferred definitive surgery, is the correct sequence; her diabetes and fever lower the threshold for IV therapy and admission.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
WBC 13.6 ×10³/µL (neutrophil predominant)
Blood glucose 212 mg/dL
Gram stain of expressed pus: gram-positive cocci in clusters
Culture: pending
CRP 64 mg/L

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication should you watch for that would indicate spread beyond the lacrimal sac?
options: Mild residual erythema as the infection settles as the leading concern
Development of proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, or vision change signaling orbital cellulitis
Resolution of tearing after antibiotics as the leading concern
Transient blurring relieved by blinking as the leading concern
stages.explanation: The feared complication of acute dacryocystitis is posterior spread of infection into the orbit, producing orbital cellulitis—heralded by proptosis, painful or restricted extraocular movements (ophthalmoplegia), chemosis, decreased vision, or an afferent pupillary defect; this requires urgent imaging and aggressive intravenous antibiotics, sometimes surgical drainage. Her diabetes increases the risk of severe spread. Resolution of tearing and settling erythema reflect expected improvement, not a complication. Transient blurring relieved by blinking is a benign tear-film phenomenon. Recognizing the early orbital signs is essential because orbital extension threatens vision and can progress to cavernous sinus thrombosis. Monitoring for proptosis and motility/vision changes is the key safety check during treatment.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After the acute infection resolves with antibiotics, what definitive treatment prevents recurrence?
options: Repeated lacrimal sac massage indefinitely
Punctal plug insertion with appropriate follow-up
Dacryocystorhinostomy to bypass the obstruction
Lifelong daily topical antibiotic prophylaxis
stages.explanation: Because acute dacryocystitis in adults reflects an underlying nasolacrimal duct obstruction, the definitive treatment to prevent recurrent infection is dacryocystorhinostomy (DCR), which creates a new drainage pathway from the lacrimal sac into the nasal cavity, bypassing the blocked duct; it is performed electively once the acute inflammation has resolved. Lifelong topical antibiotics do not correct the anatomic obstruction and only delay recurrence. Repeated sac massage is a temporizing infant measure and does not resolve fixed adult obstruction. Punctal plugs deliberately block tear outflow and would worsen stasis and infection—exactly the wrong move. Correcting the outflow obstruction with DCR addresses the root cause and is the standard definitive management after the infection settles.

cases.diagnosis: Acute dacryocystitis from underlying nasolacrimal duct obstruction
pearls: Acute dacryocystitis is a tender, red swelling below the medial canthus with purulent reflux from the punctum, arising from nasolacrimal duct obstruction.
Treat acutely with systemic antibiotics and warm compresses; do NOT probe or irrigate during the acute infection.
Watch for orbital extension—proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, or vision change—especially in diabetics; escalate to IV antibiotics and imaging.
Definitive prevention of recurrence is elective dacryocystorhinostomy after the acute episode resolves.

cases.title: A Boy Who Squints and Sits Close to the Television
cases.chiefComplaint: My son seems to see poorly out of one eye and squints at things far away.
cases.history: A 5-year-old boy is brought by his mother after a failed preschool vision screening, which flagged reduced acuity in the right eye. She has noticed he sits very close to the television and closes or covers his right eye when concentrating. He has never complained of pain, redness, or double vision, and there is no history of eye trauma, eye turning, or drooping eyelids. He was born at term without complications and meets all developmental milestones; he is otherwise thriving. He has never worn glasses and has had no prior eye examination. PMH is unremarkable. Medications: none. Allergies: NKDA. Immunizations are up to date. Social: lives with both parents, attends preschool, no daycare illnesses recently. Family history: his father wore 'thick glasses' as a child and a maternal aunt had a 'lazy eye' treated with a patch. The mother is anxious because she read that vision problems must be treated early and worries they may have waited too long for her son to benefit from any treatment.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, cooperative child. Uncorrected visual acuity 20/30 left eye but only 20/100 right eye, not improving with pinhole on the right beyond 20/80. No strabismus on cover-uncover testing; corneal light reflexes symmetric. Pupils equal, round, reactive with no relative afferent pupillary defect. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Anterior segments clear; conjunctivae white. Red reflex present and symmetric bilaterally with no leukocoria. Fundoscopic exam (dilated) shows normal optic discs and maculae bilaterally with no lesions. Cycloplegic refraction reveals +1.00 D in the left eye and +5.00 D of hyperopia in the right eye.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given unilateral reduced acuity with a normal eye exam and a large interocular refractive difference, what is the most likely underlying mechanism of the visual deficit?
options: Malingering or poor cooperation with the screening test as the first step today
A refractive error that will fully correct to 20/20 with glasses alone
An organic macular or optic nerve lesion in the right eye as the first step today
Cortical visual suppression of the chronically blurred right eye
stages.explanation: The right eye has +5.00 D hyperopia versus +1.00 D on the left, a large anisometropia. During the critical period of visual development, the chronically more-blurred eye is cortically suppressed, producing amblyopia even though the eye is anatomically normal. The normal red reflex, disc, and macula make an organic lesion (A) unlikely. Refraction alone (C) will not immediately restore acuity because the cortical suppression must also be reversed with active therapy; correction is necessary but not sufficient. Malingering (D) is implausible in a cooperative 5-year-old with a clear, reproducible refractive substrate. The absence of strabismus points specifically to anisometropic (refractive) amblyopia rather than strabismic amblyopia.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding would most strongly argue AGAINST a diagnosis of amblyopia and prompt a search for organic pathology instead?
options: A large hyperopic refractive error in the affected eye
Acuity that improves with cycloplegic correction over months
A relative afferent pupillary defect in the worse-seeing eye
Failure of a routine preschool vision screen
stages.explanation: A relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) indicates asymmetric optic nerve or extensive retinal dysfunction and is NOT a feature of pure amblyopia; its presence demands imaging or a retinal workup for organic disease. Improvement with correction over time (A) is expected and supports amblyopia, not against it. A large hyperopic error (C) is the very substrate of refractive/anisometropic amblyopia and supports the diagnosis. A failed preschool screen (D) is the typical way amblyopia is detected and is nonspecific. Because amblyopia is a diagnosis of exclusion of organic disease, an RAPD is the single exam finding that should redirect the workup away from amblyopia.

stages.label: OPTOMETRIC & SENSORY TESTING
stages.content:
Cycloplegic refraction OD: +5.00 D sphere
Cycloplegic refraction OS: +1.00 D sphere
Best-corrected acuity OD with full correction: 20/80
Best-corrected acuity OS with full correction: 20/20
Stereoacuity (Titmus): reduced (200 arcsec)
Cover-uncover test: orthophoric, no manifest or latent deviation
Dilated fundus exam: normal optic nerves and maculae, no organic lesion

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After prescribing full-time spectacle correction of the hyperopia, the right eye remains amblyopic. What is the next best step to treat the amblyopia?
options: Patch the amblyopic right eye to protect it from overuse as initial therapy
Begin part-time occlusion of the better-seeing left eye to force use of the right eye
Observe with annual screening since the critical period has closed as initial therapy
Refer immediately for strabismus surgery as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Once optimal optical correction is in place but amblyopia persists, the standard treatment is to penalize the dominant eye, classically by patching the better-seeing (left) eye, forcing cortical use of the amblyopic right eye; atropine penalization of the good eye is an accepted alternative. Surgery (A) treats misalignment, not amblyopia, and this child is orthophoric. Patching the amblyopic eye (C) is exactly backward and would worsen the deficit. Observation (D) is wrong: at age 5 the critical period (roughly up to 7-10 years for amblyopia) is still open, so active treatment is both indicated and likely effective.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: The mother asks why early treatment matters so much. Which statement best explains the prognostic principle?
options: Amblyopia spontaneously resolves at puberty regardless of intervention as the immediate next step
Treatment is most effective during the critical period of visual cortical plasticity
Amblyopia responds equally well to treatment at any age, so timing is irrelevant as the immediate next step
Once glasses are worn the brain corrects the suppression automatically without further therapy in this setting
stages.explanation: Visual cortical connections are most plastic in early childhood; amblyopia therapy capitalizes on this critical period, and efficacy declines as the child ages, with limited gains expected once plasticity wanes (broadly after about 7-10 years, though some response can occur later). So this 5-year-old has a favorable window. Equal response at any age (A) is false. Glasses alone (C) often do not reverse established suppression, which is why active penalization is added. Spontaneous resolution at puberty (D) does not occur; untreated amblyopia generally persists as permanent vision loss, underscoring the urgency of early detection and treatment.

cases.diagnosis: Anisometropic amblyopia of the right eye due to uncorrected hyperopia
pearls: Amblyopia is reduced best-corrected acuity from abnormal visual development; it is a diagnosis of exclusion once organic disease and an RAPD are ruled out.
Anisometropic amblyopia arises from a large interocular refractive difference; treat by first correcting the refractive error, then penalizing the dominant eye with patching or atropine.
Treatment efficacy depends on the critical period of cortical plasticity, so detection via preschool vision screening and prompt therapy are key.
An RAPD or leukocoria is never explained by amblyopia and mandates a search for organic pathology such as a tumor or retinal lesion.

cases.title: An Infant Whose Eyes Constantly Turn Inward
cases.chiefComplaint: Both of my baby's eyes have been crossing inward since he was very young.
cases.history: An 8-month-old boy is brought by his parents for constant inward crossing of both eyes, present since around 2 months of age and not improving. The crossing is large and obvious in all photographs; the parents say he seems to alternate, sometimes looking with the right eye and sometimes the left, and turns his head to look at objects to the side. He fixes and follows faces, reaches for toys, and has no abnormal eye shaking. There is no eyelid droop, no white pupil reflex in photos, and no excessive tearing. He was born at 39 weeks without complications, has normal growth and development, and no history of prematurity, seizures, or neurologic concerns. PMH unremarkable. Medications: none. Allergies: NKDA. Immunizations current. Social: lives with both parents; no tobacco exposure. Family history: mother had strabismus surgery as a child; no known retinoblastoma or congenital cataracts. The parents have read that crossing 'might go away on its own' and want to know whether their son needs glasses, an operation, or simply time, and whether his depth perception can still develop normally.
cases.exam: Alert, interactive infant. Large-angle constant esotropia (eyes crossed inward) on corneal light reflex, with the reflex displaced temporally bilaterally. He cross-fixates: uses the right eye to view the left visual field and the left eye to view the right field, with apparent limitation of abduction on each side that resolves with the doll's-head maneuver. He alternates fixation freely, suggesting roughly equal vision. No ptosis, no leukocoria; red reflex symmetric. Pupils equal and reactive. No nystagmus. Cycloplegic refraction shows only mild, age-appropriate hyperopia (+1.50 D each eye). Dilated fundus exam normal bilaterally.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The infant appears unable to abduct either eye on direct testing, but full abduction is demonstrated with the doll's-head (oculocephalic) maneuver. What does this finding indicate?
options: Restrictive myopathy from congenital fibrosis of the extraocular muscles
Bilateral sixth nerve palsies requiring urgent neuroimaging as the first step today
Duane retraction syndrome with globe retraction as the first step today
Cross-fixation in large-angle infantile esotropia, with intact abduction
stages.explanation: In large-angle infantile esotropia, the child cross-fixates: each eye is used for the opposite field and is simply never asked to abduct, mimicking an abduction deficit. The doll's-head maneuver drives full abduction, proving the lateral recti and sixth nerves work, so this is pseudo-paralysis, not a true palsy. True bilateral sixth nerve palsies (A) would not abduct even with the oculocephalic maneuver and would prompt imaging. Duane syndrome (C) shows globe retraction and palpebral fissure narrowing on attempted adduction, absent here. Congenital extraocular fibrosis (D) is restrictive with ptosis and limited movement in multiple directions, not seen here.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Cycloplegic refraction shows only +1.50 D of hyperopia in each eye. How does this finding help distinguish the type of esotropia?
options: It indicates the crossing is caused by uncorrected myopia as the primary diagnosis
It confirms refractive accommodative esotropia, which glasses alone will cure
It is irrelevant because refraction does not influence esotropia
It argues against an accommodative component, supporting infantile esotropia
stages.explanation: Accommodative esotropia is driven by significant hyperopia (often +3 to +6 D or more) that forces excessive accommodation and convergence; correcting it with glasses straightens the eyes. This infant has only mild, age-appropriate hyperopia (+1.50 D), so accommodation is not the cause, supporting infantile (congenital) esotropia, where the large constant deviation is present early and is not eliminated by glasses. Option A is wrong because the small refractive error will not be the driver. Myopia (C) is not present and is not a typical cause of esotropia.
