Corneal pachymetry assesses glaucoma risk and corneal disease, not the retina. Color vision testing screens for optic nerve and macular disease and is not the priority in acute floaters. Tonometry alone measures pressure but cannot evaluate the peripheral retina for the tears that this presentation puts at risk.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her careful peripheral exam shows no tear and an attached retina. What is the appropriate management?
options: Start oral prednisone taper as initial therapy
Reassurance with education on retinal detachment warning signs
Pars plana vitrectomy as initial therapy
Immediate laser retinopexy of the posterior pole as monotherapy
stages.explanation: An uncomplicated PVD with no tear on a thorough exam is managed with reassurance, education about warning signs (a sudden increase in floaters, new flashes, or a curtain/shadow in the vision), and instructions to return urgently if these occur, often with a re-examination in a few weeks since some tears develop later. Laser retinopexy is only indicated when an actual tear or hole is found. Pars plana vitrectomy is not warranted for an uncomplicated PVD. Oral prednisone treats inflammatory disease and has no role in a benign vitreous separation.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which new symptom should prompt this patient to return immediately for urgent re-dilation?
options: Watery eyes in the wind with appropriate follow-up
Mild eye strain after reading based on current risk
Gradual fading of the floaters over weeks
A dark curtain or shadow advancing across her vision
stages.explanation: A dark curtain or shadow progressing across the visual field is the classic warning sign of a rhegmatogenous retinal detachment and requires urgent re-examination and likely surgical repair, so it mandates immediate return. Gradual fading of floaters over weeks is the expected benign course of a PVD as the brain neuroadapts and the opacity settles. Mild eye strain after reading reflects accommodative fatigue or uncorrected refractive error, not detachment. Watery eyes in the wind is a benign reflex tearing phenomenon unrelated to retinal pathology.

cases.diagnosis: Acute posterior vitreous detachment (with Weiss ring), no retinal tear
pearls: A Weiss ring is pathognomonic for acute posterior vitreous detachment.
About 10 to 15 percent of symptomatic acute PVDs harbor a retinal tear, so a dilated scleral-depressed peripheral exam is mandatory.
Uncomplicated PVD is managed with reassurance, warning-sign education, and a return precaution.
New worsening floaters, flashes, or an advancing curtain warrant urgent re-examination for retinal tear or detachment.

cases.title: A Persistent Gray Spot in the Center of Vision
cases.chiefComplaint: A gray-black spot blocking the center of vision in the right eye that has worsened over two months.
cases.history: A 67-year-old woman presents with a two-month history of a central gray spot in her right eye that obscures faces and printed words, with associated distortion of straight lines. She first noticed difficulty threading a needle and now cannot read a clock face with the right eye. Peripheral vision is preserved and she denies flashes, a curtain, pain, or redness. She has hypertension and well-controlled hypothyroidism. Medications are amlodipine 5 mg daily and levothyroxine 88 mcg daily. She has no drug allergies. She is a retired seamstress, never smoked, and drinks rarely. Family history is notable for age-related macular degeneration in her mother. She had uncomplicated cataract surgery in both eyes three years ago. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable, with no headaches, weight loss, or other neurologic symptoms. She is anxious about losing her independence and her ability to do fine handwork.
cases.exam: Best-corrected visual acuity is 20/100 OD and 20/25 OS. Pupils are equal and reactive without an afferent pupillary defect. Intraocular pressures are 14 mmHg OD and 15 mmHg OS. Both eyes are pseudophakic with well-positioned posterior chamber intraocular lenses and clear posterior capsules. On dilated fundus exam of the right eye there is a small, round, sharply demarcated reddish lesion at the center of the fovea with a surrounding cuff of subretinal fluid; the foveal reflex is lost. When a thin vertical slit-beam is projected across the fovea, the patient reports a break in the line (positive Watzke-Allen sign). The left macula appears normal with a few small drusen, and both peripheral retinas are attached without tears.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A positive Watzke-Allen sign (a perceived break in a slit-beam centered on the fovea) most strongly suggests which diagnosis?
options: Central serous chorioretinopathy
Dry age-related macular degeneration
Full-thickness macular hole
Epiretinal membrane
stages.explanation: A positive Watzke-Allen sign, where the patient perceives a true break in a slit beam aimed across the fovea, reflects the absence of functioning foveal tissue and is characteristic of a full-thickness macular hole. An epiretinal membrane causes metamorphopsia and a wavy or thickened slit beam, not a clean break. Central serous chorioretinopathy produces a serous neurosensory detachment and may distort the beam but does not create a discrete central gap. Dry AMD causes drusen and geographic atrophy with scotomas but typically does not yield a positive Watzke-Allen sign across the fovea.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which imaging study best confirms the diagnosis and stages this macular lesion?
options: Fundus autofluorescence
Optical coherence tomography of the macula
Electroretinography in this clinical context
B-scan ultrasonography
stages.explanation: Macular OCT is the definitive test for a full-thickness macular hole, directly visualizing the full-thickness foveal defect, cuff of subretinal fluid, intraretinal cysts, and any vitreomacular traction, and it stages the hole and guides surgical planning. Fundus autofluorescence shows a hyperautofluorescent window at the hole but is less precise than OCT for staging and assessing traction. B-scan is used when media opacity blocks the view, which is not the case here. Electroretinography measures global retinal electrical function and is used for diffuse photoreceptor disease, not focal macular holes.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Macular OCT (OD): Full-thickness foveal defect spanning all neurosensory retinal layers
Minimum hole diameter 480 µm
Cuff of subretinal fluid and intraretinal cystoid spaces at the hole margins
Operculum suspended on the partially detached posterior hyaloid; residual vitreomacular adhesion present
Macular OCT (OS): Normal foveal contour with a few small drusen

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this symptomatic full-thickness macular hole with reduced vision, what is the standard definitive treatment?
options: Observation and serial OCT only as initial therapy
Intravitreal anti-VEGF injections as initial therapy
Pars plana vitrectomy with internal limiting membrane peel
Focal laser photocoagulation to seal the hole as monotherapy
stages.explanation: Pars plana vitrectomy with internal limiting membrane peeling and intraocular gas tamponade is the standard treatment for a symptomatic full-thickness macular hole, achieving high closure rates and visual improvement. Observation alone is reasonable only for very small, recent holes or vitreomacular traction that may release spontaneously, not a chronic 480 µm hole with reduced vision. Anti-VEGF treats exudative conditions like neovascular AMD or DME and does not close macular holes. Laser photocoagulation would destroy foveal tissue and is not used to repair macular holes. Pharmacologic vitreolysis (ocriplasmin) is limited to small holes with vitreomacular traction.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which postoperative instruction is most critical to maximize the chance of hole closure after gas tamponade?
options: Avoiding all eye drops for the first week
Strict face-down positioning for the prescribed period
Sleeping flat on the back at all times
Immediate air travel to return home quickly
stages.explanation: Face-down (prone) positioning keeps the intraocular gas bubble pressed against the macula, providing the surface tension that promotes hole closure, so adherence to prescribed prone positioning is critical. Air travel is strictly contraindicated while an intraocular gas bubble is present because reduced cabin pressure causes the bubble to expand, raising intraocular pressure dangerously. Sleeping supine would float the bubble away from the macula and impair closure. Postoperative antibiotic and anti-inflammatory drops are an essential part of care, so avoiding all drops would be harmful and is incorrect.

cases.diagnosis: Full-thickness macular hole (right eye)
pearls: A positive Watzke-Allen sign (perceived break in a foveal slit beam) suggests a full-thickness macular hole.
OCT confirms and stages macular holes by showing the full-thickness defect and any vitreomacular traction.
Vitrectomy with internal limiting membrane peel and gas tamponade is the standard surgical repair.
Strict face-down positioning aids closure, and air travel is contraindicated while an intraocular gas bubble persists.

cases.title: Distorted, Wavy Vision in One Eye
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of mildly blurred and distorted vision in the left eye, with straight lines appearing wavy.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man presents with a 5-month history of gradually distorted vision in his left eye; door frames and the edges of his newspaper columns appear bent or wavy, and there is mild central blur. He denies pain, redness, flashes, floaters, or any curtain over the vision. Symptoms have been slowly stable rather than rapidly progressive. He has hypertension, benign prostatic hyperplasia, and remote cataract surgery in both eyes. Medications are losartan 50 mg daily and tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily. He has no drug allergies. He is a retired accountant, former social smoker who quit decades ago, and drinks one beer most evenings. Family history includes glaucoma in his father. He has no history of ocular trauma, uveitis, or retinal tears. Review of systems is otherwise negative, including no headaches, weight change, or limb weakness. He is functioning well overall but finds the distortion bothersome when reading and doing crossword puzzles.
cases.exam: Best-corrected visual acuity is 20/25 OD and 20/40 OS. Pupils are equal and reactive with no afferent pupillary defect. Intraocular pressures are 16 mmHg bilaterally. Both eyes are pseudophakic with clear capsules. Amsler grid testing of the left eye reveals metamorphopsia (wavy lines) in the central field without a frank scotoma. Dilated fundus exam of the left eye shows a glistening, semi-translucent sheen over the macula with fine retinal striae and mild distortion of the foveal vessels, producing a cellophane-like reflex; there is no subretinal fluid, hemorrhage, or hard exudate. The right macula is normal, and both peripheral retinas are attached without tears or holes.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The glistening cellophane macular reflex with fine retinal striae and metamorphopsia is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Neovascular age-related macular degeneration
Epiretinal membrane
Full-thickness macular hole
Central retinal vein occlusion
stages.explanation: A cellophane macular reflex with fine surface striae and contraction of inner retinal vessels causing metamorphopsia is the classic appearance of an epiretinal membrane (macular pucker). Neovascular AMD typically causes subretinal fluid, hemorrhage, and rapid central distortion or scotoma, not a fine cellophane sheen. Central retinal vein occlusion produces diffuse retinal hemorrhages, dilated tortuous veins, and edema, which are absent here. A full-thickness macular hole gives a central scotoma and a positive Watzke-Allen sign rather than a glistening surface membrane with preserved foveal tissue.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which test best characterizes the membrane and assesses its effect on the underlying retina?
options: Pachymetry as the primary diagnosis
Optical coherence tomography of the macula
Visual evoked potentials
Gonioscopy as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Macular OCT best characterizes an epiretinal membrane, showing a hyperreflective layer on the inner retinal surface, retinal wrinkling, thickening, and any traction or pseudohole, and it correlates structure with symptoms. Gonioscopy evaluates the anterior chamber angle and is irrelevant to a macular surface membrane. Pachymetry measures corneal thickness and has no role here. Visual evoked potentials assess the visual pathway from retina to cortex and are used for optic nerve and neurologic disease, not for imaging a macular membrane.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Macular OCT (OS): Hyperreflective contractile membrane on the inner retinal surface
Loss of the normal foveal depression with retinal thickening
Inner retinal wrinkling and mild traction; no full-thickness defect
No subretinal fluid; ellipsoid zone intact
Central subfield thickness 355 µm

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His symptoms are mild and acuity is 20/40. What is the most appropriate initial management of this epiretinal membrane?
options: Immediate pars plana vitrectomy with membrane peel
Observation with Amsler grid monitoring
Intravitreal anti-VEGF injection as initial therapy
Intravitreal gas injection (pneumatic vitreolysis)
stages.explanation: For an epiretinal membrane causing only mild symptoms and acceptable acuity, observation with Amsler grid self-monitoring and periodic follow-up is appropriate, since many membranes remain stable and surgery carries risks such as cataract and rare retinal complications. Pars plana vitrectomy with membrane peel is reserved for membranes causing significant visual decline or bothersome metamorphopsia, and is offered when symptoms warrant rather than reflexively. Anti-VEGF has no role because an ERM is a fibrocellular membrane, not a neovascular process. Pneumatic vitreolysis is used for vitreomacular traction, not for an established epiretinal membrane.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which development would most appropriately shift management toward surgical membrane peeling?
options: Appearance of a posterior vitreous detachment as the immediate next step
Progressive vision loss to 20/80 with worsening, disabling metamorphopsia
A new small cataract in the fellow eye as the immediate next step
Intraocular pressure rising to 18 mmHg as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Progressive visual decline (e.g., to 20/80) with worsening, functionally disabling metamorphopsia is the principal indication to proceed with vitrectomy and membrane peel, as the potential visual benefit then outweighs surgical risk. An intraocular pressure of 18 mmHg is normal and unrelated to ERM management. A posterior vitreous detachment is common and often coexists with or precedes ERM but does not itself mandate surgery. A new cataract in the fellow eye is managed separately with cataract surgery and does not influence the decision to peel a membrane in this eye.

cases.diagnosis: Epiretinal membrane (macular pucker), left eye
pearls: A cellophane macular reflex with retinal striae and metamorphopsia characterizes epiretinal membrane.
OCT shows a hyperreflective contractile membrane with loss of the foveal contour and retinal thickening.
Mildly symptomatic ERMs are observed with Amsler grid monitoring; surgery is for disabling symptoms or progressive vision loss.
Pars plana vitrectomy with membrane peel is the treatment when symptoms warrant intervention.

cases.title: A Blurry Spot and Dim Vision in a Stressed Executive
cases.chiefComplaint: Three weeks of a blurry, dim spot in the center of vision of the right eye with objects appearing smaller and yellow-tinged.
cases.history: A 40-year-old man presents with three weeks of a central blurry spot in the right eye; he notes that the vision is dim, objects look slightly smaller (micropsia) and have a yellow-brown tint, and straight lines bend toward the center. There is no pain, redness, flashes, or floaters. He is a high-strung corporate executive working 80-hour weeks under intense deadline pressure. He recently completed a 10-day course of oral prednisone and has been using an over-the-counter intranasal corticosteroid for allergic rhinitis, and he occasionally takes a relative's leftover steroid cream for eczema. His medical history is otherwise unremarkable. He has no drug allergies. He drinks three to four cups of coffee daily, sleeps poorly, does not smoke, and drinks alcohol socially. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is negative for headache, weight change, or neurologic symptoms. He describes himself as a perfectionist who has trouble relaxing and is frustrated that the vision is interfering with his presentations.
cases.exam: Best-corrected visual acuity is 20/40 OD (correctable to 20/30 with a small hyperopic shift) and 20/20 OS. Pupils are equal and reactive without an afferent pupillary defect. Intraocular pressures are 15 mmHg bilaterally. Anterior segments are quiet with clear corneas and no cells or flare. Dilated fundus exam of the right eye reveals a round, shallow, dome-shaped elevation of the neurosensory retina at the macula (serous detachment) with a blunted foveal reflex; there is no hemorrhage, hard exudate, or drusen, and the retinal vessels are normal. The left macula is normal. Both peripheral retinas are attached without tears, and there is no vitritis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A young man with a high-stress, type A personality and recent corticosteroid exposure who develops a serous macular detachment most likely has which condition?
options: Neovascular age-related macular degeneration
Posterior uveitis
Optic neuritis in this clinical context
Central serous chorioretinopathy
stages.explanation: Central serous chorioretinopathy classically affects young to middle-aged men with type A personalities, stress, and corticosteroid exposure (oral, inhaled, nasal, or topical), producing a localized serous neurosensory detachment of the macula with micropsia and a hyperopic shift. Optic neuritis causes painful vision loss with an afferent pupillary defect and dyschromatopsia, not a serous macular detachment, and the pupils are equal here. Neovascular AMD occurs in older patients with drusen and subretinal hemorrhage, not in a 40-year-old with a clean macula otherwise. Posterior uveitis would show vitritis and inflammatory signs, which are absent.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: On fluorescein angiography, which finding is most characteristic of this diagnosis?
options: A focal leak that expands upward in a smokestack or inkblot pattern
Petaloid macular leakage (flower-petal pattern)
Lacy choroidal neovascular membrane with late staining
Disc leakage with peripheral vascular sheathing
stages.explanation: A focal point of leakage at the level of the retinal pigment epithelium that expands and ascends in a smokestack (or fills as an inkblot) pattern, pooling within the serous detachment, is the classic angiographic sign of central serous chorioretinopathy. Petaloid macular leakage indicates cystoid macular edema, seen after surgery or with uveitis, not CSCR. A lacy choroidal neovascular membrane points to neovascular AMD or CNV. Disc leakage with peripheral vascular sheathing suggests an inflammatory or vasculitic process such as uveitis or papillitis, not the focal RPE leak of CSCR.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
OCT (OD): Dome-shaped serous neurosensory detachment at the fovea with subretinal fluid
Small pigment epithelial detachment; no intraretinal fluid; no subretinal hyperreflective material
Choroid appears thickened (pachychoroid)
Fluorescein angiography (OD): Single focal hyperfluorescent leak that ascends in a smokestack pattern, pooling in the subretinal space
Fundus autofluorescence: Mild changes at the leak site

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first step in managing this patient's condition?
options: Perform immediate pars plana vitrectomy as initial therapy
Start high-dose oral prednisone as initial therapy
Begin intravitreal anti-VEGF injections as initial therapy
Discontinue all corticosteroids
stages.explanation: Most acute central serous chorioretinopathy resolves spontaneously within a few months, so the key first steps are eliminating all exogenous corticosteroids (a major risk factor) and addressing stress, with observation. Anti-VEGF is not first-line for typical acute CSCR and is reserved for cases complicated by choroidal neovascularization. Starting high-dose oral prednisone is exactly the wrong move, as corticosteroids cause and worsen CSCR. Pars plana vitrectomy plays no role in uncomplicated CSCR. For persistent or chronic cases, focal laser to an extrafoveal leak or photodynamic therapy may be considered after conservative management fails.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If his serous detachment persists beyond about four months despite stopping steroids, which treatment is most appropriate?
options: Scleral buckle surgery as the immediate next step
Lifelong oral corticosteroids as the immediate next step
Reduced-fluence photodynamic therapy
Panretinal photocoagulation as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: For chronic or persistent CSCR (fluid lasting beyond about three to four months), reduced-fluence photodynamic therapy is a well-supported treatment, and focal thermal laser is an option when the leak is discrete and extrafoveal; both aim to resolve the subretinal fluid and protect the photoreceptors. Lifelong oral corticosteroids would perpetuate the disease and are contraindicated. Panretinal photocoagulation treats proliferative ischemic retinopathies, not CSCR. Scleral buckle surgery treats rhegmatogenous retinal detachment and has no role in this serous, non-rhegmatogenous process.

cases.diagnosis: Central serous chorioretinopathy (right eye)
pearls: Central serous chorioretinopathy typically affects young to middle-aged men with type A personalities, stress, and corticosteroid use.
Fluorescein angiography shows a focal RPE leak with a classic smokestack or inkblot pattern; OCT shows subretinal fluid over a thickened (pachy) choroid.
First-line management is stopping all corticosteroids and observing, since most acute cases resolve spontaneously.
Persistent or chronic CSCR is treated with reduced-fluence photodynamic therapy or focal laser to an extrafoveal leak.

cases.title: Trouble Seeing at Night Since Childhood
cases.chiefComplaint: Long-standing difficulty seeing in the dark with recent trouble navigating in dim rooms and bumping into objects at the sides.
cases.history: A 26-year-old man presents with lifelong difficulty seeing in dim light that he says has worsened over the past few years; he stumbles in dark restaurants, cannot find his seat in movie theaters, and increasingly bumps into furniture and door edges at the periphery. His central reading vision remains good in daylight. He denies pain, redness, flashes, or floaters. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. He has no drug allergies. He works as a software developer, does not smoke, and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is significant: a maternal uncle and a maternal grandfather had similar night-blindness and progressive vision loss, while his parents and sisters are unaffected. He is recently engaged and is concerned about passing the condition to future children. Review of systems is negative for hearing loss, polydactyly, neurologic deficits, and systemic symptoms. He has never had this formally evaluated and wants to understand his diagnosis and prognosis.
cases.exam: Best-corrected visual acuity is 20/25 in each eye. Pupils are equal and reactive without an afferent pupillary defect, and color vision is normal. Intraocular pressures are 14 mmHg bilaterally. Anterior segments are clear with early posterior subcapsular lens changes. Dilated fundus exam of both eyes reveals waxy pallor of the optic discs, marked attenuation of the retinal arterioles, and clumps of dark pigment in a bone-spicule configuration scattered through the mid-peripheral retina; the maculae are relatively preserved. Confrontation fields show constricted peripheral vision bilaterally. There is no vitritis or active hemorrhage. The remainder of the neurologic screening exam, including extraocular movements, is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The triad of waxy disc pallor, attenuated arterioles, and mid-peripheral bone-spicule pigmentation with nyctalopia is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Vitamin A deficiency reversible with supplementation
Retinitis pigmentosa
Diabetic retinopathy as the first step today
Choroideremia confined to the macula
stages.explanation: The classic triad of waxy optic disc pallor, attenuated retinal arterioles, and mid-peripheral bone-spicule pigmentation, together with night blindness and progressive peripheral field loss, defines retinitis pigmentosa, a hereditary rod-cone dystrophy. Choroideremia causes diffuse choroidal and RPE atrophy in an X-linked pattern but lacks the typical bone-spicule pattern and waxy disc, and it is not confined to the macula. Vitamin A deficiency can cause nyctalopia but produces a different fundus picture and lacks this familial, progressive bone-spicule pattern. Diabetic retinopathy shows microaneurysms, hemorrhages, and exudates, not this triad.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnostic test is most useful to confirm the rod-cone dystrophy and characterize photoreceptor function?
options: Optical coherence tomography of the optic nerve
B-scan ultrasonography
Full-field electroretinography (ERG)
Fluorescein angiography
stages.explanation: Full-field electroretinography is the key functional test in retinitis pigmentosa, demonstrating markedly reduced or extinguished rod responses early with later cone involvement, confirming a generalized rod-cone dystrophy. Fluorescein angiography can show RPE window defects but does not measure photoreceptor function and is not diagnostic. OCT of the optic nerve assesses the nerve fiber layer and is more relevant to glaucoma or optic neuropathy than to characterizing photoreceptor function. B-scan ultrasonography is used for media opacity or mass lesions and does not evaluate retinal electrical function. Visual field testing and genetic testing are complementary.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTICS
stages.content:
Full-field ERG: Severely reduced scotopic (rod) responses; markedly diminished photopic (cone) responses
Humphrey visual field: Mid-peripheral ring scotoma with constricted residual central island
Macular OCT: Loss of the ellipsoid zone outside the fovea with preserved central foveal photoreceptors
Fundus autofluorescence: Hyperautofluorescent perifoveal ring
Genetic panel pending

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given his pedigree (affected maternal uncle and maternal grandfather, unaffected parents and sisters), which inheritance pattern is most likely and what counseling follows?
options: Mitochondrial; transmitted only through the father as initial therapy
Autosomal recessive; only consanguineous unions are at risk as monotherapy
Autosomal dominant; each child has a 50 percent risk as initial therapy
X-linked recessive
stages.explanation: The pedigree, with affected males connected through unaffected females (maternal uncle and grandfather) and no father-to-son transmission, fits X-linked recessive inheritance. As an affected male, he passes his X chromosome to all daughters (making them obligate carriers) and his Y to all sons (who will be unaffected), so all his daughters are carriers and none of his sons inherit the disease. Autosomal dominant would show affected individuals in every generation including a parent. Autosomal recessive does not require consanguinity and would not show this male-predominant maternal-line pattern. Mitochondrial inheritance is maternally transmitted, not paternal, so it is incorrect.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which intervention is most appropriate to offer this patient as part of comprehensive management?
options: Prophylactic panretinal photocoagulation as the immediate next step
Systemic corticosteroids to slow degeneration as the immediate next step
Genetic testing and counseling, low-vision support, and screening for treatable complications such as cataract and macular edema
High-dose vitamin A palmitate started immediately without monitoring as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Comprehensive RP care centers on genetic testing and counseling (which also informs gene-specific therapies such as voretigene neparvovec for RPE65 disease), low-vision rehabilitation, and monitoring for and treating reversible complications like posterior subcapsular cataract and cystoid macular edema. High-dose vitamin A has limited, debated benefit, requires hepatic monitoring, and is contraindicated in certain genotypes, so it should not be started reflexively without specialist guidance. Panretinal photocoagulation treats ischemic neovascular disease and has no role in RP. Systemic corticosteroids do not slow this degeneration and carry significant risks.

cases.diagnosis: Retinitis pigmentosa (X-linked recessive pattern)
pearls: Retinitis pigmentosa presents with nyctalopia, ring/peripheral field loss, and the triad of waxy disc pallor, attenuated arterioles, and bone-spicule pigmentation.
Full-field ERG confirms a rod-cone dystrophy with reduced scotopic then photopic responses.
X-linked recessive pedigrees show affected males through carrier females with no father-to-son transmission; affected males' daughters are obligate carriers.
Management includes genetic counseling, low-vision support, and treating reversible complications like cataract and cystoid macular edema.

cases.title: An Incidental Finding of Swollen-Looking Optic Nerves
cases.chiefComplaint: Referred after a routine eye exam noted elevated, blurry-margined optic discs in both eyes.
cases.history: A 22-year-old woman is referred to ophthalmology after a routine optometry visit for new glasses noted bilaterally elevated optic discs with indistinct margins, raising concern for swelling. She is asymptomatic: she denies headaches, transient visual obscurations, pulsatile tinnitus, double vision, or nausea, and her vision feels normal. She has no significant medical history. Medications are a combined oral contraceptive pill only; she takes no tetracyclines, vitamin A derivatives, or other implicated drugs. She has no drug allergies. She is a graduate student of average build, does not smoke, and drinks rarely. Family history reveals that her father was also told he had unusual optic nerves but never had problems. Review of systems is negative for weight gain, weakness, numbness, and menstrual changes. She is anxious because the optometrist mentioned the word papilledema and the possibility of a brain problem, and she wants to know whether she needs an emergency scan.
cases.exam: Best-corrected visual acuity is 20/20 in each eye with normal color vision. Pupils are equal and reactive without a relative afferent pupillary defect. Intraocular pressures are 15 mmHg bilaterally. Anterior segments are normal. Dilated fundus exam shows both optic discs elevated with blurred, lumpy (scalloped) margins and an anomalous branching vascular pattern, but there is no disc hyperemia, no peripapillary nerve fiber layer hemorrhage, no cotton-wool spots, no exudates, and no obscuration of vessels crossing the disc; spontaneous venous pulsations are present. The cup is small in both eyes. The maculae and peripheral retinas are normal, and there is no vitritis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which exam feature most argues against true papilledema and in favor of pseudopapilledema in this patient?
options: Visual acuity of 20/20 as the first step today
Use of oral contraceptives as the first step today
Small optic cup as the first step today
Presence of spontaneous venous pulsations with no hemorrhages, exudates, or vessel obscuration
stages.explanation: Spontaneous venous pulsations imply normal intracranial pressure and, combined with the absence of disc hyperemia, peripapillary hemorrhages, cotton-wool spots, exudates, and obscuration of crossing vessels, strongly favor pseudopapilledema (such as optic disc drusen) over true papilledema. Normal visual acuity is reassuring but can also be present early in true papilledema, so it is not specific. Oral contraceptive use is actually a risk factor for idiopathic intracranial hypertension and does not argue against true swelling. A small cup is a feature of buried drusen but is less specific by itself than the venous pulsations and absence of true swelling signs.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which non-invasive imaging test best distinguishes optic disc drusen from true disc edema by exploiting the autofluorescent property of the drusen?
options: Visual evoked potentials
Optical coherence tomography angiography
Fluorescein angiography
Fundus autofluorescence
stages.explanation: Optic disc drusen are calcified, intrinsically autofluorescent deposits, so fundus autofluorescence shows characteristic hyperautofluorescent foci at the disc and reliably distinguishes drusen from true disc edema. Fluorescein angiography can show nodular staining without leakage in drusen versus leakage in true papilledema, but it is invasive and less specific than autofluorescence for this purpose. OCT angiography images vascular flow and is not the primary tool for identifying drusen. Visual evoked potentials assess conduction along the visual pathway and do not differentiate drusen from edema. B-scan ultrasonography is also highly useful, showing calcified hyperechoic foci.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Fundus autofluorescence: Discrete hyperautofluorescent nodular deposits at both optic nerve heads
B-scan ultrasonography: Highly reflective calcified foci at both optic discs that persist at low gain
OCT of optic nerve: Lumpy internal contour with subretinal hyperreflective material; no smooth nerve fiber layer thickening typical of edema
Humphrey visual field: Full fields with an enlarged blind spot OD

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Imaging confirms calcified optic disc drusen and excludes true papilledema. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Urgent lumbar puncture to measure opening pressure as initial therapy
Reassurance with baseline visual fields
Start acetazolamide to lower intracranial pressure as initial therapy
Emergent neurosurgical referral for shunt placement as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Once optic disc drusen are confirmed and true papilledema is excluded, management is reassurance with baseline and periodic visual field testing, because drusen can slowly cause nerve fiber layer and field loss over years that should be monitored. An urgent lumbar puncture is unnecessary once pseudopapilledema is confirmed and intracranial pressure is presumed normal. Acetazolamide lowers intracranial pressure for idiopathic intracranial hypertension, which has been excluded here, so it is not indicated. Emergent neurosurgical referral and shunting are reserved for true raised intracranial pressure with vision-threatening papilledema, not for drusen.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Over long-term follow-up, which complication of optic disc drusen should be monitored for?
options: Rapidly progressive cataract as the leading concern
Corneal neovascularization as the leading concern
Acute angle-closure glaucoma as the leading concern
Progressive visual field defects from nerve fiber layer loss
stages.explanation: The principal long-term concern with optic disc drusen is slowly progressive visual field loss from chronic compression and attrition of the retinal nerve fiber layer, which is why periodic perimetry is recommended; rarely, drusen are also associated with peripapillary choroidal neovascularization and disc hemorrhages. Acute angle-closure glaucoma relates to a narrow anterior chamber angle and is unrelated to disc drusen. Rapidly progressive cataract is not a recognized complication of drusen. Corneal neovascularization arises from corneal hypoxia or inflammation (e.g., contact lens overwear) and has no link to optic disc drusen.

cases.diagnosis: Optic disc drusen causing pseudopapilledema
pearls: Pseudopapilledema from optic disc drusen lacks the hyperemia, hemorrhages, exudates, and vessel obscuration of true papilledema, and spontaneous venous pulsations are often present.
Optic disc drusen are calcified and autofluorescent; fundus autofluorescence and B-scan ultrasonography confirm the diagnosis.
Confirming drusen avoids unnecessary neuroimaging, lumbar puncture, and IIH treatment.
Monitor for slowly progressive visual field loss and the rare complications of peripapillary choroidal neovascularization.

cases.title: Sudden Loss of the Lower Half of Vision on Waking
cases.chiefComplaint: Painless loss of the lower portion of vision in the right eye noticed upon waking this morning.
cases.history: A 61-year-old man presents with painless loss of the inferior half of vision in his right eye that he noticed on waking this morning; the deficit has been stable, not progressive, over several hours. He denies eye pain, pain with eye movement, headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, fevers, weight loss, or proximal muscle aches. His central vision is partly preserved but he cannot see anything below the midline on the right. He has hypertension, type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidemia, and obstructive sleep apnea for which he is poorly adherent to CPAP. Medications are amlodipine, metformin, atorvastatin, and a recently started erectile dysfunction medication (sildenafil) taken last night. He has no drug allergies. He smoked for 25 years and quit recently and drinks moderately. Family history includes coronary artery disease. Review of systems is otherwise negative, specifically no temporal headache or constitutional symptoms suggesting giant cell arteritis. He is alarmed because the visual loss appeared abruptly and overnight.
cases.exam: Best-corrected visual acuity is 20/50 OD and 20/20 OS. There is a right relative afferent pupillary defect. Color vision is mildly reduced in the right eye. Intraocular pressures are 16 mmHg bilaterally. Confrontation and formal fields show a dense inferior altitudinal defect in the right eye respecting the horizontal midline. Anterior segments are normal. Dilated fundus exam of the right eye reveals a diffusely swollen, hyperemic optic disc with blurred margins and a few flame hemorrhages at the disc margin; the disc is small with little or no physiologic cup. The left optic disc is also small and crowded with a tiny cup (a disc at risk) but is not swollen. The maculae and peripheral retinas are normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Painless acute altitudinal field loss with a swollen disc in a small, crowded optic nerve (disc at risk) is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Central retinal artery occlusion
Optic neuritis as the first step today
Arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy from giant cell arteritis
Nonarteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy
stages.explanation: Painless, sudden, altitudinal visual field loss with a swollen hyperemic disc in a structurally crowded small optic nerve (the disc at risk, with the fellow disc also small-cupped) is classic for nonarteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy, especially in a patient with vasculopathic risk factors and nocturnal hypotension factors. Optic neuritis causes pain with eye movement and usually a normal or only mildly swollen disc in a younger patient. Central retinal artery occlusion produces a pale retina with a cherry-red spot and diffuse vision loss, not an altitudinal pattern with a swollen disc. Arteritic AION is possible but is less likely without any GCA symptoms, though it must be excluded.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Despite the absence of classic symptoms, which workup is mandatory in any patient over 50 with anterior ischemic optic neuropathy to exclude the dangerous arteritic cause?
options: Carotid duplex ultrasound as the immediate next step
24-hour ambulatory blood pressure monitoring as the immediate next step
Stat ESR, C-reactive protein,
MRI of the orbits with contrast as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: In any patient over 50 with AION, giant cell arteritis must be excluded urgently because untreated GCA can cause rapid bilateral blindness; the immediate workup is a stat ESR and C-reactive protein (and platelet count), with temporal artery biopsy if clinical or laboratory suspicion persists, and empiric high-dose corticosteroids if GCA is suspected. MRI of the orbits is useful for optic neuritis or compressive lesions, not for diagnosing GCA. Carotid duplex evaluates embolic sources for retinal artery occlusion and amaurosis, not arteritis. Ambulatory blood pressure monitoring addresses vasculopathic risk later but does not exclude the emergent arteritic cause.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
ESR 12 mm/hr (normal for age)
C-reactive protein 0.4 mg/dL (normal)
Platelets 245 ×10³/µL (normal)
HbA1c 7.8%
LDL cholesterol 118 mg/dL
No temporal artery tenderness; no jaw claudication

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the normal inflammatory markers and absence of GCA features, which finding here best supports nonarteritic rather than arteritic disease?
options: Markedly elevated platelet count as the primary diagnosis
A chalky-white, pallid swollen disc as the primary diagnosis
Visual acuity of count fingers or worse as the primary diagnosis
A hyperemic swollen disc in a small, crowded nerve with normal ESR
stages.explanation: Nonarteritic AION typically shows a hyperemic (not pallid) swollen disc occurring in a structurally small, crowded optic nerve, with normal inflammatory markers, which together support the nonarteritic diagnosis here. A chalky-white, pallid swollen disc and markedly elevated ESR, CRP, or platelets point toward arteritic AION from giant cell arteritis. Severe vision loss to count fingers or worse is more characteristic of arteritic disease, whereas nonarteritic AION often spares central acuity somewhat, as in this patient. An elevated platelet count is an inflammatory marker favoring GCA, so it would argue against the nonarteritic cause.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For confirmed nonarteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy, what is the most appropriate management approach?
options: Risk-factor modification with no proven acute therapy
Immediate high-dose intravenous corticosteroids as initial therapy
Emergent ocular massage and anterior chamber paracentesis as initial therapy
Intravitreal anti-VEGF injection as initial therapy
stages.explanation: There is no proven acute treatment that restores vision in nonarteritic AION; management focuses on optimizing vasculopathic risk factors (blood pressure, diabetes, lipids, smoking, and treating obstructive sleep apnea), avoiding nocturnal hypotension and PDE5 inhibitors that may precipitate episodes, and protecting the fellow eye, which is at elevated future risk. High-dose corticosteroids are the treatment for arteritic AION (GCA), not the nonarteritic form. Ocular massage and paracentesis are emergency maneuvers for central retinal artery occlusion, not AION. Intravitreal anti-VEGF treats exudative retinal disease and has no established role in nonarteritic AION.

cases.diagnosis: Nonarteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (right eye)
pearls: Nonarteritic AION causes painless, sudden altitudinal field loss with a hyperemic swollen disc in a small, crowded disc at risk.
Always exclude giant cell arteritis in patients over 50 with AION using stat ESR, CRP, and platelets, with biopsy if suspicion remains.
Pallid disc swelling, severe vision loss, and elevated inflammatory markers suggest the arteritic form needing urgent high-dose steroids.
Nonarteritic AION has no proven acute therapy; manage vasculopathic risk factors and avoid nocturnal hypotension and PDE5 inhibitors.

cases.title: Bumping Into Door Frames and Fading Side Vision
cases.chiefComplaint: Gradually worsening peripheral vision with frequent collisions into door frames and objects on both sides.
cases.history: A 48-year-old man presents with about a year of gradually worsening side vision; he repeatedly bumps into door frames, sideswipes his car mirrors, and startles when people approach from the side. He has also noticed mild central blur. He denies pain, redness, flashes, or floaters. On further questioning he reports several months of decreased libido, occasional headaches, and increased fatigue, and his wife notes he seems less interested in intimacy. He has hypertension treated with lisinopril 10 mg daily and no other medical problems. He has no drug allergies. He is a married accountant, does not smoke, and drinks alcohol socially. Family history is unremarkable. Review of systems is positive for low energy and reduced shaving frequency; he denies galactorrhea, cold intolerance, or polyuria. He is concerned because the side-vision problem is affecting his driving and he has nearly walked into people on the sidewalk who he simply did not see coming from the sides.
cases.exam: Best-corrected visual acuity is 20/25 in each eye. Pupils are equal and reactive without a relative afferent pupillary defect; color vision is mildly reduced. Intraocular pressures are 15 mmHg bilaterally. Confrontation testing reveals loss of the temporal fields in both eyes, and red desaturation is noted straddling the vertical midline. Anterior segments are normal. Dilated fundus exam shows mild bilateral temporal optic disc pallor with otherwise normal maculae, vessels, and peripheral retinas. Extraocular movements are full with no diplopia or ptosis. The remainder of the cranial nerve examination is normal, and there is no proptosis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Bilateral temporal field loss respecting the vertical midline (bitemporal hemianopia) localizes the lesion to which structure?
options: A single optic nerve
The optic chiasm
The occipital cortex
The right optic tract
stages.explanation: A bitemporal hemianopia that respects the vertical midline localizes to the optic chiasm, where the nasal retinal fibers (subserving the temporal visual fields) cross and are selectively compressed, classically by a lesion from below or above the sella. A single optic nerve lesion causes monocular vision loss, not a bilateral pattern. An optic tract lesion produces a contralateral homonymous (not bitemporal) hemianopia. Occipital cortex lesions cause homonymous hemianopias, often with macular sparing, again not the bitemporal pattern that points specifically to chiasmal compression.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given chiasmal compression with the associated systemic symptoms, what is the most appropriate next diagnostic test?
options: Carotid duplex ultrasound as the primary diagnosis
Lumbar puncture as the primary diagnosis
MRI of the brain and pituitary with and without contrast
CT head without contrast as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: MRI of the brain and pituitary region with and without contrast is the test of choice to image the sella and chiasm, characterizing a pituitary adenoma or other parasellar mass compressing the chiasm with superior soft-tissue resolution. Non-contrast CT head can miss soft-tissue sellar detail and is far inferior for pituitary imaging, though it may show gross masses or calcification. Carotid duplex evaluates extracranial carotid stenosis and is irrelevant to chiasmal compression. Lumbar puncture assesses cerebrospinal fluid and intracranial pressure and is not indicated and could be hazardous in the setting of a mass lesion.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
MRI sella with contrast: 1.8 cm sellar mass with suprasellar extension elevating and compressing the optic chiasm (macroadenoma)
Humphrey visual field: Dense bitemporal hemianopia respecting the vertical midline
Prolactin 38 ng/mL (mildly elevated)
Morning testosterone 180 ng/dL (low)
TSH 1.8 µIU/mL; free T4 0.9 ng/dL
Morning cortisol 14 µg/dL; IGF-1 normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: His prolactin is only mildly elevated (38 ng/mL) despite a large macroadenoma. How is this best interpreted?
options: It rules out any pituitary tumor as the primary diagnosis
It likely reflects the stalk effect from a nonfunctioning adenoma, since a true macroprolactinoma usually causes markedly higher prolactin
It confirms a true prolactin-secreting macroprolactinoma that will respond to dopamine agonists alone as the primary diagnosis
It indicates primary hypothyroidism as the cause as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: A large macroadenoma producing only a mildly elevated prolactin (typically under about 100 to 200 ng/mL) suggests the stalk effect, where the mass disrupts dopaminergic inhibition of normal lactotrophs rather than secreting prolactin itself, pointing to a nonfunctioning adenoma. A true prolactin-secreting macroprolactinoma of this size usually causes markedly higher prolactin (often in the hundreds or thousands), so a dopamine-agonist-only strategy is not assured here. A mildly elevated prolactin does not rule out a tumor; the MRI confirms one. Primary hypothyroidism can mildly raise prolactin, but his TSH and free T4 are normal, excluding it.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this nonfunctioning pituitary macroadenoma compressing the chiasm with progressive bitemporal field loss, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Observation with repeat MRI in one year as initial therapy
Dopamine agonist (cabergoline) as definitive monotherapy for this mass as initial therapy
Primary whole-brain radiation therapy as initial therapy
Transsphenoidal surgical resection , reserving dopamine agonist therapy for confirmed prolactinomas
stages.explanation: A nonfunctioning macroadenoma causing chiasmal compression with progressive visual field loss is a surgical indication, and transsphenoidal resection (with perioperative endocrine evaluation and replacement of any deficient hormones) is the treatment of choice to decompress the chiasm and preserve vision. Observation is inappropriate when vision is actively threatened. Whole-brain radiation is not first-line and is reserved as adjuvant therapy for residual or recurrent tumor. Dopamine agonists are definitive only for true prolactinomas; given the stalk-effect pattern suggesting a nonfunctioning adenoma, relying on cabergoline alone would risk continued compression and vision loss.

cases.diagnosis: Nonfunctioning pituitary macroadenoma compressing the optic chiasm (bitemporal hemianopia)
pearls: Bitemporal hemianopia respecting the vertical midline localizes to the optic chiasm, classically from a pituitary mass.
MRI of the brain and pituitary with and without contrast is the imaging study of choice for sellar lesions.
A large adenoma with only mildly elevated prolactin suggests the stalk effect of a nonfunctioning adenoma, not a macroprolactinoma.
Chiasmal compression with progressive visual field loss warrants transsphenoidal surgery, while true prolactinomas are treated medically with dopamine agonists.

cases.title: Bumping Into Things on One Side
cases.chiefComplaint: She keeps bumping into door frames and people on her right side since waking up two days ago.
cases.history: A 66-year-old woman is admitted to the medical ward after sudden onset of difficulty seeing to her right. On waking two days ago she found she could not see objects on the right, bumped her right shoulder into a doorway, and missed food on the right side of her plate. She denies eye pain, redness, diplopia, headache, weakness, or speech difficulty. Vision in each eye individually feels normal to her. PMH: hypertension, type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.1%), hyperlipidemia, and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation for which she admits poor adherence to apixaban 5 mg twice daily. Other meds: amlodipine 10 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 40 mg nightly. Allergies: none. Social: retired teacher, never smoked, rare alcohol, lives with her husband. Family history: mother had stroke at 70. Review of systems otherwise unremarkable; no recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. She is right-handed and was fully independent at baseline.
cases.exam: Alert, fully oriented, fluent speech, no neglect on line bisection. Visual acuity 20/25 OD and 20/25 OS with correction. Pupils equal and reactive, no relative afferent pupillary defect. Extraocular movements full; no nystagmus. Confrontation fields reveal a dense right homonymous visual field loss in both eyes that respects the vertical meridian. Fundoscopy: sharp disc margins, no hemorrhage or papilledema bilaterally. No ptosis or anisocoria. Cranial nerves otherwise intact. Motor 5/5 throughout, sensation intact, no dysmetria, gait not formally tested. Carotid auscultation without bruits; irregularly irregular pulse.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: Her field defect is a right homonymous hemianopia that respects the vertical midline, with normal pupils, no RAPD, and normal optic discs. Where is the lesion most likely located?
options: Optic chiasm as the immediate next step
Both retinas as the immediate next step
Left retrochiasmal visual pathway
Right optic nerve as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: A homonymous hemianopia (same-side field loss in both eyes) localizes to the retrochiasmal pathway contralateral to the deficit; a RIGHT field defect implies a LEFT-sided lesion, so the left optic tract, radiations, or occipital cortex is correct. A right optic nerve lesion would cause monocular vision loss with a RAPD in that eye, not a binocular homonymous defect. A chiasmal lesion classically produces a bitemporal hemianopia (heteronymous), not homonymous. Bilateral retinal disease would not respect the vertical meridian and would show fundus abnormalities. The preserved acuity, normal pupils, absence of RAPD, and normal discs all point away from anterior visual pathway pathology and toward a retrochiasmal cortical or radiation lesion.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given her acute presentation, atrial fibrillation, and missed apixaban doses, what is the most appropriate immediate diagnostic step?
options: Urgent neuroimaging of the brain (CT/CTA or MRI)
Lumbar puncture for cerebrospinal fluid analysis
Outpatient referral to neuro-ophthalmology in 4 weeks
Temporal artery biopsy as the first step today
stages.explanation: An acute homonymous hemianopia in a patient with vascular risk factors and atrial fibrillation is a stroke until proven otherwise; urgent neuroimaging (noncontrast CT to exclude hemorrhage, often with CTA, or MRI with DWI for ischemia) is the immediate step to localize and characterize the lesion. Delaying with an outpatient referral risks missing a treatable, evolving stroke and secondary-prevention opportunities. Lumbar puncture is not indicated for an isolated homonymous field defect and could be hazardous if there is a mass or hemorrhage. Temporal artery biopsy targets giant cell arteritis, which causes anterior ischemic optic neuropathy or monocular loss with elevated inflammatory markers, not an isolated congruous homonymous hemianopia.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Noncontrast head CT: no hemorrhage
MRI brain DWI: acute infarct in the LEFT occipital lobe (medial, calcarine territory)
MRA/CTA: occlusion of the left posterior cerebral artery (PCA)
ECG: atrial fibrillation, ventricular rate 88
Formal Humphrey perimetry: dense right homonymous hemianopia with a small spared central island (macular sparing)
LDL-C 138 mg/dL
INR 1.1

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Perimetry shows a congruous right homonymous hemianopia with macular sparing, and MRI confirms a left medial occipital (calcarine) infarct. What explains the macular sparing?
options: Crossing of macular fibers at the lateral geniculate nucleus
Sparing of the optic radiations in Meyer's loop as the primary diagnosis
Bilateral or dual blood supply to the occipital pole representing the macula
Compensatory eye movements during testing as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Macular (central) vision is represented at the occipital pole, which frequently receives collateral supply from the middle cerebral artery in addition to the PCA; in a PCA infarct this dual supply can preserve the macular representation, producing macular sparing. Congruity (near-identical defects in both eyes) is characteristic of occipital, rather than optic tract, lesions. There is no decussation of macular fibers at the lateral geniculate nucleus that would explain sparing. Meyer's loop carries inferior radiation fibers producing superior 'pie-in-the-sky' quadrantanopias, not central sparing. Macular sparing is a true anatomic/vascular phenomenon, not a testing artifact; fixation losses are monitored and excluded during reliable perimetry.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her infarct is attributed to cardioembolism from atrial fibrillation with subtherapeutic anticoagulation. For long-term secondary stroke prevention, what is the most appropriate strategy?
options: No antithrombotic therapy; treat blood pressure only as initial therapy
Aspirin monotherapy 81 mg daily as initial therapy
Resume and optimize therapeutic oral anticoagulation with adherence support
Dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin and clopidogrel indefinitely
stages.explanation: For cardioembolic stroke due to nonvalvular atrial fibrillation, therapeutic oral anticoagulation (a DOAC such as apixaban, or warfarin) is the evidence-based secondary prevention; her event occurred while subtherapeutic, so resuming and ensuring adherence is key, along with risk-factor control. Aspirin monotherapy is inferior to anticoagulation for AF-related cardioembolism. Indefinite dual antiplatelet therapy is not the standard for AF and raises bleeding risk without the embolic protection anticoagulation provides. Withholding antithrombotic therapy leaves her at high recurrent-stroke risk (elevated CHA2DS2-VASc). Adjunctive measures include intensive blood pressure and LDL lowering, glycemic control, and low-vision rehabilitation with scanning training and discussion of driving restrictions given the hemianopia.

cases.diagnosis: Congruous right homonymous hemianopia with macular sparing due to a left occipital (calcarine) infarct from cardioembolic PCA occlusion
pearls: Homonymous hemianopia localizes to the retrochiasmal pathway on the side opposite the field loss.
Congruity increases as the lesion moves posteriorly; occipital lesions produce the most congruous defects.
Macular sparing reflects dual MCA/PCA blood supply to the occipital pole and points to an occipital localization.
An acute homonymous field defect with vascular risk factors is a stroke until proven otherwise—image urgently and address secondary prevention, including driving and low-vision rehab.

cases.title: Red, Gritty Eyes Worse Every Morning
cases.chiefComplaint: He has months of red, irritated, mucousy eyes that are always worst when he wakes up.
cases.history: A 53-year-old man presents with about eight months of bilateral ocular redness, foreign-body sensation, and stringy mucous discharge that is reliably worst on waking and improves through the day. He has been treated with several courses of topical antibiotics and lubricants by his primary doctor with only transient relief. He notes the symptoms are sometimes worse on whichever side he slept on. He denies pain, photophobia, or vision loss. PMH: obesity (BMI 38), hypertension, type 2 diabetes, gastroesophageal reflux, and loud snoring with witnessed apneas; his wife says he stops breathing at night and he reports daytime sleepiness (Epworth 16). He has never had a sleep study. Meds: lisinopril 20 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, omeprazole 20 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: long-haul truck driver, 20-pack-year smoker, drinks alcohol on weekends, sleeps on his stomach or side. Family history: father with sleep apnea and heart disease. He has fallen asleep at the wheel once, prompting concern.
cases.exam: Visual acuity 20/25 OU. External exam: rubbery, lax upper eyelids that evert spontaneously with minimal upward traction, and a soft, easily folded upper tarsus. Lashes point in a slightly ptotic, downward direction (lash ptosis). Upper palpebral conjunctiva shows a velvety papillary reaction with mild mucoid discharge bilaterally. Cornea: scattered inferior punctate epithelial erosions staining with fluorescein, no infiltrate or ulcer. No preauricular lymphadenopathy. Anterior chambers quiet, no cells. Lids close fully; Bell's phenomenon intact. No proptosis, no thyroid stare. Remainder of the slit-lamp exam unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given chronic morning-predominant papillary conjunctivitis that failed antibiotics, with strikingly lax eyelids that evert with minimal traction, what is the single most useful bedside maneuver to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: Easy upper-eyelid eversion / distraction test demonstrating lid laxity
Schirmer tear test as the first step today
Intraocular pressure measurement as the first step today
Conjunctival swab for bacterial culture as the first step today
stages.explanation: Floppy eyelid syndrome is defined by markedly lax, rubbery upper tarsal plates that evert spontaneously or with minimal upward traction; demonstrating this easy eversion/distraction is the most specific bedside confirmation and links the morning symptoms to nocturnal lid eversion against the pillow. A Schirmer test assesses aqueous tear production and may be normal or mildly reduced but does not establish the diagnosis. Repeat bacterial culture is low yield in a condition that already failed antibiotics and is mechanical, not infectious. Intraocular pressure is relevant for glaucoma screening but is unrelated to this lid-laxity syndrome. The papillary reaction here is a chronic mechanical/inflammatory response to lid eversion during sleep.

stages.label: ASSOCIATION
stages.question: Floppy eyelid syndrome has a strong, clinically important systemic association that this patient's history suggests. What is it?
options: Hyperthyroidism (Graves disease)
Obstructive sleep apnea
Multiple sclerosis in this setting
Rheumatoid arthritis
stages.explanation: Floppy eyelid syndrome is strongly associated with obstructive sleep apnea—this patient has obesity, loud snoring, witnessed apneas, an Epworth score of 16, and daytime sleepiness with a near-miss driving event. Recognizing FES should prompt screening for OSA, which carries cardiovascular and accident risk. Rheumatoid arthritis is linked to keratoconjunctivitis sicca and scleritis, not lid laxity. Graves disease causes proptosis, lid retraction, and a thyroid stare, the opposite of the lash ptosis and floppy lids seen here, and his exam shows no thyroid signs. Multiple sclerosis causes optic neuritis and internuclear ophthalmoplegia, unrelated to eyelid laxity. The OSA association makes the diagnosis systemically significant.

stages.label: SLEEP STUDY
stages.content:
In-laboratory polysomnography:
Apnea-Hypopnea Index (AHI) 42 events/hour (severe OSA)
Lowest oxygen saturation (nadir) 78%
Total sleep time 6.2 h
Predominantly obstructive events, worse in supine position and REM
BMI 38 kg/m²
Epworth Sleepiness Scale 16/24

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Polysomnography confirms severe OSA (AHI 42). Beyond ocular lubrication, what management approach best addresses BOTH his ocular and systemic disease?
options: Refer immediately for eyelid-tightening surgery before any sleep treatment
Topical antibiotic-steroid drops indefinitely as initial therapy
Initiate CPAP for OSA plus ocular protective measures
Oral doxycycline alone as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Management of FES is twofold: treat the eye and treat the associated OSA. CPAP addresses the severe OSA (AHI 42, nadir 78%), reducing cardiovascular and motor-vehicle risk, and may improve symptoms. Ocular care includes aggressive lubrication, nighttime lid taping or a shield to prevent nocturnal eversion against the pillow, and sleeping supine; surgical horizontal lid tightening (e.g., full-thickness wedge) is reserved for refractory cases. Indefinite antibiotic-steroid drops risk steroid complications and ignore the mechanical and systemic problems. Rushing to surgery before treating OSA neglects the dominant systemic risk and may be unnecessary if conservative measures and CPAP help. Doxycycline targets meibomian gland dysfunction/rosacea and does not address lid laxity or OSA.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If his lid laxity and nocturnal exposure go untreated, which sight-threatening ocular complication is he most at risk of developing?
options: Exposure/punctate keratopathy progressing to corneal ulceration
Acute angle-closure glaucoma as the leading concern
Central retinal vein occlusion as the leading concern
Cataract as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Chronic nocturnal lid eversion and exposure cause progressive corneal surface disease—punctate epithelial erosions that can advance to persistent epithelial defects, ulceration, neovascularization, scarring, and even keratoconus-like ectasia associated with FES; these are the sight-threatening risks of leaving the laxity untreated. Acute angle-closure glaucoma results from a crowded anterior chamber angle and is unrelated to eyelid laxity. Central retinal vein occlusion is a retinal vascular event tied to hypertension/hyperviscosity, not lid mechanics. Cataract is age- and metabolic-related and not a direct consequence of lid laxity or exposure. Protecting the cornea (lubrication, nighttime shielding, lid tightening when needed) prevents this complication.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: He is a long-haul truck driver with severe untreated OSA (AHI 42) and a prior near-miss falling asleep at the wheel. Beyond eye care, what is an essential safety and disposition consideration?
options: Recommend he simply drink caffeine before long drives with appropriate follow-up
No restrictions are necessary once lubricating drops are started with appropriate follow-up
Clear him immediately for unrestricted commercial driving with appropriate follow-up
Counsel him on the high motor-vehicle-crash risk of untreated OSA, urge prompt CPAP initiation,
stages.explanation: Severe untreated OSA markedly increases motor-vehicle-crash risk, and as a commercial driver with a prior near-miss he must be counseled on this danger, encouraged to start CPAP promptly, and advised about commercial-driver medical fitness and reporting/certification requirements that govern untreated OSA—this protects him and the public. Saying no restrictions are needed once drops are started ignores the dominant systemic and public-safety risk, which lubricants do nothing to address. Immediately clearing him for unrestricted commercial driving before OSA is treated is unsafe and inappropriate. Caffeine is not a substitute for treating severe OSA and does not mitigate the crash risk. Documentation, sleep-medicine follow-up, and confirming CPAP adherence are part of responsible disposition.

cases.diagnosis: Floppy eyelid syndrome with chronic mechanical papillary conjunctivitis and exposure keratopathy, associated with severe obstructive sleep apnea
pearls: Suspect floppy eyelid syndrome in an overweight patient with chronic, antibiotic-refractory, morning-worse conjunctivitis and easily everted, rubbery upper lids.
FES is strongly linked to obstructive sleep apnea—diagnosing FES should trigger OSA screening (and vice versa).
Treatment is twofold: protect the ocular surface (lubrication, nighttime shielding, lid tightening if refractory) and treat OSA with CPAP.
Untreated nocturnal exposure can progress from punctate keratopathy to corneal ulceration and ectatic change.

cases.title: Watery, Scratchy Eye With Turned-In Lid
cases.chiefComplaint: Her right eye feels scratchy and waters constantly, and it looks like the lower lid is rolling inward.
cases.history: A 79-year-old woman presents with several weeks of right-eye foreign-body sensation, tearing, and redness. She feels as if there is sand or an eyelash rubbing against her eye, especially when she forcefully closes the lids or squints. She has tried over-the-counter artificial tears with minimal relief and has been pulling the lower lid down with her finger for temporary comfort. She denies trauma, prior eye surgery, purulent discharge, or significant vision change other than intermittent blurring from tearing. PMH: hypertension, osteoarthritis, and well-controlled hypothyroidism. Meds: amlodipine 5 mg daily, levothyroxine 75 mcg daily, acetaminophen as needed. Allergies: none. Social: widowed, lives alone, independent, never smoked, no alcohol. Family history: noncontributory. She has noticed her skin and tissues 'getting looser' with age. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable; no symptoms of dry mouth, joint inflammation, or rash to suggest a systemic autoimmune cause.
cases.exam: Visual acuity 20/30 OD (improves to 20/25 after blinking), 20/25 OS. Right lower eyelid margin is rotated inward so the lashes contact the globe; the lashes abrade the inferior cornea and conjunctiva. Forced lid closure exaggerates the inward turning. Slit lamp: inferior corneal punctate epithelial erosions staining with fluorescein, conjunctival injection, and reflex tearing; no corneal ulcer or infiltrate. Lid skin is normal in position (not scarred or contracted), and there is no eyelid mass or notching. Left eye lid position normal. Anterior chamber quiet; no discharge. Horizontal lid laxity is evident on distraction testing.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: An inward-rotated lower eyelid margin with lashes rubbing the cornea, worsened by forced lid closure, in an elderly patient is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Blepharitis in this clinical context
Entropion
Trichiasis without lid malposition
Ectropion as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Entropion is inward rotation of the eyelid margin so that the lashes and keratinized skin abrade the cornea and conjunctiva; in this elderly woman it is worsened by forced lid closure (orbicularis override) and produces the foreign-body sensation, tearing, and inferior punctate keratopathy seen here. Ectropion is the opposite—an outward-turned lid—causing exposure and epiphora but not lashes rubbing the globe. Isolated trichiasis is misdirected lashes with a normally positioned lid margin; here the entire margin is rotated inward, so the malposition is the primary problem. Blepharitis causes lid-margin inflammation and crusting but does not rotate the margin inward. The lid-margin rotation defines entropion.

stages.label: MECHANISM
stages.question: This is the most common form of lower-lid entropion in older adults. What is the underlying involutional mechanism?
options: Cicatricial shortening of the posterior lamella from scarring in this setting
Horizontal lid laxity, disinsertion of lower-lid retractors,
Congenital absence of the inferior tarsus as the immediate next step
Acute spasm from a seventh-nerve palsy as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Involutional (age-related) entropion results from a combination of horizontal eyelid laxity, attenuation or disinsertion of the lower-lid retractors, and override of the preseptal orbicularis over the pretarsal portion, allowing the margin to rotate inward—exactly the lax, normally-skinned lid seen here without scarring. Cicatricial entropion is caused by conjunctival/tarsal scarring (e.g., trachoma, Stevens-Johnson, ocular cicatricial pemphigoid) that shortens the posterior lamella; her conjunctiva is not scarred. Congenital entropion is rare and presents in infancy. Seventh-nerve palsy causes orbicularis weakness leading to ectropion and lagophthalmos, not entropion. The triad of laxity, retractor disinsertion, and orbicularis override is the classic involutional mechanism.

stages.label: SLIT-LAMP FINDINGS
stages.content:
Fluorescein staining: confluent inferior corneal punctate epithelial erosions, OD
No corneal ulcer, infiltrate, or stromal thinning
Lid distraction test: >8 mm horizontal laxity, lower lid OD
Lower-lid retractor function: attenuated (reduced lower-lid excursion on downgaze)
Conjunctiva: injected, no scarring or symblepharon
Intraocular pressure: 15 mmHg OD, 14 mmHg OS

stages.label: INITIAL MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While arranging definitive care, which measure provides immediate symptom relief and protects the cornea?
options: Patching the eye tightly for a week as the immediate next step
Oral antibiotics as the immediate next step
Topical glaucoma drops as the immediate next step
Lubricating ointment
stages.explanation: Temporizing measures aim to protect the cornea and rotate the margin outward: copious lubrication (drops and ointment) plus taping the lid to evert the margin relieves the lash-cornea contact, and temporary everting (Quickert) sutures can buy time until definitive surgery. Topical glaucoma drops have no role—intraocular pressure is not the problem. Oral antibiotics are unnecessary in the absence of infection; the keratopathy is mechanical. Tight patching can worsen orbicularis-driven inward rotation and risks abrasion; it does not address the malposition. Definitive correction is surgical lid tightening (e.g., lateral tarsal strip) with retractor reattachment, but lubrication and everting measures relieve symptoms in the interim.

stages.label: DEFINITIVE TREATMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive treatment for her involutional lower-lid entropion?
options: Long-term artificial tears alone as the immediate next step
Botulinum toxin to the levator muscle as the immediate next step
Permanent lid taping as the immediate next step
Surgical lid repair addressing horizontal laxity
stages.explanation: Definitive treatment of involutional entropion is surgical, correcting the underlying anatomy: horizontal lid tightening (commonly a lateral tarsal strip) combined with reattachment/plication of the lower-lid retractors restores normal margin position and resolves the lash-cornea abrasion. Artificial tears alone manage symptoms but do not correct the malposition and allow ongoing keratopathy. Botulinum toxin to the levator would cause ptosis and does not fix laxity; small doses of toxin to the orbicularis are only an occasional temporizing option, not definitive repair. Permanent taping is impractical and does not restore anatomy. Surgery offers durable correction and prevents progression to corneal scarring or ulceration.

cases.diagnosis: Involutional lower-eyelid entropion with mechanical (lash-induced) keratopathy
pearls: Entropion = inward-turned lid margin; lashes abrade the cornea, causing foreign-body sensation, tearing, and inferior punctate keratopathy.
Involutional entropion stems from horizontal lid laxity, retractor disinsertion, and orbicularis override—worsened by forced lid closure.
Distinguish from cicatricial entropion (posterior-lamella scarring) and from isolated trichiasis (normal lid position).
Temporize with lubrication and everting tape/sutures; definitive care is surgical lid tightening with retractor reattachment.

cases.title: Constant Tearing and a Sagging Lower Lid
cases.chiefComplaint: His left eye waters down his cheek all day and the lower lid looks like it is hanging away from the eye.
cases.history: An 81-year-old man reports months of constant tearing from the left eye that runs down his cheek and forces him to dab it with a tissue. He also describes a chronically red, irritated eye that feels dry and exposed, especially overnight, despite the excessive tearing. He has noticed the lower lid appears to droop outward and that the pink inner surface is visible. He denies pain, purulent discharge, or sudden vision loss. PMH: hypertension, benign prostatic hyperplasia, prior basal cell skin cancers on the face treated years ago, and significant chronic sun exposure as a farmer. No prior facial palsy. Meds: amlodipine 5 mg daily, tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: retired farmer with extensive sun exposure, former smoker (quit 20 years ago), occasional alcohol. Family history: noncontributory. He frequently rubs and wipes the eye downward, which he thinks may be making it worse. There is no history of facial weakness, asymmetric smile, or trauma.
cases.exam: Visual acuity 20/30 OD, 20/30 OS. Left lower eyelid margin is rotated outward and sags away from the globe, exposing palpebral conjunctiva that is chronically reddened and keratinized; the inferior punctum is everted and not in contact with the tear lake. Slit lamp: inferior corneal exposure with punctate epithelial erosions staining inferiorly, conjunctival injection; no ulcer. Snap-back test of the left lower lid is markedly delayed—the lid does not return to the globe promptly after distraction. Facial nerve function intact, symmetric smile and forehead, normal eyelid closure strength. No lid mass, notch, or scar tethering the lid downward. Right eye normal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: An outward-turned lower lid with an everted punctum, exposed keratinized conjunctiva, tearing, and a markedly delayed snap-back test is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Dacryocystitis
Allergic conjunctivitis
Ectropion
Entropion as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Ectropion is outward turning of the eyelid margin away from the globe; it exposes the palpebral conjunctiva (which becomes red and keratinized), everts the punctum so tears cannot drain (causing epiphora), and produces exposure keratopathy—precisely this picture, confirmed by a markedly delayed snap-back test indicating lid laxity. Entropion is the opposite, an inward-turned margin with lashes rubbing the eye. Dacryocystitis is infection/obstruction of the lacrimal sac, presenting with a tender, swollen medial-canthal mass and discharge, not lid eversion. Allergic conjunctivitis causes bilateral itching, chemosis, and papillae without lid malposition. The everted punctum and exposure findings define ectropion.

stages.label: MECHANISM
stages.question: His snap-back test is markedly delayed and there is no facial palsy or scar. Which mechanism best explains his ectropion, and what does the snap-back test assess?
options: Mechanical: a lid tumor pulling the lid down; snap-back assesses pupil reactivity
Paralytic: seventh-nerve palsy; snap-back assesses corneal sensation in this setting
Cicatricial: vertical skin shortening; snap-back assesses tear production
Involutional: age-related horizontal lid laxity
stages.explanation: The most common ectropion in the elderly is involutional, driven by age-related horizontal eyelid and canthal tendon laxity; the snap-back test specifically assesses lid laxity/tone—pulling the lid away from the globe and timing its return, a markedly delayed return indicates poor tone and confirms involutional laxity.
