Cicatricial ectropion is from anterior-lamella (skin) shortening due to scarring or actinic damage and shows a tethering scar; the snap-back does not measure tear production. Paralytic ectropion follows seventh-nerve palsy with orbicularis weakness, but his facial function is intact, and snap-back does not test corneal sensation. A mechanical tumor would be visible on the lid, and snap-back does not assess pupils. Snap-back = laxity test.

stages.label: EXAM FINDINGS
stages.content:
Snap-back test, left lower lid: markedly delayed return (>5 seconds, does not reappose to globe)
Lid distraction: >9 mm horizontal laxity
Medial canthal tendon: lax (punctum displaces laterally on traction)
Fluorescein: inferior corneal punctate epithelial erosions, no ulcer
Facial nerve (CN VII): intact, symmetric, full lid closure
No anterior-lamella scar or skin shortening

stages.label: INITIAL MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Pending definitive repair, what is the best interim management to protect his ocular surface?
options: Aggressive lubrication with artificial tears by day
Atropine drops to reduce tearing as the immediate next step
Topical antibiotic drops four times daily for a month before discharge planning
Bandage contact lens with no lubrication as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Until surgical correction, the priority is protecting the exposed cornea and conjunctiva: frequent artificial tears during the day, lubricating ointment at night, and horizontal taping of the lid toward the globe to reduce exposure and improve tear drainage. Topical antibiotics are unwarranted without infection and do not address exposure. A bandage contact lens without lubrication risks worsening a dry, exposed surface and infection. Atropine is an anticholinergic mydriatic/cycloplegic that does not treat the epiphora of ectropion (which is from punctal malposition/overflow, not overproduction) and could blur vision and raise angle-closure risk. Lubrication plus taping is the appropriate bridge to definitive surgery.

stages.label: DEFINITIVE TREATMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive treatment for his involutional lower-lid ectropion?
options: Oral steroids as the immediate next step
Horizontal lid-tightening surgery to address canthal/lid laxity
Punctal plugs in the everted punctum as the immediate next step
Lifelong lubricants only as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Definitive treatment of involutional ectropion is surgical horizontal lid tightening—most commonly a lateral tarsal strip procedure—to correct the canthal tendon and lid laxity and reappose the margin and punctum to the globe, resolving both exposure and epiphora. Lifelong lubricants only manage symptoms and allow ongoing exposure keratopathy. Punctal plugs would worsen tearing by further blocking an already malpositioned drainage system and do not correct lid position. Oral steroids have no role in a mechanical, age-related laxity problem and carry systemic risks. Surgery restores normal anatomy and is the standard of care; medial spindle or retractor procedures may be added for punctal eversion.

cases.diagnosis: Involutional lower-eyelid ectropion with epiphora and exposure keratopathy
pearls: Ectropion = outward-turned lid with everted punctum → epiphora (overflow tearing) plus exposure keratopathy.
The snap-back test assesses horizontal lid laxity; a delayed return supports involutional ectropion.
Classify ectropion as involutional, cicatricial (skin shortening), paralytic (CN VII palsy), or mechanical—management differs by cause.
Bridge with lubrication and taping; definitive treatment is horizontal lid tightening such as a lateral tarsal strip.

cases.title: Drooping Eyelid That Worsened Gradually
cases.chiefComplaint: Her upper eyelids have slowly drooped over the past few years and now block the top of her vision.
cases.history: A 75-year-old woman reports several years of gradually drooping upper eyelids, right worse than left, with no diurnal fluctuation. She notices she must tip her head back and raise her brows to read and that the droop blocks the superior part of her vision while driving. She denies double vision, difficulty swallowing, limb weakness, fatigue that worsens through the day, or pain. There is no history of trauma, prior eyelid surgery, or recent illness. She has worn rigid contact lenses for decades. PMH: hypertension, osteoporosis, and cataract surgery in both eyes two years ago. Meds: hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily, alendronate weekly, calcium/vitamin D. Allergies: sulfa (rash). Social: retired, never smoked, social alcohol, lives with spouse. Family history: noncontributory. She specifically denies the eyelid being worse at night or after prolonged reading, and her smile, speech, and breathing are normal. No headaches, no pupil changes noticed, and no recent weight loss.
cases.exam: Visual acuity 20/25 OU. Bilateral upper-eyelid ptosis, right more than left; margin-reflex distance-1 (MRD1) is 0.5 mm OD and 1.5 mm OS (normal ~4 mm). The upper-eyelid creases are abnormally HIGH and the upper lids are thinned. Levator function is GOOD (excursion 13 mm OD, 14 mm OS). Pupils equal, round, reactive; no anisocoria. Extraocular movements full with no fatigability on sustained upgaze (no increase in ptosis after 30 seconds). No proptosis, no orbicularis weakness, normal facial strength, intact eyelid closure. No lid lag, no Cogan's twitch elicited. Anterior segments show clear corneas and posterior-chamber intraocular lenses.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which combination of examination findings most strongly favors an APONEUROTIC cause of her ptosis?
options: High lid crease with GOOD levator function
Low lid crease with poor levator function
Ptosis that worsens with sustained upgaze and fluctuates
Ptosis with anisocoria and an abnormal pupil
stages.explanation: Aponeurotic (involutional) ptosis results from dehiscence or stretching of the levator aponeurosis; the hallmark is a HIGH (elevated) eyelid crease with preserved, GOOD levator function and a thinned lid—exactly her findings—because the muscle still works but its tendon insertion has slipped. A LOW crease with poor levator function suggests a congenital/myogenic ptosis where the muscle itself is dysplastic. Ptosis that fatigues with sustained upgaze and fluctuates points to myasthenia gravis (neuromuscular junction). Ptosis with anisocoria suggests a neurogenic cause—Horner syndrome (miosis) or a third-nerve palsy (mydriasis). Her good levator function, high crease, normal pupils, and absence of fatigability localize the problem to the aponeurosis.

stages.label: DIFFERENTIAL
stages.question: To exclude myasthenia gravis as a cause of acquired ptosis, which bedside finding would specifically suggest myasthenia rather than aponeurotic ptosis?
options: Good levator excursion of 13 mm as the immediate next step
Stable ptosis present equally all day for years before discharge planning
Fatigable ptosis worsening with sustained upgaze, with Cogan's lid twitch
A high, well-defined lid crease as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Myasthenia gravis is a neuromuscular-junction disorder producing FATIGABLE, variable ptosis that worsens with sustained upgaze, often with a Cogan's lid twitch (overshoot of the lid on refixation from downgaze to primary) and variable diplopia—features absent in this patient, who has stable, non-fatigable ptosis. A high, well-defined crease and good levator excursion are typical of aponeurotic ptosis, not myasthenia. Ptosis that is stable and unchanging for years argues AGAINST myasthenia, which characteristically fluctuates. Her exam (no fatigability, no diplopia, no Cogan's twitch, years of stable progression) makes myasthenia unlikely and supports an aponeurotic mechanism; if any doubt existed, ice-pack testing and acetylcholine-receptor antibodies could be checked.

stages.label: MEASUREMENTS & WORKUP
stages.content:
Margin-reflex distance-1 (MRD1): 0.5 mm OD, 1.5 mm OS (normal ~4-5 mm)
Levator function (excursion): 13 mm OD, 14 mm OS (good; normal >12 mm)
Upper-lid crease height: elevated (~12 mm) bilaterally
Ice-pack test: no improvement in lid height
Acetylcholine-receptor antibody: negative
Visual field (taped vs untaped): superior field improves by 24% with lid elevation

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Considering her long history of rigid contact lens wear and prior cataract surgery, what is the most likely specific etiology of her aponeurotic ptosis?
options: Chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia as the primary diagnosis
Levator aponeurosis dehiscence/stretching related to aging, chronic contact lens wear,
Third-nerve palsy as the primary diagnosis
Horner syndrome from an apical lung tumor as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Aponeurotic ptosis is typically involutional but is accelerated by chronic rigid contact lens wear and prior intraocular surgery (e.g., cataract extraction), both of which mechanically stretch or disinsert the levator aponeurosis—she has decades of contact lens use and recent cataract surgery, fitting this etiology. A third-nerve palsy would cause ptosis with a 'down-and-out' eye and often a dilated pupil; her motility and pupils are normal. Chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia produces slowly progressive bilateral ptosis with PROGRESSIVE limitation of eye movements (ophthalmoplegia), which she lacks. Horner syndrome causes mild ptosis with MIOSIS and anhidrosis; her pupils are equal. The aponeurotic features plus her risk factors make aponeurotic dehiscence the diagnosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has functionally significant superior visual field obstruction. With good levator function and confirmed aponeurotic ptosis, what is the definitive treatment?
options: Pyridostigmine therapy as initial therapy
Observation with no intervention indefinitely as initial therapy
Frontalis sling procedure as initial therapy
Levator aponeurosis advancement/repair
stages.explanation: With GOOD levator function, the definitive repair for aponeurotic ptosis is levator aponeurosis advancement/reinsertion (external approach), or a posterior Müller muscle–conjunctival resection in selected cases; this directly corrects the dehisced tendon and is highly effective. A frontalis sling is reserved for ptosis with POOR levator function (e.g., severe congenital ptosis), where the lid is suspended to the brow—inappropriate when the levator works well. Pyridostigmine treats myasthenia gravis, which she does not have. Indefinite observation is reasonable only if ptosis is mild and non-functional, but she has documented superior field obstruction affecting driving and reading, making surgical repair appropriate. Visual-field testing documenting obstruction often supports medical necessity.

cases.diagnosis: Aponeurotic (involutional) ptosis from levator aponeurosis dehiscence, with good levator function
pearls: Aponeurotic ptosis: HIGH lid crease + GOOD levator function + thinned lid; accelerated by aging, contact lens wear, and prior eye surgery.
Distinguish from neurogenic (anisocoria—Horner miosis or CN III mydriasis) and myogenic/NMJ (fatigable, Cogan's twitch—myasthenia) causes.
Stable, non-fatigable, years-long ptosis argues against myasthenia; check ice-pack test/AChR antibodies only if fatigability is present.
Repair with levator advancement (good levator function); reserve frontalis slings for poor levator function.

cases.title: A Slow-Growing Bump on the Lower Lid
cases.chiefComplaint: He has a painless bump on his lower eyelid that has slowly grown over the past year and occasionally bleeds.
cases.history: A 68-year-old man presents with a slowly enlarging nodule on his right lower eyelid present for about a year. It is painless but bleeds slightly when he scratches it and has not healed. He has noticed loss of eyelashes over the area. He denies vision change, double vision, or pain. He has a long history of outdoor work and minimal sun protection, and prior facial 'spots' frozen by his dermatologist. PMH: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, actinic keratoses, and a prior squamous cell carcinoma on the ear. Meds: amlodipine 5 mg daily, atorvastatin 20 mg nightly. Allergies: none. Social: retired construction worker, fair-skinned, 30-pack-year smoker (quit 10 years ago), moderate alcohol. Family history: father had several skin cancers. He reports the lesion never fully scabs over and the central area sometimes ulcerates. No fevers, weight loss, or lymph node swelling. He has not sought care because it was painless and slow growing, but his wife urged him to come in after noticing it enlarging.
cases.exam: Visual acuity 20/25 OU. Right lower eyelid: a 6 mm firm, pearly, dome-shaped nodule near the lid margin with raised rolled borders, fine overlying telangiectatic vessels, and a small central ulceration. There is localized loss of eyelashes (madarosis) over the lesion and subtle architectural distortion of the lid margin. No tenderness. No regional preauricular or submandibular lymphadenopathy. The remainder of the eyelids and ocular surface are normal; cornea clear, anterior chamber quiet. Extraocular movements full, globe well positioned (no proptosis). The lesion is mobile over deeper tissue without obvious bony fixation.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A pearly, dome-shaped lower-lid nodule with rolled borders, fine telangiectasias, central ulceration, and madarosis in a fair-skinned, sun-exposed older man is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Basal cell carcinoma
Squamous cell carcinoma
Molluscum contagiosum
Chalazion as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Basal cell carcinoma is the most common eyelid malignancy and classically presents as a pearly/translucent nodule with rolled, raised borders, surface telangiectasia, and central ulceration; loss of lashes (madarosis) and lid-margin distortion are red flags for malignancy. It favors the lower lid and medial canthus and arises in fair-skinned, chronically sun-exposed patients. A chalazion is a non-tender or mildly tender lipogranuloma that lacks telangiectasia, ulceration, and madarosis and typically resolves or recurs without destroying lash follicles. Squamous cell carcinoma is possible but is usually more keratotic/scaly and less 'pearly'; it is far less common than BCC on the lids. Molluscum produces small umbilicated papules, often multiple, without rolled pearly borders or madarosis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to establish the diagnosis?
options: Topical antibiotic ointment as the first step today
Empiric warm compresses and reassess in 3 months
Cryotherapy of the lesion without biopsy as the first step today
Incisional or excisional biopsy for histopathologic confirmation
stages.explanation: Any eyelid lesion with malignant features—pearly nodule, telangiectasia, ulceration, madarosis, or lid-margin distortion—requires tissue diagnosis; an incisional (or excisional) biopsy provides histopathologic confirmation and subtype, guiding definitive treatment. Warm compresses are appropriate for a chalazion but would dangerously delay diagnosis of a malignancy with red-flag features. Topical antibiotics treat infection, not neoplasm. Cryotherapy without a tissue diagnosis is inappropriate for a suspected eyelid carcinoma because it neither confirms the diagnosis nor ensures clear margins and can obscure subsequent staging; margin-controlled surgical excision is preferred. Biopsy first, then definitive margin-controlled excision, is the correct sequence.

stages.label: PATHOLOGY
stages.content:
Incisional biopsy, right lower eyelid:
Histology: nests of basaloid cells with peripheral palisading and retraction (clefting) artifact from surrounding stroma
Subtype: nodular basal cell carcinoma
No perineural invasion identified on this sample
Margins: not assessable on incisional sample (definitive excision pending)
No features of sebaceous or squamous differentiation

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Biopsy confirms nodular basal cell carcinoma at the eyelid margin. What is the preferred definitive treatment to maximize cure while sparing tissue near the lid margin?
options: Topical imiquimod alone as initial therapy
Primary radiotherapy as first-line for all cases as initial therapy
Mohs micrographic surgery followed by oculoplastic reconstruction
Wide local excision with 1 cm margins and no margin control as monotherapy
stages.explanation: For eyelid BCC—especially margin-involving or functionally critical periocular tissue—Mohs micrographic surgery offers the highest cure rate with maximal tissue conservation via complete margin control, followed by oculoplastic reconstruction to restore lid function and protect the globe. Wide excision with arbitrary 1 cm margins sacrifices unnecessary eyelid tissue and lacks the comprehensive margin assessment Mohs provides in this cosmetically and functionally vital area. Primary radiotherapy is reserved for nonsurgical candidates or as adjuvant therapy (e.g., perineural invasion), not routine first-line, and carries ocular-surface and lacrimal toxicity. Topical imiquimod may be considered for small superficial BCCs off the lid margin but is inadequate for a nodular margin lesion with destructive features.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most important prognostic/behavioral feature of eyelid basal cell carcinoma the patient should understand?
options: It is locally invasive and destructive but rarely metastasizes
It commonly metastasizes early to the lungs as the immediate next step
It typically regresses spontaneously without treatment as the immediate next step
It is highly responsive to systemic chemotherapy as first-line therapy in this setting
stages.explanation: Basal cell carcinoma is locally invasive and tissue-destructive but only rarely metastasizes; the principal risks are local recurrence and, if neglected, deep invasion into the orbit, sinuses, or bone—hence the emphasis on complete margin-controlled excision and surveillance for recurrence and new lesions. It does not commonly metastasize early to the lungs (that behavior would be atypical and suggests another diagnosis). BCC does not reliably regress spontaneously; untreated lesions enlarge and invade. Systemic chemotherapy is not first-line; advanced/inoperable or metastatic BCC may be treated with hedgehog pathway inhibitors (e.g., vismodegib), but the overwhelming majority are cured by surgery. Sun protection and skin surveillance are essential given his field cancerization.

cases.diagnosis: Nodular basal cell carcinoma of the lower eyelid
pearls: BCC is the most common eyelid malignancy—pearly nodule, rolled borders, telangiectasia, ulceration, and madarosis are red flags; it favors the lower lid/medial canthus.
Any eyelid lesion with malignant features needs a biopsy; do not treat as a chalazion.
BCC is locally destructive but rarely metastasizes—Mohs micrographic surgery gives the best cure with tissue preservation, followed by oculoplastic reconstruction.
Loss of lashes (madarosis) and lid-margin distortion strongly suggest malignancy over a benign lesion.

cases.title: A Stye That Keeps Coming Back
cases.chiefComplaint: She has had a recurring lump on the same upper eyelid that keeps coming back despite drainage, now with redness and lash loss.
cases.history: A 72-year-old woman is referred for a 'chalazion' on her right upper eyelid that has recurred three times over 10 months despite warm compresses, two incision-and-curettage procedures, and topical antibiotics. The lump keeps returning in the same location, and she has now developed a chronically red, irritated upper lid with thickening and loss of eyelashes over the area. She also reports a separate area of chronic redness along the lid margin that her optometrist treated as 'blepharitis/conjunctivitis' without improvement. She denies pain or vision loss. PMH: hypertension, breast cancer treated 15 years ago, and hyperlipidemia. Meds: amlodipine 5 mg daily, atorvastatin 20 mg nightly. Allergies: none. Social: retired, never smoked, rare alcohol, lives with spouse. Family history: mother with various cancers. No fevers or weight loss. She is frustrated that multiple treatments have failed and the lid keeps changing. No prior radiation. The lash loss and unilateral, recurrent, fixed location of the lesion concern the referring physician.
cases.exam: Visual acuity 20/30 OD, 20/25 OS. Right upper eyelid: a firm, yellowish, indurated nodule within the tarsus with overlying lid thickening and localized loss of eyelashes (madarosis). The lid margin shows irregular thickening and a velvety, reddened area of palpebral conjunctiva extending diffusely (unilateral chronic 'blepharoconjunctivitis'). No discrete pearly border or telangiectasia. Mild preauricular fullness on the right. Cornea clear, anterior chamber quiet. The contralateral eye and lid are normal. The recurrent, unilateral, fixed lesion with madarosis and a masquerading chronic conjunctivitis is highly atypical for a benign chalazion.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A 'chalazion' that recurs in the same spot, with madarosis, lid thickening, and a unilateral chronic blepharoconjunctivitis, raises concern for malignancy. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Prolonged topical steroid drops as the first step today
A third incision and curettage with warm compresses
Full-thickness incisional biopsy of the lesion
Reassurance and repeat antibiotics as the first step today
stages.explanation: A recurrent, fixed-location 'chalazion' with madarosis, lid thickening, and unilateral chronic blepharoconjunctivitis is the classic masquerade of sebaceous gland carcinoma; the next step is a full-thickness incisional biopsy of the nodule, with map (conjunctival) biopsies to detect pagetoid spread. Repeating incision and curettage again would once more miss the diagnosis and delay cancer care. Prolonged topical steroids would not treat a malignancy and could worsen an undiagnosed neoplasm or surface disease. Reassurance and repeat antibiotics ignore the multiple red flags. The teaching point: any atypical or recurrent chalazion in an older patient should be biopsied, and the pathologist alerted to consider sebaceous carcinoma with appropriate lipid stains.

stages.label: PATHOLOGY DETAIL
stages.question: To confirm sebaceous gland carcinoma and characterize its spread, which pathology techniques are most useful?
options: Gram stain and bacterial culture as the immediate next step
Flow cytometry for lymphoma markers as the immediate next step
Lipid stains and immunostains
Acid-fast staining as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Sebaceous carcinoma shows lipid-laden, vacuolated malignant cells; lipid stains such as Oil Red O require fresh (non-formalin) tissue, and immunohistochemistry (adipophilin, EMA, often negative BerEP4 unlike BCC) supports the diagnosis. Map (multiple conjunctival) biopsies are essential because sebaceous carcinoma characteristically exhibits intraepithelial pagetoid spread that defines extent and surgical planning. Gram stain and culture target infection, not malignancy. Acid-fast staining is for mycobacteria. Flow cytometry is used for hematologic malignancies/lymphoma, not an epithelial sebaceous tumor. Alerting the pathologist to send fresh tissue for lipid stains and to evaluate for pagetoid spread is key to avoiding a missed or underestimated diagnosis.

stages.label: PATHOLOGY
stages.content:
Incisional biopsy, right upper eyelid (fresh tissue for lipid stains):
Malignant cells with foamy, lipid-laden vacuolated cytoplasm and high nuclear grade
Oil Red O: positive intracytoplasmic lipid
Immunohistochemistry: adipophilin positive, EMA positive, BerEP4 negative
Conjunctival map biopsies: pagetoid (intraepithelial) spread present in two quadrants
Diagnosis: sebaceous gland carcinoma with pagetoid conjunctival involvement

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Pathology confirms sebaceous gland carcinoma with pagetoid conjunctival spread. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Simple shave excision of the nodule only as initial therapy
Warm compresses and observation as initial therapy
Wide surgical excision with margin control, map-directed treatment of pagetoid spread ,
Topical antibiotic-steroid drops as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Sebaceous carcinoma is aggressive and can metastasize, so treatment is wide surgical excision with margin control (frozen-section or Mohs-type assessment), management of pagetoid intraepithelial spread mapped on biopsy (adjunctive cryotherapy or topical mitomycin C to the involved conjunctiva), and evaluation of regional (preauricular/parotid, submandibular, cervical) lymph nodes—sentinel node biopsy may be considered. Simple shave excision leaves disease and ignores multifocal spread. Warm compresses/observation are dangerously inadequate for a malignancy with nodal-metastasis potential. Topical antibiotic-steroid drops do not treat cancer. Because of mortality risk and metastatic potential, multidisciplinary oculoplastics/oncology care with systemic staging is warranted; orbital exenteration is reserved for extensive disease.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Compared with eyelid basal cell carcinoma, what is true of sebaceous gland carcinoma?
options: It is more indolent and never metastasizes as the immediate next step
It only occurs in young patients as the immediate next step
It is best treated with observation as the immediate next step
It is more aggressive, can metastasize to regional lymph nodes
stages.explanation: Sebaceous gland carcinoma is more aggressive than BCC: it can spread via pagetoid intraepithelial extension, metastasize to regional lymph nodes (preauricular, parotid, submandibular, cervical) and distantly, and carries meaningful mortality, with delayed diagnosis (from its chalazion/blepharitis masquerade) worsening outcomes. It is not indolent and does metastasize, so observation is inappropriate and dangerous. While more common in older adults (often women, upper lid), it is not restricted to the young. Long-term surveillance for local recurrence and nodal disease is essential, and any history that raises Muir-Torre syndrome should prompt evaluation for visceral malignancies and mismatch-repair defects.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: She has a personal history of breast cancer and a family history of multiple cancers, now with a sebaceous neoplasm. Which syndrome should be considered, and what evaluation follows?
options: Muir-Torre syndrome
Marfan syndrome; obtain an echocardiogram as the immediate next step
No syndrome consideration is warranted as the immediate next step
Neurofibromatosis type 1; obtain a brain MRI as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Sebaceous neoplasms—especially in a patient with personal and family histories of multiple cancers—should raise Muir-Torre syndrome, a variant of Lynch syndrome caused by DNA mismatch-repair gene defects; appropriate steps are immunohistochemistry/microsatellite testing for MMR deficiency on the tumor, genetic counseling, and heightened visceral cancer surveillance (notably colorectal and genitourinary). Neurofibromatosis type 1 features neurofibromas, café-au-lait macules, and Lisch nodules, not sebaceous carcinoma, so a brain MRI is not the indicated workup. Marfan syndrome involves ectopia lentis and aortic disease, unrelated to sebaceous tumors, making echocardiography irrelevant here. Dismissing syndrome consideration would miss a hereditary cancer predisposition with important screening implications for the patient and relatives.

cases.diagnosis: Sebaceous gland carcinoma of the eyelid (chalazion masquerade) with pagetoid conjunctival spread
pearls: A recurrent, unilateral, fixed-location 'chalazion' with madarosis and chronic blepharoconjunctivitis is sebaceous carcinoma until proven otherwise—biopsy it.
Send FRESH tissue for lipid stains (Oil Red O) and use map biopsies to detect pagetoid intraepithelial spread.
Sebaceous carcinoma is aggressive: wide margin-controlled excision, treat pagetoid spread, and evaluate regional nodes.
Consider Muir-Torre syndrome (sebaceous neoplasms + visceral cancers, mismatch-repair defects) in the right clinical context.

cases.title: A Fleshy Spot on the White of the Eye
cases.chiefComplaint: He noticed a slowly growing fleshy, whitish patch at the edge of the colored part of his eye with irritation and redness.
cases.history: A 64-year-old man presents with a several-month history of a slowly enlarging, fleshy lesion at the temporal limbus of his right eye, with foreign-body sensation, redness, and tearing. He denies significant pain or vision loss. He spent decades working outdoors with heavy ultraviolet exposure and rarely wore sunglasses. PMH: hypertension; he was diagnosed with HIV 8 years ago but has been inconsistent with antiretroviral therapy and missed recent clinic visits. He also has a history of smoking and prior treated condyloma. Meds: lisinopril 20 mg daily; antiretroviral therapy (reports frequent nonadherence). Allergies: none. Social: former outdoor laborer, 25-pack-year smoker, multiple prior sexual partners, occasional alcohol. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems notable for occasional thrush and recent unintentional weight loss. He has no prior eye surgery. The combination of chronic UV exposure, smoking, and immunosuppression from poorly controlled HIV are all relevant risk factors for ocular surface neoplasia.
cases.exam: Visual acuity 20/25 OD, 20/20 OS. Right eye: a gelatinous, slightly elevated lesion at the temporal limbus with a leukoplakic (white, keratinized) surface and prominent, dilated 'feeder' conjunctival vessels supplying it; the lesion encroaches onto the peripheral cornea with a frosted, grayish epithelial sheet. Rose bengal and fluorescein highlight the abnormal epithelium. No intraocular inflammation; anterior chamber quiet, cornea otherwise clear centrally. No proptosis, normal motility. No preauricular lymphadenopathy. Left eye normal. The lesion is mobile over the sclera, suggesting it is not deeply fixed, but its limbal/corneal extension is concerning.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A gelatinous, leukoplakic limbal lesion with prominent feeder vessels and a grayish corneal epithelial sheet in a sun-exposed, immunocompromised man is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Pterygium as the primary diagnosis
Ocular surface squamous neoplasia
Pinguecula in this clinical context
Episcleritis in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Ocular surface squamous neoplasia is a spectrum from conjunctival/corneal intraepithelial neoplasia to invasive squamous cell carcinoma; it classically appears as a gelatinous, papilliform, or leukoplakic limbal lesion with prominent feeder vessels and an opalescent/grayish sheet of abnormal epithelium creeping onto the cornea—exactly this presentation. A pterygium is a fibrovascular, wing-shaped growth that is not gelatinous or leukoplakic and lacks the abnormal keratinized epithelial sheet; however, atypical or rapidly growing 'pterygia' should raise suspicion for OSSN. A pinguecula is a small yellowish conjunctival deposit that does not invade the cornea. Episcleritis is a self-limited sectoral scleral surface inflammation without a mass or leukoplakia. UV exposure, smoking, and HIV are major OSSN risk factors.

stages.label: ASSOCIATION
stages.question: OSSN, particularly in younger or atypical patients or when aggressive, is strongly associated with which systemic condition that this patient has?
options: Hypothyroidism in this setting
HIV/immunosuppression
Rheumatoid arthritis
Type 1 diabetes in this setting
stages.explanation: OSSN is strongly associated with HIV/immunosuppression (and with HPV and chronic UV exposure); in HIV-endemic regions OSSN occurs at younger ages and behaves more aggressively, and a new or aggressive OSSN can be a sentinel sign prompting HIV testing. This patient has known, poorly controlled HIV with weight loss and thrush, indicating significant immunosuppression. Type 1 diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, and hypothyroidism are not established risk factors for OSSN. Recognizing the HIV link should prompt re-engagement in HIV care, adherence counseling, CD4/viral load assessment, and evaluation for other AIDS-associated conditions, as immune reconstitution improves both ocular and systemic outcomes.

stages.label: LABS & DIAGNOSTICS
stages.content:
CD4 count 180 cells/µL
HIV-1 RNA viral load 95,000 copies/mL (poorly controlled)
Anterior-segment OCT (high-resolution): abrupt transition to thickened, hyperreflective epithelium with intact basement membrane plane
Impression-cytology/biopsy: dysplastic squamous epithelial cells with full-thickness atypia, consistent with OSSN (carcinoma in situ)
No scleral invasion seen on imaging

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Biopsy confirms OSSN (carcinoma in situ) without scleral invasion. What is an appropriate definitive management approach for the ocular lesion?
options: Surgical excision with wide margins using a 'no-touch' technique plus cryotherapy to the margins, and/or topical chemotherapy
Observation alone as initial therapy
Topical antibiotics as initial therapy
Immediate orbital exenteration as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Localized OSSN is treated by complete excision with wide margins using a 'no-touch' technique to avoid seeding, combined with cryotherapy to the conjunctival margins; topical chemotherapy (interferon alfa-2b, 5-fluorouracil, or mitomycin C) is used alone for diffuse disease or as adjuvant therapy to reduce recurrence, and alcohol epitheliectomy clears corneal involvement. Observation alone risks progression to invasive carcinoma. Orbital exenteration is reserved for extensive intraocular/orbital invasion, not in-situ disease without scleral invasion. Topical antibiotics do not treat neoplasia. Crucially, managing the systemic disease—restarting effective antiretroviral therapy to improve immune function—reduces recurrence and complements local therapy.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Beyond treating the eye lesion, what is the most important systemic action given his labs (CD4 180, viral load 95,000)?
options: Begin lifelong oral antibiotics with appropriate follow-up
No systemic action needed with appropriate follow-up
Re-engage him in HIV care to restart/optimize antiretroviral therapy
Start systemic chemotherapy for the eye lesion with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: His CD4 of 180 and viral load of 95,000 indicate poorly controlled HIV; re-engaging him in care to restart and optimize antiretroviral therapy with adherence support is the most important systemic action—immune reconstitution lowers OSSN recurrence risk and addresses his broader AIDS-related risks, and opportunistic-infection prophylaxis (e.g., for Pneumocystis at CD4 <200) is indicated. Doing nothing systemically ignores a treatable driver of his neoplasia and overall health. Systemic chemotherapy is not the treatment for localized in-situ OSSN, which is managed locally. Lifelong oral antibiotics are not indicated; targeted prophylaxis is guided by CD4 count. Coordinated ophthalmology and HIV-care follow-up optimizes both ocular and systemic outcomes.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: After successful treatment of his OSSN, what is the most appropriate long-term follow-up plan?
options: Repeat full excision every 3 months regardless of findings as the immediate next step
No further ophthalmic follow-up is required as the immediate next step
Annual chest CT to screen for lung metastases as the immediate next step
Regular ophthalmic surveillance for local recurrence given OSSN's notable recurrence rate, plus ongoing HIV care
stages.explanation: OSSN has a meaningful local recurrence rate, so long-term ophthalmic surveillance—periodic slit-lamp exams with documentation/photography to detect early recurrence—is essential, alongside continued HIV care (immune reconstitution lowers recurrence) and UV protection with sunglasses/hats to reduce ongoing carcinogenic exposure. Stating no follow-up is needed ignores the recurrence risk and the patient's immunosuppression. Annual chest CT is not indicated: OSSN tends to recur locally and spread to regional nodes rather than commonly metastasizing to the lungs, and routine chest CT screening is not standard. Repeating full excision every 3 months irrespective of findings is unnecessary, harmful surgery; re-treatment (further excision/cryotherapy or topical chemotherapy) is guided by documented recurrence, not a fixed schedule.

cases.diagnosis: Ocular surface squamous neoplasia (conjunctival/corneal carcinoma in situ) in the setting of poorly controlled HIV
pearls: OSSN spans conjunctival/corneal intraepithelial neoplasia to invasive SCC—look for a gelatinous, leukoplakic limbal lesion with feeder vessels and a grayish corneal epithelial sheet.
Major risk factors: UV exposure, HPV, smoking, and immunosuppression—new/aggressive OSSN should prompt HIV testing.
Treat with wide 'no-touch' excision plus margin cryotherapy and/or topical chemotherapy (interferon, 5-FU, mitomycin C); alcohol epitheliectomy clears the cornea.
Optimize immune status (antiretroviral therapy) to reduce recurrence; an atypical 'pterygium' may actually be OSSN.

cases.title: A Dark Spot Inside the Eye Found on a Routine Visit
cases.chiefComplaint: She was told of a pigmented spot in the back of her eye during a routine exam and has noticed mild flashes and a shadow in her side vision.
cases.history: A 59-year-old woman is referred after her optometrist found a pigmented mass in the back of her right eye during a routine examination for new glasses. She reports a few weeks of intermittent photopsias (flashes) and a vague inferior shadow in the temporal field of the right eye, with mild blurring. She denies pain or redness. PMH: hypertension, hypothyroidism, and a history of multiple atypical nevi on her skin. Meds: amlodipine 5 mg daily, levothyroxine 88 mcg daily. Allergies: none. Social: light-skinned with light eyes, significant lifetime sun exposure, never smoked, social alcohol. Family history: a sister with cutaneous melanoma. No prior ocular trauma or surgery. Review of systems negative for weight loss, abdominal pain, or jaundice. She is anxious because of the family history of melanoma and the new findings. The combination of a pigmented choroidal mass, light pigmentation, and family melanoma history heightens concern for a primary uveal malignancy.
cases.exam: Visual acuity 20/25 OD, 20/20 OS. Pupils equal, no RAPD. Intraocular pressure normal. Anterior segments unremarkable; no iris lesions. Dilated fundus exam OD: an elevated, dome-shaped pigmented choroidal mass in the temporal periphery, approximately 8 mm in basal diameter with overlying clumps of orange pigment (lipofuscin) and an associated localized serous (subretinal) fluid causing a shallow exudative retinal detachment over the mass. No vitreous hemorrhage. The left fundus is normal with no nevi or masses. No proptosis; extraocular movements full.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: An elevated dome-shaped pigmented choroidal mass with overlying orange pigment and associated subretinal fluid is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Central serous chorioretinopathy
Benign choroidal nevus
Choroidal (uveal) melanoma
Choroidal hemangioma
stages.explanation: These features—an elevated/dome-shaped (often >2 mm thick) pigmented choroidal mass with overlying orange pigment (lipofuscin) and associated subretinal/serous fluid—are classic high-risk findings for choroidal (uveal) melanoma, the most common primary intraocular malignancy in adults. A benign choroidal nevus is typically flat or minimally elevated (<2 mm), lacks orange pigment and fluid, and is usually asymptomatic; orange pigment, thickness, fluid, symptoms, and margin near the disc are warning signs of transformation/malignancy. A choroidal hemangioma is an orange-red (not brown) vascular lesion. Central serous chorioretinopathy causes serous detachment but produces NO pigmented mass. The constellation here points to melanoma and mandates oncologic evaluation.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which imaging modality is most useful to characterize the lesion's internal reflectivity and measure its dimensions to support the diagnosis of uveal melanoma?
options: B-scan and standardized A-scan ocular ultrasonography
Schirmer test as the first step today
Plain skull radiograph as the first step today
Corneal topography as the first step today
stages.explanation: Ocular ultrasonography is the key diagnostic tool: B-scan demonstrates a dome- or collar-button-shaped mass with acoustic hollowness and choroidal excavation, while standardized A-scan classically shows LOW-to-medium internal reflectivity—features supporting uveal melanoma—and both allow accurate measurement of tumor height and basal diameter for staging and treatment planning. A plain skull radiograph cannot characterize an intraocular soft-tissue mass. A Schirmer test measures tear production and is irrelevant. Corneal topography maps the anterior corneal surface and has no role in evaluating a choroidal mass. Adjuncts include fundus photography, autofluorescence (orange pigment), OCT (subretinal fluid), and fluorescein/ICG angiography; ultrasonography remains central to diagnosis and monitoring.

stages.label: IMAGING & STAGING
stages.content:
B-scan ultrasonography: dome-shaped choroidal mass, height 4.2 mm, basal diameter 9.0 mm, with acoustic hollowness and choroidal excavation
Standardized A-scan: low-to-medium internal reflectivity
OCT: overlying subretinal (serous) fluid confirmed
Fundus autofluorescence: hyperautofluorescent orange pigment (lipofuscin) over the lesion
Liver function tests: within normal limits
Liver imaging (MRI abdomen): no metastatic lesions identified

stages.label: SURVEILLANCE
stages.question: Given the diagnosis of uveal melanoma, which organ is the most common site of metastasis and the focus of systemic surveillance?
options: Kidney as the immediate next step
Bone marrow in this setting
Liver
Brain as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Uveal melanoma metastasizes hematogenously, and the LIVER is by far the most common site of metastatic disease; lifelong systemic surveillance therefore centers on the liver with periodic liver function tests and hepatic imaging (ultrasound or MRI), as her normal baseline LFTs and liver MRI reflect. The brain, bone marrow, and kidney are not the predominant metastatic targets for uveal melanoma (unlike cutaneous melanoma, which more commonly involves the brain, lung, and other sites). Because metastases can appear years later, ongoing monitoring is essential, and prognostic testing (e.g., gene-expression profiling, monosomy 3/BAP1 status) helps stratify metastatic risk and surveillance intensity.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For a medium-sized uveal melanoma like hers, which treatment offers eye-preserving local tumor control with survival comparable to enucleation per the Collaborative Ocular Melanoma Study?
options: Plaque brachytherapy or other globe-sparing radiotherapy
Observation only as initial therapy
Topical chemotherapy drops as initial therapy
Immediate enucleation for all medium tumors as monotherapy
stages.explanation: For medium-sized choroidal melanomas, the Collaborative Ocular Melanoma Study showed that plaque brachytherapy (e.g., iodine-125 radioactive plaque) provides local tumor control with overall survival comparable to enucleation, making globe-sparing radiotherapy the standard option that preserves the eye; charged-particle radiotherapy is an alternative. Observation only is inappropriate for a documented growing/symptomatic melanoma with high-risk features. Topical chemotherapy drops cannot reach or treat an intraocular choroidal tumor. Enucleation is reserved for large tumors, painful blind eyes, or those with extensive optic-nerve/extraocular extension, not routinely required for medium tumors. Treatment is individualized with ocular oncology, alongside prognostic genetic testing and lifelong hepatic metastatic surveillance.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Which tumor genetic/molecular features carry the WORST prognosis (highest metastatic risk) in uveal melanoma and help stratify surveillance intensity?
options: Trisomy 21 as the immediate next step
Monosomy 3 and/or BAP1 loss
Disomy 3 with gene-expression-profile Class 1
BRCA1 mutation as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: In uveal melanoma, monosomy 3 and loss of the BAP1 tumor-suppressor (corresponding to gene-expression-profile Class 2) confer the highest risk of metastasis and the worst prognosis, justifying more intensive hepatic surveillance and consideration of clinical trials. Disomy 3 with a Class 1 gene-expression profile is associated with a comparatively LOW metastatic risk and better prognosis, the opposite of the worst-prognosis group. Trisomy 21 (Down syndrome) is a constitutional chromosomal disorder unrelated to uveal melanoma prognostication. BRCA1 mutations relate to breast/ovarian cancer risk and are not the prognostic marker used in uveal melanoma (though germline BAP1 syndrome can predispose to uveal melanoma). Prognostic testing thus tailors metastatic surveillance and counseling.

cases.diagnosis: Choroidal (uveal) melanoma, medium-sized, with exudative subretinal fluid
pearls: Warning signs of choroidal melanoma vs benign nevus: thickness >2 mm, orange pigment (lipofuscin), subretinal fluid, symptoms, and margin near the optic disc.
Ultrasonography is central: B-scan shows acoustic hollowness/choroidal excavation; A-scan shows low-to-medium internal reflectivity, and both measure tumor size.
Uveal melanoma metastasizes hematogenously, predominantly to the LIVER—lifelong surveillance with LFTs and liver imaging is essential.
Per COMS, plaque brachytherapy gives medium tumors eye-preserving control with survival comparable to enucleation; obtain prognostic gene-expression/monosomy-3 testing.

cases.title: Something Flew Into His Eye at Work
cases.chiefComplaint: He felt something hit his eye while grinding metal and now has pain, tearing, and a constant feeling that something is stuck under his lid.
cases.history: A 34-year-old man presents to the emergency department after a metal fragment flew into his right eye while he was grinding metal at work without eye protection two hours ago. He has constant foreign-body sensation, pain, tearing, and light sensitivity, and reports it feels worse when he blinks or looks down. He has tried rinsing with water at the worksite without relief. He denies decreased vision other than blurring from tearing, and denies prior eye problems. PMH: none. Meds: none. Allergies: none. Immunizations: unsure of last tetanus booster. Social: machinist, occasional alcohol, nonsmoker. Family history: noncontributory. He is anxious about damage to his vision and emphasizes he 'definitely felt it hit hard.' He has not worn safety glasses. There is no history of a high-velocity projectile beyond grinding metal, but the mechanism and 'felt it hit hard' warrant ruling out deeper penetration before assuming a superficial foreign body.
cases.exam: Visual acuity 20/30 OD (improves with blinking), 20/20 OS. Conjunctival injection of the right eye with reflex tearing and blepharospasm. Slit lamp after topical anesthetic: a small metallic fleck embedded in the superficial cornea with a surrounding early rust ring; multiple VERTICAL linear corneal epithelial abrasions ('ice-rink' tracks) staining with fluorescein, suggesting a foreign body retained under the upper lid that scratches the cornea with each blink. Anterior chamber deep and quiet, no cells, no hyphema. Seidel test negative (no aqueous leak). Pupil round and reactive; no iris defect. Fundus exam grossly normal. No proptosis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The presence of multiple VERTICAL corneal abrasions ('ice-rink tracks') points to a foreign body in a particular location. What is the essential examination maneuver?
options: Dilated fundus exam first as the first step today
Immediate orbital CT for all cases as the first step today
Gonioscopy of the anterior chamber angle as the first step today
Eyelid eversion to find
stages.explanation: Vertical, parallel corneal abrasions are caused by a foreign body lodged under the upper eyelid that drags across the cornea with each blink; everting the upper lid to inspect the superior tarsal conjunctiva and remove the retained subtarsal foreign body is the essential maneuver and is frequently curative. A dilated fundus exam is reasonable later but does not address the cause of the vertical tracks. Routine orbital CT is not needed for a clearly superficial mechanism without signs of globe penetration (Seidel negative, quiet deep chamber); CT is reserved when intraocular/intraorbital foreign body or open globe is suspected. Gonioscopy evaluates the angle for glaucoma and is irrelevant to a subtarsal foreign body.

stages.label: RULE-OUT
stages.question: Before treating this as a superficial foreign body, which finding would most increase concern for an open globe / intraocular foreign body requiring urgent ophthalmology and imaging?
options: Mild conjunctival injection as the immediate next step
A positive Seidel test, peaked/teardrop pupil, shallow anterior chamber, or visible scleral/corneal full-thickness wound
Reflex tearing and blepharospasm as the immediate next step
Foreign-body sensation that improves with topical anesthetic as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Signs of globe penetration—a positive Seidel test (fluorescein-highlighted aqueous leak), a peaked or teardrop-shaped pupil pointing toward the wound, a shallow anterior chamber, hyphema, or a visible full-thickness corneal/scleral laceration—mandate urgent ophthalmology consultation, a protective shield (no pressure), tetanus update, systemic antibiotics, and CT (avoid MRI if a metallic foreign body is possible). Reflex tearing, blepharospasm, and conjunctival injection are nonspecific surface irritation signs. Improvement of foreign-body sensation with topical anesthetic indicates a surface (corneal/conjunctival) process, reassuring against deep penetration. This patient's negative Seidel, deep quiet chamber, and round reactive pupil support a superficial injury, but the high-velocity 'felt it hit hard' history makes deliberately excluding these signs important.

stages.label: EXAM & IMAGING
stages.content:
Seidel test: negative (no aqueous leak)
Anterior chamber: deep and quiet, no cell/flare, no hyphema
Pupil: round and reactive, no iris transillumination defect
Fluorescein: vertical linear corneal abrasions plus a focal corneal epithelial defect at the foreign-body site
Upper-lid eversion: 1 mm metallic fleck on the superior tarsal conjunctiva (removed)
CT orbits (ordered for hard-impact history): no intraocular or intraorbital foreign body, intact globe

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After everting the lid and removing the subtarsal fleck, a corneal metallic foreign body with a rust ring remains. What is the appropriate management?
options: Leave the foreign body and rust ring; prescribe oral steroids as initial therapy
Patch the eye tightly and discharge without removal as initial therapy
Start topical anesthetic drops for the patient to use at home for a week as initial therapy
Remove the corneal foreign body
stages.explanation: A superficial corneal metallic foreign body is removed at the slit lamp under topical anesthesia using a fine needle or rotating burr, including the rust ring (which can be removed at the same visit or shortly after as it demarcates); afterward, prescribe a topical antibiotic to prevent infection of the resulting epithelial defect and update tetanus prophylaxis given the metallic, potentially contaminated injury. Leaving the foreign body and rust ring causes ongoing inflammation, infection risk, and scarring; oral steroids are not indicated. Tight patching is generally avoided and does not remove the foreign body. Prescribing topical anesthetics for home use is contraindicated—they impair epithelial healing and corneal sensation, risking ulceration. Oral analgesics and a cycloplegic for comfort are appropriate.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After removal and antibiotic prophylaxis, what is the appropriate disposition and prevention counseling?
options: Admit for intravenous antibiotics with appropriate follow-up
Refer for corneal transplant with appropriate follow-up
Discharge with a topical antibiotic, oral analgesia,
No follow-up needed; injury fully resolved with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Appropriate disposition is outpatient discharge on a topical antibiotic with oral analgesia and a recheck in 24-48 hours to confirm epithelial healing and address any residual rust ring (which may be easier to remove once demarcated), plus strong counseling to wear protective eyewear at work to prevent recurrence—this is an occupational, preventable injury. Saying no follow-up is needed risks missing a residual rust ring, infection, or non-healing defect. Admission for IV antibiotics is unnecessary for a superficial corneal foreign body without signs of infection or globe penetration. A corneal transplant is not indicated for a small superficial injury that heals with conservative care. Documenting visual acuity and ensuring tetanus coverage complete the visit.

cases.diagnosis: Corneal and retained subtarsal metallic foreign body with rust ring and secondary corneal abrasions
pearls: Vertical, linear 'ice-rink' corneal abrasions signal a subtarsal foreign body—always evert the upper lid to find and remove it.
Rule out open globe/intraocular foreign body (Seidel test, peaked pupil, shallow chamber); if suspected, shield the eye, give antibiotics/tetanus, and order CT (not MRI for metal).
Remove corneal metallic foreign bodies and rust rings at the slit lamp, then give a topical antibiotic and update tetanus.
Never prescribe topical anesthetics for home use—they impair healing; counsel on protective eyewear to prevent recurrence.

cases.title: Sharp Eye Pain on Waking
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent episodes of sudden, severe right-eye pain when she first opens her eyes in the morning.
cases.history: A 39-year-old woman presents with a 4-month history of recurrent right-eye attacks. The episodes begin abruptly on awakening or when she opens her eyes after a nap: sharp, stabbing pain with profuse tearing, redness, photophobia, and blurred vision lasting minutes to a few hours, then resolving. She recalls scratching that same eye on a tree branch roughly 8 months ago; the original abrasion healed in days. Episodes now recur 1-2 times weekly and seem worse after dry, air-conditioned nights. Over-the-counter artificial tears help only mildly. PMH is unremarkable. Medications: a daily multivitamin; she takes occasional ibuprofen 400 mg for the pain. No known drug allergies. She wears no contact lenses and has no prior eye surgery. Social: works as a graphic designer with long screen hours; nonsmoker; drinks socially. Family history is notable for a mother with similar morning eye pain and dry eyes. Review of systems is otherwise negative; no purulent discharge, no contact-lens use, no systemic illness.
cases.exam: Visual acuity 20/20 OD (after blinking) and 20/20 OS. Conjunctiva mildly injected OD; OS quiet. Slit-lamp of the right cornea shows a focal area of irregular, loosely adherent epithelium inferonasally that stains faintly with fluorescein; surrounding map-like lines and microcyst-like dots are visible in both corneas on retroillumination. No stromal infiltrate, no hypopyon, no satellite lesions. Anterior chamber is deep and quiet. Intraocular pressures 15 mmHg OD and 14 mmHg OS. Lids and lashes normal; no follicles. Dilated fundus exam is normal bilaterally.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of the history most strongly points toward the underlying diagnosis?
options: Sharp pain that begins specifically on opening the eyes after sleep
Gradual painless decline in vision over months in this clinical context
Pain that is worst in bright sunlight midday in this clinical context
Purulent discharge that mats the lashes overnight
stages.explanation: Pain that strikes on awakening, when the lids peel away from a poorly anchored epithelium, is the hallmark of recurrent corneal erosion. During sleep the tear film thins and lid-to-epithelium adhesion increases; on lid opening the loose epithelial sheet is torn off, reproducing an abrasion. Midday photophobia (A) is nonspecific and not pattern-defining. Purulent matting (C) suggests bacterial conjunctivitis or keratitis, not erosion. A gradual painless vision drop (D) describes cataract or refractive change, not the abrupt, episodic, painful course here. The temporal signature, sharp pain at the moment of eye opening, combined with a remote abrasion, is classic.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The bilateral map-like lines and microcysts seen on retroillumination indicate which predisposing condition?
options: Epithelial basement membrane dystrophy
Keratoconus as the primary diagnosis
Fuchs endothelial dystrophy
Band keratopathy as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Map-dot-fingerprint changes, irregular geographic (map) lines, putty-gray dots (microcysts), and fine fingerprint whorls, are the signature of epithelial basement membrane dystrophy (EBMD), the most common corneal dystrophy and a leading cause of recurrent erosion because the epithelium is poorly anchored to an abnormal basement membrane. Fuchs dystrophy (A) affects the endothelium, causing guttae and stromal edema, not epithelial maps. Keratoconus (B) causes corneal thinning and steepening with Vogt striae, not map-dot patterns. Band keratopathy (D) is calcific gray-white plaque in the interpalpebral cornea. EBMD explains both the spontaneous bilateral findings and the recurrent erosions.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for this patient's recurrent erosions?
options: Topical antiviral therapy for presumed herpetic disease as initial therapy
Immediate phototherapeutic keratectomy (PTK) as initial therapy
Topical corticosteroid four times daily for one month as initial therapy
Topical aggressive lubrication plus a hyperosmotic ointment at bedtime
stages.explanation: First-line therapy for recurrent corneal erosion is conservative: frequent daytime artificial tears and a bedtime hyperosmotic 5% NaCl ointment, which dehydrates the epithelium and tightens its adhesion overnight, the period of greatest risk. Bandage soft contact lenses are an adjunct. Topical steroids (B) are not first-line and risk delayed healing/infection in an epithelial defect. PTK (C) is reserved for refractory cases after conservative and in-office measures (debridement, anterior stromal puncture) fail. Antivirals (D) are wrong: there is no dendrite, vesicular history, or decreased corneal sensation to suggest herpes simplex keratitis. Start lubrication and nightly ointment before escalating.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If erosions recur despite 3 months of lubrication and bedtime ointment, which in-office procedure is the most appropriate next step?
options: Penetrating keratoplasty as the immediate next step
Lateral tarsorrhaphy as the immediate next step
Intravitreal antibiotic injection as the immediate next step
Anterior stromal puncture or epithelial debridement of the affected area
stages.explanation: When conservative therapy fails, the next step is a focal in-office procedure: epithelial debridement to remove the abnormal basement membrane and allow re-adhesion, and/or anterior stromal puncture, which creates micro-scars that anchor the epithelium (best for small extramacular lesions). Phototherapeutic keratectomy is the next tier if these fail. Penetrating keratoplasty (A) is far too aggressive for a superficial epithelial adhesion problem. Intravitreal antibiotics (C) treat endophthalmitis, irrelevant here. Tarsorrhaphy (D) is reserved for severe exposure or neurotrophic keratopathy, not isolated recurrent erosion. Debridement and stromal puncture are the standard escalation.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If this patient self-treats an acute attack by patching the eye tightly and the pain worsens with increasing discharge over 24 hours, what is the most important concern?
options: Optic neuritis as the leading concern
Central retinal artery occlusion
Acute angle-closure glaucoma in this presentation
Secondary microbial keratitis at the erosion site
stages.explanation: An open epithelial defect is a portal for bacteria; tight patching of a contaminated or non-sterile abrasion can promote secondary microbial keratitis, signaled by worsening pain, increasing purulent discharge, and a stromal infiltrate or hypopyon. This warrants urgent slit-lamp re-evaluation, corneal scraping/cultures, and topical broad-spectrum antibiotics, not continued patching. Angle-closure (A) presents with a mid-dilated fixed pupil, high IOP, and a hazy cornea, not purulent discharge. CRAO (C) causes sudden painless vision loss with a cherry-red spot. Optic neuritis (D) gives pain on eye movement and an afferent pupillary defect, not discharge. Suspect infection and re-examine promptly.

cases.diagnosis: Recurrent corneal erosion secondary to epithelial basement membrane dystrophy
pearls: Sharp eye pain on first opening the eyes after sleep is the classic temporal signature of recurrent corneal erosion.
Map-dot-fingerprint changes (epithelial basement membrane dystrophy) are the most common predisposing factor and are typically bilateral.
First-line therapy is lubrication plus a bedtime 5% hypertonic saline ointment; escalate to debridement/anterior stromal puncture, then PTK.
A history of a healed abrasion months earlier with recurring attacks at the same site is highly suggestive even when the cornea looks near-normal between episodes.

cases.title: Worsening Eye Pain After a Farm Injury
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive right-eye pain, redness, and blurred vision for 10 days after being struck in the eye by a corn stalk.
cases.history: A 45-year-old farm worker presents with 10 days of worsening right-eye pain, foreign-body sensation, photophobia, tearing, and declining vision. Ten days ago a dried corn stalk whipped across his open eye while he was harvesting. He saw a local urgent-care clinician who started topical moxifloxacin for a presumed bacterial corneal ulcer, but the eye has steadily worsened despite 6 days of antibiotic. He now describes a white spot on the cornea and a film at the bottom of the eye. PMH: well-controlled type 2 diabetes (metformin 1000 mg twice daily; last HbA1c 6.9%). No contact-lens use. NKDA. Social: lifelong agricultural work, frequent exposure to soil and plant matter; nonsmoker; rare alcohol. Family history noncontributory. ROS: no fever or chills, no other joint or skin complaints. He has been rubbing the eye and using a leftover steroid drop a relative gave him, which initially soothed it. No prior ocular surgery or trauma to the eye.
cases.exam: Visual acuity 20/200 OD, 20/20 OS. Marked conjunctival injection and lid edema OD. Slit-lamp: a 4 mm central-paracentral corneal infiltrate with gray-white, dry-appearing, feathery indistinct margins; several smaller satellite infiltrates surround it. Mild overlying epithelial defect. A 1.2 mm layered hypopyon is present in the anterior chamber with 3+ cell and flare. Corneal sensation is intact. No dendrites. IOP 17 mmHg OD. The left eye is entirely normal. No proptosis, no restricted motility, no preauricular node.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the mechanism and slit-lamp appearance, which diagnosis is most likely?
options: Fungal (filamentous) keratitis
Pseudomonas bacterial keratitis
Herpes simplex stromal keratitis
Acanthamoeba keratitis
stages.explanation: Trauma with plant/vegetable matter, an indolent course that worsens despite broad-spectrum antibiotics, and a dry, gray-white infiltrate with feathery margins plus satellite lesions are classic for filamentous fungal keratitis (e.g., Fusarium, Aspergillus). Prior topical steroid use accelerates fungal growth. HSV stromal keratitis (A) typically follows recurrent epithelial dendrites with reduced corneal sensation, intact here. Pseudomonas (C) causes a rapidly progressive, soupy, mucopurulent ulcer with a wet-looking infiltrate, usually in contact-lens wearers, not a slow feathery one. Acanthamoeba (D) is linked to contact lenses/water exposure and causes a ring infiltrate with pain out of proportion, not vegetable-matter trauma.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step to confirm the cause?
options: Empirically continue antibiotics and observe for 1 week as the primary diagnosis
Serum 1,3-beta-D-glucan and blood cultures as the primary diagnosis
Corneal scraping for smears
Anterior chamber paracentesis for cytology only as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Corneal scraping for microbiology is the cornerstone of infectious keratitis workup. For suspected fungus, send smears (KOH or calcofluor white, Gram, Giemsa) and plate on Sabouraud dextrose agar (plus blood/chocolate to cover bacteria). In-vivo confocal microscopy can rapidly reveal hyphae. Continuing antibiotics and observing (A) wastes time while the fungus progresses. AC paracentesis (C) is lower yield and riskier than corneal scraping for a corneal process and is reserved for deep or refractory cases. Serum beta-D-glucan and blood cultures (D) are tests for systemic/endogenous fungal disease, not localized exogenous keratitis. Scrape, stain, and culture the cornea.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Corneal scraping KOH prep: branching septate hyphae visible
Gram stain: no bacteria seen
Giemsa: filamentous fungal elements present
Sabouraud dextrose agar (48 h): early filamentous fungal growth, speciation pending
In-vivo confocal microscopy: high-reflectivity branching linear structures consistent with hyphae
Random glucose: 142 mg/dL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which is the most appropriate first-line topical antifungal therapy?
options: Topical trifluridine 1%
Topical prednisolone acetate 1%
Topical natamycin 5% suspension
Topical fortified vancomycin
stages.explanation: Topical natamycin 5% is the first-line agent for filamentous fungal keratitis (Fusarium, Aspergillus) and is the only FDA-approved topical antifungal for the eye; the MUTT trial showed natamycin superior to voriconazole for filamentous, especially Fusarium, keratitis. Vancomycin (B) is an antibacterial with no antifungal activity. Trifluridine (C) is an antiviral for HSV, irrelevant to fungus. Topical steroids (D) are contraindicated in active fungal keratitis, they potentiate fungal replication and worsen the infection (this patient already declined on a borrowed steroid). Start frequent topical natamycin and stop the steroid immediately.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Despite 2 weeks of maximal topical natamycin, the infiltrate deepens and a small perforation is threatened. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Add systemic/oral or intrastromal antifungal therapy
Begin oral acyclovir with appropriate follow-up
Discontinue all therapy and patch the eye with appropriate follow-up
Switch to topical corticosteroid to reduce scarring with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: For deep, progressive, or perforation-threatening fungal keratitis unresponsive to topical therapy, escalate with systemic antifungals (e.g., oral voriconazole) and/or intrastromal/intracameral voriconazole, and plan therapeutic penetrating keratoplasty to remove infected tissue and preserve the globe if medical therapy fails. Steroids (A) are contraindicated during active fungal infection. Stopping therapy and patching (C) abandons treatment and invites perforation and endophthalmitis. Oral acyclovir (D) treats herpes, not fungus. Deep fungal disease often needs combined medical-surgical management; involve a cornea specialist urgently for possible keratoplasty.

cases.diagnosis: Filamentous fungal keratitis (Fusarium/Aspergillus) following vegetable-matter trauma
pearls: Suspect fungal keratitis after trauma with plant/vegetable matter and when an ulcer worsens despite antibiotics, especially after inadvertent topical steroid use.
Feathery, dry, gray-white infiltrate with satellite lesions and hypopyon is the classic look; KOH/calcofluor smear and Sabouraud culture confirm hyphae.
Topical natamycin 5% is first-line for filamentous fungi (MUTT trial favored it over voriconazole for Fusarium).
Topical corticosteroids are contraindicated in active fungal keratitis; deep or perforating disease may require oral/intrastromal antifungals and therapeutic keratoplasty.

cases.title: Severe Eye Pain in a Contact-Lens Wearer
cases.chiefComplaint: Three weeks of relentless left-eye pain far exceeding what the exam seems to show, with redness and light sensitivity.
cases.history: A 25-year-old graduate student presents with 3 weeks of progressively severe left-eye pain, photophobia, tearing, and blurred vision. The pain is constant and, she emphasizes, far worse than her doctors seem to expect from looking at her eye. She is a daily soft contact-lens wearer who admits to poor hygiene: she swims and showers in her lenses, tops off rather than replaces her saline solution, and stores lenses in tap water when she runs out of solution. She was treated empirically for bacterial conjunctivitis, then HSV keratitis, with no improvement. PMH unremarkable. Medications: oral contraceptive pill; she has been using topical antibiotic and antiviral drops without benefit. NKDA. Social: competitive swimmer at the campus pool; nonsmoker; occasional alcohol. Family history noncontributory. ROS negative for fever, rash, or systemic symptoms. She reports the pain disrupts sleep and is unrelieved by oral NSAIDs. No prior ocular surgery; this is her first episode of any eye problem.
cases.exam: Visual acuity 20/80 OS, 20/20 OD. Diffuse conjunctival injection OS with marked photophobia and blepharospasm. Slit-lamp: early disease showed patchy epitheliopathy and perineural infiltrates along corneal nerves (radial keratoneuritis); now a partial gray-white stromal ring infiltrate has developed in the mid-periphery. Corneal sensation is reduced. Mild anterior-chamber reaction; no dense hypopyon. No dendritic ulcer with terminal bulbs is seen currently. The right eye and its contact-lens-wearing cornea are normal. No preauricular adenopathy, no proptosis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which combination of features most strongly suggests the diagnosis in this contact-lens wearer?
options: Vesicular skin rash in a dermatomal distribution as the first step today
Mucopurulent discharge with rapidly forming hypopyon over 24 hours in this clinical context
Painless gradual central vision loss as the first step today
Pain out of proportion to findings, perineural infiltrates,
stages.explanation: Severe pain out of proportion to clinical signs is the classic clue for Acanthamoeba keratitis, especially with contact-lens wear plus water exposure (swimming, showering, topping off solution, tap-water storage). Early radial perineural infiltrates (radial keratoneuritis) and a later stromal ring infiltrate are characteristic, and reduced corneal sensation reflects neural involvement. Mucopurulent discharge with a fast hypopyon (A) suggests bacterial (Pseudomonas) keratitis. A dermatomal vesicular rash (C) indicates herpes zoster ophthalmicus. Painless gradual central loss (D) is not infectious keratitis. The disproportionate pain, ring infiltrate, and water exposure together point to Acanthamoeba.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate test to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Empiric trial of topical steroid to assess response as the primary diagnosis
Corneal scraping for culture on non-nutrient agar overlaid with E. coli, plus stains
Conjunctival swab for viral PCR only as the primary diagnosis
Serum IgG/IgM serology for the organism as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Diagnosis relies on corneal scraping with culture on non-nutrient agar seeded with E. coli (Acanthamoeba feed on the bacteria, producing visible tracks/trophozoites), plus stains such as calcofluor white or Giemsa to reveal cysts and PCR for the genome. In-vivo confocal microscopy can show the double-walled cysts noninvasively and rapidly. Serum serology (B) is not used for this corneal infection. A viral conjunctival swab (C) tests for HSV/adenovirus, not Acanthamoeba. A steroid trial (D) is dangerous and nondiagnostic, steroids can worsen Acanthamoeba. Scrape, stain, culture on E. coli-overlaid agar, and use confocal microscopy.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
In-vivo confocal microscopy: numerous double-walled, high-reflectivity cystic structures in the corneal stroma
Calcofluor white stain: cyst walls fluoresce; bright double-walled cysts seen
Non-nutrient agar with E. coli overlay (72 h): trophozoite tracks and cysts identified
Acanthamoeba PCR: positive
HSV PCR: negative
Bacterial culture: no growth

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of first-line medical therapy for Acanthamoeba keratitis?
options: Topical natamycin 5% as initial therapy
Topical fortified tobramycin plus cefazolin as initial therapy
Topical trifluridine plus oral acyclovir as initial therapy
Topical biguanide , often combined with a diamidine
stages.explanation: First-line therapy is a topical biguanide, polyhexamethylene biguanide (PHMB) 0.02% or chlorhexidine, which is cysticidal, usually combined with a diamidine (propamidine isethionate or hexamidine) for synergy. Treatment is intensive and prolonged (months) because cysts are highly resistant. Natamycin (A) treats fungi, not Acanthamoeba. Antivirals (C) are for HSV; this patient was already misdiagnosed and failed antiviral therapy. Fortified antibacterials (D) treat bacterial ulcers and have no activity against the amoeba. Biguanide-based dual therapy is the standard backbone of Acanthamoeba treatment.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which counseling point is most important to prevent recurrence and future cases?
options: Switch to topical steroids long-term to keep the eye comfortable with appropriate follow-up
Resume normal lens wear as soon as pain improves with appropriate follow-up
It is safe to swim and shower in contact lenses if rinsed afterward with appropriate follow-up
Avoid all water exposure with contact lenses, never use tap water or top off solution,
stages.explanation: Prevention centers on water avoidance and lens hygiene: never swim, shower, or use a hot tub in lenses; never rinse or store lenses in tap water; do not top off solution (discard and use fresh disinfecting solution each time); replace lenses and the case on schedule. These behaviors are the principal modifiable risk factors for Acanthamoeba keratitis. Swimming/showering in lenses (A) is precisely the high-risk behavior to avoid. Long-term steroids (C) can worsen the infection and are not a comfort strategy. Resuming lens wear when pain eases (D) ignores that treatment lasts months and recurrence is common; the involved eye should remain out of lenses until cleared by the cornea specialist.

cases.diagnosis: Acanthamoeba keratitis in a contact-lens wearer with water exposure
pearls: Pain out of proportion to exam in a contact-lens wearer with water exposure is the red flag for Acanthamoeba keratitis.
Early radial perineural infiltrates (radial keratoneuritis) and a later ring infiltrate are characteristic; confocal microscopy shows double-walled cysts.
Culture on non-nutrient agar overlaid with E. coli; treat with a topical biguanide (PHMB/chlorhexidine) plus a diamidine for months.
It is frequently misdiagnosed as HSV or bacterial keratitis and fails those treatments; never use tap water or top off contact-lens solution.

cases.title: Painless Vision Loss with Floaters in an Immunocompromised Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Two weeks of painless, progressive blurring and floaters in the right eye with a growing blind spot.
cases.history: A 38-year-old man presents with 2 weeks of painless, progressive blurring and increasing floaters in the right eye, plus an enlarging dark spot in his vision. He was diagnosed with HIV 6 years ago but stopped antiretroviral therapy more than a year ago and has been lost to care. Over the past months he has had oral thrush, a 9 kg weight loss, and intermittent fevers. He denies eye pain or significant redness. PMH: HIV/AIDS, prior Pneumocystis pneumonia. Medications: none currently (off ART and off prophylaxis). NKDA. Social: lives alone, former injection-drug use, currently abstinent; smokes half a pack daily. Family history noncontributory. ROS: fatigue, night sweats, intermittent diarrhea; no headache, no focal weakness. He has no prior ocular history and does not wear contact lenses. He is worried because the spot in his right eye is steadily spreading toward the center of his vision.
cases.exam: Visual acuity 20/60 OD, 20/20 OS. Pupils equal, no afferent pupillary defect. Anterior segment quiet bilaterally with minimal cell; no keratic precipitates, no hypopyon. Dilated fundus OD: along the superotemporal arcade there is a confluent area of yellow-white retinal necrosis intermixed with dense flame and dot-blot hemorrhages following the vessels ('pizza-pie' appearance), with mild overlying vitreous haze and perivascular sheathing. The lesion has a granular advancing border. OS fundus normal. No retinal detachment currently. No optic disc pallor.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In a patient with advanced HIV, which fundus appearance best fits this presentation?
options: Cotton-wool spots only, scattered and transient
A pale retina with a cherry-red spot as the first step today
Multiple discrete drusen at the macula as the first step today
Yellow-white necrotizing retinitis with hemorrhages along vessels
stages.explanation: Confluent yellow-white full-thickness retinal necrosis intermixed with retinal hemorrhages tracking along the vessels, the 'pizza-pie' or 'cheese-and-ketchup' fundus, is classic for CMV retinitis in advanced HIV. It is typically painless, slowly progressive, and begins peripherally with a granular advancing border. Isolated cotton-wool spots (A) reflect HIV retinopathy/microvasculopathy and are transient and non-necrotizing. A cherry-red spot (C) indicates central retinal artery occlusion. Macular drusen (D) are age-related macular degeneration findings. The necrotizing, hemorrhagic, perivascular pattern in an AIDS patient is the hallmark of CMV retinitis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CD4 count: 28 cells/µL
HIV viral load: 410,000 copies/mL
CMV PCR (plasma): detectable, high level
WBC: 3.1 ×10³/µL
Toxoplasma IgG: negative
RPR: nonreactive
Dilated exam confirms peripheral necrotizing retinitis OD; OS normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The CD4 count of 28 cells/µL is most consistent with which point about CMV retinitis?
options: CMV retinitis requires a CD4 below 5 cells/µL as the primary diagnosis
