Atropine to dilate and immobilize the pupil as initial therapy
stages.explanation: First-line medical therapy for pigmentary glaucoma is a topical prostaglandin analog such as latanoprost to increase uveoscleral outflow and lower IOP; additional agents (beta-blockers, alpha-agonists) can be added. Because the mechanism involves reverse pupillary block with a concave iris, laser peripheral iridotomy or miotics like pilocarpine can flatten the iris and reduce iris-zonule chafing, though evidence for preventing progression is mixed. Atropine worsens the configuration by maximizing iris-lens contact and is contraindicated. Oral steroids can raise IOP and have no role. Trabeculectomy is reserved for medically uncontrolled disease, not first-line. Near work is irrelevant; vigorous exercise is the trigger.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Which statement about the natural history of this condition is most accurate to share with the patient?
options: Pigment dispersion is more common in young myopic men,
The condition is contagious and family members are at risk of catching it as the immediate next step
Pigmentary glaucoma always progresses rapidly to blindness regardless of treatment as the immediate next step
He should stop all physical exercise permanently as the immediate next step
Laser iridotomy will cure the disease and no monitoring is needed as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Pigment dispersion classically affects young, myopic men, and over time—often by the fifth or sixth decade—pigment liberation tends to decrease as the lens enlarges and the iris configuration changes, frequently making IOP easier to control; this is reassuring and accurate counseling. The disease does not invariably cause rapid blindness when treated and monitored. Permanently stopping exercise is unnecessary; instead, monitor and treat IOP, and patients can usually continue activity once pressures are controlled. It is genetic, not contagious, though relatives (like his father) may share predisposition and should be screened. Iridotomy may help but is not a cure, and lifelong monitoring is essential.

cases.diagnosis: Pigmentary glaucoma (from pigment dispersion syndrome)
pearls: Classic triad of pigment dispersion: Krukenberg spindle, radial mid-peripheral iris transillumination defects, and a concave peripheral iris; typically a young myopic man.
Reverse pupillary block lets the iris chafe on the zonules; exercise and pupil movement release pigment showers that spike IOP—document post-exercise pressures.
Treat with a prostaglandin analog first; laser peripheral iridotomy or pilocarpine can flatten the iris, and laser trabeculoplasty often works well in heavily pigmented angles.
Reassure patients that pigment release and IOP often become easier to control with age, but lifelong glaucoma monitoring is still required.

cases.title: Asymmetric High Pressure With Flaky Deposits
cases.chiefComplaint: Routine follow-up reveals high pressure in one eye; she has no visual symptoms.
cases.history: A 73-year-old woman presents for a routine eye examination; she is asymptomatic but her optometrist noted markedly elevated pressure in the right eye and referred her. She reports no pain, redness, halos, or vision change, though she admits she has not had a comprehensive dilated exam in several years. Past medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and well-controlled hypothyroidism. Medications are amlodipine 10 mg daily, atorvastatin 20 mg daily, and levothyroxine 75 mcg daily; no steroids. She has no drug allergies. She is a lifelong non-smoker, drinks tea, and is a retired schoolteacher of Northern European descent. Family history includes a mother with glaucoma and cataracts. Review of systems is unremarkable; she specifically denies headache, transient vision loss, jaw claudication, and neurologic symptoms. She mentions a prior cataract evaluation at which the surgeon commented that her 'lens supports looked weak,' which made her anxious about future cataract surgery. She drives during the day and reads without difficulty currently.
cases.exam: Best-corrected visual acuity is 20/30 OD and 20/25 OS. Slit-lamp examination of both eyes shows grayish-white flaky, dandruff-like deposits on the anterior lens capsule arranged in a classic pattern: a central disc, a clear intermediate zone, and a peripheral granular band; similar flecks are seen at the pupillary margin. There is loss of pupillary ruff and poor pupillary dilation. Phacodonesis (subtle lens tremor) is noted in the right eye, suggesting zonular weakness. Gonioscopy shows open angles with patchy dense trabecular pigmentation and a Sampaolesi line. Intraocular pressure is 34 mmHg OD and 19 mmHg OS. Dilated fundus exam reveals a cup-to-disc ratio of 0.8 OD with inferior rim thinning and 0.4 OS. There is no corneal edema, no rubeosis, and no cells or flare.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The grayish-white flaky deposits on the anterior lens capsule with a central disc, clear zone, and peripheral band are characteristic of:
options: Pigment dispersion syndrome
True exfoliation (glassblower's cataract) from infrared exposure
Anterior subcapsular cataract
Pseudoexfoliation syndrome
Vossius ring from prior trauma
stages.explanation: The target-like pattern of fibrillar grayish-white material on the anterior lens capsule (central disc, clear intermediate zone from iris friction, and peripheral granular band) plus deposits at the pupillary margin is pathognomonic for pseudoexfoliation syndrome, a systemic fibrillopathy. Pigment dispersion produces a Krukenberg spindle and radial iris transillumination, not flaky capsular material. True exfoliation is a rare delamination of the lens capsule from infrared/heat exposure, distinct from pseudoexfoliation. Anterior subcapsular cataract is an opacity within the lens, not surface flakes. A Vossius ring is imprinted iris pigment on the anterior capsule from blunt trauma, not fibrillar deposits. The classic pattern identifies pseudoexfoliation.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Why does this patient have asymmetric, markedly elevated intraocular pressure?
options: The optic nerve is compressed, raising pressure as the immediate next step
Exfoliative material
Inflammatory cells from uveitis are blocking outflow in this setting
New blood vessels have grown over the trabecular meshwork in this setting
The lens has swollen and closed the angle as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: In pseudoexfoliation, fibrillar exfoliative material plus pigment released from the iris accumulates in and damages the trabecular meshwork, decreasing aqueous outflow and producing often asymmetric, high, and fluctuating IOP—pseudoexfoliation glaucoma. The angle is open here, so the lens has not closed it (that would be phacomorphic glaucoma). There is no rubeosis, excluding neovascular glaucoma. There are no cells or flare, so uveitic outflow obstruction is not the cause. Optic nerve compression does not raise intraocular pressure. The deposition of exfoliative material and pigment in the meshwork is the established mechanism, and it explains why pseudoexfoliation glaucoma often presents with higher and more asymmetric pressures than primary open-angle glaucoma.

stages.label: ANCILLARY TESTING
stages.content:
Pachymetry: 540 µm OD, 542 µm OS
Visual field 24-2: dense superior arcuate defect OD; full OS
OCT RNFL: marked inferior thinning OD; normal OS
Gonioscopy: open angles, patchy trabecular pigment, Sampaolesi line OD>OS
Pupil dilation: poor mydriasis despite drops, both eyes

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for the elevated pressure and glaucomatous damage in the right eye?
options: Systemic corticosteroids as initial therapy
Observation with repeat pressure in one year as initial therapy
Immediate enucleation as initial therapy
Laser peripheral iridotomy as definitive cure as initial therapy
Topical IOP-lowering therapy , with selective laser trabeculoplasty as an effective option in these heavily pigmented angles
stages.explanation: Pseudoexfoliation glaucoma is treated like other open-angle glaucomas: start a topical prostaglandin analog (often very effective) and add other agents as needed; selective laser trabeculoplasty works well because the meshwork is densely pigmented, though IOP control can be less durable over time. Observation is inappropriate given an IOP of 34 mmHg with established optic nerve and field damage. Enucleation is never indicated for controllable glaucoma. Systemic steroids raise IOP and are contraindicated. Laser peripheral iridotomy treats angle closure from pupillary block, but this is an open-angle mechanism, so it is not the treatment. Aggressive, prompt pressure lowering is warranted because pseudoexfoliation glaucoma progresses faster than primary open-angle glaucoma.

stages.label: SURGICAL CONSIDERATIONS
stages.question: The patient asks about eventual cataract surgery. Which feature of pseudoexfoliation most increases her surgical risk?
options: Spontaneous resolution of the cataract as the immediate next step
Zonular weakness and poor pupillary dilation, raising the risk of lens dislocation and capsular complications
Excessive tear production as the immediate next step
A tendency to develop bacterial keratitis as the immediate next step
Increased corneal thickness as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Pseudoexfoliation weakens the zonules (causing phacodonesis, as seen here) and impairs pupillary dilation, both of which markedly raise the risk of intraoperative zonular dehiscence, capsular rupture, vitreous loss, and lens/IOL dislocation during cataract surgery; surgeons take extra precautions such as capsular tension rings and pupil-expansion devices. Tear overproduction, increased corneal thickness, and a predisposition to keratitis are not features of pseudoexfoliation that drive surgical risk. Cataracts do not spontaneously resolve. The surgeon's earlier remark about 'weak lens supports' reflects exactly this zonular instability, which is the key counseling and planning point for her future surgery.

stages.label: SYSTEMIC ASSOCIATION
stages.question: Pseudoexfoliation material is increasingly recognized as a systemic disorder. Which systemic association is most relevant to mention?
options: It predisposes to type 1 diabetes mellitus as the immediate next step
It causes chronic kidney failure in most patients as the immediate next step
It is strongly linked to colon cancer as the immediate next step
It is associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular
It has no associations outside the eye as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Pseudoexfoliation is a systemic elastosis/fibrillopathy with exfoliative material deposited in blood vessels and visceral organs; epidemiologic studies link it to cardiovascular and cerebrovascular disease and to elevated plasma homocysteine, so it is no longer considered purely ocular. It is not a primary cause of chronic kidney failure in most patients, nor is it strongly tied to colon cancer or type 1 diabetes. Saying it has no systemic associations is outdated. Recognizing the vascular associations is clinically relevant for overall risk-factor counseling, although the dominant management priority remains controlling the glaucoma and planning safe cataract surgery.

cases.diagnosis: Pseudoexfoliation glaucoma
pearls: Pseudoexfoliation shows a target-pattern of grayish-white flaky deposits on the anterior lens capsule (central disc, clear zone, peripheral band) plus pupillary-margin flecks.
Exfoliative material and pigment clog the trabecular meshwork, producing asymmetric, high, fluctuating IOP that progresses faster than primary open-angle glaucoma.
Zonular weakness (phacodonesis) and poor dilation raise the risk of capsular rupture and lens dislocation during cataract surgery—plan accordingly.
Pseudoexfoliation is systemic, with associations to cardiovascular/cerebrovascular disease and elevated homocysteine.

cases.title: A Tearful, Light-Sensitive Infant With Cloudy Eyes
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents report constant tearing, light sensitivity, and that the baby's eyes look unusually large and cloudy.
cases.history: A 5-month-old boy is brought to pediatric ophthalmology by his parents, who report several weeks of constant watering of both eyes, marked discomfort in bright light (he buries his face and squeezes his eyes shut outdoors), and frequent eyelid squeezing. They note his eyes look large and the dark part appears hazy. There is no discharge suggesting infection and no history of a cold. He was born full term by vaginal delivery without complications and has met developmental milestones. He takes no medications and has no known allergies. He is formula fed and growing well. Family history: parents are first cousins (consanguineous), and a paternal cousin had childhood eye surgery for high pressure. Pregnancy was uncomplicated with no known intrauterine infections, and prenatal care was routine. Review of systems is otherwise negative; no fever, rash, joint swelling, or developmental regression. The parents initially thought he had a blocked tear duct but became concerned when the corneas appeared progressively cloudy and the eyes seemed to enlarge over weeks.
cases.exam: Examination (aided by chloral sedation) shows the classic triad of epiphora, photophobia, and blepharospasm. Both corneas are enlarged, with horizontal corneal diameters of 13.5 mm (megalocornea/buphthalmos). The corneas show diffuse haze and curvilinear breaks in Descemet membrane (Haab striae). The globes appear enlarged. Intraocular pressure under sedation is 30 mmHg OD and 32 mmHg OS. The anterior chambers are deep. After clearing of media, the optic discs show increased cup-to-disc ratios (0.6 OU) with cupping. There is no conjunctival discharge, no corneal ulcer or fluorescein uptake suggesting abrasion, no leukocoria suggesting retinoblastoma or cataract, and no proptosis. Axial length is increased for age on ultrasound.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The triad of epiphora, photophobia, and blepharospasm in an infant with enlarging cloudy corneas should immediately raise concern for:
options: Congenital nasolacrimal duct obstruction
Corneal abrasion
Congenital cataract
Primary congenital glaucoma
Viral conjunctivitis
stages.explanation: The classic triad of epiphora, photophobia, and blepharospasm together with corneal enlargement (buphthalmos) and clouding is the hallmark presentation of primary congenital glaucoma and demands urgent evaluation. Congenital nasolacrimal duct obstruction causes tearing and discharge but not corneal enlargement, haze, photophobia from raised pressure, or Haab striae. Viral conjunctivitis causes redness and discharge, not progressive corneal enlargement. Congenital cataract causes leukocoria and a white reflex, not buphthalmos with Descemet breaks. A corneal abrasion causes acute pain and fluorescein uptake, not chronic enlargement. The combination strongly localizes to congenital glaucoma.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: The curvilinear breaks in Descemet membrane (Haab striae) and corneal enlargement in this infant are explained by:
options: Deposition of mucopolysaccharides in the cornea as the immediate next step
Stretching of the immature, elastic infant cornea
An intrauterine viral infection scarring the cornea as the immediate next step
Birth trauma from forceps delivery as the immediate next step
Vitamin A deficiency causing keratomalacia as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The infant eye is highly distensible, so chronically elevated IOP from impaired aqueous outflow stretches the cornea and sclera, producing buphthalmos, increased axial length, and characteristic horizontal or curvilinear breaks in Descemet membrane known as Haab striae; the haze results from corneal edema. Forceps trauma can cause vertical Descemet breaks but the history is a normal vaginal delivery and the breaks here are bilateral and pressure-related. Mucopolysaccharide deposition (mucopolysaccharidoses) causes diffuse clouding without the pressure triad and buphthalmos. Intrauterine infection and vitamin A deficiency cause different corneal pathologies. The pressure-driven stretching mechanism explains all the findings.

stages.label: EXAMINATION UNDER ANESTHESIA
stages.content:
Horizontal corneal diameter: 13.5 mm OD, 13.6 mm OS (normal infant <11 mm)
Intraocular pressure: 30 mmHg OD, 32 mmHg OS
Cup-to-disc ratio: 0.6 OD, 0.6 OS
Gonioscopy: high, flat iris insertion with a featureless angle (trabeculodysgenesis)
Axial length: increased for age
No cataract, no retinoblastoma, retinas attached

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive treatment for primary congenital glaucoma?
options: Corneal transplantation as initial therapy
Observation until age 2 years as initial therapy
Lifelong topical prostaglandin drops as monotherapy
Patching of the better-seeing eye as initial therapy
Surgical angle procedure to relieve the outflow obstruction
stages.explanation: Primary congenital glaucoma is fundamentally a surgical disease; angle surgery—goniotomy (if the cornea is clear enough to view the angle) or trabeculotomy—relieves the trabeculodysgenesis and is the definitive first-line treatment, often curative. Topical medications are used only as a temporizing bridge to surgery or as adjuncts, not definitive monotherapy. Corneal transplantation does not address the elevated pressure and the corneal haze often clears once IOP is controlled. Patching treats amblyopia later but does not lower pressure. Observation risks irreversible optic nerve damage and blindness; early surgery is essential. The featureless, high iris insertion confirms the angle anomaly that surgery corrects.

stages.label: DIFFERENTIAL
stages.question: Which condition is most important to distinguish from primary congenital glaucoma because it can also cause a cloudy, tearing eye in infancy?
options: Allergic conjunctivitis as the immediate next step
Ptosis as the immediate next step
Retinopathy of prematurity as the immediate next step
Strabismus as the immediate next step
Congenital nasolacrimal duct obstruction
stages.explanation: Tearing and corneal clouding in infancy have a differential that must be sorted: congenital nasolacrimal duct obstruction (tearing/discharge but normal corneal size and clear cornea), forceps birth trauma (vertical Descemet breaks, unilateral, normal IOP), and metabolic causes such as the mucopolysaccharidoses (diffuse clouding without buphthalmos). Distinguishing these from congenital glaucoma is critical because only glaucoma needs urgent IOP-lowering surgery. Allergic conjunctivitis, strabismus, and ptosis do not cause corneal clouding. Retinopathy of prematurity affects the retina in premature infants, not the cornea, and this baby was full term. Measuring IOP, corneal diameter, and examining the angle differentiate congenital glaucoma from these mimics.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Even after successful pressure-lowering surgery, what is the most important ongoing concern that determines this child's visual outcome?
options: Inability to ever wear glasses as the immediate next step
Recurrent bacterial conjunctivitis as the immediate next step
Amblyopia from corneal scarring, anisometropia/refractive error,
Development of cataract from the drops as the immediate next step
Permanent loss of color vision as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Visual outcome after congenital glaucoma surgery depends heavily on managing amblyopia: corneal scarring/astigmatism from Haab striae, high myopia from the enlarged eye, and anisometropia can all cause deprivation or refractive amblyopia, requiring refractive correction and patching, plus lifelong surveillance because IOP can rise again and the optic nerve must be monitored. Recurrent conjunctivitis is not the central issue. The drops do not typically cause cataract in this setting. Color vision loss and an inability to wear glasses are not the key concerns. Comprehensive amblyopia management and ongoing glaucoma monitoring are what ultimately preserve vision after the pressure is surgically controlled.

cases.diagnosis: Primary congenital glaucoma (buphthalmos with Haab striae)
pearls: The triad of epiphora, photophobia, and blepharospasm in an infant with an enlarging, cloudy cornea is congenital glaucoma until proven otherwise.
Chronically high IOP stretches the distensible infant eye, producing buphthalmos, increased axial length, and Haab striae (Descemet breaks).
Treatment is surgical (goniotomy or trabeculotomy) to correct the angle dysgenesis; medications are only a bridge.
Long-term vision hinges on managing amblyopia (corneal scarring, myopia, anisometropia) and lifelong IOP and optic-nerve monitoring.

cases.title: Slowly Fading Side Vision With Normal Eye Pressure
cases.chiefComplaint: She was told her optic nerves look damaged on a routine exam despite never having high eye pressure.
cases.history: A 65-year-old woman is referred to the glaucoma clinic after a routine optometry visit found suspicious-looking optic nerves and an abnormal visual field, even though her measured eye pressures have always been normal. She has noticed no symptoms, though on questioning she admits to a few near-misses while merging in traffic and bumping into door frames on her left. Past medical history includes migraine with aura, Raynaud phenomenon, and a remote episode of fainting; she also reports cold hands and feet and occasional nocturnal low blood pressure documented during a prior cardiology evaluation. Medications are a calcium channel blocker for Raynaud and as-needed sumatriptan. She has no drug allergies. She is a non-smoker, drinks coffee, and is a retired librarian. Family history is notable for a mother who went blind from 'glaucoma' despite treatment. Review of systems is otherwise negative; she denies eye pain, redness, halos, headache at presentation, jaw claudication, or scalp tenderness. She is anxious because her mother lost vision from glaucoma and wonders whether her normal pressures mean she is safe.
cases.exam: Best-corrected visual acuity is 20/20 OU. Pupils are equal; there is a subtle left relative afferent pupillary defect. Slit-lamp exam shows clear corneas, deep and quiet anterior chambers, and open angles on gonioscopy without pigment, exfoliation, or neovascularization. Intraocular pressure by Goldmann applanation is 15 mmHg OD and 16 mmHg OS at 10 a.m., and diurnal measurements over the day stay between 13 and 18 mmHg. Central corneal thickness is 525 µm OD and 522 µm OS. Dilated fundus exam reveals glaucomatous optic discs with cup-to-disc ratios of 0.8 OD and 0.85 OS, focal inferior and superior rim notching, and a small splinter (disc) hemorrhage at the inferior pole of the left optic disc. The maculae and retinal vasculature are otherwise normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given glaucomatous optic nerve damage with consistently normal intraocular pressures, what is the most appropriate next diagnostic consideration before labeling this normal-tension glaucoma?
options: Order immediate cataract surgery as the first step today
Begin antibiotics for presumed infection as the first step today
Assume measurement error and discharge the patient as the first step today
Start oral steroids empirically as the first step today
Confirm true diurnal IOP, verify central corneal thickness,
stages.explanation: Before diagnosing normal-tension glaucoma, one must confirm that IOP is genuinely normal across the diurnal cycle (pressure can spike at unmeasured times), account for a thin cornea that can underestimate true IOP, and exclude masqueraders—prior pressure spikes, intermittent angle closure, and especially non-glaucomatous causes of optic nerve cupping/field loss such as compressive lesions, ischemic optic neuropathy, or prior optic neuritis. Discharging the patient ignores real optic nerve damage. Empiric steroids, cataract surgery, and antibiotics do not address the diagnostic question. A careful exclusion process is essential because normal-tension glaucoma is partly a diagnosis of exclusion, and missing a compressive lesion would be dangerous.

stages.label: RED FLAGS
stages.question: Which feature in this case would most strongly prompt neuroimaging to rule out a compressive lesion rather than attributing everything to normal-tension glaucoma?
options: A diurnal IOP of 13–18 mmHg as the immediate next step
Central corneal thickness of 525 µm as the immediate next step
Cup-to-disc ratio of 0.8 typical of glaucoma as the immediate next step
Visual field loss or optic atrophy that respects the vertical midline, disproportionate pallor, or visual acuity loss out of proportion to cupping
A family history of glaucoma as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Findings atypical for glaucoma—field defects respecting the vertical midline (suggesting a chiasmal/retrochiasmal lesion), optic disc pallor exceeding the degree of cupping, or central acuity loss out of proportion to the structural damage—should trigger MRI to exclude a compressive or other neurologic cause. Glaucomatous field loss typically respects the horizontal midline and the disc shows cupping more than pallor. A cup-to-disc ratio of 0.8, a normal diurnal IOP, a positive family history, and a corneal thickness of 525 µm are all consistent with normal-tension glaucoma and do not by themselves demand imaging. Recognizing the atypical red flags prevents both unnecessary scans and missed tumors.

stages.label: WORKUP RESULTS
stages.content:
Humphrey 24-2 visual field: dense superior arcuate (Bjerrum) defect respecting the horizontal midline, both eyes, worse left
OCT RNFL: inferior and superior thinning, both eyes
MRI brain/orbits with contrast: normal; no compressive or chiasmal lesion
ESR 14 mm/hr, CRP 3 mg/L (normal)
24-hour ambulatory BP: nocturnal dips to 88/52 mmHg

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: With normal imaging, normal inflammatory markers, glaucomatous fields respecting the horizontal midline, and consistently normal IOP, the diagnosis is:
options: Optic neuritis in this clinical context
Normal-tension glaucoma
Giant cell arteritis
Compressive optic neuropathy
Primary open-angle glaucoma with high pressure
stages.explanation: Glaucomatous optic nerve and visual field damage (arcuate defects respecting the horizontal midline, RNFL thinning) with IOP that stays in the normal range, after excluding compressive and inflammatory causes, defines normal-tension glaucoma. Normal MRI excludes a compressive optic neuropathy, and a field defect respecting the horizontal midline argues against a chiasmal lesion. Normal ESR/CRP and absence of systemic symptoms exclude giant cell arteritis. It is not primary open-angle glaucoma with high pressure because the IOP is never elevated. Optic neuritis presents with acute painful central vision loss in a younger patient and would not produce this chronic cupping pattern. The disc hemorrhage and vasospastic profile further support normal-tension glaucoma.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of evidence-based treatment for normal-tension glaucoma?
options: Hyperbaric oxygen therapy as initial therapy
Lowering intraocular pressure by approximately 30% from baseline using drops, laser, or surgery, since IOP reduction slows progression even when starting pressure is normal
High-dose IV corticosteroids as initial therapy
Systemic anticoagulation for all patients as initial therapy
No treatment is needed because the pressure is normal as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The Collaborative Normal-Tension Glaucoma Study showed that lowering IOP by about 30% slows visual field progression even when baseline pressure is statistically normal, so the cornerstone of therapy is IOP reduction with prostaglandin analogs or other drops, laser trabeculoplasty, or surgery. Doing nothing is wrong because the disease progresses despite 'normal' pressure. Corticosteroids treat inflammatory optic neuropathies, not glaucoma, and can raise IOP. Routine anticoagulation is not standard. Hyperbaric oxygen has no established role. Additionally, addressing vascular contributors—avoiding excessive nocturnal hypotension and managing vasospasm—is reasonable adjunctive care given her Raynaud, migraine, and nocturnal BP dips.

stages.label: RISK FACTORS
stages.question: Which set of features in this patient is most characteristically associated with normal-tension glaucoma?
options: Obesity, hypertension, and high myopia as the immediate next step
A history of ocular trauma as the immediate next step
Diabetes and proliferative retinopathy as the immediate next step
Vasospastic conditions , nocturnal hypotension,
Chronic corticosteroid use as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Normal-tension glaucoma is associated with vascular dysregulation: migraine, Raynaud phenomenon, systemic and especially nocturnal hypotension (reducing optic nerve perfusion pressure), and a higher frequency of optic disc (splinter) hemorrhages, all of which this patient has. These features support a vascular/perfusion contribution to optic nerve damage beyond pressure alone. Obesity and hypertension are not its hallmark, and high myopia is more linked to other glaucoma risk. Diabetes/proliferative retinopathy points to diabetic eye disease and neovascular glaucoma. Steroid use causes steroid-induced ocular hypertension. Trauma causes angle-recession glaucoma. The vasospastic, hypotensive, disc-hemorrhage profile is the classic normal-tension glaucoma phenotype.

cases.diagnosis: Normal-tension glaucoma
pearls: Normal-tension glaucoma is glaucomatous optic neuropathy and field loss with consistently normal IOP—confirm diurnal pressure and account for thin corneas.
Exclude mimics: image (MRI) when field loss respects the vertical midline, pallor exceeds cupping, or acuity loss is disproportionate.
Lowering IOP ~30% slows progression even at normal baseline pressure (CNTGS); also address nocturnal hypotension and vasospasm.
Classic associations: migraine, Raynaud, nocturnal hypotension, and recurrent optic disc (splinter) hemorrhages.

cases.title: Double Vision When Looking to One Side
cases.chiefComplaint: Horizontal double vision and a sense that her eyes do not move together when she looks to the right.
cases.history: A 32-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with two days of horizontal double vision that is worst when she looks to the right and improves when she looks straight ahead. She also reports mild blurring and a vague off-balance feeling. Several months ago she had an episode of numbness and tingling in her right leg lasting two weeks that resolved without treatment, and a year ago she had transient painful vision loss in the left eye that recovered, neither of which she reported at the time. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable. She takes only an oral contraceptive and a daily multivitamin and has no allergies. She is a graduate student, does not smoke, drinks socially, and uses no recreational drugs. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for the diplopia, prior transient sensory symptoms, and heat-related fatigue; she denies headache, ptosis, jaw or limb fatigability, weakness now, fever, or recent infection. She notes her symptoms feel worse after a hot shower. She is otherwise healthy and physically active, which makes these episodes especially alarming to her.
cases.exam: Alert and oriented. Visual acuity is 20/20 OD and 20/25 OS, with mild left optic disc pallor from a prior event and a subtle left relative afferent pupillary defect. On testing horizontal gaze to the right, the left (adducting) eye fails to move past the midline while the right (abducting) eye demonstrates horizontal jerk nystagmus. Convergence is intact: both eyes adduct normally when focusing on a near target. The pupils are equal and reactive without anisocoria. There is no ptosis and no fatigable weakness of the lids on sustained upgaze. Facial sensation and strength are normal, hearing is intact, and the remainder of the cranial nerve exam is normal. Mild dysmetria is noted on left finger-to-nose testing. Strength is 5/5 in all extremities; reflexes are slightly brisk.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: An adduction deficit of the left eye on rightward gaze with abducting nystagmus of the right eye, but intact convergence, localizes the lesion to:
options: The left medial rectus muscle itself in this clinical context
The left medial longitudinal fasciculus
The right abducens (CN VI) nerve as the first step today
The left oculomotor (CN III) nerve in this clinical context
The neuromuscular junction as the first step today
stages.explanation: Failure of adduction in one eye with nystagmus of the abducting fellow eye, plus preserved convergence, is the signature of internuclear ophthalmoplegia caused by a lesion of the medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF), which connects the contralateral abducens nucleus to the ipsilateral medial rectus subnucleus; the side of the adduction deficit names the lesion (left INO here). A CN III palsy would also cause ptosis, a dilated pupil, and deficits in elevation/depression, none present. A CN VI palsy causes an abduction deficit, the opposite pattern. Neuromuscular junction disease (myasthenia) can mimic INO but is fatigable and pupil-sparing without true MLF localization. A medial rectus muscle problem would impair convergence too, which is intact here.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given her age, sex, prior transient neurologic episodes, and heat sensitivity, the most likely underlying cause of this internuclear ophthalmoplegia is:
options: Brainstem ischemic stroke
Myasthenia gravis
Multiple sclerosis
Thyroid eye disease
Wernicke encephalopathy
stages.explanation: A young woman with an INO plus a history of episodes separated in time and space (prior optic neuritis causing the left disc pallor and afferent defect, and a prior sensory episode) and worsening with heat (Uhthoff phenomenon) is the classic picture of multiple sclerosis; bilateral or unilateral INO in a young patient is highly suggestive of demyelination. Brainstem stroke causes INO in older patients with vascular risk factors and an abrupt, isolated event. Myasthenia can mimic INO ('pseudo-INO') but is fatigable, pupil-sparing, and lacks central signs like the afferent defect and dysmetria. Wernicke causes ophthalmoplegia with confusion and ataxia in malnourished/alcoholic patients. Thyroid eye disease causes restrictive motility with proptosis, not an MLF syndrome.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
MRI brain with contrast: T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in the dorsal pons at the MLF; additional periventricular and juxtacortical ovoid lesions, one enhancing
MRI cervical spine: a short-segment demyelinating lesion
CSF: 3 oligoclonal bands not present in serum; mildly elevated IgG index
TSH normal; acetylcholine receptor antibody negative
Visual evoked potentials: prolonged P100 latency, left eye

stages.label: CONFIRMATION
stages.question: Which finding best satisfies the diagnostic requirement of dissemination in space and time for this condition?
options: A normal MRI with only clinical symptoms as the immediate next step
A single enhancing pontine lesion alone as the immediate next step
A positive acetylcholine receptor antibody as the immediate next step
Multiple lesions in characteristic locations with both enhancing
Elevated TSH as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Multiple sclerosis diagnosis (McDonald criteria) requires dissemination in space (lesions in two or more typical CNS regions—periventricular, juxtacortical/cortical, infratentorial, spinal cord) and dissemination in time (simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing lesions, a new lesion over time, or CSF-specific oligoclonal bands as a substitute). This patient's multifocal lesions plus enhancing and non-enhancing lesions and oligoclonal bands fulfill both. A single lesion alone does not satisfy dissemination in space. A normal MRI cannot confirm MS. Elevated TSH would point to thyroid disease, and a positive acetylcholine receptor antibody would point to myasthenia—both of which are normal/negative here, supporting MS instead.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this acute demyelinating episode and the underlying disease?
options: Thymectomy as initial therapy
Emergent thrombolysis (tPA) as initial therapy
Oral pyridostigmine as initial therapy
High-dose IV corticosteroids for the acute relapse, followed by initiation of a disease-modifying therapy
Lifelong warfarin as initial therapy
stages.explanation: An acute MS relapse such as this INO is treated with high-dose intravenous corticosteroids (e.g., methylprednisolone) to speed recovery, and the patient should be started on a disease-modifying therapy to reduce future relapses and disability accrual. Pyridostigmine treats myasthenia gravis, which is excluded by the negative acetylcholine receptor antibody and central MRI findings. Thrombolysis treats acute ischemic stroke, not demyelination, and would be inappropriate and harmful here. Lifelong warfarin has no role in MS. Thymectomy is a treatment for myasthenia gravis, not multiple sclerosis. Recognizing the relapse and instituting steroids plus long-term disease modification is the correct, guideline-based approach.

stages.label: DISTINGUISHING FEATURE
stages.question: Which single bedside feature most reliably distinguishes a true MLF internuclear ophthalmoplegia from the 'pseudo-INO' of myasthenia gravis?
options: Fatigability and improvement with rest or an ice-pack, with sparing of the pupil and no central signs, point to myasthenia, whereas a true INO is non-fatigable and accompanied by other CNS findings
Whether the patient is female as the immediate next step
The presence of horizontal diplopia as the immediate next step
The presence of an afferent pupillary defect ruling out both as the immediate next step
The age of the patient as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Myasthenia gravis can perfectly mimic an INO ('pseudo-INO'), but its ophthalmoplegia is fatigable, variable, improves with rest or cooling (ice-pack test), and is accompanied by other fatigable features like ptosis—never accompanied by true central signs. A genuine MLF INO is constant (non-fatigable) and, in demyelination, sits among other CNS findings such as the afferent defect, dysmetria, and brisk reflexes seen here. Horizontal diplopia occurs in both. Age and sex shift probability but do not distinguish them at the bedside in an individual. An afferent pupillary defect indicates optic nerve disease (supporting MS), not a feature that rules out both. Fatigability is the key discriminator.

cases.diagnosis: Internuclear ophthalmoplegia due to an MLF lesion in multiple sclerosis
pearls: INO = ipsilateral adduction deficit + contralateral abducting nystagmus + preserved convergence; the lesion is in the MLF and is named by the side that fails to adduct.
Bilateral or unilateral INO in a young woman suggests multiple sclerosis; INO in an older patient with vascular risk suggests brainstem stroke.
Myasthenia can cause 'pseudo-INO'—distinguish by fatigability, ice-pack response, pupil sparing, and absence of central signs.
Treat an acute MS relapse with high-dose IV steroids and start a disease-modifying therapy; confirm dissemination in space and time (McDonald criteria).

cases.title: Droopy Eyelids and Double Vision That Worsen by Evening
cases.chiefComplaint: Drooping eyelids and intermittent double vision that are mild in the morning and worse at the end of the day.
cases.history: A 46-year-old woman presents to the clinic with several weeks of drooping eyelids and intermittent double vision. She reports that in the morning her eyes feel almost normal, but as the day goes on her lids droop and she sees double, especially when reading or driving in the evening or after watching television. Resting briefly or closing her eyes helps. She has also noticed mild difficulty keeping her eyes open during prolonged tasks. She denies pain, eye redness, headache, or vision loss in either eye independently (covering one eye eliminates the double vision). Past medical history includes autoimmune hypothyroidism. Medications are levothyroxine 88 mcg daily; she has no allergies. She is a marketing manager, married with two children, does not smoke, and drinks occasionally. Family history includes a sister with rheumatoid arthritis. Review of systems is positive for the variable ptosis and diplopia and mild fatigue; she specifically denies difficulty swallowing, slurred speech, limb weakness, shortness of breath, or chewing fatigue, and reports no bowel or bladder symptoms.
cases.exam: Best-corrected visual acuity is 20/20 OU. There is asymmetric, variable ptosis (right greater than left) that worsens after sustained upgaze for 30 seconds (fatigable ptosis). On sustained upgaze, an upward twitch of the lid is seen when she refixates to primary position (Cogan lid twitch). Extraocular movements show variable, inconstant limitations in several directions that do not fit a single cranial nerve pattern, producing binocular diplopia that resolves on monocular cover. Crucially, both pupils are equal, round, and briskly reactive (pupil-sparing), and there is no proptosis, lid retraction, chemosis, or conjunctival injection. Orbicularis oculi strength is mildly reduced. The remainder of the cranial nerve exam, facial sensation, and limb strength are normal. There is no relative afferent pupillary defect.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Fatigable, variable ptosis and binocular diplopia that worsen with use and improve with rest, with normal pupils, most strongly suggest a disorder at the level of:
options: The cavernous sinus as the first step today
The extraocular muscles from thyroid infiltration
The medial longitudinal fasciculus in this clinical context
The oculomotor (CN III) nerve as the first step today
The neuromuscular junction
stages.explanation: Fatigability (worsening with sustained use, improvement with rest), variability of the ptosis and ophthalmoparesis, a Cogan lid twitch, and crucially pupil sparing point to a postsynaptic neuromuscular junction disorder—ocular myasthenia gravis—where antibodies attack acetylcholine receptors. A CN III palsy is typically constant and, if compressive, involves the pupil; it would not fatigue. An MLF lesion causes a fixed INO pattern, not diffuse fatigable weakness. Thyroid eye disease causes restrictive, often non-fatigable motility limitation with proptosis and lid retraction, absent here. Cavernous sinus pathology produces multiple cranial neuropathies with sensory loss and is not fatigable. The fatigable, pupil-sparing, variable pattern is classic for the neuromuscular junction.

stages.label: BEDSIDE TESTING
stages.question: Which simple bedside test is most appropriate and specific to support this diagnosis in a patient with ptosis?
options: Schirmer tear test as the immediate next step
Intraocular pressure measurement as the immediate next step
Fluorescein corneal staining as the immediate next step
Color vision (Ishihara) testing as the immediate next step
The ice-pack test
stages.explanation: The ice-pack test—placing ice over the closed ptotic lid for about two minutes—improves the ptosis in myasthenia because cooling slows acetylcholinesterase activity and enhances neuromuscular transmission; a clear improvement (often ≥2 mm of lid elevation) is a simple, safe, fairly specific bedside clue. A rest test and observation for fatigability are complementary. The Schirmer test measures tear production, fluorescein staining detects corneal epithelial defects, IOP assesses glaucoma, and Ishihara testing evaluates optic nerve/color vision—none of which address neuromuscular fatigability. The ice-pack test is especially useful and avoids the cardiac risks of edrophonium, making it the best bedside maneuver for suspected ocular myasthenia.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC WORKUP
stages.content:
Ice-pack test: 3 mm improvement in right upper lid ptosis
Acetylcholine receptor (AChR) binding antibody: positive
MuSK antibody: negative
Repetitive nerve stimulation: decremental response (>10%) in orbicularis oculi
TSH and free T4: normal on levothyroxine
CT chest: no thymoma; mild residual thymic tissue

stages.label: ESSENTIAL EVALUATION
stages.question: After confirming the diagnosis, which evaluation is essential because of a well-known association with this disease?
options: Renal biopsy as the immediate next step
Carotid ultrasound as the immediate next step
Colonoscopy as the immediate next step
Bone density scan as the immediate next step
Imaging of the chest to evaluate the thymus for thymoma or hyperplasia
stages.explanation: Myasthenia gravis is strongly associated with thymic abnormalities—thymic hyperplasia in younger patients and thymoma in roughly 10–15%—so chest imaging (CT or MRI) to evaluate the thymus is essential; identifying a thymoma changes management because thymectomy is indicated. This patient's CT appropriately shows no thymoma. Colonoscopy, bone density scanning, carotid ultrasound, and renal biopsy are unrelated to the core workup of myasthenia. (Bone density may become relevant later if chronic steroids are used, but it is not the essential disease-specific evaluation.) Because thymic pathology directly affects treatment decisions, thymus imaging is a mandatory part of the initial evaluation in newly diagnosed myasthenia.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line symptomatic treatment for her ocular myasthenia?
options: Oral pyridostigmine , adding corticosteroids/immunosuppression if symptoms are inadequately controlled
Immediate plasmapheresis for all patients as initial therapy
Intravenous immunoglobulin as first-line monotherapy as initial therapy
Botulinum toxin injection to the eyelids as initial therapy
High-dose IV antibiotics as initial therapy
stages.explanation: First-line symptomatic therapy for myasthenia is pyridostigmine, an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor that increases acetylcholine at the neuromuscular junction and improves ptosis and diplopia; if control is inadequate, corticosteroids and steroid-sparing immunosuppressants are added. IVIG and plasmapheresis are reserved for myasthenic crisis or severe refractory disease, not routine ocular symptoms. Antibiotics have no role (and some antibiotics can worsen myasthenia). Botulinum toxin would weaken muscles further and is contraindicated. Stepwise therapy starting with pyridostigmine and escalating to immunotherapy as needed is the standard approach, while monitoring for progression to generalized disease.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Which statement is most important to convey to this patient about her ocular myasthenia going forward?
options: The disease is curable with a single course of antibiotics as the immediate next step
She should avoid all physical activity permanently as the immediate next step
Pupil involvement is expected and reassuring as the immediate next step

A substantial proportion of patients with purely ocular myasthenia progress to generalized disease within a couple of years, so she should report new swallowing, speech, breathing, or limb weakness urgently
Ocular symptoms always remain confined to the eyes and never generalize as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Many patients who present with purely ocular myasthenia (often around half, frequently within the first 1–2 years) generalize to involve bulbar and respiratory muscles, so the most important counseling point is to seek urgent care for new dysphagia, dysarthria, breathing difficulty, or limb weakness, which can herald a myasthenic crisis. Ocular disease does not always stay ocular. The disease is autoimmune and not cured by antibiotics. Pupil involvement is NOT expected in myasthenia—pupil sparing is a defining feature—so pupil involvement would suggest a different diagnosis. Permanently avoiding activity is unnecessary; patients should pace activity and avoid known triggers and offending medications while staying as active as tolerated.

cases.diagnosis: Ocular myasthenia gravis
pearls: Fatigable, variable ptosis and binocular diplopia that worsen with use and improve with rest, with pupil sparing, define ocular myasthenia gravis.
Useful bedside clues: ice-pack test improvement, Cogan lid twitch, and fatigability on sustained upgaze; confirm with AChR antibodies and repetitive nerve stimulation.
Always image the thymus—thymoma occurs in ~10–15% and mandates thymectomy.
First-line is pyridostigmine, escalating to steroids/immunosuppression; warn that ocular MG frequently generalizes—new bulbar/respiratory symptoms are emergencies.

cases.title: A Swollen, Bulging Eye in a Septic Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe headache, a bulging painful eye with swelling, and high fever after a worsening facial infection.
cases.history: A 40-year-old man is admitted to the ICU with high fever, severe bifrontal headache, and a rapidly worsening painful, bulging right eye. Over the past week he had a tender pustule on his nose and upper lip ('the danger triangle') that he repeatedly squeezed; in the last 48 hours he developed facial swelling, eye redness, drooping, and double vision, then confusion. Past medical history includes poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus. Medications at home were metformin and an SGLT2 inhibitor, with poor adherence. He has no known drug allergies. He is a construction worker, smokes one pack daily, and drinks alcohol on weekends. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for fever, rigors, headache, eye pain and swelling, diplopia, and lethargy; he denies cough, dysuria, or diarrhea. On arrival he is drowsy but rousable. Collateral history from his wife confirms the facial lesion preceded the eye symptoms and that he had recently been feeling 'fluish' with chills and night sweats before the eye became prominent and the second eye started to feel uncomfortable as well.
cases.exam: The patient is toxic-appearing and drowsy. The right eye shows marked proptosis, periorbital and lid edema, conjunctival chemosis with injected, engorged ('cork-screw') episcleral vessels, and near-complete ophthalmoplegia in all directions of gaze; there is ptosis. The right pupil is mid-dilated and sluggish. Sensation is decreased over the forehead and cheek (V1–V2 distribution). Funduscopy shows venous engorgement and early disc edema. Crucially, the left eye is now developing early chemosis and mild limitation of abduction, indicating spread. Facial cellulitis is present over the nose and upper lip with a draining pustule. Neck is mildly stiff; the patient is confused (GCS 13). Lungs are clear and the abdomen is soft. Skin shows no other lesions.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Proptosis, chemosis, painful ophthalmoplegia, V1–V2 sensory loss, and a sluggish pupil that is now becoming bilateral after a midface infection should immediately raise concern for:
options: Acute angle-closure glaucoma
Carotid–cavernous fistula from trauma
Thyroid eye disease in this clinical context
Cavernous sinus thrombosis
Orbital cellulitis confined to one orbit
stages.explanation: Septic cavernous sinus thrombosis is the unifying diagnosis: the cavernous sinus carries CN III, IV, V1, V2, and VI plus the internal carotid and sympathetics, so thrombosis from a spreading facial infection produces proptosis, chemosis, multidirectional ophthalmoplegia, V1–V2 sensory loss, and a pupil abnormality—and because the two cavernous sinuses communicate, signs characteristically become bilateral, as seen here. Orbital cellulitis is usually unilateral and does not spread to the other eye or cause V2 loss. Acute angle closure causes a red painful eye with high IOP but not ophthalmoplegia or sensory loss. A carotid–cavernous fistula follows trauma and is not septic. Thyroid eye disease is chronic and afebrile. Bilateral spread is the clinching feature for cavernous sinus thrombosis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC TEST
stages.question: What is the most appropriate imaging study to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Contrast-enhanced CT or MRI/MR venography of the brain
Orbital ultrasound alone as the initial study
A nuclear medicine bone scan as the initial study
Carotid duplex ultrasound as the initial study
Plain skull radiographs as the initial study
stages.explanation: Contrast-enhanced CT (with CT venography) or MRI with MR venography of the brain and cavernous sinuses is the imaging of choice; it can show filling defects, an expanded sinus with convex lateral walls, thrombosis, dilation of the superior ophthalmic vein, and associated sinusitis or intracranial complications. Plain skull films cannot assess the sinus. Orbital ultrasound is limited and cannot evaluate the cavernous sinus or brain. Carotid duplex evaluates the neck arteries, not the cavernous venous sinus. A bone scan is irrelevant here. Cross-sectional contrast imaging with dedicated venography is essential to confirm cavernous sinus thrombosis and to plan urgent treatment, including identifying the source (e.g., sinusitis or facial infection).

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 19.6 ×10³/µL with left shift
Glucose 312 mg/dL; anion-gap acidosis, beta-hydroxybutyrate elevated
Blood cultures: gram-positive cocci in clusters pending speciation
MR venography: filling defect in the right cavernous sinus extending toward the left; dilated superior ophthalmic vein
Lumbar puncture (after imaging): mildly elevated WBC, normal opening pressure
HbA1c 11.2%

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of management for septic cavernous sinus thrombosis?
options: Antifungal therapy alone without antibacterial coverage as initial therapy
Immediate enucleation of the proptotic eye as initial therapy
Prompt high-dose broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics covering Staphylococcus aureus , with source control,
Topical antibiotic eye drops only as initial therapy
Observation and oral antibiotics as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The mainstay is urgent, high-dose intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics. Because Staphylococcus aureus (often from facial furuncles, as here) is the most common organism, empiric coverage must include anti-staphylococcal therapy with MRSA coverage (e.g., vancomycin) plus broad gram-negative and anaerobic coverage, alongside source control of the facial/sinus infection. Anticoagulation is often added to limit thrombus propagation, weighing bleeding risk. Oral antibiotics or topical drops are grossly inadequate for this life-threatening infection. Enucleation does not treat the sinus thrombosis. Antifungal coverage (for mucormycosis) is important specifically in a diabetic with ketoacidosis and must be considered, but it should be added to—not replace—antibacterial therapy; empiric antibacterial coverage remains the cornerstone here given the staphylococcal facial source.

stages.label: CRITICAL DIFFERENTIAL
stages.question: Given his diabetic ketoacidosis, which additional life-threatening diagnosis must be urgently excluded?
options: Allergic rhinitis in this setting
Migraine with aura in this setting
Open-angle glaucoma in this setting
Rhino-orbital-cerebral mucormycosis
Bacterial conjunctivitis
stages.explanation: A poorly controlled diabetic in ketoacidosis with orbital/sinus signs is at high risk for rhino-orbital-cerebral mucormycosis, an angioinvasive fungal infection that also causes proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, and cranial neuropathies and can secondarily thrombose the cavernous sinus; it requires emergent endoscopy looking for black necrotic eschar, tissue biopsy, liposomal amphotericin B, and aggressive surgical debridement. Missing it is fatal. Bacterial conjunctivitis, allergic rhinitis, migraine, and open-angle glaucoma neither explain this fulminant toxic picture nor pose the same mortality. The acidotic, hyperglycemic milieu uniquely favors Mucorales, so this must be actively excluded with nasal/sinus examination and biopsy while broad antibacterial therapy continues.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Which statement about cavernous sinus thrombosis is most accurate to guide expectations and follow-up?
options: It does not require monitoring once antibiotics are started as the immediate next step
Antibiotics can be safely delayed until cultures return as the immediate next step
Even with treatment it carries significant mortality
It is a benign, self-limited condition that resolves without treatment as the immediate next step
Vision is never affected as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Cavernous sinus thrombosis remains a feared emergency: despite modern antibiotics it carries substantial mortality and a high rate of residual deficits such as persistent cranial nerve palsies and visual loss, requiring coordinated care among ophthalmology, neurology/neurosurgery, infectious disease, and ENT, with close monitoring for intracranial spread (meningitis, abscess, sepsis). It is not benign or self-limited. Vision can be severely and permanently affected from optic nerve compromise or venous congestion. Antibiotics must be started emergently and empirically—not delayed for cultures. Ongoing monitoring is mandatory for complications and treatment response. Setting realistic expectations and ensuring multidisciplinary, intensive management are central to optimizing outcomes.

cases.diagnosis: Septic cavernous sinus thrombosis
pearls: Cavernous sinus thrombosis: proptosis, chemosis, painful ophthalmoplegia (CN III, IV, VI), V1–V2 sensory loss, and a pupil abnormality—classically becoming bilateral due to intercavernous communication.
It often follows infection of the midface 'danger triangle' (furuncles squeezed) or paranasal sinuses; Staphylococcus aureus is the most common organism.
Confirm with contrast CT/MRI and MR venography; treat with urgent high-dose IV antibiotics (include MRSA coverage), source control, and consider anticoagulation.
In a diabetic with ketoacidosis, urgently exclude rhino-orbital-cerebral mucormycosis (black eschar, biopsy, amphotericin B, debridement).
